### Post by nereid on 2008-04-24
The upgrade was rather flawless (besides the point, which haunts me since Dapper, that my boot partition is too small with 32mb ;) ).

I just encountered another problem. Since the upgrade all of my KDE apps need very long to start, if they even do it. Firefox and Thunderbird work without a hitch, but using KDE without the inbuild apps is sometimes a little bit hard to do.

EDIT: I think that this has something to do with the scim-bridge. I haven't installed the scim-client for GTK and this just reinforced my first impression.

2nd EDIT: Yep, removing scim did the job. Now everything is as it should be :)

### Post by Neobuntu on 2008-04-24
Now is the time when everyones patience runs out and they just have to try the new "release". The problem is, this should (generally and recommended) only be done as a separate install; for testing. The real (production) stability comes at about 3 months after now. Yes, Three months before the next release. It's a catch, but the way things work, we get the most testing and hardening started now (New stuff with stability **and compatibility**; ) after the "official" release.

I, myself, am about to pop. I want to use a better; new version on my main systems. Yet, I write to remind myself and you, this now, is the real beta test (IMHO). Think of it as a post beta tester. Just do not, upgrade your main and only working system and expect perfection at this point. While you may skate by, experience has shown, it's a gamble at this point. 

This is especially difficult with (dist) upgrading your system that is now much used and in place. Multiple OS systems take up time and you can only upgrade an existing system. This all leads to "trying it" on your one and only system. About half the people then needing an unbeatable, clean install and about as many then giving up, on open software because of (dist)"upgrading" instead of clean installing! 

**If you are not an "expert" and you have the one install, I'm writing this for you. You'd probably be wise, to wait. See, I can hopefully work through any breakages and maybe you can too. Yet, I've learned, I don't really have the time for that. Therefore, the battle within, rages heavily. **

I sincerely hope this helps someone.

I still observe, a new (managed/Dist) up-grader can't be stable, until it is too old (3 months plus, give or take.) anymore, so what's the point? How can you upgrade to what's not really stabilized yet??? Clean installing is faster, safer and better. That includes time for easy and fast backups of your stuff. The total time is less. Often, the nature of a new Six month release, includes foundational improvements (which is a good thing). Foundational changes strongly suggest, clean installs are better. I hope the clean install has (surely) come to be more intelligent/enhanced. I still wonder if a convoluted "managed dist-upgrade" effort would be better directed at super managed clean install instead. Maybe that's what's happening. I don't know. One can hope. It seems to me, pushing the one choice of super easy and managed clean install (like "/home" retention) takes away half the development work and time. Allowing a better (k)ubuntu. That, IMHO.

This would undoubtedly be somewhat of a hybrid; by nature. Semantics and vocabulary aside, it could be better. For example: Think of a live upgrade that is also a true clean install, thus a standard starting point for **everyone** using it. Then think about it also being a super intelligent, clean installer that handles all your needed transition. Would we not **ALL** be better off then?

---

### Post by Therion on 2008-04-24
Did a fresh install but still have one, big, problem that remains unresolved: Two optical (DVD-RW) drives that HH will not recognize.  I could only do a fresh install once I removed the DVD-RW and replaced it with a DVD-ROM after reading about this problem elsewhere.  Drive one is a Plextor 716A and the other a Sony DRU190.  Both drives work fine in other PC's and were working fine under Gutsy Gibbon.

In Hardy, I can see the icon for the drive in Computer, but it won't allow me to mount the drive; whichever one is installed.  Boot times are also *signifcantly* longer with a DVD-RW installed.  Installing the DVD-ROM drive solves both issues.  But of course it's a ROM; which obviously sucks.

Long story short neither of two entirely different DVD-RW drives will mount in Hardy.  I'm stuck using an ancient, slow as molassses, DVD-ROM drive until a solution is found or I downgrade.

### Post by timhodkinson on 2008-04-24
Once I fixed a malformed line in /etc/apt/sources.list my upgrade when perfectly and quickly too.

I used the feature in Synaptic under "settings/repositories/download from/select best server" and took the one it said was fastest and then ran the Update Manager.  This was around noon EDT (UTC -4) in Canada and I got a regular download rate of about 500kbps and downloaded everything in 30mins.

Last time when upgrading to Gutsy on the first release day, I used the Main Ubuntu server and it took 12 hours.

Another great job by the Ubuntu developers!

### Post by seasleepy on 2008-04-24
I did a fresh install from the mini ISO this past weekend.  The install went fine, but I had some odd graphical glitching once I logged in to the desktop, followed by my keyboard locking up.  This happened basically any way I tried to log in graphically, even the failsafe gnome.  Running xfix didn't seem to do anything.  
I thought initially this was something caused by the mini ISO, so I burned the RC live CD and tried to install from there -- same deal.

I managed to get the computer working somehow by hitting ctrl+alt+F1 to get to a text terminal immediately after logging in to Gnome, and then flipping back and forth between text/graphics while installing the restricted Nvidia drivers.  After a reboot, I logged in and got a white screen until I toggled the desktop effects off and then on again.

It's been fine since then, but... not exactly the smoothest install for me.

### Post by Neobuntu on 2008-04-24
For me personally, I am about to kick ANY Windows to the curb; for good. I do not believe new open software users need to delete Windows! The only reason I keep it around is for comparison (on same hardware too) and to easily check if problems are hardware or software. Overall, I am sick to death, of the time wasting crap in Windows that is pointed out so starkly **only** by using Kubuntu (after being released for **about** 3 months). Nothing is perfect and the troubles may differ but overall, the time, trouble and aggravation (That all OS'es have!) is FAR less with updated (easily-inter-release-upgraded) Kubuntu, than anything else. I have tried them all! Also, whatever new advantage another fine distro may have, you can invariably add it to Kubuntu without much to-do. 

It's the best, of the best, of the best. It's a bundle extraordinare; with a free (E) ticket to ride(more online). Yet, You will have to give it a fair shake; (use newish, **stable** hardware, not your crap system) for at least Six months of regular (heavy) usage. 

Just remember, the system goes deep and does it all for you. **You don't have to be anything but a simple user!** If you don't understand why it works in such a manner, often it's for a genuinely superior, technical advantage. This is good for the end user, too.

...and that's all I got to say about that.

### Post by los_lobos on 2008-04-24
I did a fresh install of 8.04-desktop-64. Some issues I ran into:
- Had to add **irqpoll** to boot options (w/o it I got *ata3: failed to recover some devices* error) to be able to start installation
- While editing boot options I couldn't enter some normal letters like 'k' but weirdly 'K' worked. Switching from Finnish kb-set to Swedish fixed that.
- After the installation was complete the system wouldn't reboot (freeze at empty desktop, mouse pointer stopped) and had to press reset button.
- I had to edit hard disks in grub from (hd1,5) to (hd0,5). I guess installation got confused with my old PATA hd which has only one windows partition.
- Also had to add **all_generic_ide** to boot options as with previous Ubuntu versions.

E4300, Abit AB9, WD320GB SATA + 80GB PATA HD's, 8800GTS

### Post by mock on 2008-04-24
My experience;

i have had Hardy since Beta and it's been going pretty well.

The only problem was installing (had to press "esc" at boot to enable noapic and nosplash at grub ( and that might lead to some consequences as of every kernel update i must reedit grub menu.lst (enable apic spash so i get my dual core2 working.

(i used wubi to install within windows and apart from windows boot not liking the partition (says i must use fdisk to correct some things) it's OK.

My hardware is

2.4 Dualcore2 (working)
4 GB ram ( yes 64 bit now i can use all!)
8800 GTS (nvidia works)
SATA, all seems to be detected - althou i would like to have them mounted at boot time so i can use my symbolic links on them (now i have to click on them via "computer" to be able to access them.

x-fi extreme muisc (not working :( althou i heard that creative has released some specs to the alsa guys so i keep my fingers crossed)

webcam: Philips 7000 NC (works great in ekiga, but not in cheese (works but not good)

Ipod classic (plug it in and rythmbox can play my songs.

I use a usb headset for sound, and if i plug them in and out my sound control is whacked.

But mostly all of my programs seems to work, i use ccxstreamserver for my xbox, wine for some windowssoftware, cedega for some games (hard to install thou it's not a 32 bit os)

got my ip 5200 printer working with some closed drivers but thats ok.


the only bugg i got was that the default  compiz configuration make your windows spams if you move the close to the top edge (not nice) (the windows twitch and finaly settles behind the top menu)

there are some minors setbacks but for all i care Hardy is my main os since last week (i dualboot with Vista)

### Post by Baphijmm on 2008-04-24
Haven't been able to finish the upgrade yet due to a problem I had with the previous upgrade as well - the upgrade is gigantic.  At least with this upgrade it stops itself before it actually begins installing things if it realizes there isn't enough disk space (the last one, I had to go into recovery mode and move a few things out of my home folder in order to get my computer back); problem is, every time I get the required amount of space free, it claims it needs even more - the number keeps going up. ### Post by lyricmuse on 2008-04-24
The first thing I must say is....BRAVO!!!  I don't know if it's just that I'm getting better or Ubuntu is getting better or both (both).  

First here are my hardware specs:

Architecture:  64 bit
Motherboard:  Intel 945GNT
Processor(s):  Pentium 4  3gHz HT
Video:  Palit 8600GT Super+ 1GB (excellent inexpensive card)
Memory:  4 Gbyte DDR2
Soundcard:  Diamond XtremeSound - 7.1/24 bit Sound Card
Hard Drive:  160 GB Maxtor Sata 3

When I first took the jump, I installed Ubuntu 8.04 x386 on an external usb drive.  The install went without a hitch.  I then decided to shrink my XP partition and install Ubuntu alongside of XP.  It worked...kind of.  I had some issues with nvidia drivers for my card.  When I did the dual boot I did an install off of the last alpha cd and went right into updates.  I then installed the nvidia drivers and had some issues with login screen being skewed and compiz being semi-enabled.  I took a deep breath and decided to try an install of the x64 alternate cd and it went without a problem at all.  In fact, I had to double check and make sure I had actually installed the 64 bit version.  It was just so damn smooth that I couldn't believe it.

The only strange thing is that when I first installed onto the usb drive I did it in a certain order
1.  Base install from cd
2.  Install Nvidia drivers
3.  Complete update of the system
It worked flawlessly!!!

During the second install I changed the order,
1.  Base install from cd
2.  Complete update of system
3.  Install Nvidia drivers
Much to my dismay.  It wasn't completely broken, just enough to concern me regarding stability.
When I installed the 64 bit alternate cd I used the install order from my first install.  No problems whatsoever and the system is rock solid stable.  

It jsut makes Windows look stupid.  Thank you Ubuntu Team!!!

---

### Post by CatKiller on 2008-04-24
> **mock said:**
> The only problem was installing (had to press "esc" at boot to enable noapic and nosplash at grub ( and that might lead to some consequences as of every kernel update i must reedit grub menu.lst (enable apic spash so i get my dual core2 working.

If you put your changes in the Default Options of the Automagic Kernels List then they will be applied to all subsequent kernel entries in menu.lst automagically.

> SATA, all seems to be detected - althou i would like to have them mounted at boot time so i can use my symbolic links on them (now i have to click on them via "computer" to be able to access them.

You can use /etc/fstab to control which partitions are mounted at boot.

> **hollowroom said:**
> Bit of a disaster to be honest. The upgrade(from 7.10 to 8.04) is on a dual boot machine, and I had to stop it rewriting the grub menu.list as I didn't trust it to keep XP on there. (maybe I was wrong but I couldn't take the chance)

If it had broken your menu.lst (which it shouldn't have done, depending on how your menu.lst had been laid out) it's an easy fix. It's just a text file, after all. You can edit it from a live cd if you'd manage to get your Ubuntu install in an unbootable state.

> **ctsdownloads said:**
> I will have to scrap this release and wait for Ubuntu to provide a means of turning off a driver I do not want or use.

That would be /etc/modules, I believe.

I stalled Hardy beta on a spare hard drive and everything works just fine, upgrades and detecting/installing ATI restricted driver(fglrx), everything. No. I burned the CD and did a fresh install on my main drive, clicked "Hardware Drivers" in the menu and it failed to detect my ATI card. (What the??)

Anyway, after some tweaking I finally got it to work. I think I'll file a bug report....

My onboard ATI graphic card:
ATI Technologies Inc Radeon HD 3200 Graphics
(ATI 780G, integrated video)

It showed "ATI Fire GL , enabled, Not in use" (or something to that effect)

I finally got it to show "ATI accelerated graphics driver, enabled, In use". (Just right before I was ready to give up and about to do a fresh "Beta" install....)

### Post by denham2010 on 2008-04-24
I'm in the middle of an upgrade from Xubuntu 7.10 to Xubuntu 8.04. Packages downloading at the moment.

Although it did take me a while to get the process started. I found I had to revert my repositories to the default, remove all other entries (and GPG keys) even though they were disabled, before it would make any progress.

Prior to "cleaning" my sources.list, it would constantly hang on "downloading file 20 of 29" while reading the repositories, and eventually fail.

This was the same issue I had upgrading to Gutsy back in October.

But all appears to be working fine now, just have to wait a few hours for the downloads (along with everyone else).

So for those having issues with getting the process started, I suggest backing up your sources.list and then clean it out of all but the default entries (is the same for Update Manager as it is for apt-get dist-upgrade).

I'll post an update when done.

### Post by Dale61 on 2008-04-24
I upgraded from Dapper to Heron 2 weekends ago, and other than a few minor problems, I have had a trouble-free run so far.

I have had to run Amarok as my music player as Songbird failed, but that's not a big deal.  In fact, I'm starting to like Amarok better.

I am yet to experience FF3 (by all reports, I should be thankful for that) as I installed FF2 manually, so that has remained as the default browser.

I used the Dapper How-To to get NumLock to default, and my nVidia FX5200 wasn't initially recognised, but is working fine now (at 1440 x 900).

I tried to finalise some updates overnight, but the last six failed due to not being able to access the server, but I am assuming that this could be due to a server overload. ### Post by sridenour on 2008-04-24
Painfull and filled with agony, defeat and pure terror. All before final elation from success. 
Apparently my system refuses to get past 69% install when trying to use the reiserfs for root. Live CD/Alternate both crapped at the same point. So on my 3rd try I just manually part the root to ext3 and off we go. Never have I  fretted and loathed the number 69. Those final seconds before it changed to 70% were excruciatingly pleasurable as it continued to soar to 100%.

### Post by Saya on 2008-04-25
> **ThomasWM said:**
> The sound is very distorted. (Still, that's nothing to do with the upgrade!)
Preferences -> Sound -> Devices, change everything to ALSA -> Sounds, uncheck Enable software sound mixing (ESD). Restart or do pulseaudio -k.

### Post by user11 on 2008-04-25
let's see, where do I start? Upgrade was useless so I burned an ISO and installed via CD. When I got it installed, some hardware wasn't automatically found like in gutsy(had to reboot MANY times to get restricted drivers to notice wireless card, but nothing was automatic), have difficulty switching between wireless networks/internet keeps losing connection(even with a hard line!). Compiz fusion has errors when I type CCSM in terminal, reinstalling Compiz through Synaptic Package Manager brings tons of errors saying software is out of date or unverified. installing apps and programs like Envy and LimeWire brings up never before seen errors with "/E" and "package not found". Fortunately I found a workaround after hours of trouble-shooting, look for your old 7.10 gutsy CD, open CD tray, pop in CD, reboot, install. Now my laptop as well as all my other friends' laptops/ desktops work just fine-problem solved. 

Don't bother with asking me to "paste in errors" (it would be PAGES of them, plus I threw the CD in the trash) or say "All you have to do is...or you can solve by...but if you just try" -been there, done that. I hate windows, but I hate things that don't work like the way they are supposed to even more, especially if I am trying to sell people the idea of using Ubuntu. Rather stick with something that "just works" like gutsy...:mad:

### Post by disneyfriedhistory on 2008-04-25
Upgrade from Gutsy worked great on Thinkpad T30.  Only problem was getting Compiz to work.  Found the solution [here]("http://ohioloco.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=746298&page=2").
```
echo SKIP_CHECKS=yes > ~/.config/compiz/compiz-manager
```

### Post by unbuntued on 2008-04-25
I upgraded on two machines (desktop and laptop), went as follows:
* Desktop [upgrade was fairly fine]
Update time: about 1 hour
Problems:
- Had to re-install ATI driver (didn't get it to work correctly yet)
- VM-Ware doesn't seem to be working anymore
- I cannot shutdown or restart properly (the shutdown status bar gets stuck at nearly the end)

* Laptop [upgrade was more messy], problems:
Update time: about 3 hours
Problems:
- Cannot log in to GNOME anymore, getting some Error messages than nothing. I tried to uninstall and reinstall or boot GNOME part by part through the failsafe terminal, but I'm landing on an execution error (mentioning 'g_file_query_exists') when execution gnome-panel.
Current solution: I installed Xfce and am using it instead.
- It reinstall usplash, which I had to remove previously because it was wasting 3 min for booting time (see [http://www.ubuntued.com/?p=43](http://www.ubuntued.com/?p=43))
- VM-Ware doesn't seem to be working as well
- On start-up, some applications are launched two times
+ I had a problem with gutsy that caused the computer to hang up randomly, I didn't experience it yet on hardy

Update verdict: keeping 7.10 would have saved me a day of work.

### Post by pbpersson on 2008-04-25
You know......I agree with the poster who said now the real Beta testing begins and it will be rock solid in three months.  During the initial Beta tests there is no way every line of code can be exercised with every combination of hardware and software.

I just did a fresh install and really the only problem I have had THUS FAR was with the network card.  Networking worked fine from the Live CD but when I installed Ubuntu it had problems.  Oh.....and each time I rebooted, the network control panel would only find my network cards ONE time.  After that if I opened the network control panel it indicated I had no interfaces, not even the loopback.

I never did get DHCP to work - I went with a static IP just to get the machine to work.  However, it could be my network - the Fedora machine is seeding three versions of Hardy and it seems like half the planet is trying to grab it from me.  :o

On the plus side I installed the restricted drivers for the ATI Radeon 9550 card and so far so good.  The screen flickers a little when the screen saver is running but if that is my only problem that is not bad.

Right now I'm downloading 154 packages.  Tomorrow I am on vacation and I can see how flash, movies, and Lame work in Hardy.  There used to be an item called "Ubuntu restricted extras" or something like that in Synaptic and now I don't see that.  Those are the things I need - for MP3 encoding, for instance.

More excitement tomorrow - time for bed now.  Well.....after I test Freecell to make sure it is working properly.  ;)

### Post by Ubangi on 2008-04-25
I am running an AMD68 architecture with two disks: one has Vista, the other should have Ubuntu.

After many bad experiences with past upgrades, I decided to take the easiest/safest route.

I decided to sacrifice my previous Ubuntu version, despite having much software on it, for the safety of a fresh install.

Further, I decided to try wubi, lured by the promises of an easy first install, with an easy subsequent move to dedicated disk/partitions.

Here is my experience. After installing wubi, on reboot, I got an error "failed to find the partition" and Ubuntu fails to boot up. Thank god Vista still does but there is no way to remove the buggy/corrupted GRUB. Believe me, I tried all the tricks I could find but none of them have any effect.

I then attempted full install from a CD but that failed too, only adding more buggy records to the unremovable GRUB menu.

I eventually found hidden somewhere on these forums a mention that there is a "well known" bug with GRUB, which writes totally wrong disk numbers into its bootup record. Changing them manually according to the advice, I can now get wubi installation of Ubuntu 8.04 up and running.

However, I am put off by the whole experience and two problems remain:

1) I now have several defunct entries in the GRUB menu and don't know how to remove them.

2) Searching the forums further, I find that, after all, there is no way to upgrade from wubi to dedicated partitions on 8.04?

---

### Post by tjajab on 2008-04-25
I missed this sticky, so I double post:

I upgraded to Hardy yesterday, making all possible mistakes, mostly because of lack of patience: I did not backup my data, nor did I put my home folder on a separate partition, and I used the http dist-upgrade feature.

Luckily, the upgrade went very well, there were two minor things which I could correct, and one thing which I haven't yet sorted out:
1) New Firefox beta seemed to run slow and was buggy - I purged it and reinstalled it, then it worked flawless.
2) Nvidia drivers seemed to be disabled, compiz advanced desktop settings were enabled but also a bit buggy. Under System->Administration->Hardware drivers the Nvidia driver was marked enabled but said "not in use". I disabled it, enabled it and restarted and things started to work fine again.
3) Startup takes somewhat longer now (I thought it was supposed to go faster), and I see a lot of text flashing by, for example that /etc/inittab was not found (wasn't it removed by default several distros ago?) - so I guess there is something I could do to make the startup smoother, I just have no idea what.

---

### Post by rjmoerland on 2008-04-25
I updated my Acer 1350 laptop from Xubuntu 7.10 to 8.04 today, and run into some trouble. I used the update manager to upgrade to 8.04. After preparing the system and downloading all the necessary packages, it began replacing the 7.10 packages. However, at a certain point, something happened that caused the top panel of my desktop to disappear and then reappear. At that point the window showing the progress of the upgrade simply disappeared and never returned. The upgrade was not yet finished as far as I could tell. I did not want to reboot, being afraid of a having system that is only upgraded half. I opened a terminal and tried to use 
```

sudo do-release-upgrade

```
but the response was that there was no newer distribution available. Then I tried 

```

sudo aptitude update

```
and I got a message that dpkg was interrupted (indeed!) and I got the suggestion to use 
```

dpkg --configure -a

```
which I did. The package replacement restarted or resumed, and after a while all was done. After a reboot, I'm now typing this from version 8.04 :)

### Post by Circus-Killer on 2008-04-25

only one problem came up, seemes to be common problem. if you use a device not supported by network manager (eg. 3G), then network manager incorrectly reports to firefox that you're offline, so firefox always starts in offline mode.

other than having to continuously set firefox to online mode whenever i open it, everything else seems to be running smoothly.

### Post by PhilB on 2008-04-25
> **djronh said:**
> Monitor and graphics card not detected. Rest okay.
sudo dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg only reconfigures keyboard???????

My two solution's

1.Run sudo displayconfig-gtk which brings up a gui from which I could specify the required screen resolution and driver.

2.Copy xorg.conf from working ubuntu 7.10

My graphics are an onboard  sis651 and a dell 19" crt monitor.


Did an upgrade as I had quite a lot of extra stuff installed. After 9 hours and 1.2Gb later, unable to get the nVidia card and display working. Tried every method mentioned but no joy-until I found the above.
Was almost ready to go back to 7.10.
Not the best Ubuntu install experience.

Postscript-nice to have a full 1600 1200 display, but nvidia drivers still not enabled (despite both Hardware Drivers and xorg config saying they are). Seems to be unable to see the config file on boot and defaults to vga and vesa. Just about livable but no Awn and no Compiz annoying.
Also have problems with boot scripts that appear after the Ubuntu logo bar reaches the end, (never seen them before and have no idea what they are), and fsck wont continue after checking a partition-have to reboot.
Other niggles are a loss of recent Firefox bookmarks from last month or so, and certain things like Desktop Appearance and Shutdown taking up to two minutes to appear.

### Post by faithsnathan on 2008-04-25
I did a fresh install of Hardy last night.  Thankfully, all of our documents were there!  I'd backed them up to my 4GB SD card (which plugs in directly to my laptop) by first making them .tar files, and then dragging them over to the flash drive.  That worked great.

I wasn't that concerned about settings and all the free programs that you can easily download, so that's what I'm working on now.  Or that's what I thought.  How in the world do you get flash installed in the new Firefox 3 Beta 5???  I tried searching around for the answer last night, but I must have been tired or something, because I couldn't find anything.  :(

Other than that, I'm liking 8.04 pretty well.  I don't really notice that much of a change from Gutsy, though (and this could just be an initial reaction as well).  When I tried the LiveCD at work, there were some cool visual effects when you'd switch from one workspace to another.  It doesn't do that on my computer at home.  Does this have to do with graphics cards or something?

I know that all the devs worked hard on Ubuntu 8.04 ... it really shows with another great release of the number 1 Linux distro out there!!!  :)

### Post by QauNuckShin on 2008-04-25
The upgrade (from 7.10) went pretty smoothly. It took some more time than it could've because I was a little scared of deciding whether to keep some configuration files intact or not.

So far, I've noticed three problems. 
1. The thumb button on my mouse doesn't work for going backwards in firefox.
2. For some reason, my windows have red shadows. The shadow color in "Window Decoration" in "Advanced Desktop Effects Settings" is set to white. If I change the color, the shadows disappear altogether. When the computer is restarted, there are red shadows again. 
3. Some icons in my (autohidden) panel like "Mail Notification" are weird. For example, when I get mail the Mail Notification icon shows a little letter icon. When my inbox no longer has unread mail, the "empty inbox" icon is displayed over the letter icon. It seems the old icon isn't cleared or something.

No major problems. Number 1 is kind of annoying, but should be fixable with some xorg.conf-ing. I don't really know what to do about number 2. Maybe nuking my old settings would help. Number 3 is something I'm hoping will be fixed by.. someone.

### Post by rrwo on 2008-04-25
I started having some problems with a garbled display in Gutsy a month ago. The bug fix was released in Hardy, so I said tried an upgrade to Hardy. Several bad experiences:

[LIST=1]
[*]As usual, the upgrade never backs up files in /etc/. So you have to back those up manually before upgrading, and restore the settings I needed afterwards.

[*]The upgrade in the developer version decided I wanted to use Gnome rather than Xfce, so it uninstalled Xfce and installed Gnome.  I had to find the Xubuntu Hardy CD and reinstall from scratch. (The developer release for Xfce was much more stable than Gnome.)

[*]Networking has been a mess.  I use /etc/network/interfaces to manage my network, not NetworkManager.  The upgrade fails to realise this, so I had to manually recover my old interfaces file and learn how to disable NM.  Still having problems with the network daemons (networking, ifplugd) not automatically starting.

[*]Problems with authenticating WPA-EAP wireless connections (for Eduroam).

[*]It upgraded a stable, nice-working Firefox 2 to an unstable Firefox 3 which most of my addons still don't work.  It did this without warning or asking.  FF3 is very unstable on my system, and I don't think it's ready for prime time. (The speed improvement isn't noticeable, and extra features don't make the recurrent lock-ups that take my entire system down worthwhile.)

[/LIST]

There seems to be a noticeable speedup and less memory used, but these aren't enough to get me to put up with a machine that isn't working as well as it should.  Nor is eye candy like Compiz.  

Since the upgrade is affecting some critical things for me (networking!), and since each upgrade affects something critical, I may just switch to another distribution if no fix is released.

---

### Post by crosswalker21 on 2008-04-25
I've been running hardy since beta 1, so my upgrade has been slow and steady, but mostly without flaws.  Everything seems to work better, with a few notable exceptions:
[LIST=1]
[*]Pulseaudio is not quite there yet.  There are a few issues regarding volume control and device setup where alsa apparently still has ultimate control.  This causes mild confusion for a new user.
[*]Since the official release, I haven't been able to update any of my ubuntu.com based repos, and thus, haven't been able to update much of anything.  I assume this is just a bandwidth issue and will be shortly resolved.
[*]FireFox3 beta 5 likes to crash when I close tabs while others are loading.  I don't think this is Ubuntu's fault, but it's a little annoying, and makes the experience a little iffy.
[/LIST]
All-in-all, I'm quite happy with this release, and can't wait to see how some of the back-end changes will affect user-experience in future builds.  Great job everybody!

### Post by matja on 2008-04-25
Hi all,

I experienced one critical error, but it was easy to solve (thanks to [[COLOR="Sienna"]The Fold[/COLOR]]("http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=138013")). The installation went flawless with Hardy 64-bit on a Dell Vostro 400 desktop machine, using software raid 1 on two sata drives. Upon booting into the newly installed system the splash screen newer went away, it just kept sliding. Booting in failsafe mode, the terminal read
```

ata1.00: **qc timeout** (cmd 0x27)
ata1.00: failed to read native max address (err_mask=0x4)
ata1.00: revalidation failed (errno=-5)

```
After searching the forums I found [[COLOR="Sienna"]this[/COLOR]]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=748585") post by The Fold who got the same error and he solved it by adding **irqpoll** to the kernel boot options. This solved the issue for me as well.

Since I didn't have this problem in Gutsy, I thought I'd share it here if anyone else encounters a similar error.

Oh, and a huge thanks to the Ubuntu team, keep up the great work!

---

### Post by jr3us on 2008-04-25
Install of 8.04 LTS on Compaq Presario V2312US:

The first try at installing resulted in a blank black screen. Knowing that this has the ATI Radeon Express 200M, I rebooted and used the Low video mode.

I then got the 1024x768 graphics screen on my 1200x800 wide screen and began the install. 

The install went well and rebooted to the same graphic screen as above.

I have the broadcom wireless as shown below, and so connected to the wired network connection. I then enabled the ATI graphics, and once complete and rebooting, I had full resolution as well as the default sliding desktop 3d effects. 

Still in wired network mode, I then enabled the broadcom wireless as well as the softmodem. I joined the wireless network for my house with WPA encryption and had no problems with that. I disconnected the wired network connection.

I proceeded to then install the gsynaptics utility for the touchpad, and received the message that SHMConfig wasn't configured.

To remedy this, I put edited the /etc/X11/xorg.conf section as below:

Section "InputDevice"
        Identifier      "Synaptics Touchpad"
        Driver          "synaptics"
        Option          "SendCoreEvents"        "true"
        Option          "Device"        "/dev/psaux"
        Option          "Protocol"      "auto-dev"
        Option          "HorizEdgeScroll"       "0"
        Option          "SHMConfig"             "on"
EndSection

The line with "SHMConfig" is the new information. Logging out of the X session and logging back in then fixed this.

My lspci follows:

00:14.6 Modem: ATI Technologies Inc SB400 AC'97 Modem Controller (rev 02)
00:18.0 Host bridge: Advanced Micro Devices [AMD] K8 [Athlon64/Opteron] HyperTransport Technology Configuration
00:18.1 Host bridge: Advanced Micro Devices [AMD] K8 [Athlon64/Opteron] Address Map
00:18.2 Host bridge: Advanced Micro Devices [AMD] K8 [Athlon64/Opteron] DRAM Controller
00:18.3 Host bridge: Advanced Micro Devices [AMD] K8 [Athlon64/Opteron] Miscellaneous Control
01:05.0 VGA compatible controller: ATI Technologies Inc Radeon XPRESS 200M 5955 (PCIE)
05:00.0 Ethernet controller: Realtek Semiconductor Co., Ltd. RTL-8139/8139C/8139C+ (rev 10)
05:02.0 Network controller: Broadcom Corporation BCM4318 [AirForce One 54g] 802.11g Wireless LAN Controller (rev 02)
05:09.0 CardBus bridge: Texas Instruments PCIxx21/x515 Cardbus Controller
05:09.2 FireWire (IEEE 1394): Texas Instruments OHCI Compliant IEEE 1394 Host Controller
05:09.3 Mass storage controller: Texas Instruments PCIxx21 Integrated FlashMedia Controller
05:09.4 SD Host controller: Texas Instruments PCI6411/6421/6611/6621/7411/7421/7611/7621 Secure Digital Controller

### Post by Mopana on 2008-04-25
only major problem was with my synaptic touchpad not working. I have a dell 1420, and I had modified my xorg.conf to increase cursor speed, etc. I couldn't get any response from the touchpad until I commented out one line in xorg

```

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Synaptics Touchpad"
	Driver		"synaptics"
	#Option		"SendCoreEvents"	"true"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/psaux"
	Option		"Protocol"	"auto-dev"
	Option		"HorizScrollDelta"	"50"
	Option		"SHMConfig"	"true"
	#Option		"AlwaysCore"
	Option		"LeftEdge"	"120"
	Option		"RightEdge"	"840"
	Option		"TopEdge"	"130"
	Option		"BottomEdge"	"640"
	Option		"FingerLow"	"7"
	Option		"FingerHigh"	"8"
	Option		"MaxTapTime"	"0"
	Option		"MaxTapMove"	"0"
	Option		"ClickTime"	"0"
	Option		"EmulateMidButtonTime"	"75"
	Option		"VertScrollDelta"	"20"
	Option		"MinSpeed"	"0.60"
	Option		"MaxSpeed"	"1.10"
	Option		"AccelFactor"	"0.030"
	Option		"EdgeMotionMinSpeed"	"200"
	Option		"EdgeMotionMaxSpeed"	"200"
	Option		"UpDownScrolling"	"1"
	Option		"CircularScrolling"	"1"
	Option		"CircScrollDelta"	"0.1"
	Option		"CircScrollTrigger"	"2"
	Option		"Emulate3Buttons"	"on"
EndSection
```

### Post by DigitaLink on 2008-04-25
Wish I could say things went well, but so far, no joy.  I tried running the upgrade when I popped the alternate CD in, and the installer crashed and bailed on me at step 2.  

So I popped in the livecd and decided to go with a fresh install.  Apparently that's all well and good so long as you don't try changing your partitions before/during the installation.  First time, I changed some partitions (manual selection, not guided) and on reboot it gave me some disk error about a bad superblock, and following the directions to run fsck did nothing.

Back to the desktop CD.  Run the installer again.  Change the troubled partition from reiser back to ext3, run install again.  Now I have the GRUB Error 17 on reboot.  No joy.

So at lunch today I ran the live desktop, used gparted to set/format the partitions, and fired up the installer before I left, leaving it to copy files and do all that joy while I'm at work this afternoon.  We'll see if it gets it right this time when I get home tonight.

What sucks is that I'm out of town on business all weekend, and can't spend any time with it.  This is the first time I've EVER had a problem with an Ubuntu install, and it surprised me that it gave me problems.  Guess I should have just did the old apt-get upgrade distro routine.  Ah well.  This way I cleared out an old unused partition and an unused Fedora 8 install too.  Fresh starts are fun anyway.

Here's hoping this round goes better than the last one!

### Post by jmacak on 2008-04-25
Hi 

I installed into another partition and am dual booting with 7.10.

The 8.04 installation went fine, but when I tried to boot into 7.10, I found out that my fstab was now incorrect and caused the mounting of all the mountpoints to fail.

I manually edited the fstab, replacing the UUID= parameters that were in there with /dev/sxxx 

Apparently the UUIDs changed with the installation?

cheers

joe in seattle

### Post by jimbo99 on 2008-04-25
My experience during the whole development of 8.04 was not very good primarily because I use laptops to test this stuff on.

My first experience with an upgrade was that nothing worked.  The mouse stopped working, the compiz didn't work, sound didn't work, wifi didn't work.

To this point none of these still work.

I've tried on two of my laptops.  One is a Compaq R3000 and the other is an HP zv5000.

The official release CD has exactly the same problems as the beta and as the RC.  Reports were made throughout beta testing on the state of compiz, wifi and the lack of sound but nothing was done.  On the failed install that caused the laptop to essentially become worthless when I wrote it up all I got from the development people was that there wasn't enough info.  What was I supposed to do?  I couldn't give them more details.  I simply wrote down what happened and the messages I got.  Other than that the notebook was totally non-functional.  I had to wipe my install and start over.  It was obvious from their response that the hadn't read the write-up nor any of my follow-up comments.

That makes their reporting tool almost totally worthless.

With the official release of 8.04 I went and tried the wifi thing with and it HARD LOCKED my computer.  Totally and utterly hard locked.  I Tried the live CD on another ZV5000 and it did the same thing.  It hard locked my computer, completely.

In my testing when I tested sound what I found was this.  If I set either of these notebooks up with anything other that OSS sound sound will not work, period.  That's it.  There's no reprieve.

If I get sound working and I then go and set up the notebook to do the auto login you hear sound at start (the start up sound) which is cut short, then after that no sound at all. Period.  If I try to play a sound file the CPU utilization goes into 100% use.  If I quit or kill the processes related to the sound play back program it stays at 100% cpu utilization.

If I go and switch it back to manual log in it works again.  Though throughout I often have to change the audio device properties back and forth in various programs used to play back audio or video.

So, if I have OSS sound working then set to auto login it stops working and the cpu utilization is 100% even if I kill the apps related to it (or even close them down normally).  If I set it back to manual login then the sound plays.

This is a 100% true situation for multiple notebooks.  If I am successful at playing sound then often, in programs like VLC, I have to adjust the audio properties and usually from video file to video file I may have to switch back and forth.  So, one file requires I switch, and the next file requires I switch back.

WiFi has become all but broken.  I can't use it at all.  It hard locks my computer up to load the restricted drivers.  No ndiswrapper will work.

Compiz is a total gonner.  It doesn't work at all any more.  Prior to that it worked well.  I had it working on both the laptops with 7.10.  In fact, it worked and I didn't have to change a thing to make it work.  It was one of the main reasons I chose to install it on the laptops, because it just worked.

So, my story so far is that it has too many show stoppers for me.  I haven't even gotten into actually trying to make it function, with the exception of trying to get CIFS working. It does work but the implementation of CIFS perm connections causes totally odd behavior on shut down, getting CIFS errors when I go to shut down the book.

They lacked attention to detail when putting together the laptop support in 8.04.  It doesn't matter that these are p4 class notebooks because they are solid units capable of running most anything under windows including to some extent gaming.  A laptop that powerful shouldn't be neglected.

Laptops support has been gravely neglected in 8.04 and it has actually taken large strides backwards.

---

### Post by ralyon on 2008-04-25
I had to do a fresh install because I was switching from UE 1.7 to 8.04 x64. I kept my home directory though.

I did the upgrade last weekend so it was rc and this problem may be fixed now, but when I booted, HAL crashed. I had to remove my pcHDTV card, install, upgrade the kernel to the rt and then I was able to put my pcHDTV card back in.

Majority of the rest of the install went fine but I ended up with a few problems. First, the restricted drivers are not available now, at least not from the menu. I got passed this by using envy to build the latest so graphics work great now.

Second is my microphone problem has not gone away (problem since feisty) and I'm still getting an error about gconfaudiosink profile=chat when I try and test it. I'll need to put some more time into that one of these days.

Other that that everything works great. Mythtv .21 now works better with my ps3 over upnp and wine seems to run better as well (HoM&M 5 TotE doesn't have any more audio stutters).

My first experience with an upgrade was that nothing worked.  The mouse stopped working, the compiz didn't work, sound didn't work, wifi didn't work.

To this point none of these still work.

I've tried on two of my laptops.  One is a Compaq R3000 and the other is an HP zv5000.

The official release CD has exactly the same problems as the beta and as the RC.  Reports were made throughout beta testing on the state of compiz, wifi and the lack of sound but nothing was done.  On the failed install that caused the laptop to essentially become worthless when I wrote it up all I got from the development people was that there wasn't enough info.  What was I supposed to do?  I couldn't give them more details.  I simply wrote down what happened and the messages I got.  Other than that the notebook was totally non-functional.  I had to wipe my install and start over.  It was obvious from their response that the hadn't read the write-up nor any of my follow-up comments.

That makes their reporting tool almost totally worthless.

With the official release of 8.04 I went and tried the wifi thing with and it HARD LOCKED my computer.  Totally and utterly hard locked.  I Tried the live CD on another ZV5000 and it did the same thing.  It hard locked my computer, completely.

In my testing when I tested sound what I found was this.  If I set either of these notebooks up with anything other that OSS sound sound will not work, period.  That's it.  There's no reprieve.

If I get sound working and I then go and set up the notebook to do the auto login you hear sound at start (the start up sound) which is cut short, then after that no sound at all. Period.  If I try to play a sound file the CPU utilization goes into 100% use.  If I quit or kill the processes related to the sound play back program it stays at 100% cpu utilization.

If I go and switch it back to manual log in it works again.  Though throughout I often have to change the audio device properties back and forth in various programs used to play back audio or video.

So, if I have OSS sound working then set to auto login it stops working and the cpu utilization is 100% even if I kill the apps related to it (or even close them down normally).  If I set it back to manual login then the sound plays.

This is a 100% true situation for multiple notebooks.  If I am successful at playing sound then often, in programs like VLC, I have to adjust the audio properties and usually from video file to video file I may have to switch back and forth.  So, one file requires I switch, and the next file requires I switch back.

WiFi has become all but broken.  I can't use it at all.  It hard locks my computer up to load the restricted drivers.  No ndiswrapper will work.

Compiz is a total gonner.  It doesn't work at all any more.  Prior to that it worked well.  I had it working on both the laptops with 7.10.  In fact, it worked and I didn't have to change a thing to make it work.  It was one of the main reasons I chose to install it on the laptops, because it just worked.

So, my story so far is that it has too many show stoppers for me.  I haven't even gotten into actually trying to make it function, with the exception of trying to get CIFS working. It does work but the implementation of CIFS perm connections causes totally odd behavior on shut down, getting CIFS errors when I go to shut down the book.

They lacked attention to detail when putting together the laptop support in 8.04.  It doesn't matter that these are p4 class notebooks because they are solid units capable of running most anything under windows including to some extent gaming.  A laptop that powerful shouldn't be neglected.

Laptops support has been gravely neglected in 8.04 and it has actually taken large strides backwards.

Quick google for the HP ZV5000 shows that with all distributions, ndiswrapper is required for the wireless.  Can not blame Ubuntu/Linux for the lack of hardware drivers...

There is also mention of problems with sound.

[Ubuntu Forums]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=194713")

I did not look into the Compaq.

> Laptops support has been gravely neglected in 8.04 and it has actually taken large strides backwards.

I think this statement is made more out of frustration with your hardware not supporting Linux rather than actual fact.  I use Ubuntu on more than one laptop and have 0 problems with hardware.  Considering the thousands (millions?) of people using Ubuntu, the problem posts are a very small percentage and most of those problems are caused by hardware.

---

I was crossing my fingers and I was happy to see that Hardy Heron came though and I was able to completely boot to desktop without any graphic issues! ### Post by linuxd00 on 2008-04-25
Thinkpad X31 upgrading from 7.10 gutsy to 8.04 HArdy heron:

1.- tried using update manager: manager froze forever when "checking manager"
2.- downloaded Alternate CD: ./cdromupgrade crashed ("cant find bla bla")
3.- tried synaptic: it froze too.. whatahell?

what worked for me: commandline

sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get dist-upgrade (or similar)


you can add the CDRom (or the iso if its mounted) to the sources using the sources manager(never add it by editing the sources.list by hand!!)
The updater will not use it though.. it will prefer the online sources.
then you can disable by hand all online sources on sources.list.

but then the upgrade wanted to uninstall all my programs which were not on the CD.(some 400 packages!)


so i went to uni and did the ****.


All went well except for Compiz was gone as well as my DRI.

i fixed it by adding SKIP_CHECKS=yes to the compiz configuration file

now only thing to fix is the log out button which takes aages to show the logout dialog... until then it will disable all input devices :\

[I]The device /dev/sdb1 doesn't exist.
ntfsresize v1.1.3.1 (libntfs 9:0:0)
ERROR(2): Failed to check '/dev/sdb1' mount state: No such file or directory.

Unable to read the contents of this filesystem!

### Post by MorningWood on 2008-04-25

### Post by rblyngso on 2008-04-25
So far one major problem,

For some reason Hardy now sees my IDE like SCSI/SATA - i.e. hda<foo> is now sda<foo>. It seems that the new sda style has the same limitations as for SCSI/SATA - for SCSI/SATA only up to 11 logical partitions named sda5-sda15 are supported.

Now I am not able to mount my old /dev/hda16 /dev/hda17 /dev/hda18 /dev/hda19 and /dev/hda20 - and Hardy hangs during boot in a maintenance console :(

I know - use LVM - I will - but right now I need to get in contact with the aforementioned partitions.

### Post by esdaniel on 2008-04-25
Upgrade of 64bit Kubuntu Feisty began well - couldn't leave unattended though... having to confirm things along the way for the usual suspects: squid, php, eclipse and a few other .conf files where custom configs could not be replicated in the new files (why? thus far the process albeit manual is satisfactory I believe however where's the smarts that automates the merge of custom config with new conf files and only tells you of the conflicting configs between old and new - an improvement for later perhaps?). FTP download of Flash 2.9MB package from Adobe was a total joke adding 17mins to the upgrade duration.

Now kicking myself I did not wait a couple of weeks as I'm in display hell presently, this has happened each time I upgrade and has been more a learning exercise about Ubuntu/'nix for me so at the beginning I was confident I'd know / discover what to fix, I was wrong.  

First off after reboot was the message I did not have displayconfig-gtk installed to configure video drivers.  Installed that then found out that this handy tool was not doing what it should.  It could not test the config selected when I chose the nvidia driver.

A while back I remember restricted drivers coming out later than the kernel release and I wonder if this is case now?  After experimenting with envyng as a last resort (last resort because warnings in forum posts in past exclaimed this route was fraught as that of Automatix) still getting no joy.

I've got paranoid that my Grub2 (note '2') has not correctly updated to the latest kernel installed, feel free to confirm the correct one is 2.6.22.14 (generic).

So I'm not sure where the disconnect is happening between my nvidia drivers, xorg.conf and Kubuntu - I'm able to get it up on basic vesa mode using dpkg to configure X and that allows me to inspect using the GUI but currently stumped as to what needs tweaking.:confused:

** UPDATE **

Having slept on this and now in a better state of mind... was right about the Grub2 config not updated to 24.16 from 22.14 - as I use Grub2 I guess.  So, at boot went into the Grub editor and altered 22.14 to 24.16 for the two lines where it appears eh voila I'm up and running again.

Compiz settings have been borked though I suspect that's perhaps due to the screw-up I was taken down when first messing around with the video settings earlier after the upgrade, plus as running KDE needed to add new panel applets (task/pager) for Compiz.

Also - I echo other peoples thoughts on FF3 - helping spread love for your Mozilla chums by promoting FF3 should not be at the expense of a stable release and who ever took that decision needs to think long and hard about why they took it and whether they were doing it in the interest of the community, Mozilla or some other motivation - I'm suspicious for sure but that aside many of us are using plenty o' plugins with FF2 and as FF3 is STILL beta, thus not all our plugins are ready for FF3 yet, I suspect back scratching taking place because it's not about FF3 beta being stable, it's about your user base upgrading to Hardy as much as doing clean installs - I was not amused to see someone post 'the secret to Hardy is a clean install' - that rather defeats the object and purpose of the hard work community devs do on the upgrade tooling, doesn't it?

### Post by jimbo99 on 2008-04-25
> **Perpetual said:**
> Quick google for the HP ZV5000 shows that with all distributions, ndiswrapper is required for the wireless.  Can not blame Ubuntu/Linux for the lack of hardware drivers...

There is also mention of problems with sound.

[Ubuntu Forums]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=194713")

I did not look into the Compaq.



I think this statement is made more out of frustration with your hardware not supporting Linux rather than actual fact.  I use Ubuntu on more than one laptop and have 0 problems with hardware.  Considering the thousands (millions?) of people using Ubuntu, the problem posts are a very small percentage and most of those problems are caused by hardware.


Actually the statement is out of experience as laptop issues have been ongoing and getting worse for the past 2 releases.  One expects them to get better not worse.

Also, just like PCs there are chipsets that control various functionality.  The wireless card is a plug and play device.  If you turn the laptop over you'll find the slot.  Open it and you can remove the wifi.

What's happened is that Canonical must have decided that they would not continue forward progression on certain models.  This is concluded because they appear to have just let all fixes go.

WiFi is important but if you read my full post you would have seen that compiz no longer works and that sound is haphazard at best and it requires stuff be done that no "human being" would really be interested in doing, as most of us human beings just want to use it, not dicker around with settings, debug 100% cpu utilization issues, figure out why our COMPIZ doesn't work work when it worked before.

My recommendation was that no one with a laptop should attempt this upgrade unless it is much newer, even though the zv5000 and the r3000 are solid systems.

Also, I stated that ndiswrapper DOES NOT WORK.  I tried it over and over.  I wrote up updates about my success rate with ndiswrapper in posts throughout this year and the result is that maybe 25% success rate with any/all ndiswrapper attempts.  That's a 75% failure rate.

Canonical dropped the ball on us and are essentially letting these laptops (solid machines) fall to obscurity.  This should never ever happen.

As I said, it also HARD LOCKED both machines, even when running the LiveCD.  It only HARD LOCKED when I attempted to install the WiFi.  If I don't attempt it won't lock up.

I also noted that these same facilities:  sound, graphics, wifi, etc all work well under Windows, so the machine aren't the issue.

Also, the average mom and pop to which this should be targeted as they are human beings too, would never even attempt to deal with ndiswrapper and hence, Canonical needs to put much more effort into resolving issues.

There were also many issues I didn't write up, so don't get me started.  Most of these were similar to posts written before the official release, and it, frankly, is laughable that they did a RC only 2-3 days before they officially released it.  And if they don't consider these types of issues as show stoppers then nothing is to them.

I've counted and the vast majority of people that installed Hardy have had problems, and quite a few people said they had a flaweless install except they had to do this or that.  That's not flawless if you ask me.

So, if you are addressing the wifi issues, then why not address the sound issues?  Why not address the fact that this thing HARD LOCKED linux right off the LiveCD?  Why not address the fact that Compiz worked under 7.10 but won't work at all in 8.04 (on both laptops)?

There are not millions of people using Hardy 8.04.  As I said, most of the stuff worked under 7.10.  It is only with 8.04 that it fails to work.  This was written up to Canonical but they did nothing about it.  Newer laptops are not the same animal here.  Even if this is an older laptop, it is still a 3ghz machine with 160gig hdd and 1.5 gigs of RAM each.

To me then spent much more time working on the picture of the bird then they did on resolving problems that were reported.  And, this is a new release, things are supposed to improve, not get worse.

### Post by invise on 2008-04-25
The lack of (initial) support for modern RAID arrays is a sticking point for me.  Every motherboard I've owned in the last 5 years has had a RAID controller onboard.  I expected this OS to make recognition of arrays easier (no F6, no floppies, no slipstreaming required) rather than harder ([fakeRAID](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/FakeRaidHowto), worse than F6).  

Of course, not using Windows would mean I could just use Linux's softRAID and have no problems.  But like many of the new users Linux is trying to attract, I need Windows.  If automatic recognition of fakeRAID arrays can't be built into the installer's Partition Manager, then either (1) a notification that arrays created by an onboard RAID BIOS are not recognized, so that I could choose not to install Ubuntu, or (2) an automated process helping Linux newbies set up fakeRAID without destroying their existing arrays, so that I could install Ubuntu correctly, would be useful.

I would say the installation was successful, with a few GRUB-related boot problems that were solved.  But I'm uninstalling Ubuntu because I need my array.

### Post by pbpersson on 2008-04-25
Hardy bugs:

1.  Apparently there is and has been a known issue with the etc/hosts file being setup wrong.  Instead of adding an entry for machine-name it does machine-name.domain-name and then sudo and apt cannot handle this and will not run, the error is "unable to resolve host <machine-name>"  The solution is to remove the ".domain-name" from the end so only the host name remains.

2.  While the network control panel would display the problem with the host name, it would not allow me to change it.  I could change it and save it, but upon checking the values again they would still be the same

3.  I was told to manually edit /etc/hosts but since sudo is broken I cannot get in there.  I was told to boot into recovery mode and use that to edit it.  In recovery mode I cannot get xserver to start - it does not like the machine host name which is stupid - it worked fine in regular mode.  Therefore, I have no way to start gedit to edit anything.

4.  I booted from the livecd, mounted the hard drive, but could not edit the /etc/hosts file as I do not have write access to the directory

So in my mind here are four bugs for the developers to work on.  This all started because I could not locate "lame" in Synaptic and decided to install it from the command line.  That might be a fifth bug.

Here is how I solved it:  logged into Ubuntu in "normal" mode I did an edit on the hosts file, then saved it in /home/phil and then booted into recovery mode, renamed the /etc/hosts file and then moved the new hosts file over from my home directory and rebooted.

Now everything is working  :)

### Post by DarkSim on 2008-04-25
My upgrade to Heron experience was pretty hairy. When installing all the packages a few got borked along the way and screwed up nautilus, apt, and about 400 other packages which wouldn't install. I fumbled around for about fifteen minutes until I lucked out and ran ```
sudo apt-get upgrade
``` which got that squared up. 

Next step that got me was when it asked what to use for a default sound card. I said ca0106. I wouldn't find out until 4 hours later that it should have been CA0106. Foiled by my nemesis case-sensitivity.

Then it was my nvidia video driver which I thought was working but the Hardy glx-new doesn't agree with my FX5500. For some reason the restricted driver manager wouldn't let me switch to nvidia-glx. I had to get that up through EnvyNG (96.something.something). Then it was all good.

Other minor things that were easy enough to fix after searching. Five-button mouse only recognized the left-right click and mouse wheel. There is a How-to here that fixed it again. Audacious was segfaulting. This was fixed by removing audacious-crossfade in the package manager.Emerald Themer wouldn't work until I used ```
emerald --replace
``` My login screen was too big of a resolution.I fixed this with ```
sudo dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg
```

It was quite of a rough go.I feel like I was just in a 12-round prizefight won by a split decision but its good to be kept on my toes. I get to learn more of the cli and have a good laugh at my boneheaded mistakes.

---

### Post by AndyCee on 2008-04-25
Upgraded from Gutsy to Hardy.

Few small glitches:

* gdm resolution so high the logon box was just inside the screen. Found a solution in the forums :D (as always) which happened to solve another one I'd had since Gutsy.

*A few apps were deleted (pioneers, lxdoom, 60% of my kde apps I was planning to remove anyway...) Nothing important lost.

*graphics card (old nvidia TNT2) was disabled. Solution: enabled it.

Update: Fixed all problems, except now only boots with power cable removed (WTF?). Kernel issue.

### Post by Snyper64 on 2008-04-25
Installation wise, this is one of the best installations yet. Did a clean install after running Killdisk to clean off all of my junk completely off of my drive. The only 2 problems I had were with my second monitor(which I fixed with a copy of my old xorg.conf file) and with thumbnails/previews in nautilus working(which I have partially fixed except for some mkv files still not displaying or playing properly :( ). Bravo Hardy Development Team!!

### Post by TheZergcorp on 2008-04-25
Knock on wood, but I believe I've found a way to fix my sound problem.

My roommate, who also had sound issues with Gusty previously had to download GNOME Alsa Mixer in order to play sound normally. I hope this helps others with this same problem.

### Post by phill0 on 2008-04-26
Alright this is a story of how I updated to version 8.04 of Ubuntu. I write it to maybe save you from getting as much trouble as I did. Think twice before doing the update.

First part the update itself didn't go particularly well. Packages downloaded for ~8 hours then they were installing for 2 or so hours and then... Update Manager just crashed, displayed an error message that it couldn't update Update Manager and exited. That's it. So I started it again, but it said no updates. Fine, I restarted the system and it worked, everything seemed to be working almost normal (except some config changes in gdm that got overwritten after update, but that's not a problem).

Then I started looking around if everything is alright. I fired up firefox (my workhorse) and surprise it's Firefox 3 _BETA_. Apparently I cannot use some important for me extensions, like Sage feed reader and SessionSaver. Of those two SessionSaver is most important for me, I organized my work in sessions and then just loaded session when I needed to do something specific. Well that's gone to hell. There are many other extensions not working, like: "Copy Link Name", "Undo Closed Tab". Now those are very important for comfort but one can live without them. So after trying to extract information about my saved sessions from my profile, which turned out to be fruitless, I decided that I'm just gonna install Firefox 2 and run it, it seemed like a solution. Unfortunately it seems that when you start Firefox 3 Beta 5 on a particular profile it will modify the profile in a way that will make Firefox 2 being unable to do all the things right, at least Ubuntu Firefox 3 does something like that. So when I ran Firefox 2 on my profile I couldn't do much, for example I couldn't enable/disable extensions, but when I started it on another profile (the one which wasn't touched by Firefox 3) I was able to do that. No matter how you look at it I was screwed -- Firefox 3 was less usefull to me, while Firefox 2 couldn't start with SessionSaver anymore. In one day my browser has suddenly become much less productive.
 
All of this brings three questions:
1. Why wasn't I asked during installation whether I would like to update to a frigging beta version? At the very least why not make a profile backup? I guess Ubuntu developers don't want to scare people off with questions so instead they make decisions for them.    

2. Why did they decide to put Firefox 3 Beta 5 on the update when so little extensions are available for it. I guess Ubuntu developers think that their users are not using extensions and sit on bare bones firefox.

3. Why the hell would they update to Beta?! I mean it's beta, it doesn't matter that it can be already used. It has tons of fresh code, which must have bugs and vulnerabilities. Why?!

Anyway, it is mostly my fault. I should have known better, and wait for at least month or so. :(

---

### Post by ddwaner on 2008-04-26
**_Fresh install of Ubuntu 8.04 as a Windows install on Windows 2000 SP 4._**
I got the Beta about 15 days before the release on the 24 and installed it in the system as a 4GB install and it worked fine but was limited for space, I only had about 700 meg left for programs and such so 4 or 5 days before the final of 8.04 I removed Ubuntu and that also when just fine (ALL WITH THE BETA VERSION of WUBI).
The Ubuntu release came the 24th so I did a download of the desktop Ubuntu 8.04 and repeated the install with Wubi and this time I had a area in my system on the F drive that WUBI defaulted to and suggested a 15GIG part which I used. The installer copied all the files from the CD and wanted a restart which gave me a Windows 2000 Pro or Ubuntu choice on the next boot. I choose Ubuntu and again Ubuntu started setting up the programs and when finised did a restart without a wait but I got a glimpse of a text line that the Grub boot was being modified. This time when the Ubuntu choice was made the install CRASHED at....
[B]Kernal /boot/vlinuz-2.6.24-16-generic root=UUID=**************** Loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro quiet spash
Error 15: File not Found[/B]
Press any Key to Continue (after finding the "ANY KEY", I continued to try different ways to get the RELEASE version of 8.04 to install in the system), the BETA installed fine, RIGHT???.
I tried a install on the "C:" drive a 4 GIG part and that didn't work any better with the final so I removed that tried again on the F drive.
[COLOR="DarkGreen"]Bottom line...(USING THE DEFAULTS WUBI wanted), the system was trying to boot from the wrong drive location. It is calling hd4 and it should be hd2, now all I have to do is find out how to edit the Ubuntu boot script..., turn on my second video card and monitor for multi monitor and install wine and get it to run ALL my special Windows programs that I can't duplicate on the Linux circuit (LIKE REFLEX RC Simulator), Yeh, may take a week or two... [COLOR="Black"]For now I have to temporary edit the boot line from HD4 to a HD2 on each boot and press b.[/COLOR]
Editing in Ubuntu the [File System/boot/grub/menu.lst] fixed the boot up problem for me, it works like normal now on each reboot of the system'S.
 
[/COLOR]:guitar:

My second Cyrux 500 system with about 178megs of mem installed with the alt i386 8.04 CD just fine with Ubuntu as the main (and only) operating system on a 12 gig drive. I did a complete install with format over the Ubuntu 7.10 setup.
I did notice that the GUI for SAMBA that I put in 7.10 and got to work well crashes EVERY TIME in 8.04 so config of SAMBA in 8.04 may have been upgraded to a command line configuration (I'm not sure yet), I do know that I can READ a shared drive that exists on one of my Windows computers with 8.04 without installing any SAMBA anything.
Ubuntu and Linux seems to continue be in the prehistoric DARK AGES concerning MULTI Video Cards & Monitor setups (especially with ATI cards). I got used to using Duel and triple Video cards and monitors on Windows so when multi monitor was added to Windows it helped a lot, I kinda got used to having 2 or more desktops showing at one time, its just easier to read from one while using the other to configure with. 

P.S.
I did find that removing ALL instances of "Totem" and then visiting a Flash site, Java site and a site using the VLC plugin's with Firefox kinda fixes "my" systems up for video and multimedia. I did have to configure the audio in the VLC setup for what audio scheme worked in MY systems.

### Post by drewbarr on 2008-04-26
Upgrade went very well for me. 

I have 4 machines and did one totally via the Internet (download speed was very slow but I guess that's to be expected on a new release with the servers having heavy loads) and the other 3 I did offline from a alternate cd I had downloaded. Once the 3 machines were up and running I did updates via the Internet.

On previous upgrades I have had some issues with monitor resolution but this time all machines except one came up straight away at 1280 x 1024 resolution. The one that didn't had an ATI card. Getting it sorted was easy as you dont drop back to command line now but get a GUI. Once I had restarted was able to select restricted driver and that fixed it.

All in all very pleased.

### Post by rossmoore on 2008-04-26
Well, that was was a disaster. Where do I start?

The upgrade manager warned me about something called dbus a few times, but didn't copmlain enough to abort, or give me any options. On rebooting I got as far as the Ubuntu splash screen with the bar a quarter of the way across the screen. It then froze for 5 minutes, before then going to a black screen and giving me lots of message.

I couldn't capture all of these, but some I did see included:
Loading hardware drivers - Failed
wlan0: error fetching interface information: Device not found
rt2500 vendor request failed [something...]

It then successfully started X. However, lots of applications reported crashing, and I got a load of pop-ups telling me this. The screen resolution is stuffed, and the whole thing is running really slowly.

Any help fixing this would be greatly appreciated, although I know everyone will be pretty busy now. I'm quite disappointed with this upgrade, Gutsy seemed to be going so well. There's not even an easy downgrade option if it went wrong.

EDIT: I've finally got there. The rt2500 problem is a driver for my wifi card. The wrong driver, and one that I'd blacklisted before. It was now not blacklisted and causing havoc. I re-blacklisted it, and now we boot quickly and problem-free.

For the graphics problems, the answer was simple. You no longer seem to need xserver-xgl. Perhaps you never did? Anyway, I got rid of that and I know have quick and smooth video, and quick and smooth compiz effects. Took me a while to figure it all out though, so not a smooth upgrade process.

---

### Post by geeree on 2008-04-26
Any third-party repositories in /etc/apt/sources.list always trouble an upgrader but there is no upgrade documentation that warns her about it. See, for example: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=579680](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=579680), [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=766086](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=766086). Maybe that needs to be looked into for the next release. Once that was settled, everything else was smooth for me. 

After finishing with the upgrade though, I again had reasons to worry: 

[LIST=1]
[*]The flash browser plugin that comes with Hardy seems to have problems: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=768151](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=768151)
[*]Hardy has probably taken Ubuntu one step back in terms of font rendering. I cannot have real sub-pixel smoothing on my laptop screen now. Firefox 3.0b2 that comes with it doesn't seem to have pango support built-in. Devanagari characters (in languages such as Marathi and Hindi) are malformed as a result. In fact I cannot enter devanagari characters in most fields, in gnome-terminal, or in Pidgin.
[*]I had some ssh tunnelling scripts in $HOME/bin, which no longer work. These scripts were important for synchronization and web proxy setting, but now each one of them needs to be run as root. Why?
[*]I learnt that Hardy uses PolicyKit instead of gksudo. What does this mean? Is my script problem related to this? 
[/LIST]

Maybe I should write these points in a separate thread! But otherwise, this release is wonderful. The weather/time applet in GNOME is cute!

Girish.

---

2. It didn't detect my wireless network card.

3. Someone said incorrectly this bug had been corrected in hardy, buy laptop users are reporting the problem still exists. This is probably the main reason I am not going to install hardy on my laptop just yet.

It is really tempting, but I prefer to play conservative until these issues, especially the last one, are resolved.

### Post by 8oluf7 on 2008-04-26
Upgraded to Hardy from an up-to-date, newly installed Gutsy without any apparent problems on a machine I was about to deliver to someone for basic use, including playing DVDs. For this I re-enabled the Medibuntu repository using this post:

[http://www.ubuntu1501.com/2008/04/medibuntu-for-hardy-heron.html](http://www.ubuntu1501.com/2008/04/medibuntu-for-hardy-heron.html)

so 'sudo' was working.

Emboldened by this good experience, I then upgraded my own machine. OUCH. 'sudo' didn't work and I could no longer access the shared area on another, networked, Windows XP machine.

Got 'sudo' working using PmDematagod's advice here:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=723361](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=723361)

A temporary solution to the networking problem is given by nlangmaid here:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=762525&highlight=smb+access+windows](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=762525&highlight=smb+access+windows)

but it doesn't survive a re-start.

Overall - far too bumpy an experience for me to advise other members of my local U3A FOSS group to make the upgrade.

### Post by BattlePanic on 2008-04-26
I upgraded from the alternate CD image last night and, although I have things mostly working now, it took some tweaking.

1) Under Gutsy 7.10 the upgrade script was choking with a vague error message relating to package dependencies or something to that effect.  Turns out I had to remove nvidia-glx package from my system which fixed the problem.

2) After the upgrade my Nvidia restricted driver was not working.  This confused be for a bit until I update the database of package listing in Synaptic and then tried adding nvidia-glx once more and enabling it under the menu item "System -> Administration -> Hardware Drivers"

3) Firefox 3 is missing it's icon on the menu bar.  Not sure why this happened.  Not hard to fix, though.

4) I had problems with the Adobe flash plugin in Firefox 3.  It was saying that I needed to install the plugin but then when I gave the go-ahead I was told the plugin was already installed.  After some messing around with packages I got it straightened out.

5) My system now takes a LONG TIME to boot.  I disabled the Ubuntu splash screen as per my preference, and after passing through the Grub boot menu my system seems to wait at least a minute or two at:

```
Starting up ...
Loading, please wait...
```

I've yet to resolve the slow boot issue.  I'm not sure why it's waiting so long at this initial point in the boot sequence.  The hard drive doesn't look like it's being used during this time.  In fact if you didn't wait you might think the system is frozen.  This pause didn't exist under Gutsy.

---

### Post by focusrite on 2008-04-26
Several things broke for me after upgrade, some I've managed to fix, others not so lucky so far...

I run an ASUS V1J laptop with Hardy upgraded from Gutsy which worked 99.999% perfectly, issues were:

Keyboard: CTRL key ceased working, was generating focus events instead of key events so things like save, cut, paste, copy, close ctrl+xxx shortcuts didn't work. Renamed some Metacity config files after I discovered the problem didn't happen with a newly created user. Issue went away.

VMware - still not been able to recompile it, big problem for me as it runs Dreamweaver and Photoshop which I need to use for my current client. Not Ubuntu's fault though, but still gotta have it solved this weekend.

Sound - intel hda needed the model=3stack line putting back into the Alsa modules config. No biggie, took 10 mins to re-work-it-out and do it.

Display - seems to be doing 3D but screensaver is REALLY slow (MatrixView), ran perfectly in Gutsy. ATU Radeon X1700 graphics on this, using fglrx. Other graphics seems choppy like switching desktops with ctrl-alt-arrow.

There's a lot to be impressed and pleased about overall though so I'm not disappointed. Might have been better to wait a month though perhaps!
