### Post by piusvelte on 2008-04-26
I upgraded 2 machines last night, neither are fully functional now. 

The first machine started out as xubuntu 7.04, which I recently upgraded to 7.10 with no problems whatsoever, thank you! Upgrading to 8.04 went smoothly, though I'm curious as to why I was never prompted for my password. After rebooting, the network card isn't recognized. When I run lshw or lspci, I can see it, but the network manager doesn't. It's a 3com 3c905c. I moved the card to another pci slot, and when I plugged in the power, the status lights on the  card went on again. I tried booting, and the nic was still not recognized, and the lights on the card went out again. I'm not really sure where to go with this, but I'm looking.
*** network is working again after removing /etc/network/interfaces and a custom file I created to try to get wol working, /etc/modprobe.d/network***

The second machine started out as xubuntu 7.10. I was prompted for my password to perform the upgrade, as expected, and the upgrade went smoothly, but the machine won't boot anymore. It goes through POST, and then GRUB starts, but then it goes to a black screen with a flashing cursor in the upper left. There's no '$' or sign of a shell, so I'm hoping that I just need to recover GRUB somehow. I'm still researching what to do about this also. Ironically, this machine is a webserver, so loosing ethernet support is not something I can ignore.

I'd really, REALLY, appreciate any help resolving these. I've had a great experience with ubuntu so far, and I'm imagining that there's just one line in some file on each machine that needs to be adjusted to resolve all of this. I just have to find that line!

Thank you!

### Post by nvteighen on 2008-04-26
My Hardy experience! I did a clean install, as always (I like it better), and it worked!

Great improvements:
1. Firefox 3b5 (and finally integrated to GTK+!)
2. GNOME 2.22
3. fsck no longer running when laptop used with battery.
4. Boot up seems faster than before!
5. No more proprietary drivers for me! (Intel Wireless 3945ABG driver is now FOSS?)

But:
1. I couldn't burn the iso image in Gutsy (Are CD-RW supported??). I had to do it in Windows! 
2. I had to modify /etc/gdm/gdm.conf to set Standard Server's dpi to 96. Otherwise I get enormous fonts on login screen.
3. Installing msttcorefonts was really complicated as never. Surely not Ubuntu's fault but Sourceforge mirrors'. Finally I got it work after several retrials.

(And have to blame my stupid router that made everything just more difficult, but it has been always that way).

Other issues I had were caused exclusively by my own, not to blame Hardy.

In summary: a great release!!

### Post by Mega_Mike on 2008-04-26
Relatively painless upgrade for me.  I am using a Dell 6400 e1505.

[LIST]
[*]The upgrade took a long time due to the demand on the Ubuntu servers (that was expected)
[*]I had to do a package cleanup in Synaptic afterwards to remove the obsolete and orphaned packages (again expected).  Plus I had to do sudo apt-get autoclean to clean up the cached packages (again expected).
[*]I had to remove XGL since after upgarde my graphics perfomance was choppy.  I used ENVY to reinstall the fglrx drivers for my Radeon Mobility x1400 and my graphics performance is now better than ever!
[*]Using the hardware switch to control the brightness on my laptop now works (+).  In Gutsy it would reset to original levels after a couple of minutes.
[*]The only issue I am having is that while my wireless switch works, the wireless light indicator no longer turns on or off (-).
[/LIST]

**EDIT***
Fixed my wireless light problem by following the instructions here!
[http://linuxtechie.wordpress.com/2008/04/24/making-intel-wireless-3945abg-work-better-on-ubuntu-hardy/](http://linuxtechie.wordpress.com/2008/04/24/making-intel-wireless-3945abg-work-better-on-ubuntu-hardy/)

### Post by Adam Moreno on 2008-04-26
Just loaded Ubuntu Studio 8.04 for AMD64 on my HP pavilion laptop and the installation went flawlessly. I'm cabled into my router at the moment because I think I need a software/firmware update to get my wireless network card working, but other than that, the distro simply works.

Anyone who's looking for an awesome looking environment pre-loaded with lots of cool audio/video/image editing tools would do well to check out Ubuntu Studio.

It's been 8 or 9 years since I last installed a Linux distro (have a box set of RedHat 5.2 gathering dust on my bookshelf:)). I must say that I am blown away by how far things have come.

Update: I was able to download an updated driver and firmware and got laptop's wireless network adapter working very quickly.

I then installed Ubuntu Studio on my HP Media Center PC --another easy install --and ran through the same quick update to get the wireless network adapter working on that box.

So far, my impression of this distro is very positive. However, both machines are AMD64, so I chose the AMD64 version of the distro, and am now having issues with not being able to install non-64bit software, such as Flash and Open Office. I may need to go back to a 32bit distro in order to use the software I want.

### Post by WiFi Ed on 2008-04-26
My Hardy experience has been pretty good overall.

I did a clean install of Hardy 32bit. The initial startup screen from the LiveCD, like Gutsy, wouldn't respond to the keyboard so I had to wait for the 30 second timeout to run it's course. Hardy then booted up without drama.

I installed Hardy and then installed the nvidia restricted driver. Like others, I then had a weird yellow-pinkish glow around the windows but after installing the Advanced Desktop Settings app I turned the shadows off and the glow went away.

I then installed the necessary plug-ins for my mp3's and such and Rhythmbox plays them all. One minor annoyance was that Rhythmbox wouldn't play any shoutcast streams, Firefox insisted on using Movie Player. I figured out how to change that in Firefox and now all is well.

I copied my old xorg.conf from Gutsy into Hardy and downloaded nvidia-settings and now I bask in twin screen goodness.

So far, so good! Many thanks to all those who have labored mightily and delivered to us another great version of Ubuntu!!:guitar:

Oh yeah, one more thing...now my mouse backwards/forwards buttons work in Firefox!!

### Post by RDV on 2008-04-26
Here is my experience with an upgrade from Ubuntu 7.10 to 8.04

First I upgraded as soon as 8.04 was released (after a of full backup) and of course experienced a very long download session with all the others trying to download at the same time. My download took 6 hours and 15 minutes.

The installation process was fairly smooth and trouble free.

Here is a list of the issues I had to deal with post installation reboot:
1) My screen resolution was only 800x600 (low resolution)
a) Installing the Nvidia graphics drivers via EnvyNG did not work. In the past I have exclusively used Envy for both my old ATI card and my current EVGA 8600 GTS driver installs.
b) I found many others having problems with getting the Nvidia restricted drivers work. Both new installs and upgrades.
c) The final solution that worked for me but not others was:
------------ From my post: start -------------
1) I un-installed any drivers I had installed with EnvyNG.
2) Re-installed the restricted drivers per a suggestion in another post.
sudo apt-get install --reinstall linux-restricted-modules-$(uname -r) nvidia-glx

3) Installed the following modules as suggested by apt during the reinstall.
sudo apt-get install nvidia-kernel-source nvidia-settings

4) Rebooted
5) Enabled the Nvidia restricted drivers through "Settings>Administration>Hardware Drivers"
6) Rebooted
7) My usual resolution worked again! - Driver 169.12
8) Turned on Compiz-Fusion
9) Used Nvidia settings program I enabled both switches for "Sync VBlank"

Now Compiz-fusion, Awn and video play back are working again.
------------ From my post: end -------------

2) My sound did not work although I had heard the logon sound effect once during several reboots. I use two sound cards and output through spdif to my 5.1 sound system. After a little web searching I found out that PulseAudio does not support AC3 Passthough so I disabled PulseAudio. I am back using ALSA and all is well with my sound on both cards.

3) I had been using a svn compiled copy of Miro which would no longer start. A recompile of the latest svn had the same error reference that  starting the compiled svn had. I ended up installing the Miro package through Synaptic and all is well again.

4) With Gutsy my desktop would show my hard drives labeled from information in fstab. Those labels no longer appeared being replaced with labels like "300Gb". This was fixed by actually adding volume labels to my drives with "e2label device [ new-label ]".

5) I had to get a customized gedit profile from backups which was overwritten during the upgrade. 

6) The one that ticked me off was that there was changes to the python libraries that broke an application I wrote. The module "xml.dom.ext.reader" could no longer be found. After a few hours I replaced the code by using the "pyparsing" modules. In the end the code was smaller and slicker.

7) I usually use the AWN PPA repository as I like using their latest releases. I am doing so in Hardy but I get a strange message from "Update Manager" which says "Not all updates can be installed" and gives me various possible reasons for the issue. I have not worked on this problem but it looks solvable.

I mainly use this computer as a media centre plus general computing. My monitor is a HDTV with a surround sound system for audio. Excellent Video and audio play back are essential for my use of any distribution (Linux or Windows). In fact I left Windows (Vista) and went full time Ubuntu because of Vista's terrible audio and video performance. I could never go back to Microsoft and have been thrilled with the quality of Ubuntu Gutsy as a media center.

In general I like the upgrade but I have noticed that video playback stutters slightly in Hardy which was totally smooth in Gutsy. The stutter is slight but I did not expect this to be worse than in previous releases. I will note that I am using the latest Nvidia driver in Hardy while I was using an older driver under Gutsy.

I was surprised that the old Gutsy kernel was left for me to clean up eventually saving around 117 Mg. I removed the Gutsy kernel with the following command:
sudo apt-get remove linux-image-2.6.22-14-generic linux-headers-2.6.22-14-generic linux-headers-2.6.22-14 linux-restricted-modules-2.6.24-12-generic linux-ubuntu-modules-2.6.22-14-generic

### Post by adk46er on 2008-04-26
i am a total newbie to linux. once i decided to dump windows and switch over, i did weeks of research and landed in ubuntu land. Personally, i openly embrace my 'nerdity' (and so should you), and feel that linux of any flavor provides the ultimate geek cheap thrill. my old dell laptop only found hardy agreeable, so that's what i went with and so far no major issues. you have all breathed new life into an old hobby of mine; with windows i had grown so bored with computing that i had grown to hate it.... i am falling in love all over again... (sniff-sniff). thanks again!:guitar:

### Post by Bruce H. McCosar on 2008-04-26
I upgraded 3 computers from Kubuntu Gutsy to Hardy.  All three worked fine, except for one minor problem -- they wouldn't shut down.

That's right, I had my own Ubuntu version of "I am Legend" going.

Anyway, I found a solution here:

[[Solved] Shutdown Problem]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=396418") (see post #6 by user [spydon]("http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=336908")).

All three systems work fine now.  It's a seamless transition.

### Post by jromer on 2008-04-26
I Upgrade from 7.10 using the 8.04 alternate CD. All worked!

However, when restarting it didn't boot. I triple-boot and my working grub menu is located in my LinuxMint filesystem (the most recent to be installed) so I only had to cut and paste the Grub entries for 8.04 from it's /boot/Grub/menu.lst file to the /boot/Grub/menu.lst file in my LinuxMint file system. All is now working without a hitch.

### Post by pm124493 on 2008-04-26
I have four Ubuntu systems. All running v7.10 except one which I was running Hardy heron v8.04 Alpha.
1. Kept getting "Could not resolve host name" regardless if I did a clean or upgrade install. Fixed by rebooting to recovery (root) mode and in etc/hosts on line "127.0.0.1 localhost" change to "127.0.0.1 localhost UBUNTU". You know adding what ever your hostname is.
2. Could not get SYSTEM>ADMINISTRATION>NETWORK to work with static IP address. Did not matter if I did a clean install or upgrade. Fixed by first resolving issue 1 so I could use "SUDO" and edit /etc/network/interfaces. That file has auto eth0 below the configuration of iface eth0. Moved auto eth0 to before iface eth0 as it is with ifce lo where auto lo comes first. You must select static interface and configure before changing /etc/network/interfaces.

I did not erase or re-format the /home partition before doing a clean install or upgrade. There should be no reason to re-format home in my opinion. If this upgrade picked something left from my home folder that is not good.
Other than these two major items this upgrades and clean installs went well. 

Thanks,
Pete

---

### Post by denham2010 on 2008-04-26
Compared to some, I have had a rather painless upgrade experience.

The only way I could get the upgrade going was by removing all 3rd party repositories and GPG keys from my sources.list and telling synaptic to revert the repositories to default settings.

After that, the whole process proceeded smoothly.

Since the upgrade, there have just been a few minor issues that have come to the fore:

MythTV will no longer read or write to it's MySQL database. I'm still trying different things before resorting to removing and re-installing again. I'm thinking it's something to do with user groups, as the MythTV group is not a default in Ubuntu.

My hard drive is split into 4 partitions; /, /home, /swap and /files. On the desktop, there were links to Home, Root (Filesystem) and Files. After the upgrade, the link to Files no longer appears on my desktop. I have confirmed though, the partition is mounted and is accessible through thunar in the /media folder (the partition no longer appears in the left hand pane of thunar either).

The window decoration shadows had changed colour, but that was easily fixed by going into Emerald Theme Manager. Even though the colour was set to black in the theme manager, just moving the opacity slider fixed the problem.

And lastly (from what I have discovered so far.....), not so much a problem, but a dislike with the change, after opening CSSM to check on my Compiz settings, yes there are plugins that are now turned off, and will not be turned back on again, so need to wait for updates to those plugins, but I really do not like how a lot of the plugins have reverted to key only bindings (Expo, Shift Switcher, Widget Layer etc). I previously had these mapped to my middle mouse button and corners of the screen only to find now, I only have a key binding option.

So now I'm off to work on these minor problems, and see if I can knock together a work around for the compiz bindings issue.

If anyone has suggestions or solutions, it would be greatly appreciated.

---

### Post by DRHamp on 2008-04-26
Everything is now working for me -- with a little help from this board:

First: **From pbpersson post #169 Re: Share with the community your Hardy install/upgrade experience** 

"Hardy bugs:

1. Apparently there is and has been a known issue with the etc/hosts file being setup wrong. Instead of adding an entry for machine-name it does machine-name.domain-name and then sudo and apt cannot handle this and will not run, the error is "unable to resolve host <machine-name>" The solution is to remove the ".domain-name" from the end so only the host name remains.

2. While the network control panel would display the problem with the host name, it would not allow me to change it. I could change it and save it, but upon checking the values again they would still be the same

3. I was told to manually edit /etc/hosts but since sudo is broken I cannot get in there. I was told to boot into recovery mode and use that to edit it. In recovery mode I cannot get xserver to start - it does not like the machine host name which is stupid - it worked fine in regular mode. Therefore, I have no way to start gedit to edit anything.

4. I booted from the livecd, mounted the hard drive, but could not edit the /etc/hosts file as I do not have write access to the directory

So in my mind here are four bugs for the developers to work on. This all started because I could not locate "lame" in Synaptic and decided to install it from the command line. That might be a fifth bug.

Here is how I solved it: logged into Ubuntu in "normal" mode I did an edit on the hosts file, then saved it in /home/phil and then booted into recovery mode, renamed the /etc/hosts file and then moved the new hosts file over from my home directory and rebooted."

So, with that bit of help -- I fixed the problematic "hosts" file.

Next:  

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php...5&postcount=18](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php...5&postcount=18) 

Following instructions in this link fixed my wireless problem.

It should be noted that I do run an HP laptop with a Broadcom 43xx wireless adapter.

Thanks to those above -- everything is now working for me

### Post by zachthejones on 2008-04-26

Overall, though, pretty flawless. Thumbs up to the developers and all the people involved - awesome job!

### Post by daaxix on 2008-04-26
I have the following hardware specs :

MSI P965 Platinum MB
E6600 Core 2 Duo
2GB Patriot Memory
500GB SATA Seagate Barrucuda HD
Pioneer IDE DVD/CD drive
Creative Soundblaster Live
eVGA Nvidia 7300 GS Video Card

I upgraded to 8.04 using the "Upgrade" option under System>Administration>Update Manager.

I had the "Busybox" issue where after the upgrade I was dumped to the Busybox prompt.  My system is dual-booted Ubuntu/Win XP.  I mostly use Ubuntu, but for work I occasionally have to use XP.  I have not tried to boot into WinXP yet, so I do not know if the upgrade hosed the MBR for XP or not.  

I first tried to fix the Busybox problem by booting with "recovery" mode, that showed me a bunch of ATA errors like "classification failed."  I eventually fixed this issue by turning all "IDE" drive settings to "RAID" in the BIOS.  This is a major issue, and in 7.10 it worked fine with the "IDE" settings.  I suspect it is going to make my WinXP partition unusable.  

I then had the Nvidia driver issue where the highest resolution setting was 800x600.  I poked around for awhile and discovered that it was not the Nvidia driver itself, but the monitor/display settings.  I had to select a non-generic monitor to get the higher resolution settings to work. The fact that the upgrade changed (read HOSED!) this setting is stupid.  It took alot of time for me to figure out, and I am a software engineer.  I am sure a less savvy user would have become exasperated and given up.

The final major issue that I have had is the fact that the upgrade replaced my compiz-decorator script and in that script has emerald = "no".  This caused some grief because me and everyone else who customizes their windows decorations uses the emerald manager and emerald themes.  I finally figured it out though and set emerald = "yes".

A minor issue is that in the file manager the "tree" list on the left hand side does not use the icons that I have set in my ".icons" directory for the folders.  I have not yet figured out how to fix this.  Anyone have a suggestion/solution?

Another minor issue (for me) is that now my Apache/PHP webserver is not working.  I do not know what was replaced, but I suspect all my custom startup scripts etc. were overwritten.  I have not had time to fix it yet.

--Israel

### Post by jviscosi on 2008-04-26
Fairly painless, but a few issues.  I don't run a "vanilla" Ubuntu but rather spend a lot of time tweaking, compiling my own software and installing it with checkinstall, etc.  Also, I run Fluxbox, not Gnome.  

With that said, the issues that I've run into so far include:

[LIST]
[*]800x600 screen resolution even though I entered my monitor (HP w2207 widescreen) and video card (nVidia GeForce 7000 series) information during install.  **FIXED.**
[*]All my custom mount points in /media went away.  **FIXED.**
[*]All the addresses for my devices (iPod, external Seagate drive, card reader, etc.) changed and had to be updated in fstab.  I don't let Ubuntu maintain them dynamically because I like to control how these things mount.  This is actually something that happens every time I reboot so it's not really a Hardy issue and it wouldn't occur at all if I weren't such a control freak.  **FIXED.**
[*]Unable to mount any of my three DVD drives.  I had to change the /etc/fstab entries from /dev/hdd etc. to /dev/sdc0 etc.  **FIXED.**
[*]My sensors dockapp (wmSuperMon) isn't working because the sensor information is evidently no longer being written to /sys/bus/i2c/drivers/it87-isa/9191-0d00/.  (In fact the it87-isa directory no longer exists.)  I do have all the i2c stuff installed and I've run sensors-detect.  Still working on this one, but it's hardly a showstopper.
[/LIST]
The forums were very helpful in fixing some of these items, so thanks.  :-)

I'm sure I'll run into more stuff later on but I expect it to be minor.  All in all, a good experience.  It sure beats upgrading Windows.

### Post by HappyHenry on 2008-04-26
I'm guessing you have a video card problem. I have had the live cd run fine but on trying to install it all falls apart because the video drivers. What kind of video card to you have. If you have an onboard video and an actual pci/pcie video card you may be able to switch your bios for the onboard video until your instal is done and then instal the driver you need, reboot and change your bios back to the pci/pcie vid card. If that doesn't work for you try [http://www.namanb.com/2007/11/ubuntu-710-bootup-resolution-problem.html](http://www.namanb.com/2007/11/ubuntu-710-bootup-resolution-problem.html) this place has some good info on video probs. Im running a 8800gt nvidia card with no problems of a clean fresh install. I just had to update for the gl driver. Hope this helps. Im not very knoledgable but maybe this gives you hope long enough for someone to give you more, clear, help.

### Post by Rallg on 2008-04-26
For those who were beta-testing Hardy, then decided to do a fresh install from CD: The UUID of your partition will be changed. If you are in a multi-boot environment, and your bootloader is not updated within the fresh install, be sure to change your grub menu.lst to reflect the new UUID. If you don't, then the fresh install will hang during hardware detection. If your bootloader is the one installed within the fresh Hardy, you should not encounter this issue.

### Post by felixdzerzhinsky on 2008-04-27
I installed using the alternate installer because I require full disk encryption. That worked flawlessly.

After installation during the first boot my Xorg failed and gave me a kind of grey/opaque screen.

I reboot with Xubuntu 7.04 cd I had lying around which I knew worked on my laptop. I copied the xorg.conf file to a usbstick.


Then I rebooted into Hardy Heron. I dropped to the root shell and:

mkdir /media/usbstick

mount /dev/sdb1 -t vfat /media/usbstick

cp /X11/xorg.conf  /X11/xorg.conf.bak

cp /media/usbstick/xorg.conf /X11/xorg.conf

exit

continue to boot

And it worked.

So backup your working xorg.conf or have a livecd handy. Knoppix or Ubuntu or any other as long as it has a working Xorg.conf on it.

Full Install Ubuntu 8.04.

AMD Duron 1.3Ghz 640MB

Although I can hardly complain about speed - given hardware age - it seems to run slower than 7.10 Gutsy. I like the new, more intuitive, Manual partition editor during install. The Advanced button, where we can specify where to place Grub, should BE A LOT CLEARER. 

After install, I rebooted and ran into "Error 15: File not found"  which effected not only my new installation on hdb, but also old 7.10 installation on hda. This is VERY DISAPPOINTING. It could be much development work went into a single disk installation, as this is the majority scenario. I physically swapped the disks making hdb the Master drive and that fixed the problem. But who the hall would think of that as a solution?? 

As per others here, the fonts looked horrid. They still look funky, even though I have msttcorefonts installed and have followed forum advice on settings.

### Post by billbear on 2008-04-27
[COLOR="Red"][B]Hardy is dangerous for Apple's intel Macs. After installing, people encounter a 'no bootable device' error. Their windows also become unbootable. They can only boot into Mac OS X. Fortunately, their data are still accessible from OS X.
The madness is caused by the new way Hardy handles the GPT/MBR hybrid HD. Apple uses a hybrid GPT/MBR partition table for multi-booting with 'legacy' OSes. Gusty is confused by the hybrid but leaves it untouched, Hardy hates this hybrid and erases the MBR partition table to convert the disk to pure GPT. It is bad because grub still needs an MBR partition table, and windows needs too. Most people rebuilt their MBR table and solved the problem but some people encountered other problems. So if you want to install hardy on your mac, backup everything first, it could potentially destroy everything.[/B][/COLOR]

[8.04 cleared the MBR partition table]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=766172")
[no bootable device ?]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=761023")
[Intel Macs with Hardy 'no bootable devices']("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=767677")
[problem with the latest 8.04 install]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=766711")
[ubuntu 8.04 rc on a macbook, problem with instalation]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=763027")
[Where did Ubuntu go?]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=762817")
[no bootable devices]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=767641")
[8.04 won't boot]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=766074")

### Post by sanjinko on 2008-04-27
Hello everybody!

This is my very first post on Ubuntu Forums.

I'd like to refer to billbear's comments few minutes ago.

I dowloaded Ubuntu 8.04 (desktop version, AMD64) few days ago for my Intel iMac 24".
Would like saying "so far, so good" BUT:

1) partitioning: had to install rEFIt so my Intel iMac can offer me choose between Macintosh HD and :lolflag: Windows; eventually Ubuntu starts loading and I reach login screen and Desktop afterwards; Fine!

2) Bluetooth keyboard & mouse (Apple): keyboard cannot be recognized, mouse is recognized and "bonded", but when trying to connect it a message appears: "Couldn't display "obex://[00:1D:4F:A6:37:D1]/"." The same goes for keyboard.

3) Sound not working at all: I have tried installing the latest Alsa driver (according to some hint found here on forums), but still - no sound

That's it. Disregard an odd behavior when booting (remark 1), I still have two major issues (remarks 2 and 3) to resolve before starting a quality time with Hardy Heron. And of course I'll have to learn the keyboard and its shortcuts: found that "at sign"/monkey is shown by typing "Right-Alt-V" on my old 2001 iMac (whose keyboard I have to use until Bluetooth issues are resolved).

If someone could help me with these obstacles, I'd be grateful.

Greetings from Croatia,

sanjinko

### Post by starfighter on 2008-04-27
Upgraded to 8.04 directly via the network. Although the upgrade process worked OK, I struck problems when I came to reboot. Firstly I had to modify menu.lst as it pointed to the wrong partition (the partition it pointed to was correct if all the partition had been created sequentially but they were not). My installation includes separate partitions for /home and /opt, and the boot process failed. Both of the old image boot options booted OK but the new image could not find /home /opt or swap!  The obvious culprit was fstab but why couldn't the new image find the /home and /opt partitions. I decided to install 8.04  into a new partition, and struck another problem the 8.04 install image did not boot properly on my ACER 1672! It hung part way through the process looking for the CD-ROM it booted from on hdc. After successfully installing 8.04 from the alternative image and checking the fstab all became clear, the disk partition  addresses have changed from hda to sda and the cd-rom from hdc to sdc -- thanks for the heads up guys.

I've still got a few problems to resolve:

1/ running the synaptic package manager results in a clock timer for a minute or so then just fails to load. I tried switching to root before running the synaptic package manager but this had no effect. However if you run the update manager, and get a prompt for the root password the synaptic package manager works again.
2/ Firefox takes an absolute age to load, and this is on 3.2Mhz Pentium IV with 1024Mb ram.
3/ Indeed 8.04 seems decidedly slower than 7.10. It gives you the impression that it has hung on certain operations.

Ciao

starfighter (Ian)

I noticed some people didn't get the "no bootable device" error. It's worth further investigations. Can you provide detailed information on how you installed Hardy? How did you install other operating systems? Detailed hardware information? How many internal disks? GPT or MBR format?
Please open a terminal in OS X and type
diskutil list
sudo fdisk /dev/rdisk0
and copy the output of the command here.

And also run rEFIt Partition Inspector, copy what you get. Thank you.

About sound, I had 'no sound' on my macbook at first and one night i realized that it was not 'no sound' but very low sound that can hardly be heard. Double-click on the volume icon on the top of screen and raise the volume of 'front' to the top then it will be ok. Don't know if this will work for your iMac.
My wifi not work with the old madwifi driver i kept. I downloaded the new madwifi driver and it worked with no problem.
Haven't try bluetooth yet.

### Post by jjthomas on 2008-04-27
Still have the same problem I have always had with my previous installations.  The installer has my drives in one order and GRUB has them in a different order.  The BIOS has my SCSI drive as the boot drive, my ATA controller as the second controller and my Promise controller as the third controller.  When Ubuntu installs, it has the Promise controller as the first controller, the SCSI controller as the 2nd controller and finally the ATA (motherboard) controller as the 3rd controller.  When I boot it thinks the ATA controller is the first controller, the SCSI controller is the 2nd controller and finally the Promise controller is the 3rd controller.

I would be helpful if when the installer sees multiple controllers it would ask which drive to put GRUB on.  And let me choose which is my boot drive.

-JJ

### Post by wickerman1413 on 2008-04-27
AMD 64 x2 4000+Mhz
2GB RAM 
Single SATA drive, seperate partitions for /, /home and /data
ATI X1550

Fresh install, reformatting / partition. A few niggles, but it correctly detected all my hardware apart from monitor type, prompted me to enable restricted ATI drivers, which I did, it then auto downloaded them, installed and rebooted into ful compiz mode - I was especially pleased with this after having to jump through hoops to get it to work in 7.10

Experienced problems running sudo due to hostname not found. Rebooting into recovery mode and adding the hostname without domain into /etc/hosts resolved this.

Some niggles getting F-Spot to work - had to move old ~/.gnome2/f-spot folder to one side and just copy the photos.db over. Also, the Open With menu doesnt find any registered MIME applications for my raw digital camera files (not yet solved)

Installing ufraw doesnt install the GIMP plugin - had to use apt-get

Monodevelop fails to run ....
 Cannot find Mozilla directory containing libgtkembedmoz.so. Some Addins may not be able to function. Please set MOZILLA_FIVE_HOME to your Mozilla directory

Other than the above problems that I am slowly working through - I am very pleased with this new release - hats off to the development team.

### Post by pierre.s.rosen on 2008-04-27
I had a very mixed experience upgrading to Hardy.  

I normally do a clean install of Ubuntu, so I downloaded the .iso on bittorrent on Thursday, and attempted a clean install that night.  The CD install failed three times. The first two times because I ran out of disk space.  Whose bright idea was it to allow users to import their entire windows "My Documents" and "My Pictures" folder by default if you chose to import profile settings?  The third time failed about 90% through the upgrade process while detecting my hardware.

So after two days of installation frustration, I reinstalled Gutsy Gibbon, and couldn't get software updates for almost a day...leaving my machine vulnerable...not cool at all.  Plus, there is nothing as disconcerting as having screwed up a linux install and not being able to boot into windows on restart because grub has been modified...

Finally, this morning, I was able to update my software, and crossed my fingers, and chose the upgrade to Hardy option.  Took about 45 minutes, and went very smoothly.  NVIDIA drivers were installed without a hitch.  

My X-Fi sound card doesn't work yet, and ALSA still doesn't have a working driver, but Creative apparently has a proprietary beta driver for 32-bit that works on 7.10 that I might try to install.

### Post by Jazman on 2008-04-27
Upgrade from 7.10 to 8.04
Problems:
Attempted upgrade with alternate install CD. CD md5 OK, would not autorun in Ubuntu, CD booted from cold start - wanted to do full install with partitioning - I did not want that.
In Ubuntu apt, synaptic, upgrade manager would not accept SATA DVD\CD-ROM drive as source.
Upgrade via shell command failed - CD drive not found?

Problems solved:
Swapped out SATA DVD\CD-ROM drive for IDE one.
Alternate CD did autorun from Ubuntu, upgrade manager took over.
Choose option to NOT include updates from internet - update manager ignored that and after reading from the CD for a while proceeded to d/l updates from internet.  That took a while - I had to restart the process after it stalled out after getting 1280 of 1611 files. Otherwise upgrade proceeded without problems.
Reboot - new Ubuntu 8.04 system.  SE linux asked question about access to my vfat partition - I said yes (?).
Shutdown - reinstall the SATA DVD\CD-ROM drive - reboot everything OK.  SATA DVD\CD-ROM drive shows up however vfat partition desktop icon is gone.

Problems unsolved:
vfat partition cannot be mounted.  My setup: sda1 - WindowsXP - read/write - icon available on desktop; sdb1 - Ubuntu 8.04 read/write - icon on desktop; sdb5 - linux swap - I think is OK; sdb6 - vfat - no icon - mount says not mounted; sudo mount command says partition busy or already mounted; fdisk shows partition available.
I haven't tested everything yet - would like to get vfat partition back it is handy for file xfers, etc.
Looking for solution to this one problem.

Apr 28, '08
Solution to vfat mount problem:
Found sdb6 at /dev/mapper/sdb6 - edited fstab - vfat partition icon now shows on desktop

### Post by la3875 on 2008-04-27
First. Thank you Neobuntu for taking the time to put in words what I have discovered for my self - That this release is nothing more than another beta or release candidate. I was duly impressed with the Hardy beta and willing to put it in production only to find the release candidate broke many things from wireless, (its the reason you have a laptop) to desktop effects, to resolving the host with sudo. As I'm not a programmer and impatient by nature, I'm back to 7.10 on the laptop, with no complaints and all things working right out of the box.

As a result of this experience and having been using Ubuntu for about 1.5 years now, I feel there is a bit too much hype being generated about each new release. Hardy being a LTS should have been perfect - building on 7.10 which I feel is the community's best effort yet. 

Keep up the great work all and I'll reconsider upgrading again in 3-6 months.

### Post by Uwe907 on 2008-04-27
Hi
Hardy installed fine on a couple of computers using the live cd but initially I got nowhere when trying an install on a **Hewlett Packard t760uk** desktop machine.

The install locked up for a few minutes, then the progress bar screen gave way to a rapidly scrolling text screen of what appeared to be squashfs errors. I gave up for the night and this morning I tried reinstalling again, this time removing 'quiet' and 'splash' from the default install options. By itself this didn't help much *but at least I was able to see what was going on in the early part of the install*: I don't find the progress bar very informative. :)

I had been using the dvdRW drive for the install, this being the way HP had configured the bios.  I put the live install cd in the second optical drive and rebooted, this time pressing ESC to start the boot drive selection screen and then selected the second optical drive.

The install then went perfectly normally from that point.  

Potentially, the squashfs errors were a show stopper in that I'd had no previous problems installing a number of earlier versions of Ubuntu on that machine and I was not able to figure out what to do next from the limited amount of info available to me.:confused:

This seems to be a fairly painless way to get around this problem on the HP machine and it could well be a solution for those having similar problems who also have a second optical drive available. I just wish I'd thought of it yesterday! :mad:

Uwe

---

### Post by Chris Sharman on 2008-04-27
Upgrade from Gutsy.

It broke my 5 button mouse.

The how-to starts with "go to the device manager".
I could mess about with all the command line tools, but I couldn't make it work before, and it doesn't look like it's likely to be better.

Chris

I gave up for the night and this morning I tried reinstalling again, this time removing 'quiet' and 'splash' from the default install options. By itself this didn't help much *but at least I was able to see what was going on in the early part of the install*: I don't find the progress bar very informative. :)

I had been using the dvdRW drive for the install, this being the way HP had configured the bios.  I put the live install cd in the second optical drive and rebooted, this time pressing ESC to start the boot drive selection screen and then selected the second optical drive.

The install then went perfectly normally from that point.  

Potentially, the squashfs errors were a show stopper in that I'd had no previous problems installing a number of earlier versions of Ubuntu on that machine and I was not able to figure out what to do next from the limited amount of info available to me.:confused:

This seems to be a fairly painless way to get around this problem on the HP machine and it could well be a solution for those having similar problems who also have a second optical drive available. I just wish I'd thought of it yesterday! :mad:

Uwe

---

### Post by gmvolk on 2008-04-27

Also, the windows drives do not stay mounted, but that could be due to the fact that I have not been able to shut down correctly:confused:.  Otherwise, I like what I see so far, but it does seem like this may have been rushed a bit to be released.

I've personally had a number of issues with the new Install, some I've been able to solve, and some I haven't-

3: Unexplained crashes: The system has been crashing / halting multiple times when it is idle, and is not putting any useful information in the logs

Apr 27 03:32:53 Sparta dhcdbd: message_handler: message handler not found under /com/redhat/dhcp/ath0 for sub-path ath0.dbus.get.nis_servers

### Post by shinkaide on 2008-04-27
This is my fourth Ubuntu install on the same machine (I prefer clean installs to upgrades), and it was okay. Not bad, but not that good either.
[B]
The Bad:[/B]
[LIST]
[*]**Getting the Nvidia Driver to work was a pain**. Had to go through the forum to pick out a thread which basically told me to install the **nvidia-settings** package... complicating that is the fact that I've been used to just installing the driver from Nvidia and editing the Xorg.conf file. Anyway, everything worked after that. Just saying that that'll be a headache for a linux newbie.
[/LIST]
[LIST]
[*]**Some scanner/hardware issue(s) dating to 7.10 still remain.** I have a scanner that worked perfectly on Ubuntu 6, and when I upgraded to 7.10, I kept getting an error message and it wouldn't work. I couldn't get any answers from the forums or the XSane site. That was still the case until I came across **[this thread]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=669135")** which solved it just now. I had to jump through some hoops to get a device to work on a new version of Ubuntu when it was just fine on an older version (although this was more of an issue with Xsane, I guess).
[/LIST]
[LIST]
[*]**The default, out-of-the-box volume setting is still too low!** I've had this problem since 6.06, 7.10, and now here on 8.04. Even if the volume knob is maxed out on your speakers the sounds are still only audible if you're directly in front of the computer. Forums came to the rescue, thankfully. **Solution:** Right-click on the speaker icon > Open Volume Control > File > Change Device > Analog Devices (OSS Mixer) > Set PCM 2 to the max.
[/LIST]

**The Good:**
[LIST]
[*]Programs seem to start and run a bit faster compared to the last version.
[/LIST][LIST]
[*]**Installing the restricted codecs and files (such as Flash and MP3s) were much easier**, as they were all in the Add/Remove Applications program. In 7.10, it was incomplete and not everything worked; I still had to manually hunt down some packages. Here in 8.04, everything works great. Adding some of the Firefox extensions in that program was also pretty cool.
[/LIST]
[LIST]
[*]**Firefox 3 beta 5 is more stable on Ubuntu** than it is on Windows. For some reason... I dunno. On XP 3beta5 crashes on certain pages with lots of Flash ads, but on Ubuntu it holds up.
[/LIST]

That's about it for now...Another thing is that I got Compiz to work on 8 when I couldn't on 7.10... on a 32MB video card. Me likey.

### Post by FuzzyUnicorn on 2008-04-27
Did 3 installs on release day, with varying degrees of difficulty.

First was on my workstation at work (Dimension 3100, Intel graphics). I'm required to use XP there, so I installed using WUBI. Everything went smoothly, worked straight out of the box.

Second install was on my Inspiron B130 laptop. Did a clean install but kept my old home partition. On initial logon my previously customized panels were messed up and nearly unusable. Opened home folder, moved all settings folders to a folder called oldprefs, logged out and back in, and everything was fine, just have to re-customize the panels.

Third install was my home desktop (Asrock 939Dual-SATA2, Nvidia GeForce 6200LE, dual monitors). Initially the live cd would not boot, just got garbage on one monitor and solid beige on the other. Chose safe graphics mode and it booted fine. I have 2 drives with several partitions each. I don't like that the partitioner now asks what file system to use before allowing you to chose the mount point. It tried to format one of my FAT32 partitions with all of my media on it when I accidentally selected etx3 instead of fat32. Luckily I always triple check before I commit any partition changes, and I was able to go back and fix it. After install I had the same panels issue as I did on the laptop, fixed it the same way. On trying to set up dual monitors again, I went to "Hardware Drivers" to install the Nvidia driver. It claimed to install it, but after a reboot I found it was still using the vesa driver. Opened the terminal, apt-get installed nvidia-glx-new, rebooted and was using the nvidia driver, but video was still cloned. Tried running nvidia-settings, and found it wasn't installed. I swear in previous versions it was installed along with nvidia-glx. Anyway, after installing it, I was able to easily turn on dual monitors.

After re-reading this, it sounds like I might scare some newbies off. This was actually one of the easiest installs I've ever done. The clean install on hardware with an open video driver (Intel) went perfectly, and only took about 30 minutes. The issues I had on the other 2 systems were easily fixed. If I didn't already know how to fix them (I've been using Ubuntu since the Breezy beta, so I've dealt with them several times before), I know I could always come to this awesome forum and find the answer. I don't think there has been a problem yet that I didn't find the answer to in here.

---

### Post by spoj on 2008-04-27
My heron upgrade just stuck for some hours saying "8 minutes to go" and showing in the terminal window "setting up acidlab".

So I tried to search here for awhile but frankly I got paint to watch dry, so I just tried using Google.  I immediately came across a bugreport (here: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/acidlab/+bug/207753](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/acidlab/+bug/207753)) that seems to indicate the problem.

I pressed RETURN in the terminal window, and the installation continued!  (whew!)

So anyone else out there with it stuck on acidlab, just click on the window and hit return!

### Post by Smaskens on 2008-04-27
**Problems after upgrade:**

Firefox 3b5 was extremely slow. It is quite often few seconds jammed and after that Firefox suddenly works again. For example pressing a "Submit" button causes 10 seconds freeze without any reason, I just have to wait with 100% processor load until submit is finished :/. Otherwise Firefox seems to be ok, even Youtube works now (maybe correcting the sound settings under pulse audio was one solution).
29 Apr 2008: I deleted .mozilla folder from my home folder but no help. Firefox still frozes in some certain cases for a moment (processor load is not high). Hopefully rc1 corrects this problem, from my point of view including Firefox 3 to Hardy Heron was a mistake. Long term version should contain only stable software and its installation as update shouldn't be painful experience :/.

Urban terror 4.1 sounds were really strange (crackled, distorted). I changed sounds to work under Pulse audio and now it seems to work. My sound card is Sound Blaster Audigy.

Weather Report 2.22.1 decided to use Fahrenheits instead of Celcius -> corrected it.

Clock uses 12 hour settings instead of 24 hours settings -> corrected it.

My monitor, Dell M992, was not properly recognized by Ubuntu. Maximum refresh rate is 65Hz with 1280 x 1024 :confused:. Also it is still very annoying that Ubuntu upgrade changes screen resolution to 1600 x 1200 even I had 1280 x 1024 already on Ubuntu 7.10. I don't want 1600 x 1200 resolution because these icons and text is so damn small ](*,).
29 Apr 2008: Problem solved. I installed nvidia-settings and with it was possible to refresh rate to 85hz. Also monitor was properly recognized :).

**New problems**
29 Apr 2008: Deluge stops all downloads by some reason. Looks like tracker causes it because I have one torrent that is banned from tracker.
29 Apr 2008: Fonts that I installed on 7.10 for Firefox usage are lost and now these Firefox fonts looks quite annoying. Well, maybe I get used with.

No other problems noticed :).

### Post by marvelljones on 2008-04-27
Fresh install of 64-Bit Ubuntu.  Smooth, fast startup once installed to the hard drive.  Lots of little things (NVidia card, sound too low, etc.) to fiddle with initially as usual, no big deal.

I figured no big deal, I'll just uninstall it and replace it with Firefox 2.  Much to my chagrin, ubuntu-desktop (and a bunch of other stuff) gets dumped with it if I uninstall it using Synaptic. I considered jumping to Kubuntu 8.04 and installing Firefox 2 (and actually did for about 2 days) but I like GNOME better and always experience more problems with KDE based systems.  I'm sure I can figure most of this out by bouncing around the 'Net and the forums.  However, in my opinion Hardy should have been left in Beta since it is inextricably tied to Firefox 3b5 which still is.

### Post by ArcticD00d on 2008-04-27
Gutsy-> Hardy- Overall, a much smoother upgrade than Feisty to Gutsy. It took me a while to get my head around the new way audio is handled with PulseAudio, but once I realized I was gonna have to ditch VLC in favor of totem for surround sound, I was good to go.

Just a personal report on the PulseAudio experience- I consider myself a technically proficient user, but the interfaces for PulseAudio are ridiculous. They provide very little control and a bunch of extraneous information. Simple dialogs (that, for example, control the routing of sound from application to sound system, like input/output routing in the JACK fashion) would be much appreciated. As it stands, I had to uncomment and change default-sample-channels to 6 in /etc/pulse/daemon.conf to get it to work. Now amarok pumps out all 6 speakers, and surround sound works in totem.

Otherwise, this is the first upgrade to leave my xorg configs intact, a definite plus. I had to disable the tracker service again (honestly, I keep my filesystem relatively organized, so I have no need for it). Not too painful, overall. Next up for upgrade is my laptop...

If vlc worked with PulseAudio, I think I would've been completely happy.

### Post by x1a4 on 2008-04-27
I upgraded yesterday and it was almost perfect.  

After upgrading Xubuntu from Gutsy to Hardy using the Update Manager, I ended up with a half installed package (debreaper) which the upgrade couldn't remove, preventing me from removing/installing packages.  For some reason it tried to remove a nonexistant file from a nonexistant directory.  I created the entire directory path the error was complaining about, created the file (an ampty one) and the remove script had the correct file and directory to remove and was able to finish the process. 

Another issue was that a library needed by rpm was not upgraded and rpm itself wouldn't upgrade.  After removing the old library and installing the new one, rpm upgraded.

Last issue (let's hope) was jEdit.  Because Java has changed since Gutsy I had to remove jEdit then reinstall it.  

All in all, the upgrade went well and the minor issues I was able to solve easily. 

I would like to add that this was my best upgrade experience to date.  I started using Xubuntu with Edgy and the upgrade to Feisty went fairly well though not without problems.  Feisty to Gutsy was a mess and I had to do a clean install, which went well.  Gutsy to Hardy was almost perfect. I am impressed. :guitar:

EDIT: My hardware is as follows:
An SiS 741GX (?) motherboard
AMD Sempron 2400+ processor
8xAGP MSI video card with a nVidia GeForce FX 5200 GPU
1GB RAM
SiS SI7012 on-board sound

### Post by bsell on 2008-04-27
I have upgraded to every version since 5.10. The upgrade to 8.04 required the most tweaking and breath-holding, but it was well worth it. 
    
    During the installation of the upgrades, I had a crash with exim and nautilus crashed before the installation completed and left me with the cryptic warning that my system may be unusable upon reboot and the upgrade was cancelled. The System Monitor showed Ubuntu 8.04 Hardy Heron as the OS, so I restarted the computer. The system froze requiring a hard reboot. 
    
    When I restarted Ubuntu, the login screen was 4 times the size it was before, but  I could log in even though I couldn't see the login text box. When Gnome started, my display resolution was too low, my customized Compiz settings were gone, the Restricted Drivers Manager showed my Nvidia card driver was Enabled but Not In Use, OpenOffice claimed it did not have a JRE or the one I had specified no longer worked properly and the fonts in OpenOffice looked horrible since they were not antialiased. 
    
    It wasn't as bad as it seemed, and took less than an hour to fix everything. Instead of installing the new Nvidia drivers, I used EnvyNG, which uninstalled the old drivers and installed the new ones and nvidia-settings. Compiz runs better and seems more stable now than it ever has and the fonts look better not only in OpenOffice, but system wide. Removing the javasettings XML file in the openoffice2 config directory fixed the JRE problem. Editing xorg fixed the login resolution and I used Nvidia X Server settings to set my desktop display resolution. 

    Firefox 3 is impressive, even for a beta, although not all of my extensions or themes will work yet. The Hardy Heron theme and wallpaper look great and Ubuntu continues to get easier to use with each release. I may wait until the next LTS to upgrade again.

---

### Post by yowshi on 2008-04-27
> **Neobuntu said:**
> Now is the time when everyones patience runs out and they just have to try the new "release". The problem is, this should (generally and recommended) only be done as a separate install; for testing. The real (production) stability comes at about 3 months after now. Yes, Three months before the next release. It's a catch, but the way things work, we get the most testing and hardening started now (New stuff with stability **and compatibility**; ) after the "official" release.

I, myself, am about to pop. I want to use a better; new version on my main systems. Yet, I write to remind myself and you, this now, is the real beta test (IMHO). Think of it as a post beta tester. Just do not, upgrade your main and only working system and expect perfection at this point. While you may skate by, experience has shown, it's a gamble at this point. 

This is especially difficult with (dist) upgrading your system that is now much used and in place. Multiple OS systems take up time and you can only upgrade an existing system. This all leads to "trying it" on your one and only system. About half the people then needing an unbeatable, clean install and about as many then giving up, on open software because of (dist)"upgrading" instead of clean installing! 

**If you are not an "expert" and you have the one install, I'm writing this for you. You'd probably be wise, to wait. See, I can hopefully work through any breakages and maybe you can too. Yet, I've learned, I don't really have the time for that. Therefore, the battle within, rages heavily. **

I sincerely hope this helps someone.

I still observe, a new (managed/Dist) up-grader can't be stable, until it is too old (3 months plus, give or take.) anymore, so what's the point? How can you upgrade to what's not really stabilized yet??? Clean installing is faster, safer and better. That includes time for easy and fast backups of your stuff. The total time is less. Often, the nature of a new Six month release, includes foundational improvements (which is a good thing). Foundational changes strongly suggest, clean installs are better. I hope the clean install has (surely) come to be more intelligent/enhanced. I still wonder if a convoluted "managed dist-upgrade" effort would be better directed at super managed clean install instead. Maybe that's what's happening. I don't know. One can hope. It seems to me, pushing the one choice of super easy and managed clean install (like "/home" retention) takes away half the development work and time. Allowing a better (k)ubuntu. That, IMHO.

This would undoubtedly be somewhat of a hybrid; by nature. Semantics and vocabulary aside, it could be better. For example: Think of a live upgrade that is also a true clean install, thus a standard starting point for **everyone** using it. Then think about it also being a super intelligent, clean installer that handles all your needed transition. Would we not **ALL** be better off then?
yeah but like you said 90% of users have one system i suppose i could i dont know install the upgrade to a different partition and if breaks i have the old install there working but well maybe they should change the update to a slower pace i have always thought 6 months is to often especially since i tend to wait a month before upgrading. and your suggesting 3 months!!! like yeah i do believe every 6 months is to frequent if it takes 3 months to be truely stable

### Post by calibre97 on 2008-04-27
Strangest thing. Here are my experiences in order.
1. Installed Kubuntu 8.04 RC. Worked great. Never got built-in Broadcom working like I did with 7.04 (updated to 7.10), but did get a PC Card D-Link DWA-652 working.
2. Decided to try out this "Gnome" thing all the kids were raving about.
   a. Couldn't install to the MBR, but could get GRUB on the Linux partition.
   b. Got the D-Link working but only the first time. After a reboot, no joy. No matter what I tried, I couldn't get Broadcom to go away so I could establish the D-link.
3. Tried installing Kubuntu 8.04 LTS with KDE 3.5 from DVD. Couldn't install GRUB, at all. Another strange anomaly: With Kubuntu, only choice is (hd0) but with Ubuntu, I was able to choose anywhere. I wouldn't think the choice of window manager would affect the install at so base a level. Weird.
4. Failing the DVD, I installed Kubuntu 8.04 LTS with KDE 3.5 from CD.
   a. Got D-Link working with Wireless Windows Drivers app. Still working.
   b. Had to do a bunch of rebooting trickery to get System Commander back to dual-boot with XP. Grub is fine, but I prefer System Commander. Here's what I did:
      I. Post install, boot to Grub, select Kubuntu.
      II. In Kubuntu, run grub-install /dev/hdN.
      III. Reboot to Grub, select XP.
      IV. In XP, run System Commander and re-enable to reclaim the MBR.
      V. Reboot to System Commander, make Linux Partition bootable.
      VI. In System Commander, both XP and Linux are choices, so pick Linux and boot.
      VII. In Kubuntu, post this message.

So I'm completely off Gnome. It's nice and all, but the glitches and general wonkiness totally turned me off to it. I like KDE (KMyMoney is a fantastic Quicken clone!!!) so I'm ok anyway. KDE 4 was cool, but not all the way there as many other posters and reviewers have been saying. Can't wait for 4.1.

So bottom line, I tried KDE4, Gnome and KDE 3.5 and I'm here to stay on Kubuntu 3.5 until 4.1 is available. I even got a TrueCrypt container file to load in the shared partition in both Kubuntu and XP. Muy bien.

---

### Post by paleheretic on 2008-04-27
Sheesh.

I've been using Windows as a graphic designer / animator / web developer / developer for about 15 years. 4 weeks back I switched to Ubuntu studio 7.10.

I was amazed at how well it worked and how I could get everything up and working really fast.

Then Hardy comes out and basically breaks stuff.

Wireless is busted, NVidia drivers don't work. The basic login theme (the default one - think about that) is corrupted and kills the boot until I get into recovery and set it to something else. And that's the things I've found.

I have to say, I'm shocked. The vote at the top of this post says it all: only 20% of the people doing an upgrade had it work without having to fix anything by hand.

The other thing that is worrying me is that some of this seems to have been caused by problems with the HAL that were picked up during beta and listed  as 'not that important - we'll fix it next release'....

.... some one please tell me it ain't so!

### Post by SABCD on 2008-04-27
Notes from upgrading Gutsy to Hardy...annoyances:
1. Better to upgrade from the repository than the disk..When I use the disk, it will only go and re-download all the packages from the repository after the installation anyway, saving you nothing..and have the machine in an unstable state in the interim...or perhaps I experiences a crash halfway through????
2. Glipper breaks.  You have to delete the old ~./glipper directory to get it to run.  Even then it seems a little flaky.  You put something on the clipboard, but when you go to paste (ctrl-c, ctrl-v) it sometimes pastes an old entry from the clipboard. But what you just copied IS in Glipper, just not selected as the current item.
3. Wine totally broken.  You can do a bunch of kludges to get it to work (search the internet), but even doing the kludges and starting from scratch, it will not install Office 2000(which was no problem in Gutsy). 
4. Virtualbox requires a manual upgrade, by loading a new virtualbox-ose-module for the new linux kernel from apt and then it WILL work..sound and all (and as always...better than wine). The Virtualbox team is great!, lets hope that they continue to be so under Sun.
5. xorg.conf file had to be manually edited for my old Dell 19" crt.  Would not do 1152x864(which was the old setting!).  Also nvidia-settings was uninstalled.  (Nvidia-settings is still a useful tool, even though there is now no persistence for the screen resolution over sessions using it.). Backup your xorg.conf before upgrading!
6. Mounting of the cdrom in fstab screwed up.  They changed the drive letters (again).  Was /dev/hda for a PATA ide cdrom, now it is /dev/scd0.  Even with fixing that, after the upgrade, Synaptic can't find the cdrom drive..you have to additionally manually mount it to a /cdrom folder. (So are they going to change it again to /dev/hcd0 in the next kernel update?)
7. Some compiz hot key settings (for still supported features) were deleted.
8. File-roller "extract to" dialog screwed up. Say you want to save the files it '/home/me/Desktop'.  You have to navigate to 'Desktop' and then click on 'me'(the parent folder) in the breadcrumb bar in order to force it to extract (since it now requires text in the location edit box which it did not in Gutsy.)
9.When tried to run winecfg after upgrade, the screen corrupted and I had to re-start X.  When I attempted to re-install wine, my /var/lib/dpkg/status became corrupted. This is a major! thing..without this file the package manager is dead in the water.  Luckilly there was an auto-created backup.
10. My development volume was eliminated from menu.lst during the upgrade and I had to manually add it back in so it would boot. Backup your menu.lst!
11. Pulseaudio...all audio worked but I have yet to get two streams to play simultaneously...but I have not banged on it too hard.
12. After running and re-booting for a day, I rebooted and got a  message that my .dmrc permissions and the permissions on my home directory were not correct.  At no time had I changed the permissions on either of these!...yes they were incorrect and I needed to adjust them back...very scary!
13. Sudo broke after the upgrade because  127.0.0.1 was not  mapped in the network hosts file to the local  machine NAME!.....(I am sure you all get how the two are connected). Needed to change this manually...I used the Network configuration button in the panel and was prompted for a password...that works.
14. (U.S. English language)upgrade will not happen if you have any! French language packs install. Can only upgrade if you uninstall these.  (Is someone out there still blaming the French for the Iraq fiasco?)

Sorry, but for the average person who just wants the thing to work, and does not know how to or want to mess with manually configuring an O.S., this is not good!  You could argue that some of these would not be a problem with a clean install, but you should be able to upgrade and not have to completely re-build your operating system.

On a positive note, sleep to ram/disk (hibernation-standby) actually worked without any hassles this time.

### Post by Rockin Robbins on 2008-04-27
I've had some weird problems this morning that I never had with gutsy.

Decided to go with a fresh installation instead of an upgrade. Everything went smoothly until I shut it down after hooking up Compiz and making desktop shortcuts for my hard drives. I have a 40 gig ATA drive where Kubuntu lives with both its partitions, a 320 Gig Serial ATA drive for Windows XP and a 320 Gig Serial ATA for backup disk images of the other two drives.

I shut down for a restart when installing Compiz. Upon restart all my disk drive designations had changed! Sda1 was supposed to be the Kubuntu disk. It now pointed to my backup disk. The Windows disk shortcut now pulled up Kubuntu! And the backup shortcut was OK. As I was checking out the system settings to confirm that Kubuntu really did acquire brain damage, the sound quit working and I was unable to fix that either. A reboot reassigned the drive designations a second time.

I have reinstalled twice more after similar operating system stunts. The sound problem didn't recur. I really appreciate Windows XP now and think it's worth the cost. To be fair, I never had this happen with Feisty or Gutsy. I think it is related to the change in handling of PATA disks as scsi, just like the SATA drives.

My last installation from about 5:00 pm is working well so far. The drives have not been reassigned yet. If they are I have a nice OpenSUSE 11 Live CD I'm going to give a try.

I know that someone who eats, sleeps and breathes Linux could figure this out. But I'm spoiled by being good at Windows over a 17 year period. I expect Linux to be a little less opaque. Any explanation on the rotation of disk designations would be welcome. I'd love to stay with Kubuntu.:lolflag:

---

### Post by yeats on 2008-04-27
I've now upgraded three computers from Gutsy to Hardy in the last week.  The first was a WinBook laptop that I upgraded to Hardy Beta, and aside from a problem with the sources list (now solved), everything went very well.  I then decided to upgrade my main laptop on Friday, but I ended up using the alternate CD install because all the mirrors were jammed full.  It went well, too, though it didn't fix the [sound card problem]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=764337") I'm working on.

My desktop, however, caused me great horror today.  I started to upgrade last night, but had second thoughts and decided to keep Gutsy for a while - the desktop is my main computer (dual boot with XP).  This morning I woke to see that the Update Manager wanted to add upgrade packages, and I couldn't figure out how to revert to my system's previous state, so I thought, "What's the harm?" :-\"

I started the network upgrade again, but every time I tried, the distribution upgrade window would gray out and freeze when checking the package manager.  At this point, my system was in need of either the full upgrade, or a way to revert to Gutsy.  Eventually I backed up my important files and went for a fresh install of Hardy from the Desktop CD.  It errored out about halfway through the installation and I was left without GRUB or any way to boot. :confused:  So I tried to boot from the Live CD, just to see something work, but it errored out too!  Suddenly I was in BusyBox, which I'm sure is a fine program, but I needed my Ubuntu back and I don't know how to use busy box.

I then used the alternate CD to try again, which worked until it timed out with the software installation section.  At least at that point I had a base system to work with.  I began searching the web for ways to proceed, but finally settled on trying the install from CD again.  It just wouldn't work!  I was getting I/O buffer errors and decided to switch out my second CD/DVD drive for the first one, which worked.  I had to continue using the alternate text install disk, but this proved a good option, since it is exactly like the Debian installation CD I had used in the past.

Now it's up, and I'm VERY pleased with the result!:-)

### Post by randlieb on 2008-04-27
THE GOOD............
Well I did an upgrade on my new box, custom built Shuttle xPC, and everything looks pretty good so far. I have 2 panels at the top of the screen and they flipped places after I re-booted, but no biggy there.

CONS
I lost some very useful Firefox add-ons with Firefox 3 being the default. May try installing FF2 to see if I can re-install them there.

CD's open in Rhythmbox but don't start automatically. Would like to use a different player but don't see a way to change the default player.

DVD's play but Totem Xine no longer automatically starts the movie.

PROS
Evolution seems to be working better than ever. My alarms are showing on all work spaces and I found a workaround here on the forums to enable the window to display the alarms instead of the panel pop-ups. Love the new weather info with the calendar applet.

Flash Player still good with my Mozilla browsers.

THE BAD.................
Did a fresh install from an ISO on my old 1999 Gateway computer. Duel boot with Windows XP PRO. Only see half a screen playing DVD's with Totem. That is the only problem so far but I haven't played with it much yet to check for other issues. I want to make sure the install from a disc goes smoothly because I want to start promoting Ubuntu here in Fayetteville AR and want to make sure the newest Ubuntu is installing ok.

THE UGLY...............
Nothing to report here. No freezes, no slow downs, no burbs, gurgles or farts...! My main system is running smooth as silk.

### Post by idsfa on 2008-04-27
I did both an upgrade (my machine) and a new install (my wife's).

My upgrade went fairly cleanly.  Biggest problem was that several packages which were installed but had been disabled at start-up (via update-rc.d) were re-activated.  This should not happen.

So far things have worked just fine for me.

My wife's system had been fubar'ed upgrading from Feisty to Gutsy, so she wanted a new install this time around.  The install tried (but failed) to resize the Gutsy partition down to install Hardy, I ended up repartitioning manually.  After that, the install worked fine.

Hibernate did not work for her (no change).  Sound worked without manual tweaks (improvement).

### Post by him610 on 2008-04-27
Upgraded three machines:

Machine One (Horse) is a nine-year old Dell Optiplex GX1 with a 350MHz Pentium II, 448MB RAM with an ATI 3D Rage Pro VGA card. Upgraded from Xubuntu 7.04 to Xubuntu 8.04 using the 8.04 x86 Alternate Installation CD. No problems encountered. The Linksys BCM4306 wireless card works as before with no reconfiguration needed. The upgraded 8.04 seems to be more snappy than 7.04.

Machine Two (Shuttle) is a fairly new, a six-month old, home-assembled Shuttle SN68G2 with an Athlon64 X2 5600, 2GB RAM, integrated Nvidia 7025. Upgraded Ubuntu 7.10 to Ubuntu 8.04 using 8.04 64b Alternate Installation CD. No problems encountered. The Gigabyte Ralink RT2500 wireless card works as before with no reconfiguration needed.

Machine Three (Pilgrim) is vintage Sony Vaio PCG-FXA36 AMD Athlon 1GHz, 512MB RAM. Upgraded Xubuntu 7.10 to Xubuntu 8.04 using 8.04 64b Alternate Installation CD. Encountered one problem with the configuration of a Linksys WPC54G (BCM4306 R2) wireless adapter. Using the Hardy Bug Fix solution in [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=475963]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=475963"), I was able to reconfigure it with minimal effort. 

Overall, I am quite pleased with Release 8.04. 

Congratulations to the Release Team!

### Post by Lord Landis on 2008-04-27
I will say that the new default folders had me miffed enough to almost consider switching distros (well, that and the fact that you have to sudo su - to get true root access), but I figured out how to get rid of them, and now all is good.  It turns out you only need to edit '/etc/xdg/user-dirs.conf' and set 'enable=False' and they're gone.

I've only installed to my laptop so far, but it seems to work great.  I'll probably uninstall the bundled OpenOffice and install Sun's official version as well.  I've noticed a few oddities in my documents since the upgrade.  While working on them, random letters (usually 'i's and 't's, but not all of them) appear to be italicized, but upon switching to the print preview or just printing normally, all of the letters look fine.  I'm not sure why this is - I don't know if it's an OO.o version thing, a font rendering thing or what (the font is Arial).  On my 7.10 install w/ Sun's OO.o everything was normal, so I'm more inclined to think it has something to do with Ubuntu's build of OO.o

I don't have any problems with Pulse, my system boots in roughly the same amount of time, and I've already purged Dolphin and the extra language-based fonts.  Unfortunately my laptop can't run Compiz, so I won't know about that until I put it on my desktop.

Oh, and I'm going to have to re-compile the kernel again since it blew away my custom-rolled one (taken from the master kernel thread).

---

### Post by djrobthaman on 2008-04-27
**Cons:**

1.  The livecd as well as the out-of-the-box hardy install gives me video with an insanely heavy green tint.  That's bad and not so bad... I can chalk that up to my ati card not playing well with linux (though I've never had this problem on previous releases). Then when I installed the proprietary drivers, everything would load (I could tell because the login sounds and such came out of the speakers) but there was no video output to my monitor.  What I had to do was reinstall gutsy and copy the xorg.conf file from there, install hardy and the ati drivers, then copy the old xorg.  I've seen a couple more people with a similar problem (the green tint, not sure about the death of all video).

2.  I think this might be due to my xorg.conf being generated by an older version (a guess really) but when i navigate to System>Preference>Screen Reolution, I can't edit anything.  I just get a message that says "The X Server does not support the XRandR extension.  Runtime resolution changes to the display size are not available."

3.  I haven't been able to wrap myself around how to share folders across my home network.  Whenever I right click and try to do so I get this error:

"'net usershare' returned error 255: net usershare: cannot open usershare directory /var/lib/samba/usershares. Error Permission denied
You do not have permission to create a usershare. Ask your administrator to grant you permissions to create a share."

4.  Skype won't work if I'm also listening to music (ALSA issue? This didn't happen in gutsy, then again I wasn't using the medibuntu version)

5.  Small Gripe: In my Places menu all my internal hard drives are listed as removable drives.  Annoying but, of course, really not a big deal.

**Pros:**

1.  The bootsplash no longer has my monitors big "Output not valid" message over it (Don't know if I should list this here, since this was a problem only since gutsy... almost like one step forward after taking a stepback)

2.  I love that the version of compiz and its manager are more recent than those in the gutsy repositories.  I probably won't feel the need to move from these for a while.


I don't know if it was just an incompatibility with the new version of xorg and my system, but that video problem is definitely a deal breaker.  It didn't deter me that much because ubuntu is now my os of choice and I've been using it for a while so I made a bit of an educated guess as to how to fix it, but I don't know if less determined people would have stayed the course on that one.

Other than that and the sharing, so far it's feeling like a pretty solid release though.

### Post by doctor1doug on 2008-04-27
Installed 8.04 LTS on AMD dual core, 2300, 64 bit, 2 gig, with a series 7000 nvidia graphics card (Vista approved).  Clean install was perfect, stable, but I could not get nvidia_new to recognize my video card.  Disabling and then enabeling nvidia_new did not work. Sound worked out of the box. Wireless Airlink installed perfectly, same method as previous versions of Ubuntu.

So I downloaded and installed with Synaptic nvidia-glx-new.  I also downloaded and installed compizconfig-settings-manager.  Video card recognized and 3d effects are now great with compiz.

I have used Ubuntu as my primary OS for 2 years.  The new 8.04 LTS is awesome, should give Vista a real challenge.

### Post by Dean Powell on 2008-04-27
I have an nVidia geforce 6100 video card.  The PC itself is quite new (approximately 6 months old, an Acer Aspire w/ Vista pre-loaded).  Upon installing Hardy over top a nicely working Feisty, I found that I could only get 800x600 video resolution.  Hardware detection announced that the nvidia drivers were installed, but "not in use".  I tried several suggestions for getting the drivers to be "in use", but to no avail.  I was stuck at 800x600.

I did _not_ reformat my /home partition -- the reason for having it  on its own mount point was so I could upgrade without losing my personal files, and I _was_ having a couple of issues with the "Advanced Desktop Effects" on Feisty.  I hadn't turned these off, and perhaps that was part of the problem.  I am as yet unsure.

For now, I have re-installed Feisty.  For kicks, I tried the Zenwalk 5.0 liveCD, and it has no problem with my card -- I can get at least 1024x768 (my usual default).  I can see that a lot of other users with nVidia cards are in the same boat as me, so I'll wait for a while to see what happens with future Ubuntu releases.  For now, it's Feisty or Death!

If any Ubuntu developer wants me to re-install Hardy, and capture log files, etc. I'm willing to be a "guinea pig".

Regards,
Dean Powell
Edmonton, Canada

### Post by petenz on 2008-04-28
I had a pretty sweet upgrade over the net from 6.06 to 8.04 on my Toshiba satellite A10 laptop, dual booted with XP (alas, for work reasons...)

I initially tried to do a fresh install after downloading the iso file but ran into problems - probably just cheap cd's at fault I suspect.

After that I dug deep to remember a few command line tricks, got my repostitories sorted out for the upgrade, made sure everything was in order and backed up, and let things run overnight doing the network upgrade. Came in early the next morning, gave the tick to cleaning up and removing old files, let it do it's thing and reboot and... nothing - it hung on booting up. Panic!!!

Booted into my windows partition to make sure all was still ok there, no worries at all, and then tried booting into Ubuntu again and... it was sweet - all working. I think the complete shutdown rather than just a reboot sorted it all out.

I haven't spent too much time playing with it yet - but sound and video all seem sweet. And I'm very impressed to be able to see into my windows partition. All in all it's a beautiful thing. Congratulations to the developers and community!
