### Post by Gina on 2008-04-28
Can you tell us what your hardware is, please.

A huge number of problems have been fixed during development but the number of people using Hardy in this period is very much lower than after release.  There is a vast variety of hardware used in and with PCs (unlike Macs) and very few hardware manufacturers provide Linux drivers (unlike Windows) so Linux distros have to cope with all this hardware with virtually no support from the manufacturers.  

Hence with the vast increase in users of the new release there are bound to be some new hardware variations that haven't been met by the development testers.  This is why bug fixing will continue and there will be an 8.04.1 release in July.

### Post by yodermk on 2008-04-28
Well that was a serious WTF experience ...

Upgraded from Kubuntu via Internet.  Went fine until I rebooted.

Checked the drives, then rebooted again.  Ok.

Then, before X started, "motion" started running, telling me it was capturing images from my webcam.  That was somewhat amusing, as last time I checked my laptop's built-in webcam wasn't even supported under Linux.

I switched to another virtual console and logged in, then ran startx.  Gnome started.  Amusingly, I verified with gthumb that my webcam was indeed functioning by viewing the images, which it was still capturing.  I couldn't help but wonder, though, why did it start that without asking me, and how does one stop it?

Back in the console, I sudo killed' the bash process which started motion.  Motion didn't stop, but the init continued and kdm proceeded to start.

Logged onto KDE, everything looked OK, until a minute or so later when my laptop began whailing LOUDLY.  Scared the crap out of my sleeping wife (it's 3:30am, I work nights but am off tonight).  She tends to be grumpy after incidents like this so I hope I don't get clobbered in the morning ... :/

Since I don't have a quick way to control my laptop's volume, I force powered-off the computer.  Next time, I logged into Gnome.  It's OK for now.  I guess I'll have to suffer with Gnome until she wakes up naturally, because I don't want that to happen again!

'Motion' was still running, I had to kill its process to stop it. The init script didn't work.

That's where I am now.  I'm sure I'll get things fixed, but it wasn't the most pleasant upgrade I've ever done.  Have to say though, someone inexperienced with Linux would have been dead in the water here.  A lot of work still needs to be done. :(

### Post by dios on 2008-04-28
I downloaded the amd64 desktop version (md5 ok, written with 4x speed) but was unable to install it. When I booted from CD drive #1 I just got a blank screen. When I booted from CD drive #2 I got as far as selecting the language, then the system didn't respond to anything.

I reinstalled 7.10 (which has so far worked flawless) and updated via Update Manager. The update process was smooth but afterwards I have not been able to use my Broadcom wireless card.

With 7.10 I had used the bcm43xx firmware, copied into /lib/firmware/<kernel version>. Just copying it to the path for the new kernel version did not work (apparently the firmware has changed).

The problem is that - when I used the Restricted Driver Manager to enable the Broadcom driver - HH tried to wget the bcm43_fw_cutter from the internet. Since the wireless card is my only way to access the internet and it won't work without the firmware it's a bit of a hen-and-egg situation.

(Oh, I know about ndiswrapper but I hate it).

I'll either go back to 7.10 or switch to openSuse (from which I switched in 2007 because ubuntu 7.10 was released a few days before openSuse 10.3).

### Post by purifyyourmind on 2008-04-28
My experience as someone who ran Ubuntu full time for a few months in 2006, and then gave up on it. I'm trying it again on a small partition, dual-booting with XP.

Problems I noticed:
[LIST]
[*]Fonts still look terrible by default. I had to install MS core fonts, clear the font cache (or login/logout), and then change the anti-aliasing method to get them to look as good as in Windows.
[*]The idea of shipping with Firefox beta was questionable. I notice things that are broken in it compared to FF 2.x, like the Set Image as Background feature
[*]Sound volume seems extremely loud by default for me, which is bad because of the startup sound
[*]There should be a list of Compiz keyboard shortcuts. Clicking the Help button on the visual effects page in the system prefs does *nothing*
[*]There should be more advice on partition resizing. I found that if I didn't defrag my NTFS partition before resizing, it took a heck of a long time.
[/LIST]

### Post by bmartin on 2008-04-28
Impressive, especially after Feisty and Gutsy. I don't have time to fix all the problems after each release, so I'm going to stick with the LTS versions from now on, until a new LTS is released. Just seeing the new stuff via Live CD is enough for me.

I installed Hardy on two laptops (1 yr and 2 yrs old) and a desktop (2 yrs old). The desktop install was flawless, as would be expected.

The older laptop, as you'd expect, has better support. Using the new Broadcom kernel module and the firmware from the restricted drivers, my wireless was working better than ever (even compared to NDISwrapper).

The newer laptop had one **major** problem; it wouldn't boot **at all**. It used to boot in Feisty and Gutsy, but the ACPI wouldn't work. With Hardy, I had to disable the ACPI using kernel boot parameters. I then had to decompile, modify, and compile my ACPI DSDT file, stick it in my /etc/initramfs-tools directory, and update my initramfs files. Then I was able to remove the boot parameters from /boot/grub/menu.lst, upgrade grub, reboot, and use the laptop as Acer (ugh, don't buy an Acer computer) intended.

The wireless chipset (AR5007eg) also didn't work... but MadWiFi has released a driver that supports this chipset (finally!). I tried NDISwrapper, but it was flaky... every few seconds, the connection would get droppped... literally every three seconds.

For the first time ever, my ENE card reader worked, and it worked OOTB. Even after reading dozens of posts about the card reader, I hadn't been able to get it to work in Feisty or Gutsy.

I'd give the desktop an A for ease of installation, the old laptop an A-, and the newer laptop a D. I was only able to fix the BIOS because I spent over a month researching the problem, learned how to read DSDT code, and fixed the DSDT myself. It's really a Linux problem and not an Ubuntu problem, but it's been reported to Ubuntu many times and it hasn't been fixed yet.

---

### Post by paulvandenberg on 2008-04-28
I installed Hardy and for the most part everything went well. However, for the first time, Ubuntu is giving me the VESA driver by default. Previous versions had always configured the 'nv' driver for my NVidia graphics chip. Being greeted with a 800x600 screen is not very newbie friendly. Fortunately, I am not a newbie and went to install the 'nvidia' driver through the Hardware Drivers option. However, this indicated to me that the restricted driver was already installed, which is was not. Again, fortunately, since I am not a newbie, I went to Synaptic to install the nvidia-glx-new package and rebooted. Still VESA! Again, fortunately I am not a newbie and found the Screens and Graphics tool to configure things myself. It took me a bit to find it, since it is hidden in Hardy and was easily spotted in System | Administration in Gutsy.

Don't get me wrong, I love Linux, but these kind of things make me reluctant to recommend it to anyone who is not technically inclined. Since this all worked perfectly in Gutsy, it seems to be a serious regression. If the autoconfiguration is not going to work all the time, the configuration tool, Screens and Graphics, should be not be hidden. (It is under Applications | Other, but is not visible by default. You need to edit the menu to enable it.)

Other that, I am pleased with Hardy. The 64 bit version runs quite fast and I've had no poblems with multimedia after configuring the Medibuntu repository.

Cheers....Paul

---

### Post by dawynn on 2008-04-28
I've got issues, some have been fixed, others have not.  This is how I would rank my issues:

* Solved, in a way: Burned Kubuntu Hardy RC candidate alternate CD's with Gutsy k3b and with Nero in Windows -- both built an unusable CD.  So, went back to my trusty Feisty CD and migrated through Gutsy to Hardy.  

* Solved: Upgrade converted my system to "safe" drivers.  Had to switch back to nVidia, and use nVidia-xconfig to get things right.  Seems to be working fine now.

* Solved, by retrograde: Hardy Audacity is an unusable version.  Try to play a file and it crashes (at least on my system).  Had to back it down to the Gutsy version of Audacity.  The Gutsy version is working no worse than it did on Gutsy (sometimes races too quickly while playing a file).

* Solved, I think: This beast seems to have memory issues.  I believe I've fixed the glaring memory issues I was seeing.  But I had to delete my .kde and .kde4 personal directories to do it.  Before that, it would not remember my desktop resolution settings from one login to the next and kate was "remembering" documents that it hadn't seen since the upgrade.

* Unsolved, Priority 1: The upgrade has left a mysterious something somwhere on my system that references my swap drive using Feisty syntax.  I haven't figured out where the problem is yet, but this prevents me from just booting non-interactively from a Hardy kernel.  When it gets to the resume portion of the boot, it first checks for my swap drive from the specified UUID.  Then libgcrypt, or something related to it, checks *again* from /dev/hda10.  I have hit enter to get it to finish booting.  I still have yet to find what is referencing my swap drive as /dev/hda10 instead of /dev/sda10. 

* Unsolved, Priority 2: KDE4 does not log out.  I can get it running on my system.  And I can tell it (successfully) to either shutdown or restart my system.  But if I choose to log out, the system locks up with a black screen.

* Unsolved, Priority 3: Synaptic causing problems for me.  Once it finishes installing software, it does not close the install window, and does not return to the main Synaptic window.  It just hard-locks.  I have to either log out or shutdown to get out of Synaptic.  I can't believe that the people who put together these packages would let such a glaring error pass through, so I'm wondering if Synaptic is fighting with something else on the system (maybe KDE 4?).

These are the known broken parts.  Everything else that I've found at this point is just annoyances (nothing that crashes the program / system), attributable to specific pieces of software.

---

### Post by nodnarbmai on 2008-04-28
I have two reports:

Upgrade from Gutsy failed completely to update the kernel.  I ended up with Hardy packages installed, but Gutsy kernel.  Subsequent attempts to update the kernel to Hardy failed repeatedly with "dependency problem" messages.  Oh yeah - couldn't get past 800x600 screen due to nVidia card not being recognized either.  

I gave up and decided to do a clean install (thanks to whoever it was that recommended \home in a separate partition!!!) having done so was a real lifesaver.

Downloaded i386 iso, burned and booted up with live cd and clicked "try without changing system".  Got to a good looking 1920x1200 desktop, all hardware working well, decided to give install a try right from the desktop.

Clicked on install icon and short time later was booting up cleanly into my brand new Hardy install - perfect!!  :guitar:

Since I created a new user during install (to protect my original \home files), I then had to "add" previously existing users to the new install to enable login by the "old" users.

In a terminal window:
```
sudo adduser olduser
sudo chmod 644 /home/olduser/.dmrc
sudo chown olduser /home/olduser/.dmrc
sudo chmod -R 700 /home/olduser
sudo chown -R olduser /home/olduser
```
Logged in then using olduser id and presto !  

Hardy has been great so far.

A couple screenshots following my clean install attached.

### Post by mikeymo1741 on 2008-04-28
Not horrible...   

The download and upgrade went quicker than estimated.  The whole thing took under an hour, if I recall, although the estimated time kept jumping around.  I kept all my settings files when  prompted. 

My Broadcom 4318 wireless card did not work.  I had to play with that a bit, and I am still having trouble with WEP.  I had to do a firmware update and install a new driver.  

For some reason the install changed the default in Grub.   I was defaulted to Windows.  It changed it to Ubuntu with memory check.  

Still cannot use desktop effects, but that's just my lousy SiS video card. 

Hopefully I can get my wireless working with WEP.  I'd hate to run an open network at home.

### Post by timzak on 2008-04-28
I've been using Ubuntu since 7.04.  I backed up my data and installed 8.04 from scratch.  For the most part, it's gone well.  Incidentally, I ended up going from 7.10 64-bit to 8.04 32-bit.  I had a few issues with 64-bit (nothing unsolvable, but required more time and effort to resolve) and didn't have the time or patience to work through those issues again.

I had trouble migrating my Thunderbird profile into 8.04 (I was using Swiftdove because it had the latest (0.8) Lightning extension for 64-bit built in while Ubuntu's repositories had Lightning that was two versions old).  I could migrate all my emails just fine, but could not get the calendar (Lightning) to import my backed up data.  I decided to use this as an opportunity to try Evolution.  So far, so good.  I've had to re-create my calendar, but that's okay. I love the integration of Evolution with Gnome.  I loved Thunderbird/Lightning too (still use it at work) but unfortunately Ubuntu makes you jump through hoops to use the latest versions.

I have two 80 GB data drives in addition to my 74 GB OS drive.  Ubuntu decided to name them both "80.0 GB Media Drive" (thus making them impossible to tell apart) and took away writing privileges.  I had to do some more "hoop jumping" to get them both with full read/write privileges.  I still haven't gotten to renaming them (another aspect of Ubuntu that irks me--why so difficult to rename a drive?).

Fortunately, I saved my smb.conf file from my previous install.  Hopefully, this will help me get my networking set up again (I hope it's as simple as cut-and-paste code from smb.conf backup file to current smb.conf).  Out of the box, I can't access the other computers on my network anymore, so I will need to tinker with smb.conf to get that back up to speed.

Other than these three areas, my experience with 8.04 has been great.  I love the new FF 3.0b5 and the YouTube integration into Totem.  I also like being able to install RestrictedExtras and get pretty much full web functionality.

I'm sure as I continue getting my system set up, there will be more caveats, and more praises too.

Thanks to the Ubuntu team and everyone here in the forums that help us all out with these problems!

---

### Post by twl on 2008-04-28
Upgraded from 7.10 via Update Manager on a 4-year-old Dell Dimension 4600i

-----
Symptom:
Failed to start the X Server (aka "black screen").

Cause:
I'm using the nvidia driver via the nvidia-glx-new package. The default kernel on the GRUB menu is 2.6.24-16-386, but the linux-restricted-modules-2.6.24-16-386 package was not installed. 
(The only linux-restricted-modules-2.6.24-16-* package that was installed was linux-restricted-modules-2.6.24-16-rt, but I've never used that kernel.)

Fix:
Installed linux-restricted-modules-2.6.24-16-386 with Synaptic Package Manager.

-----
Symptom:
Sound worked with some apps (Totem, Rhythmbox, aplay, gmplayer), but not with others (mplayer, Amarok, timidity).  It worked in Firefox with some things (YouTube), but not others (mplayer plugin).

Cause:
I have two sound cards: an onboard Intel ICH5 (unused), and a Dell OEM Sound Blaster Live card (EMU10K1X).  By hooking up two sets of speakers, I determined that some apps were using one card and some the other.  The ALSA default card is the Live card, so apparently some apps are not using ALSA.  I don't know why this wasn't a problem in Gutsy, but everything worked before the upgrade.

Fix:
Disabled sound in the BIOS so the onboard card is no longer recognized.

-----
Symptom:
In Evolution, the audio alert for new mail stopped working.

Cause:
Mail notification is now handled by a plugin.

Fix:
Configure in Edit>Plugins>Mail Notification>Configuration

-----
Symptom:
In njstream, the XUL interface stopped working. (Browser-based player for Creative Nomad/Dell DJ.)

Cause:
Manifest files need to be moved manually to the Chrome directory of Firefox 3.

Fix:
Copied njb.manifest to /usr/lib/firefox-3.0b5/chrome.

### Post by matze_ on 2008-04-28
My 7.10 to 8.04 update experience on a Samsung Q70 laptop:
The update itself went smooth, don't know how long it took, I went to luch after starting it.

Good things first:
[INDENT] Suspend works now!
  System feels snappier, esp. under load[/INDENT] 


Issues i've been able to solve
sound:
[INDENT]  gtkguitune stoped working, complaining about /dev/dsp
  fix: changed menu launcher to 'padsp gtkguitune'
  Took a while to figure that out, 'apropos dsp' gave the hint[/INDENT]
video:
  [INDENT]videos in gxine and totem media players are blue
  fix: changed hue [/INDENT]


I'm still not able to change screen brightness, no improvement here
[INDENT]fn-keys not working
  brightness applet doesn't work[/INDENT]


Major annoying regressions
compiz:
[INDENT]  I got the pink shadows, No title bar on inactive windows, fading tooltips leave trails
  I turned compiz off for now.[/INDENT]

hdd:
  [INDENT]load_cycle_count ugly fix doesnt seem to work anymore, load_cycle_count increased rapidly
  applied fix in /etc/pm > lcc still increases, but slower[/INDENT]

Booting takes longer
  [INDENT]bootchart up from 25sec to 40sec
  both with readahead profile
  also shutdown takes longer[/INDENT]

I'm still happy with ubuntu and hope that the bugs get fixed soon.

> **Beamvr6 said:**
> Upgraded from Dapper Drake tonight after checking with the LiveCD main functions were OK. The whole process took about 2 hours iirc but I have a very fast ADSL. Having played around a few hours now and everything basically looks fine. 

One weird thing though. The Monitor Resolution Settings show the correct screen res (1280x960) but odd refresh rates (50 Hz, 53 Hz and 68 Hz). The screen however does not flicker. Know issue? xorg.conf still has the settings as once edited being 1280x960 at 85 Hz.

### Post by butsam on 2008-04-28
I need to slightly revise my "flawless" rating.

There was a very minor issue with grub bootloader not being updated properly. So, when I installed grub bootloader, the 4th item on the list was the default item. I had the following items:

0) Gutsy Gibbon
1) Gutsy Gibbon (recovery)
2) memory check (? I don't remember if this was actually there before but I think it was)
3) Other Operating Systems: (label)
4) Windows XP
5) Media Center Edition Recovery Mode

So, I had Windows XP set as the default (#4).

When I upgraded, it now had:

0) Hardy Heron (under the newest kernel version)
1) Hardy Heron (recovery -- under the newest kernel version)
2) Hardy Heron (under a slightly older kernel version)
3) Hardy Heron (recovery -- under slightly older kernel version)
4) Memory check
5) Other Operating Systems: (label)
6) Windows XP
7) Media Center Edition Recovery Mode

The problem: #4 was still set as the default, but this now meant that memory check was the default.

So, I would encourage something to be done when the /boot/grub.conf is added to, in order to adjust the default setting properly if the default setting is below the addition.

Other than that (which is easily fixed by my editing the /boot/grub.conf), no problems!

Sam

PS: I have an HP Pavilion dv9000 laptop, which used to be one of the laptops with its own thread segment in Gutsy Gibbon because there were a few issues. Now, no problems.

### Post by pauljoseph on 2008-04-28
I just upgrades my workstation and my laptop from Kubuntu gutsy to Hardy. 

When I upgraded my workstation, I chose to keep the setting for my xorg.conf. The result was quite bad. The reason was that Hardy&#8217;s installation disabled restricted drivers by default. And I use ATI restricted driver for my X setting. So it crashes when it tries to load the X server after the installation finish.
To overcome this problem, I need to login using terminal, and type
 $ sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

this restore the xorg.conf using the default value instead of using ATI drivers. After I reboot, I can login to my Desktop.
For my laptop, I got error when I tried to install using the alternate CD. The reason was that I previously install ubuntu-desktop package. Somehow it requires me to uninstall them before I upgrade to Kubuntu Hardy. It works fine after I remove ubuntu-desktop package.

However, for both my workstation and laptop, after updating all the package, I encouter some packages are being kept from upgrade. I search to the internet, and found that I somehow need to use aptitute. So I type
 
 $sudo aptitude update
 $sudo aptitude dist-upgrade
 
The strange thing was that, I couldn&#8217;t use apt-get. I am not sure why. Anyway, that solves my problem. :)

### Post by Complexed and Many Ways on 2008-04-28
By all means I'm no professional programmer, yet. This was my first ever experience with any OS besides Microsoft Products. I was actually forced to use a open source OS when repairing a friends computer at home. She had a bootleg copy of XP Pro and I could not work with the desktop. This was an old computer. It was 2 to begin with :P. After excavating the darn things to make one functional PC, I figured well why not try Linux for once. Well I must say, despite bringing dinosaurs back to existence, I love Hardy Heron 8.04 LTS. I was unaware that I had to be one of he first installers (OS has been running since Sunday) My biggest and only real issue with Microsoft was that too many times I ran into the problem of not running my system the way i wanted to. Now this system makes me so proud. Thank you and I will contribute as much as possible as I learn more about programming myself. Personally I use very state of the art components * Current PC (Budget Applied) AMD Opteron 64 x2 4gbram 1TB sata HDD 2 Light scribe DVDrws 2 52 3.5" Internal Multi Card Readers Genia tech HDTV DVR TV Tuner Creative Labs Audigy 2ZS w/ Front Panel I/O 17 Lcd 11Bay ATX transparent case with blacklight (slight custom case mods) Vista Ultimate 64 bit _ upgrades include Bamboo writing tablet, fingerprint reader keyboard, touch screen LCD I installed Ubuntu on a 950Mhz Intel Celeron 256mb ram 60 gb Maxtor 1 DVDrom 1 CdRW 3.5 floppy no adapte cards and only 2 pci (maybe one agp) slots. also no front panel usb support for case.

### Post by wayra on 2008-04-29

### Post by yschandra on 2008-04-29
Hi,

I just move over to Hardy from Fedora, well user experience is great with Ubuntu. For a long time I have been using Red Hat then Fedora. Here is my experience of installing Hardy.

My system Specs:
CPU: P4 3.0 GHz
Mother Board: ASUS P4c800 (i875P chipset)
RAM: 1GB
Disk: 160 GB SATA with other OS XP Professional
Video: ATI X1950Pro AGP (512MB)
Audio: SB Live!
Optical Drives: CD-ROM, SONY DVD Writer

I have used Hardy Desktop CD and booted into Live environment. Played around for some time and started installation from there. All hard disk partitions are already done with Fedora root have been formatted for Hardy and the boot partition as well. During my first attempt around 72% it gave error due read failure from the disk and suggest to re burn the image at lower speed. At this point I have moved the CD from CD-ROM drive to DVD drive and restarted from scratch. This time installation went through smoothly.

At the first boot things are good and the it has suggested to get proprietary drivers for my ATI card for desktop effects to work. I went ahead and installed them however could not enable effects used get white screen.

Investigation in to X log files revealed that fglrx is unable to acquire AGP port and fglrxinfo return still Mesa as drivers.

This problem and solution is described in the following thread
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=585783](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=585783)

After black listing i82875p_edac & edac_core (module changed from edac_mc) desktop effects started working. I also found one more consistent system freeze (many people talking about random freezes in Hardy) which I could reproduce consistently.

Have VIM installed, try to open any documents which is gzipped directly with VIM in the terminal like (VIM will internally unzip it)

vim /usr/share/doc/console-setup/FAQ.gz 

then try to scroll down using 'j' key of VIM, reach till last line in the window and now try to scroll further down using 'j' at this point system will freeze. Resetting is the solution at this point.

Other than this enjoying Hardy :-)

---

### Post by His Eminence on 2008-04-29
As it is, I think, usual with Linux on desktops the upgrade to new distribution release went well, but while some things that were a problem in the previous version (7.10?) got fixed others got broken. 

I had two problems in Kubuntu 7.10:
- KNetworkManager didn't connect the machine automatically to Wi-Fi networks - I had to select them manually,
- when headphones were plugged in sound was still coming from the speakers (thus, to some extend, defeating the purpose).

Both got fixed. However:
- now when headphones are plugged in speakers are muted - however, no sound comes out of headphones either,
- Psi stopped to work with GnuPG, problem is weird - I'm using a Jabber server that requires TLS/https encrypted connection. If I select the key to use with GnuPG encryption in the account configuration dialog I can't connect to server - the star icon pulses and it tries to connect but without success. If I unselect the GnuPG it connects ok. 

So, while I overall like Ubuntu and I found some of the KDE applications very cool I can't wait until my Mac comes back from repair and I can return to OS X's environment where everything works predictably. 

I think main issue why most people experienced some problems (aroun 88% had problems according to your survey here) is lack of extensive testing for interactions between packages, libraries and usage scenarios. You should try to do better on those fronts.

### Post by flightless bird on 2008-04-29
Upgrade from Gutsy - generally worked well apart from a few things I have had to solve, which I feel don't have to be left up to the user to fix:

_the positive_
My sound card now works (HOORAY! :D). When I first installed Gutsy on my HP G5056EA the internal laptop speaker worked but there was no automute for the headphones and the microphone didn't work at all. I managed to get the automute working some months by fiddling with alsaconf, installing alsa from svn, and tweaking my conf files. But now everything just works (tm), automute, microphone, everything. I assume this is done to the kernel upgrade, for which I am eternally grateful. The microphone was the last thing holding me back from removing vista (so I could use skype), so the upgrade has been a very positive experience for me.

_the negative_

The way third party repositories are handled, well, it sucks frankly. For all but one all that had to be done to get them to work was change 'gutsy' to 'hardy' in the distribution field. If a third party repo has an entry for the new distribution **it should be upgraded automatically**. This is not something a noob would feel comfortable with, and there is no need for it to happen. One repo had been made obsolete by its pakage (elisa) finding a home on the offical ubuntu repositories. If this happens then the upgrade should check and handle the transition itself rather than unilaterally uninstalling the package which has obviously been deliberately installed for a reason.

Sox: very odd one this. The upgraded version was crippled by not having any of the format support included. This defeats the point of having this on your system and broke one of my scripts. Again, this kind of issue lends itself to an automatic solution - if the upgrade knows it will produce a crippled package it should install the remedy.

Sadly, and I know this will find little resonance, multiple keyboard input support got borked again. My particular needs are ancient Greek, which isn't particularly common and suffers from a mess of a configuration (see [http://www.jw-stumpel.nl/stestu.html#T6.5.3]("http://www.jw-stumpel.nl/stestu.html#T6.5.3")) and involves the user editing the compose file for their locale (/usr/share/X11/locale/YOUR LOCALE HERE/Compose) and the key settings for Gr (/usr/share/X11/xkb/symbols/gr). Thankfully I have done this before and it's pretty automatic now (I might as well write myself a script) but there shouldn't be a need for this at all - Linux inherently is, or should be, the premier system for multiple language input, but what use is that if the features have to be ressurected from a crippled state? Sane defaults would result in more functionality than other systems out of the box.

_Overall_

An essential upgrade for me given the new kernel, but since I run xfce4 anyway the new gnome elements mostly pass me by. I also prefer epiphany and am eagerly looking forward to webkitism - the new firefox thus doesn't really thrill, especially since I've had it in beta for a while now. If I didn't need the hardware support it would have been far more hassle than it's worth.

---

### Post by frodon on 2008-04-29
Just a reminder for those using third party repositories :

One **must** always comment/disable all the third party repositories before the upgrade, this is a known cause of upgrade issues.

### Post by dawynn on 2008-04-29
> **dawynn said:**
> I've got issues, some have been fixed, others have not.  This is how I would rank my issues:

...

* Unsolved, Priority 1: The upgrade has left a mysterious something somwhere on my system that references my swap drive using Feisty syntax.  I haven't figured out where the problem is yet, but this prevents me from just booting non-interactively from a Hardy kernel.  When it gets to the resume portion of the boot, it first checks for my swap drive from the specified UUID.  Then libgcrypt, or something related to it, checks *again* from /dev/hda10.  I have hit enter to get it to finish booting.  I still have yet to find what is referencing my swap drive as /dev/hda10 instead of /dev/sda10. 

* Unsolved, Priority 2: KDE4 does not log out.  I can get it running on my system.  And I can tell it (successfully) to either shutdown or restart my system.  But if I choose to log out, the system locks up with a black screen.

* Unsolved, Priority 3: Synaptic causing problems for me.  Once it finishes installing software, it does not close the install window, and does not return to the main Synaptic window.  It just hard-locks.  I have to either log out or shutdown to get out of Synaptic.  I can't believe that the people who put together these packages would let such a glaring error pass through, so I'm wondering if Synaptic is fighting with something else on the system (maybe KDE 4?).

These are the known broken parts.  Everything else that I've found at this point is just annoyances (nothing that crashes the program / system), attributable to specific pieces of software.

Priority 1: This got fixed last night in the midst of several software updates.  I can't pinpoint exactly which package cured my problem.  But its now fixed.  Suggestion to anyone with similar problems: Review the results of "deborphan -a" and only keep what you really want.

Priority 2: I killed KDE4 on my machine last night.  It was proving to be far too unstable for real use.  However, I tried both IceWM and KDE 3 and neither of those were logging out last night either.  That's especially bad in IceWM -- which doesn't provide an option to shutdown or restart (have to go back to the login software).  Have not figured out why log out is such a problem yet.

Priority 3: Synaptic is working fine in KDE3 and IceWM.  I don't know exactly why it was fighting so hard with KDE4.

Cheers!

### Post by brundles on 2008-04-29
With what I must admit was a big bag of nerves, I clicked the Upgrade button on Gutsy and let it rip on Saturday night.

An hour later though and I was pleasantly surprised with an upgraded system that only had a few niggles:

**Attansic L1 wake on LAN not working**
Not a big deal initially as this didn't work on Gutsy because of a known (apparently) kernel bug. The problem in this case is that the Attansic vendor drivers that provide the support don't build on 2.6.24. I finally got some changes sorted last night to get the drivers to build - I will post a link to them shortly but want to make sure they're completely safe first.

**lircd.conf replaced**
Despite telling the upgrade process to keep my modified lircd.conf file it insisted on replacing it. Thankfully it kept the original so that was quickly fixed.

**No Firefox icon**
To be honest, I really don't care about this one - Firefox still works and webbrowsing isn't the machines primary use but the icon has been replaced by a black box.

The rest of the system including imgburn running on WINE is perfectly happy as far as I can tell. :)

### Post by Stanley Krute on 2008-04-29
[my system is a Toshiba Satellite laptop dual-booting Windows XP and Ubuntu.

One major lesson learned: clone one's HD before doing ANY ubuntu upgrade.

I admit: I'd been lulled into carelessness. All my Ubuntu 7x updates had gone flawlessly. I had networking, graphics, sound, Windows connectivity, and multimedia all working.

I never thought that an upgrade would KILL all of those items. Yes, I'd been lulled.

So I did NOT clone my HD before the upgrade. Bad. Bad. Bad.

If I had, all I'd have to do now is swap my cloned HD into the system.

Instead, since I truly don't have days to throw at figuring out these renascent once-solved windows-that-fanboys-scoff-at-doesn't-choke-on-these silly puzzles, I'll just not use Ubuntu for now. Hopefully there'll be updates to 8.04 that JUST FIX the problems, or a 9x product that JUST INSTALLS, or a tool that AUTOMATICALLY and W/O-ERRORS walks a system back from an 8.04 upgrade to a working 7.10 system. 

The planet needs an open-source OS. Someone will get us there. Ubuntu's still our best chance. But dang this upgrade fiasco hurts.

-- stan

### Post by timzak on 2008-04-29
Just an update to my first report:

I am liking Evolution and am kind of glad I had problems getting my Thunderbird/Lightning profiles imported into 8.04.  Probably one of the only times a "problem" leads to a better solution.

Took a couple of Howtos, but I got my two 80 GB drives renamed so I can tell them apart, and got full read/write privileges on them both.  Relatively painless solution.

As I suspected, all I had to do was replace 8.04's smb.conf file with my 7.10 version I backed up before installing.  My samba shares are now functioning perfectly as they were in 7.10.

In general, for those upgrading, I found this works well:  go through all configuration files you've edited in your current, working install, back them up, and re-introduce them after you install the new Ubuntu.  Be sure you make backups and know how to restore the backups before trying.

My system is already fully up and running, basically identically to my previous 7.10 install, but with updated packages and kernel.  I am happy with this version so far.  I think as updates begin to release, things will only get better.

> **timzak said:**
> I've been using Ubuntu since 7.04.  I backed up my data and installed 8.04 from scratch.  For the most part, it's gone well.  Incidentally, I ended up going from 7.10 64-bit to 8.04 32-bit.  I had a few issues with 64-bit (nothing unsolvable, but required more time and effort to resolve) and didn't have the time or patience to work through those issues again.

I had trouble migrating my Thunderbird profile into 8.04 (I was using Swiftdove because it had the latest (0.8) Lightning extension for 64-bit built in while Ubuntu's repositories had Lightning that was two versions old).  I could migrate all my emails just fine, but could not get the calendar (Lightning) to import my backed up data.  I decided to use this as an opportunity to try Evolution.  So far, so good.  I've had to re-create my calendar, but that's okay. I love the integration of Evolution with Gnome.  I loved Thunderbird/Lightning too (still use it at work) but unfortunately Ubuntu makes you jump through hoops to use the latest versions.

I have two 80 GB data drives in addition to my 74 GB OS drive.  Ubuntu decided to name them both "80.0 GB Media Drive" (thus making them impossible to tell apart) and took away writing privileges.  I had to do some more "hoop jumping" to get them both with full read/write privileges.  I still haven't gotten to renaming them (another aspect of Ubuntu that irks me--why so difficult to rename a drive?).

Fortunately, I saved my smb.conf file from my previous install.  Hopefully, this will help me get my networking set up again (I hope it's as simple as cut-and-paste code from smb.conf backup file to current smb.conf).  Out of the box, I can't access the other computers on my network anymore, so I will need to tinker with smb.conf to get that back up to speed.

Other than these three areas, my experience with 8.04 has been great.  I love the new FF 3.0b5 and the YouTube integration into Totem.  I also like being able to install RestrictedExtras and get pretty much full web functionality.

I'm sure as I continue getting my system set up, there will be more caveats, and more praises too.

Thanks to the Ubuntu team and everyone here in the forums that help us all out with these problems!

---

Have Hardy on my laptop. Have not put Hardy on either of my other two machines. 

I decided to do a clean install. First time I screwed it up, so I had to redo it. Second time worked like a charm. Since it has an ATI Mobility 7500 and I had been doing some reading on how to get it to work before ever upgrading, I saved my Gutsy Xorg then copied it to my new install. Rebooted and had Compiz working beautifully. Other minor blips. Network Manager still doesn't work for me, so I uninstall it and install Wicd instead. The other strange thing is suspend. It works, but when it wakes up, I get a cute little musical theme that wasn't there under Gutsy (Kind of startled me the first time). But, it takes a long time to wake up, then I always get an error that it didn't go to sleep (although I know it did) and everything's fine. So, I'm not sure what it is, and doesn't affect anything I'm doing, so I'm just ignoring it.

I've noticed that everything's faster though. A very nice surprise, and my Compiz effects are much faster too. So, all in all I'm very pleased. Now, just to take care of my other two computers :)

---

### Post by wanchai on 2008-04-29
Hey Sunflower1970, opposite experience here...

I have an ATI Radeon Mobility 9600 on my laptop and I'm giving up. I installed Hardy on a new disk, so it's easy for me to go back to Gutsy.

Compiz with the open driver from the repository is just awful. When I enable the cube, it becomes slow and AWN isn't usable anymore. Then I tried the fglrx driver from the repository and also the driver from the ATI website. Performance does get better, but it's still not close to what Gutsy can do with the ATI drivers that come with the system. For example, when I move the mouse over the AWN icons, xorg eats up half the CPU, on Gutsy it hardly registers.

The fresh install from the Desktop CD went well. I also tried to build from the Alternate CD with encryption and LVM, but that failed in "select and install software". I was able to finish this install manually.

On my desktop I did an upgrade which went fine. But I'm not convinced yet whether this Firefox 3b5 is good. The rightclicks sometimes do other things than the expected and several times the shift, ctrl and alt key stopped working. And when that happens, Gnome apps like gedit or whatever crash as soon as you hit a key. Logoff-logon fixes that.

All in all, I prefer Gutsy! My laptop won't be upgraded anytime soon and on the desktop I hope that these issues will be ironed out (Compiz isn't a topic there since it doesn't work on Matrox).

### Post by dcroxton on 2008-04-29
I usually wouldn't post a successful upgrade experience, but every previous upgrade caused problems, so I thought it was noteworthy.

Getting to the upgrade was a bit of a problem.  I downloaded the alternate cd and tried to upgrade from there, but it hung.  I tried upgrading from the regular update manager, but it also hung.  It would get through the part about "Reading cache," then sit for hours.  No disk activity, no cpu activity, just sit.

Finally I did apt-get dist-upgrade.  The first time, it downloaded all the packages, but it exited with an error and didn't install any of them.  The second time, it installed them all fine.

I'm just happy that everything works, including flash and sound.  After reading about some other people's experiences, I was terrified, but I'm just too impatient to wait. :)

### Post by bigred1 on 2008-04-29
I had a severe but workaround-able bug in upgrading from Gutsy to Hardy Heron 8.04.

During reboot at the end of install, the sysstem did a self check and reproted OK for all items but one, for which there was an error (I forget the exact wording -- something very technical). 

The system then did a forced filesystem-check, and got stuck on phase 1/5 of the fsck. It then printed one more line and did not proceed into booting the GUI, even when left  overnight.

This was very scary.

However, a hard reboot solved the problem, and the system works fine.

On the plus side, [a bad  problem]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=582873&highlight=3100&page=5") with Ubnutu's inability to recognize my  very common and standard graphics chip (onboard Intel GMA 3100) in Gutsy was solved by the upgrade.

### Post by HeresJohnny on 2008-04-29
Hi all,

I did a clean install.  I run a laptop and I typically always have the same problems once I do the clean install:
1.  Setting up wireless (I love Broadcom 4318!)
2.  Setting up SCIM (I like to be able to type in Korean, even if I seldom do it)
3.  Setting up my ATI Xpress 200M to use the funky eyecandy (what a pain)

After the clean install, here's the status of those three:
1.  Activate 'Windows Wireless Drivers', let the app do its work, done.
2.  Still not working (worked fine in 64-bit beta, but for some reason not here).  I'll try it again in a minute.
3.  Click a button or two in 'Restricted Drivers Manager', let the app do its work, done.

I am amazed at how much easier it was to deal with my two biggest install headaches as opposed to prior versions.  I'm pretty sure SCIM will work out fine, too; I wasn't connected to the Internet when I first set it up, so maybe that's why I had problems.  

I do have a new problem with my cd drive not mounting, but I've filed a bug and will get resolution on that soon, I hope.

### Post by peakshysteria on 2008-04-29
Downloaded the DVD ISO Torrent of latest 8.04 Hardy Heron 64. Clean install went very smooth. From an external DVD drive. Put the DVD in the Drive and rebooted the machine. Everything went very smooth. Took twice the time of Gutsy (which installed incredible fast). The install procedure is no even simpler. Which is a plus.

Compiz is working like a charm.

Flash installed smooth via Firefox

Java Installed smooth via Firefox

My Bank seems to not detect my Java (IcedTea)

Transmission Bit torrent client out of the box is old version. Newest must be installed manually (forced me to use Deluge instead)

Latest Flashblock 1.5.5 crashes FF if pages has a high content of flash (had to disable it) Otherwise Firefox 3.0 is running very smooth

Can't set the right resolution to my display.  Hardy starts off with an 800x600 resolution each time i reboot. To fix it, I have to: run sudo displayconfig-gtk from a terminal. This enables the display and graphics manager. Where I'm able to set the resolution to 1280 X 960 (and no higher). The right res is 1280 X 1024.

Dear not install the proprietary ATI driver for my card. Since it must surely will break my classy awesome compiz. Meaning no tv out for the moment. (big desktop might be a solution)

### Post by HyperHacker on 2008-04-30
For me the procedure did not go well at all and I was quite amazed to see the machine successfully boot again after upgrading. First it asked if I wanted to keep or replace a modified ntp.conf. I looked at the side-by-side comparison, but it was dumb (both files mashed into one box, wtf?) so I hit back and it decided to just cancel! After that many packages failed to install, then basically every program on the system crashed one after another, even ones that weren't running (not sure how that works). Partway through that madness it installed Firefox 3 beta without asking and two instances of it, and then every time something crashed it would open Firefox to a login form on some bug reporting site. Finally the installer aborted. I rebooted and instead of actually rebooting, it restarted X with no graphic drivers and the "low graphics mode" message, so I hit shutdown there and it came to the login screen, I hit shutdown again there and finally it did so.

Amazingly enough when I booted up again everything appeared to have gone just fine, a few things that weren't working before are fixed. Of course there are a few annoyances I've discovered:

1) As mentioned, it installed a beta version of Firefox. I posted a thread on this; basically I was rather miffed that it installed a beta without asking, especially one that's incompatible with the majority of my extensions and themes.

2) ReadProcessMemory() no longer works in Wine.

3) Various programs give me an error when saving files on my NTFS drive: "failed to set permissions on /blah/blah/blah, operation not permitted".

4) Anjuta crashes more frequently (was never stable to begin with <_<).

Other than that it seems to be fine, some nice changes and new features, Amarok is working again, no big problems so far. I can't imagine what sorts of things are secretly broken/not actually updated though given how crazy the upgrader went. O_o Nor why it failed to begin with, I'd only installed it a few days ago, hardly done anything... I swear there's something wrong with this motherboard/CPU/whatever.

---

### Post by hardhu on 2008-04-30
I rewrite here a message I have written yesterday, maybe this thread is a more appropriate place.

I have tried to install kubuntu 8.04 kde 4 remix on an Acer Aspre 5024 notebook, which has an Ati Mobility Radeon X700. I could boot the live cd, I have chosen to install, but when I got to the screen where the installation process begins, I had huge fonts (I could see only the word 'Welcome' on the half upper side of the screen), and I couldn't go on. The same happened if I only run the livecd: when I got to kde desktop the font problem was the same. And the same happened when I made an installation from the alternate cd.

After investigating a while, I discovered that this problem has certainly something to do with radeon driver. In fact, I have just been able to get the correct fonts by changing the driver with the proprietay one fglrx.
This has been not simple, the steps I have made are the following:
- first I started with the installation I have done with the alternate cd;
- then I got to a console (ctrl+alt+F1)
- then I have used aptitude to download and install fglrx video driver (remember that i couldn't do using kde hardware driver manager since I couldn't use the desktop!)
- I tried to use the command 'aticonfig --initial' to create a new /etc/X11/xorg.conf, but it failed since there was no Driver line in the video device section;
- I added the line 'Driver "fglrx" and I restarted x server;
- the screen flashed a while, and then it became black; I have been forced to make a reboot with ctrl+alt+canc since I couldn't get to the console anymore
- in the reboot process, I selected the single user mode so that the x server didn't start;
- I checked the /var/log/Xorg.log and I saw that the problem was related to depth set to 8, that was not supported by the fglrx driver, so I edited the /etc/X11/xorg.conf and I have added a line with "Defaultdepth 24" in Section "Screen", and I finally could get the desktop working.

I am an experienced linux user, and so I could solve this problem, but what would have a new user done?
If you really want to fix bug number 1 in launchpad, these installation problems should really not be present.

Update: another problem I have discovered is that when I try to log out from kde 4 session, or to shut down, the laptop hangs and I am forced to reboot it using magic sysrq key (AltGr+Prints screen+b).

### Post by jivana on 2008-04-30
upgraded from Gutsy, which seemed to work the second time of asking, after the installer reported network problems and not being able to get some libraries.
Rebooting did not work at the end.
This was caused by the broadcom b43legacy driver. 
I believe this has been confirmed as a bug: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+bug/190298](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+bug/190298)
After starting in the older kernel (cannot remember which one) recovery mode I was able to blacklist the driver as described in the bug and Hardy starts now - without wireless, but that's not new:
I have the bcm4303 wireless card, and have not been able to have any wireless connection since I started using Ubuntu, which was with Gutsy.
No news in Hardy, no wireless network connectivity at all.
Challenging.
DVDs are not mounting on Hardy, but I think that was the same in Gutsy.
Still working on those two issues before I chuck out my Windows machine...any help is very welcome!

---

### Post by Tiede on 2008-04-30
Here's another testimonial about a successful upgrade -- only, this one is more interesting than usual.
I had a few glitches to fix after upgrade, including a dpkg --configure -a because my lappy battery died while upgrading, so I can't really blame the upgrade process now can I?
More to the point though, after configuring everything and restarting, I had no internet!!!
since b43 replaced bcm43xx but couldn't update the firmware (not online), I was stuck until I could find an ethernet access - not available then.
So, I tried ndiswrapper manually. Didn't work (love that the package is pre-included, by the way.)
So, pouting, I removed the ndiswrapper driver, disabled b43 and left the system with no wireless connection at all. Then I sighed and turned it off.
Later on, I turned it back on, and lo and behold, I have wireless internet!!!
And it's waaaay better than before. See, I have a broadcom 4312 (rev 2) chipset and was suffering from the "low range" bug described elsewhere (sorry can't remember now, but it's on launchpad.) Now I get wireless where even windows cannot connect.

The most baffling thing is, since I had no internet to get updates from, and I did NOT specify where my windows bcmwl5a.inf file was located, i don't know how the system is managing to get my wireless card to work. In any way you put it, Hardy fixed itself --with no outside help, and while offline. I am amazed. and creeped out.
Otherwise, my system is now sleeker, faster, and feels stronger than gutsy.
And I am loving the new Firefox 3 beta.
It's still creepy, but in an awesome -I gotta tell this to everyone- kinda way. ;)

---

### Post by Cinnander on 2008-04-30
Upgrade process itself went well, and much much much faster (DL) than Feisty->Gutsy or Edgy->Feisty - mirrors seemed more capable this time.

My main PC went swimmingly. Sound appears to work better without needing alsa config tweaks (Abit Fatal1ty FP-IN9 onboard Nvidia jobby, previously needed an model=3stack line in the alsa config). Everything is OK so far. Not sure about FFx 3.0b5 though, as 80% of my extensions are now disabled pending updated releases which I'm concerned may not arrive until it goes live - I feel so naked.

Laptop was a bit more of an issue. I also have the **bcm43xx** issues. I did a clean install on here, formatting / (but not /home ;)) and installing Hardy. Laptop is now essentially useless until I can get this working.
The b43-fwcutter that the driver manager installed did not appear to work, so now I'm following **[thread=197102]this thread[/thread]** to see if it can be fixed.
Furthermore the laptop (Dell Inspiron 1300) no longer wants to suspend (it gets so far then gives up, however that is a lower priority than wireless atm!). Finally, the Fn+F2 to enable/disable wireless no longer works.

Summary: Mostly OK, few annoying issues. (Along the lines of "It was working before").

ciao

### Post by harpazo on 2008-04-30
Installed using Wubi on a HP zv6000 AMD Athlon64. 
(been using 7.04 on separate partition for awhile, wanted to give Wubi a spin and not destroy old partition until I was sure Hardy would work - never upgraded to Gutsy because of horror stories with HP)
-Installation was a breeze (once I downloaded .iso file via torrent and placed in a folder with Wubi)

-installed all multimedia stuff via this great! howto: [[COLOR="RoyalBlue"]Comprehensive Multimedia howto[/COLOR]]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=766683&highlight=howto")
-Installed latest ATI driver via Envy.

>>everything works great.. and I'm really surprised at the noticeable speed difference over Feisty, plus new ATI driver and graphics are MUCH better.  

Thanks so much for all the great posts and help in these forums.  It's really made my transition from Windows to Ubuntu wonderful.  Plus, I really enjoy being able to pass on what I've learned from others!

Harpazo

### Post by jdholifield on 2008-04-30
I decided to upgrade one of my Ubuntu PCs to Hardy.  First I decided to use the Update utility to perform the upgrade per the instructions here.  ( [http://www.ubuntu.com/getubuntu/upgrading](http://www.ubuntu.com/getubuntu/upgrading)  ).

In general, I don't think upgrading that way is such a great idea.  While we have reasonably fast DSL, it still took almost 24 hours for all of the files to download and for the update utility to complete its work.  Then when it got to the finishing step where you click the button to restart your PC, the system crashed with cryptic messages on the screen and then would no longer boot into either the old version of Ubuntu or the new.

I then downloaded the 8.04 desktop ISO with one of my other computers, and burned a CD which I then used to install from scratch on the PC with the damaged or failed upgrade.

The install from the CD also, I would not classify as "flawless" because on the first restart after the installation, the system crashed with similar cryptic error messages  Fortunately pressing the restart/power button on the computer was all it took to get the system to come up normally.  All in all the system install from the CD took I would guess 30-40 minutes including time to download and burn a CD, and other than a crash on the initial restart into the new system it was pretty painless.

If I were going to give the online upgrade installation a grade, I would unfortunately have to give it an F since it left the system in a basically unusable state.

The installation from the CD, I would give it a grade of B+, because even with points taken away for the crash, the installation was accomplished quickly with all hardware, network, sound, and video recognized and configured in about 30 minutes.

Unfortunately since this is my son's PC and he is fond of playing games such as Tremulous, his games will have to be reinstalled.

If you are interested in what type of system this is, its a Dell Inspiron 530n that was bought from Dell with Fiesty installed and previously upgraded to Gutsy.

---

### Post by markjensen on 2008-04-30
> **jviscosi said:**
> ...[list][*]My sensors dockapp (wmSuperMon) isn't working because the sensor information is evidently no longer being written to /sys/bus/i2c/drivers/it87-isa/9191-0d00/.  (In fact the it87-isa directory no longer exists.)  I do have all the i2c stuff installed and I've run sensors-detect.  Still working on this one, but it's hardly a showstopper.
[/LIST]I have the same problem with i2c no longer working.   I made do with using an "execi" call in conky to display the fan speed and temps - but it's a workaround, not a solution.

Also screen resolution was too low (like a VESA default) when upgrading.   xorg.conf was fine.  Not sure if the nVidia restricted driver cased this confusion when upgrading.  Putzed with settings back and forth a bit, and suddenly it started working again.

My xev key assignment to call up firefox inexplicably changed during the upgrade.   My other keys for mail and such work, but the key for web changed.   Re-did xev to get the new number and changed my keys file setting to match the new number.

kmix no longer affects volume when I mouse over it and scroll my middle mouse button.   Opening the full kmix mixer and adjusting PCM works though.   And my shortcut keys for volume (see 'xev, above) still worked after upgrade.

Overall, I was OK with the experience, but still room for improvement.   I guess a 7/10 for overall seamlessness - though if I were inexperienced in Linux, these little things would have had me very frustrated.

### Post by cpmdays on 2008-04-30
Clean install of 8.04.

Good - very fast, especially Wine

To fix the slow boot with blank screen I did what I have had to do since Edgy: change /etc/usplash.conf to 640x480, change /boot/grub/menu.lst to defoptions=splash vga=769

Bad - suspend is broken. It worked 50% of the time in Feisty, 100% of the time in Gutsy, and 0% of the time in Heron. Upon resume the screen "shakes" back and forth very quickly.

I tried to fix it by changing my monitor setting and graphic driver to what it was in Gutsy (Plug 'n Play, Intel Experimental modesetting) but then lost the ability to change keyboard layout and the screen resolution was stuck at 640x480. After a little more tweaking with the graphics I got a black screen and no response from keyboard. Had to reinstall. 

Bad - auto hide panel is excruciatingly slow
Bad - Opera keeps crashing

Not sure I will keep Heron but for now I will play with it a little more.

### Post by exoren22 on 2008-04-30
Well, I've had nothing but love for hardy on two systems. 

On my mom's machine, I have been running linux for her ever since her xp partition caught on fire (well, the damage was on that level), and the kubuntu upgrade process was the opposite of painful. I just followed the kubuntu.org documentation and it upgraded in about an hour. 

On my laptop, I did a fresh install of all three of the desktop variants (Xfce, Gnome and KDE4), but I'm still sticking to my xfce roots. Even hibernate finally works now! (ATi graphics driver and all)

Once I get my sister to love linux I can chuck windows altogether! (I'm gonna wubi her machine tomorrow)

These threads usually get bogged down with the people looking for help or ranting about a bad experience, and remembering all the forum talk from gutsy, I wanted to share my great experiences with everybody.

Edit: And I really love the new hardy wallpaper. Its no longer something that turns people off, and I even made several blue versions for kde and xubuntu (or anything, really, blue just looks better :lolflag: ).

### Post by solitaire on 2008-04-30
Ok I could not get my Internal wireless card (Broadcom BCM94301MP) to work in 8.04

So i took it out..

***update***

Rummaging around my IT spares room ;), I came across a busted Zoom X6 ADSL wireless modem. I took it to bits and took out the Mini-pci Wireless card.
Turned out to be  a InterSil 3886.

Plugged it in to my laptop rebooted now i have Wireless :D:D

It's seen as:
```

02:03.0 Network controller: Intersil Corporation ISL3886 [Prism Javelin/Prism Xbow] (rev 01)

```

So Things are peachy with me and my Hardy Heron :D

### Post by robeast on 2008-04-30
I did a fresh 64 bit install on a new hard drive. Hardy didn't setup grub properly (or at all). I already have Gutsy on one hd and XP on another, and now hardy on a third, so I can understand that grub got a little confused. 

I messed around in the grub command line to set it up again on the main hd. It didn't recognize that the new hd had Hardy on it, so I set up grub on the Hardy hd and copied the menu.lst entry from that drive into the menu.lst on the main drive. Then I received an Error 17 from grub :)

Changing the boot priority in my motherboard BIOS did the trick and now I'm loving the improvements in Hardy Heron! Only problem is that I'll probably have to go through all of this again when I make the Hardy hd a Master and the Gutsy hd a Slave.

### Post by tijmz on 2008-04-30
*Upgrade - worked but had few things to solve*

I went from Gutsy 7.10 to Hardy last night and as far as I can tell for now - you never know whether a problem still shows up - it works fine.

At first I had some difficulty with Compiz: the window animations moved less smootly than I was used to. What worked for me was disabling my ATI driver, deinstalling Compiz, then EnvyNG for a new driver and reinstall Compiz. Right now it works great.

The other problem I had was [Avant Windows Navigator malfunctioning](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=4843981), but that was solved rather easily.

Both problems would probably not have occurred with a fresh installation.

### Post by bdalzell on 2008-04-30
I upgraded two computers, my laptop and my desktop. The laptop upgrade was basically a new install from the CD because I had taken the harddrive out of my recently dead cheap pentium iii laptop and put it into a different brand cheap laptop. After that swap Edgie booted only into command line so it was easier to do a new install into / with the CD and since it was a new install i chose to use Hardy. Once Hardy was on the Laptop I decided to upgrade the desktop using the "Upgrade" feature in Synaptic.

This was not as successful. First I went from Feisty to Gutsy - that resulted in a still working system. However it took around  4 hours (I have DSL) for all the files to get into place. 

Going from Gutsy to Hardy I hoped that I could use the Hardy CD rather than downloading from the internet. However I was not clever enough to figure out how to do that so it was another 4 hours using the Synaptic upgrade feature.

The most frustrating thing that I had happen was this. While it was downloading I was called away from the computer for around an hour and when I came back the machine was frozen with a black screen. After various things were tried I finally had to power it down and then restart. This resulted in my having a broken installation without a working X server. This ment that I finally had to do a new install from the CD. I had the software screen saver turned off so I am not sure why the screen went black.

### Post by chili555 on 2008-04-30
I upgraded two desktops and two laptops. One desktop was a yawn, the other had to go Feisty -> Gusy -> Heron and, while time consuming, went smoothly. I started that one to work just before I went to bed.

On my Thinkpad T40, I had no real problems, although reading these forums lead me to think I might have an issue with my Aironet wireless card. No such bad luck.

On my T60, my 3945ABG wireless needed this: [http://linuxtechie.wordpress.com/2008/04/24/making-intel-wireless-3945abg-work-better-on-ubuntu-hardy/](http://linuxtechie.wordpress.com/2008/04/24/making-intel-wireless-3945abg-work-better-on-ubuntu-hardy/)> This resulted in my having a broken installation without a working X server.I had a similar problem in Gutsy when i knocked the plug out of the wall powering my T60 with no battery. After I paniced, cursed, broke my coffee cup on the floor, I entered the GRUB menu, booted into the previous kernel and continued the upgrade. When I ran *sudo apt-get update,* it said I had a few things broken and told me what commands to run. I ran them and the beast lives!

### Post by pjfl on 2008-04-30
Upgraded a working Thinkpad Z61m from 7.10 to 8.04. The online update program became unreponsive and I had to force a reboot near the end of the upgrade

The ipw3945 wifi driver was replaced with the ipl3945 driver. The LED indicator stopped working. It no longer connects to my WAP when the system boots. Multiple connect/disconnects in Wifi-radar will eventually result in a connection. The connection is randomly dropped. This driver bug has been known about for over a month and yet it still made it into an end user release

The smb4k client refused to connect to my server. After fixing that I'm noticing little hangs in Amorok's playback when the file is on the remote server

Usage of my / partition has increased from 40% to 60%, I think the cleanup phase failed to run

Kdesudo was broken, I had to add the host name to the localhost line in /etc/hosts

The forced and unwelcome "upgrade" to a Beta release of Firefox has left me with a butt ugly functionally challenged web browser. My theme of choice is not compatible and about 50% of my addons are borked to

Clicking on links in Thunderbird no longer causes Firefox to open the page, nothing happens

The alien splash screen resizes after the password is entered and before the desktop appears. This looks stupid when then green progress indicators miss their intended locations on the background image

I no longer have hard disk temperature monitoring

The software brightness control is broken

This is what I've discovered in the 12 hours since the upgrade completed. 

I am now depressed and have lost the will to program

---

### Post by BrownieBoy on 2008-04-30
Clean installs of both Ubuntu and Kubuntu Hardy.  Both versions installed from a live CD.  Had no problems from downloading the .iso images from local mirror.

**Kubuntu**
Install went fine.  Clicked on Restricted Drivers and it automatically downloaded the correct driver package for my Nvidia card. Sweet.  Only problem is that I have no sound whatsover.  I've yet to check into that.


**Ubuntu**
Install went fine.  But unlike Kubuntu, clicking on Restricted Drivers did nothing.  I had to download the correct driver package myself from Synaptic.  It worked fine after I installed and rebooted though.

One absolute killer bug: if I have Desktop Effects enabled, and try to drag a file to or from Nautilus, the desktop comes to halt.  You can still move the mouse about, slowly, but to all intents and purposes, it's locked.  Ctrl->Alt->Backspace doesn't break out of it either.

PulseAudio sounds like a great idea, but it doesn't work well for me.  It's fine with sound from Amarok etc, but in my games it either doesn't give any sound at all (Nexuiz, Aliens Vs Predator Gold) or all the sound effects are subject to a 3 second delay (Quake 4, Enemy Territory: Quake Wars).  I can workaround the delays be issuing a Killall PulseAudio before launching.  PulseAudio ofter refuses to reload afterwards though.

Suspend doesn't work in either Ubuntu or Kubuntu, and hasn't done so since Ubuntu Feisty for me.

All in all, Ubuntu Hardy feels like a rushed job, which is worrying seeing as it's an LTS.  I'm back to Ubuntu Gutsy until my Ubuntu Hardy problems are fixed.  Or I'll see if I can get some sound out of Kubuntu Hardy.

---

### Post by taelatus on 2008-04-30
I chose the option for a flawless upgrade but that was only after I reinstalled a clean copy of Gutsy.  

Upgrading to Hardy from Gutsy went off without a hitch the first time.  However, upon rebooting Gnome loaded but mouse support just didn't work.  I searched around and couldn't find much information on the subject so I figured that a fresh install of Hardy was probably the best route.  I rebooted into Windows to download the Hardy ISO.  Upon booting from the CD, I chose the option to install Hardy and subsequently got dumped into a strange terminal.  I couldn't figure out how I was supposed to proceed with the install and gave up after trying a few of the boot options users posted on these forums.  At this point I was frustrated and decided to cut my losses and do a fresh install of Gutsy.  I installed Gutsy, setup my partitions and all was well except for a problem where Grub was installed to the wrong hard drive (the HDD on the primary IDE instead of SATA).  I eventually managed to get grub on the right hard drive however.  Once I was in Gutsy once again I fully updated the OS and figured that one last shot at upgrading wouldn't hurt after the 6 hours I'd spent so far.  This time the upgrade worked (seemingly) flawlessly.  I'm running Hardy as I type this.

With all these issues, my faith in Ubuntu has been rattled somewhat.  I assumed Hardy would be the easiest ubuntu yet.  So far, Gutsy has been my best experience.

### Post by Zer0Nin3r on 2008-05-01
Upgraded both the desktop and laptop (Compaq R3000).  Still experiencing some problems with both

On the custom desktop everything works, but I have always had problems interfacing the desktop with my HDTV.  Usually the process goes in that I upgrade the driver from nVidia (I have a 7900) and installing it, then editing my xorg.conf to interface with the television by enabling some options.

8.04 works on the desktop, but **not** with the **latest** kernel.  I have to revert to the previous kernel if I want the display to correctly work.  Odd.  Still experience the moderately frequent hang ups in applications (Firefox, Amarok.)

Laptop works for the most part with the exception that I cannot get flash to work within Firefox.  I tried re-installing flash from Adobe's website with the RPM and converting it with *alien.*  No luck.  Tried falling back on the previous kernel, forget about it.  I then start having problems with the display in not recognizing the onboard nVidia graphic card.

Suspend and resume still seems broken.

I always look forward to the new release, but always end up disappointed in the beginning in that the issues that I expected to be resolved still remain unresolved, or some other issues come up.  Come to think about it, it seems that the major problems I have are Adobe Flash, WiFi, and Video problems, and that's with the last three versions of Ubuntu including the recent release.

**Update**
I fixed ([with help]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=695674")) Flash by doing the following:

Copy *libflashplayer.so* from '/usr/lib/flash-plugin' --> '/usr/lib/firefox-3.0b5/plugins'

When you restart the browser it **should** work.

### Post by aninaiian on 2008-05-01
Let's see... I first upgraded to hardy back when the repos first came about (before any iso's), and it was a very painless upgrade.  Considering at the time Hardy was Gutsy, that was to be expected.

:lolflag:

I eventually realized that my partion scheme wasn't ideal, and policykit never really worked correctly.  So, around 5th alpha, I reinstalled on my laptop and decided to install it on my desktop.  Again the laptop install was a very painless process and everything worked out of the box.  Granted it booted much slower than my previous very slimmed down Ubuntu from a Gutsy server install, but that was to be expected. 

I had a problem with the desktop; however, it was the same problem I always had with Ubuntu.  My wifi card didn't work so I blacklisted the kernel module for the card and used ndiswrapper which worked as it always did.

Overall, I have to say that Hardy works a bit better than Gutsy did.  After resume, my laptop will now connect to wifi networks other than the one it was connected to prior to suspend.  My desktop wireless is no longer flakey (no dropping after about three days uptime or needing another computer connected to the router to reconnect after around three days uptime).

---

### Post by Ameneon on 2008-05-01
In short: Near-flawless upgrade.

I've been using various linux and unix distro's more or less since late 90's but albeit I ran FreeBSD 4 something as main OS on an old desktop I used as test machine some years ago, I've never before used any unix or linux distro as main OS on the system I use daily, before I decided to get an additional HDD and install 7.10 on this a couple of months back. That install went relatively ok however I had to edit the startup options adding the acpi=off and noapic options in order to get normal boot into the desktop. Aside from this glitch, everything else worked flawlessly so rather than stay in there from time to time to learn but remain in WinXP most of the time, I more or less immediately found myself spending more and more time in Ubuntu and less and less in XP, and then only in order to game (the day most game developers support Linux is likely the day MS for real sees its market domination vaporize).

Anyway, while I had at the time intended to rather install the Beta of 8.04, this one would consistently dump me into the busybox terminal no matter what I tried, so I resigned myself to 7.10 for the time being. However, when 8.04 went live I downloaded the live CD to test whether I now could boot it, made a backup of the 7.10 installation just in case, and clicked "upgrade". About 30 minutes later the process was complete without a hitch, although I caused a small snag on myself - when 7.10 required the edited apci/apic settings, I edited menu.lst, and during the upgrade I decided to keep the old menu.lst rather than replace it with the new one. Unfortunately, this logically meant the old kernel was being loaded which caused the GPU driver in turn to not be loaded correctly (an Nvidia 7800GTX), but after a little pondering and tinkering I modified menu.lst again to point to the right kernel, which solved the problem. Since then, the system has been rock solid, and in combination with VirtualBox and Wine, I only need the MS installation on my old disk in order to play games. Ubuntu is nothing short of spectacular in how well it works and easy it is to use, compared the days of yore.

My system is using an Asus AM2-based MB (can't recall the exact model), AM2 6000 X2 CPU, 2x1GB Corsair RAM, the abovementioned BFG Nvidia 7800GTX GPU, and 3 HDD's (1 Seagate 160g PATA using an adaptor to SATA, 1 Seagate 750g SATA and 1 WD 160g SATA, which is the ubuntu system disk). I also use a Logitech Quickcam 5000 Pro which works but I've got a little difficulty getting anything to configure this correctly, in particular for low light conditions), a Creative Zen which is recognized and fully accessible through Rhythmbox or Gnomad 2, and a Canon SD600 camera that also works flawlessly.

So if you didn't gather yet, I'm rather pleased with 8.04 :guitar:

---

That is all I have noticed so far - please do read my other topic and advise if you can.

### Post by hvacr on 2008-05-01
I had gutsy running on my laptop, with no issues at all, other than the odd flash problem. I did a fresh install with heron, but I am not impressed. I removed FF3 as it kept crashing, and installed FF2. I find Heron slower than gutsy, even with 2 gigs of ram. Some of the new features I like in Heron though.

My desktop is dual boot, xp 64 and Gutsy, did a fresh install of Heron, and had nothing but black screens. I Removed FF3 and went back to FF2, which got rid of the black screens. The system was very slow, so I ran it with out any visual effects. Now the system is very stable, and much faster. The specs for the desktop are very good, so there should be no speed problems. Under gutsy it ran very well, other than the odd FF2 crash  because of flash.

Over all I am not happy with Heron, I will stick with it in hopes they fix it up over the next while.

### Post by technotika on 2008-05-01
Tried offficial 8.04 on desktop and it went well apart from some Bogus verbose shut down errors which meant nothing but have gone with the recent updates.

To me I have noticed better overall feel of the system and things have started working that never did before; for example WINE + NEWSLEECHER. Although since I started with HELLANZB I'm not so sure I need it any more, better to search ngindex.com than use the search function from NL.

Full of confidence in 8.04 I went to do the laptop and that was a different kettle of fish.

First thing that made my heart sink was when I typed my login in the login field the font was  MASSIVE, we're talking 72 plus. Then I had no wireless. 

Also the installer was goofed up aswell  - the menus grew to fill the screen and I couldnt even shut them down  - dont even know how I installed it really. 

Eventually I found the forum threads to fix that and things were ok again, until I played a video. It slowed down and was choppy and seemed to drag the system down. 
Again found that needed to remove xserver-xgl and then let ENVY do the video cards.

Now all is fixed and running very well. Bit disappointed that things went backwards on the laptop other than that looking forward to how it pans out over the next few weeks as they patch it up.

I have a feeling 8.10 will be what they all thought 8.04 was going to be.

No more of a ball ache than getting vista to work though remember that!!!
