### Post by vexorian on 2008-05-01
Did a feisty to hardy upgrade using

```
sudo apt-get -d dist-upgrade
```
and then:
```
sudo apt-get dist-upgrade
```

Before that I tried to strip down my stuff, getting rid of repos and .debs, my biggest mistake was IMHO that I forgot to reinstall ubuntu-desktop before doing the upgrade.

The upgrade process was chaotic since I had a lot of dependency issues and broken packages, I was able though to recover by trying to install again, and once I installed hardy's ubuntu-desktop I only needed few more tries to fix the packages issues, I did not use dist-upgrade anymore but simply synaptic.

After the upgrade process I rebooted and it was unable to use old xorg , it looks like the fix attempt after that screwed my screen and keyboard configuration. But eventually ubuntu offered me to install the restricted driver and then I used my old xorg.conf file to restore my resolution (I think though that nvidia-xconfigure would have worked as well)

Restoring the keyboard was harder since I didn't remember exactly what name my keyboard had I eventually figured it was a genius**** scroll one and language finally worked.

Even though I had full resolution working, 3d acceleration was gone, I tried a lot of things but eventually I gave up and used a driver from nvidia's site to be able to have acceleration again.

I was able to find a virtualbox .deb for hardy thanks to these forums.

As of now , everything works all right asides from a small graphic bug with custom SVG icons in desktop launchers.

I think that for the unstandard upgrade method I have used and having forgotten to initially have ubuntu-desktop, this had about the expected amount of issues, so no complaints.

I like hardy, I have noticed the few polishing that has been done, for example fsck does not require to change the boot screen into a console anymore, that's cool. I also noticed sudo specifies whose password you are supposed to type. Overall boot time has improved mildly and ubuntu seems more responsive, I already had firefox3 so I didn't have issues in that regard, I noticed though that it freezes when compiz is enabled, it is a good thing I don't like to have compiz enabled.

I am happy that at the end of all the work, everything is working ok.

---

### Post by cmmichael on 2008-05-01
8.04 = Hardly Hearing?

So far so good except for sound. Upon starting the new Hardy install, there was no sound from audio or video files, or in Firefox. After scanning the forums, I decided to deinstall PulseAudio and reconfig to ALSA. After some trial & error, I have partial success - good news: Hibernation mode works. Bad news: it resets sound settings to Pulse or something else that is silent. It takes a reboot to get the settings back.

I use Skype as a service, and PulseAudio apparently doesn't work with it. Big oops. Maybe I'll find a workaround for hibernation, but a reboot only takes a minute. 

Intel Core 2 Duo - G33 chipset - Integrated Graphics & Audio - 4 GB Ram

### Post by marvess on 2008-05-01
Found upgrade (always fresh install for me) very disappointing indeed.
Not at all what I expected from a LTS version....

Cannot agree with decision to put FF3 as default for a LTS version, as it is not quite flawless yet. For one, I cannot login to Scalix Web Access anymore which for me as well as for a lot of my clients is an absolute priority.

Also cannot agree with decision to set PulseAudio as default. For one, the darn thing just plainly refuses to support 5.1 surround....De-installing it makes a mess out of package maintenance. Again, this is not what I think should be in a LTS version.

I certainly wasn't waiting for an Ubuntu LTS release with lot's of (unstable) eye-candy and bleeding edge gizmo's....I don't care if Evolution is version such and such, as there is no working Scalix connector for a long time to come (building one seems to be sort of a painfull proces with a new one just released for 7.10 -Evolution 2.12.1)

What I did expect was 7.10 with all the (minor) flaws worked out...Sorry to say, it's quite the opposite with lot's of major flaws introduced. 

To make a long story short: IMHO, bleeding edge releases are for tinkering in spare time and for great fun, LTS versions should be rock-solid, stable business boxes....period.

At the moment, I think 6.06 LTS is for business users who don't care to have reasonably current apps, just a stable system and, for the time being, 7.10 the pseudo LTS for the rest....

---

### Post by flightless bird on 2008-05-01
> **flightless bird said:**

I have to quit this (ctl-alt-bspace) and log into gnome, log out, and then log back into xfce. This is only an irritation at start-up, but it is an irritation. Any ideas would be warmly welcomed.

Even weirder, all of a sudden my applications aren't applying the gtk theme. The theme manager knows which one I want, but whenever I start an application it appears ugly and unthemed and I have to go to the theme manager and reselect the theme that was already selected, at which point the theme is correctly applied. This one really confuses me, and again any ideas would be warmly welcomed.

cheers
It turns out the theme issue is specfically with the human themes, all other ones work fine. This issue seems to have been reported during hardy beta testing

### Post by Sunflower1970 on 2008-05-01
Finally got brave and installed Hardy on my Dell desktop. Glad I did. I was very nervous from what I was hearing about nVidia graphics so I expected to have some problems. Except for one problem that I caused (an ID-10-T error) which I had to reinstall, everything was so smooth, I was shocked. Restricted drives installed, no having to use Envy, all my Compiz effects work wonderfully, and, as with my laptop, this thing is much faster than it was under Feisty. (I never put Gutsy on this machine) 

One thing I was worried about was my combination Epson printer/scanner. I've always had to do some configuring with it. This time, to my delight, I turned it on, Hardy recognized it right away, I installed V4l and that recognized my scanner right away. Shocked. It all took less than a minute to check it out to make sure it all worked. 

Overall, I'm overjoyed. I only wish I had more time to play around with Hard on my Dell, but I'm off to Washington DC in a few minutes to visit relatives. :)

### Post by perspectoff on 2008-05-01
Big headache with monitor, monitor resolution, sound.

Everything worked with manual options in Feisty and Gutsy, but the options (for my monitor, anyway) disappeared in Hardy and the autodetect is awful, as it was in Gutsy. 

Windows would appear halfway off the screen, and as has been a defect in X windows for years, you can't move them back onto the screen if your resolution is screwed up.

Plus, the login screen works independently from the desktop, so setting options in the desktop does not help the screwed up appearance of the login screen.

Getting sound to work was also a bit of misery. Pulse Audio may be nice, but applications are all expecting ALSA.

Plus, the sound was turned off by default, which took me forever to figure out.

Also, for some reason my nVidia sound card was not recognized automatically.

Took me over 3 days to solve these problems.

### Post by ubnoobie on 2008-05-01
i've installed ubuntu 8.04 on a few systems so far... everything clean installs; i had to retire my old dapper system a few months ago.

ubuntu via wubi on a dell (dimension) athlon dual core w/ winmce. only issue is xvid video playback isn't always picture-perfect (might be same as [_*this post*_]("http://ubuntuforums.org/archive/index.php/t-489743.html")); haven't investigated possible solutions yet. this is a dual core 2.2 ghz, 1gb ram and nvidia 6150 graphics on-board (restricted drivers). haven't decided which existing system around here will be my new full-time dedicated ubuntu desktop; but for now, the wubi-installed system will work just fine.

[COLOR="DarkRed"]imho, wubi right on the cd is the _single most significant_ feature addition in hardy.[/COLOR]

ubuntu as only os on a tualatin celeron with intel i810 in oem mode. i did have to go in and uninstall compiz as it wasn't working right (no surprise there, except that it was enabled by default on this old antique) with the integrated video.

xubuntu as only os on a coppermine p3 with via (i think) graphics; also in oem mode. no problems to report.

the last two are old relics that we're getting rid of, thus the oem mode installs.

i'm chompin' at the bit to try out mythbuntu, but need to get a different (read: supported & hardware-based) tuner card first. winxp and windvr work fine with my (software) ati tuner, but the same pc (an old palomino) isn't fast enough for it when running myth.

### Post by karlatsaic on 2008-05-01
This seemed to go smoothly, but when my laptop restarted, I could not do anything at first. I would click on icons and things would appear only after many seconds. Finally, I restarted X Windows (ctrl-alt-backspace) and was able to pull down my System/Administration/Hardware Drivers (again, took about 60 seconds as response was so slow) - and then disabled my special Nvidia driver which I had enabled to get my laptop to work well with InFocus projectors. Once I did that, all seems great (InFocus projector didn't work but will troubleshoot later). This seems pretty minor compared to people who seemed to have had real problems. I was especially pleased that my full drive encryption with dm-crypt/LUKS continue to work flawlessly.
[edit]Later, I re-enabled the nvidia accelerated driver, and after futzing around a bit, resorting to my saved xorg.conf, everything is back to normal, including the nvidia driver which I need for projecting presentations.

### Post by SonicJMC on 2008-05-01
Umm... wow.

My PC on Ubuntu 7.10
Fast, zippy
Lots of pretty, thanks to emerald
Quite a nice experience

My PC on Ubuntu 8.04
SLOW
Emerald is now not running
SLOW
My Cherry-Soda icons were removed
SLOW
FireFox icon got replaced with a blank glassy square
SLOW
It's just about unusable
SLOW SLOW SLOW SLOW SLOW!

Well.. umm... Good Job?
Congrats on the new release?
Umm... Thanks?

SYSTEM STATS:
Processor: AMD Anthlon 64 x2, if I remember correctly, it's just shy of 3ghz. I'd look it up, but I don't have the patience to load the menus in the top panel.
RAM: 1024 - Dual Channel
HDD type: Ubuntu is running from 2 SATA-2 drives, in a RAID 0 (via the advanced install CD)

Well... I'm going to slowly shut down and get to bed.
I don't know what to do about this now, rolling back is a pain.
I feel like my PC just got kicked in the nuts after being so excited to meet the guy prior to said nut kick. 
I feel like I grabbed a HUGE Xmas present after staring at it FOREVER, HAPPILY tore it open, and it was full of poo. Except now the poo made my computer poo.
It's like biting in to an apetizing looking desert, and Ew, it's "diet" or "lite".
Getting in to a Ferrari... and finding somebody installed a Hyundai accent engine.
Jumping in to a refreshing pool - but finding out the water is hot.

OMG, what a let down!

### Post by stianalm on 2008-05-02
I upgraded from Gutsy which went smooth in itself. One pet peeve of mine is that the menu.lst in Grub is always overwritten whenever I get a new kernel. I have a dual-boot with Windows XP for my wife and that menu entry disappear at all kernel and grub updates. If I forget to update menu.lst manually I get a lot of flak for ruining her system.

Anyway, fortunately for me I remembered this time as trying to boot Hardy crashed and dumped me in the busybox prompt. After rebooting to windows I could see a lot of people on this forum having the same problem. It turns out that Hardy couldn't find the second of my three drives, and that's where my root partition lies. For me the solution was adding "all_generic_ide" to the kernel line in the menu.lst.

That made Hardy boot. The next problem was my nvidia driver that had been messed up so I could only get glorious 800x600 resolution. Again, searching these forums helped me find other with the same issue and a solution involving reinstalling the driver and removing a blacklisting.

All in all I spent about 4 hours upgrading and 4-5 hours troubleshooting and fixing. Not good... This is the first upgrade I've had any problems with since first installing Warty. Every other upgrade has been totally hassle-free and has also added value by recognizing more hardware. So to me Hardy feels like a step in the wrong direction.

---

### Post by frodon on 2008-05-02
> **poekie3000 said:**
> My main concern is that this will scare people off, as I' ve read that numerous people are having this issue. And if I'm not even sure what this is or how to fix this, how can we except non-linux users to use ubuntu if they run into this?If you are able to find this thread to post it's not a big deal to read a sticky thread IMO :
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=773851](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=773851)

So adding "all_generic_ide" in your menu.lst file at the end of your kernel line should solve your issue.

As for laptop designed to work on windows you can't expect a 100% result all the time. This would be different if you bought a linux pre-installed laptop.

I'm not saying linux shouldn't work on every laptop but just that you can't expect it as this is more likely to never happen. So if you buy a laptop made for windows, it's not a pain to have to tweak some little things to get linux working as the hardware is not designed for it.

### Post by mchaggis on 2008-05-02
Ok... I must admit that I am still getting used to the upgrade stuff. I have been using linux as my primary OS for about 8years now and have used many different flavours. I move to Ubuntu last April, 7.04 and fell in love, in October I dutifully upgraded, and for the first time in 8years had an upgrade work without downloading CD's and all over the network and work perfectly (well nearly due to my sound card)

This time, while it went all ok (even with the sound card issue) and I was back up and running in under 3hours (which includes the downing and teh reconfiguring of my sound card)... The fly in the ointment came when I launched firefox and my machine froze. Reboot. No more freezes but FF3? FF3? It's still beta and while I have no objections using beta software, and a lot of the new features of firefox 3 are good, it is still to flakey be installed as part of a main distro. Have now downgraded to firefox 2 and firebug now work and no more crashing.

### Post by klaasvanschelven on 2008-05-02
Hi,

I wish I could post in the poll twice for two very different experiences.

On my girlfriend's laptop (Thinkpad T60) I've upgraded from 6.10. This solved the problem of no support for ATI cards which had been showing up as
* wrong resoltion in 6.10
* black screens in all following versions
However, with Hardy 8.04 I get full resolution and all desktop effects (by accepting non-free driver) and full resolution no-effects without the driver. Big improvement.
The other problem on this laptop, no wireless, still doesn't work.

My Lenovo 3000 N100 FPG (see [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/LaptopTestingTeam/Lenovo3000N100_FPG](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/LaptopTestingTeam/Lenovo3000N100_FPG)) was made quite unworkable with an "upgrade" (reinstall really).

Sound works out of the box now (didn't before, so had to compile some stuff myself), but not for skype & music at the same time. I guess I'll have to do the recompiling regardless.

Wireless doesn't work anymore. I have an intel 3945 which worked using a partly non-free driver up to Gutsy. This option is no longer available in Hardy. more here: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=777732](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=777732)

My screen is really dark: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=778580](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=778580)

And the "del" button on the numpad doesn't work anymore.

Together it's too much to be worth it....

What I liked (at first sight): new firefox, and a trackerd that doesn't eat up my processor and I/O but is nice.

edit:
Graphics card seems to be slower too, though not profiled or further info available

### Post by kordosky on 2008-05-02
I had a strange upgrade experience, which I'll share here. I still don't really understand it.

(1) I was upgrading from 7.04 to 8.04 via CD install, rewriting my / partition. /home was on a different partition

(2) the cd was the beta 8.04 and the install worked

(3) used update-manager to update 400+ packages.  At this stage nano, cupsys, hplip and hpijs had some problems configuring themselves. cupsys configuration at least would get hungup in some infinite error loop. I had the "Details" window open and hit Ctrl-C to bypass. I only knew to do this because I'd run the configure command (dpkg --configure -a) in a terminal and it hit the same problem. 

(5) Also, logging in I found that my desktop no longer has menu bars at the top and bottom (gnome-panel).  This was present from the get go and I had hoped that the update would fix this problem, but it did not.  Now begins a rather frustrating saga.  Since I kept my /home partition I figured that the issue was with some ~/.XXX file containing settings which were not compatible with the newer version of ubuntu. I was able to support that idea by creating a new user, and logging in to find that the menu bars showed up just fine. 

(6) I created a launcher to make an xterm and then proceeded to backup and delete various ~/.XXX files (.gconf, .gconfd, .config, .metacity, .compiz, .nautilus, etc...) and restart my xsession (ctrl-alt-bksp) but I still had the problem. Interestingly, my custom background also remained.  Confusing: what file holds this bit of config?  Also gnome-panel was running, and had always been running (could see it in top). I tried to use the config GUIs (gnome-control-center, gdmsetup) and tried playing around with themes and other settings, none of which seemed all that appropriate for my problem, and none of which had any effect.

(7) Desperation, but then noticed that gnome-panel was taking nearly 100% of CPU. I recall having this problem in 7.04.  The solution was to kill it and restart.  Did this, and menu bars appear. Now can logout and back in and see menu bars. 

(5) Utterly confusing. My problem survived: (a) login/logout (ctrl-alt-bksp) (b) rebooting the machine (with 2 different kernels), (c) ctrl-alt-bksp after removing .gconf and other .XXX directories in my home area.  I seem to have stumbled on a solution but I don't understand it.

Any ideas?

### Post by oyvindaa on 2008-05-02
Install - worked flawlessly.

### Post by saordubh on 2008-05-02
> **saordubh said:**
> I just upgraded from Ubuntu 7.10 to Ubuntu 8.04 on my HP Pavilion dv6000 laptop. Everything seemed to go well and initially everything I tried worked just as before.  However, when I clicked on the "Synaptic Package Manager" the request timed out with no observable results.  I went to the terminal and attempted to run Synaptic.  It ran but in the read-only mode.  I tried to use sudo to run Synaptic but got an "Unable to resolve host xxxx" message.  I attempted to edit /etc/sudoers using sudo visudo and got the same message.  It appears that I have lost the ability to run any applications as root.  Can anyone suggest a possible course of action?


Thanks to frodon for the original clue and Larryj on the Bug#32906 forum I have successfully fixed my machine.  It was as simple as System->Administration->Network, Unlock, Authenticate, and then editing 127.0.0.1 to localhost youronewordhostname and editing 127.0.1.1 to youronewordhostname.

---

### Post by maltman on 2008-05-02
My previous upgrade to Gutsy went flawlessly. This time around, my upgrade to Hardy was quite nasty. And left a very bad taste in my mouth.

First off, during the upgrade process there was a failure with the NetworkManager package and the upgrade process died at that point and the entire computer was unresponsive.
I rebooted but all of my video settings were completely hosed (vid card and monitor not detected). So I initially attempt to upgrade again in case there were other packages not upgraded, only to find out that sudo no longer works. Reading the forum I saw others with the same issue, rebooted in recovery mode as root to manually fix the hosts file.
Then installed EnvyNG because the current nvidia drivers weren't working. Also noticed that my xorg file was overwritten so also had to manually modify that.
Everything seemed to be ok after that. Until I used FireFox. All of my settings were gone there, and when I tried to reinstall all of my add-ons and themes I was just presented with an error. Ended up switching to SwiftWeasel which works just fine. Never did get FireFox working properly.

Whew.

I was quite displeased with the poor quality of this release.

### Post by mwacky on 2008-05-02
Several Experiences:

Dell Precision 370: Installed Flawlessly
Gateway Performance 550 Mhz: Updated Flawlessly
Dell Optipex 240: Update to Beta: Host files needed editing
Hp Pavilion zv6000 laptop: Update needed to fixed broadcom wireless and host file

Host file editing, cannot sudo so you must boot into recovery mode and edit the hosts file:  pico /etc/hosts

Broadcom Wireless: sudo aptitude purge b43-fwcutter
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install b43-fwcutter

Awesome Release!

### Post by El Lance-O on 2008-05-02
So far thing have been HORRIBLE.

Too many bugs and too many unstable characteristics showing up of a badly developed release.

My CPU constantly is going crazy. Like it has a mind of it's own almost, to the point at times where it's locked up and I have to power down improperly.

Firefox is an embarrassment, and I think it gives the browser a horrible name to be included in such an unstable release.

Don't get me wrong, I LOVE Ubuntu and have been an supporter since Dapper, but Hardy is just pathetic. I mean, COME ON. It may be working for some, but overall it seems like there are more people having problems than not.

If you are thinking "This guy must just have an outdated computer" here's my specs:

Preloaded Inspiron e1505n from Dell, preinstalled with Feisty, upgraded to Gutsy (no problems) and now upgraded to Hardy.

Intel Centrino Core Duo processor T2080 @ 3.46GHz
Nvidia 7300 Go video card
1GB of RAM
80GB HD
Intel n-series network card

etc, etc.

But overall, the point is I bought this laptop preloaded for a reason: it was built specifically to be compatible with Ubuntu, COMPLETELY.

And now my wireless light doesn't work, my processor is constantly using between 20-100% (killing my battery in a heartbeat, haven't been able to take the laptop out since the upgrade), my Desktop is now a mess because I cannot make launchers with no names, and last but not least EVERYTHING IS JUST SLOW.

So the moral of the story is, Ubuntu devs, you let me down for the first time in 2 years, and I am really really disappointed.

For shame, for shame...

### Post by frodon on 2008-05-03
Common linux apps to ghost your ubuntu partition is partimage, however the partition you want to ghost must be unmounted so that's why the best way to do this is to use a liveCD.
If your system is broken due to testing or upgrade then you can boot your liveCD and use partimage to restore your ubuntu partition from the ghost image you created, you will get the exact same thing than when you ghosted your partition.

Using this method you can do any upgrade/install without any stress as you will be able to get your system back to what it was previously whenever you want.

Partimage is not the only tool, there's many on linux and many on windows which support to ghost a linux partition, i myself use norton ghost for exemple as i find convenient to be able to ghost and restore my ubuntu partition from another OS, it's the only use of my windows partition lol

### Post by guildofghostwriters on 2008-05-03
Ditched XP for Gutsy last week - originally planned to get Gutsy working then wait a few months before upgrading but trying to get ATI proprietary drivers and dual-head to work loused everything up so I decided a reinstall was easier than working out how to fix everything and so went for Hardy.

Mostly Hardy has been pretty easy. I needed to sort out firmware for my scanner and drivers for my rt73 chipset dongle (which network manager just doesn't like so I got rid and am using rutilt) but that was pretty easy. I tried many ways to get the ATI drivers working and have failed. In fact, many attempts once again loused everything up to the point where it was easier (for me) to reinstall Hardy from scratch. For now I'm going to forget the ATI drivers and dual head but will keep an eye out for newer  versions of the driver and try them once there's a body of how tos and fixes specific to Hardy. Apart from ATI/dual head, the only minor annoyance is that rutilt won't remember profiles unless I do sudo su, sudo rutilt <profilename> and so I can't get it to connect automatically on boot. For the most part, I really like Hardy - it looks good, is easy to mess around with (even for a non-techie noob like me), there's loads of documentation and willing support here and it generally seems to work faster (graphics aside) than under XP. So overwhelming positive experience with caveats here.

### Post by RDV on 2008-05-03
I have already commented on my own upgrade experience. This is commentary on a fresh install on a relatives Dell Inspiron 1520 laptop.

### Post by ccprog on 2008-05-03
Things are in a working condition after the upgrade, but not without problems. From what I have read above, there seems to be a problem wiht interdependencies when you have customized config files.

Twice during the install, I refused to let the ugrade revert files to generic versions. One of those then definitely brought down another program: First the lirc install asked me for the brand of my remote control, but since it wasn't listed, it seemed to be a safer choice to stay with the existing configuration. As a result, the new lirc-x package could not configure at all. And that was detected by virtualbox-ose which aborted with an error message about not being able to handle lirc.

At the end of the installs, the uprade routine seemed to try something like a second attempt at getting these glitches right - or so I interpreted what I saw at the time. I can't confirm that from the logs. Maybe it was the fact that the nvidia-glx driver had been automatically removed when it was still refered to in xorg.conf. Anyway, the upgrade process froze, and I had to manually kill it. Last log line is:
 DEBUG no file '/etc/apt/apt.conf.d/25adept-archive-limits'

One minor consequence was that the post-ugrade cleanup was not done. More serious was that xorg.conf was in an unstable condition: as a lot of others seem to have experienced, proper screen resolutions/refresh rates suddenly weren't available any more. I have it back working now, but only by direct edits. The gui config application still refuses to tell me about refresh rates above 64 Hz.

To sum it up: the automatic removal of restricted drivers is a seriously bad idea. And keep yourself a backup of your xorg.conf.

### Post by Cosmic Nomad on 2008-05-03
Fresh Install with (8.04) dual booting with Windows xp on a :

LENOVO 3000 N200  T2330 Dual Core


Every thing worked out the box :

Bluetooth = used

Wireless = used



Haven't used the fingerprint or card reader or modem



What did not work was the sound but was very easy to fix :)

very very soft ,have to use head phone's


This works on my 3000 N200:

By adding the following to etc/modprobe.d/alsa-base

options snd-hda-intel single_cmd=1 model=lenovo


Compiz works 100's if not thousands !!!



Every thing just works, like I had to pay for this operating system.

Great work

### Post by usernamed on 2008-05-03
My problems have been with two of the most common complaints with Ubuntu (and especially the 64 bit variety)

1.  Wireless Internet - OK, I'm prepared to accept that it isn't Ubuntu's fault that chipmakers don't provide Linux drivers or assist others to make open source drivers.  However, if you know your hardware isn't faulty because it works in WinXP it's still frustrating to have to replace your wireless USB dongle (as I'm having to) because your wireless just doesn't work.

2.  Flash (again probably exacerbated due to the fact I'm running the AMD64 build) doesn't work.  The sticky thread on the AMD64 forum for Flash states that it should as simple as getting the packages on Hardy, but that doesn't seem to be the case.


I haven't been brave enough to try and get my printer working.  Ubuntu seems to have got a lot prettier, but I still get exceedingly tense and nervous whenever I have a piece of hardware I'm going to plug in, because I think here goes a couple of days of my life, reading how-to's checking forums, trial and error etc.

A direct appeal from me on the forums to recommend a USB wireless dongle that worked on AMD64 Hardy out the box with WPA/WPA2 got no answers to the original question.  That sums up hardware support on Ubuntu for me.

It might work, it might not.  Be lucky.

### Post by Mirko on 2008-05-03
For the past two or three versions of Ubuntu, the Desktop CD would not boot on my ThinkPad T42.  (The hardware is fine, this is definitely an Ubuntu problem-- other boot CDs, such as the Alternate Install CD, work fine, and I've tested the drive thoroughly.)

I upgraded from Gutsy via "update-manager -u" using a local mirror of the Hardy repositories.  (It would be nice if there was an "advanced" button in the Update Manager that would let you enter specific apt repos...)

The upgrade went fine.

The main issue I had was that X was really slow when I first booted into Hardy.  It was using 5-10% CPU, everything was slow to render, Compiz effects were jittery.  Turned out Xgl, which I was using in Gutsy (ATI on this ThinkPad, sigh), was the culprit.  Removed Xgl, restarted X, and everything was good again.

A few annoying things about Gnome 2.22:

- had to rebind some keyboard shortcuts in ccsm, and adjust some other Compiz settings back to the way they were in Gutsy

- gnome-terminal cursor was blinking, no option to turn it off (it's now a global setting)

- Firefox logos disappeared in the Gnome menu, presumably because I modified the Firefox launcher in Gutsy's Gnome menu (I'd added a launcher for 3b5 and renamed the Firefox 2 launcher)

- Evolution is not obeying "leave messages on server, delete after X days"
 -- but this problem was present in Gutsy too

In Gutsy, full screen video had diagonal artifacts when Compiz was running.  This has been fixed in Hardy (presumably related to Xgl being replaced).

xrandr did not work for me in Gutsy (again, Xgl).  Haven't tried it yet in Hardy-- will wait until I dock and undock the laptop from its docking station a few times (which is where I need xrandr).

All in all, Hardy seems fine.  I've been using GNU/Linux on the desktop for the past 12 or 13 years, so it's hard to get excited about a new distro.  But all the hardware works, and Gnome hasn't pushed me back to OpenBox or something... yet.

Cheers.

*Update:

Resuming from putting the laptop to sleep now works in Hardy!  In Gutsy it would not work if Compiz was enabled.  Wireless even works coming out of suspension.  Excellent!

---

### Post by markbuntu on 2008-05-03
Having fooled around with Linux way back about Kernel 1.02 but not for the past 10 years or so I came at this with a little preparation and a lot of trepidation but I am so sick of windoze and I have lot of free time and the PC is no longer so critical to my life.

HP 1330n Media Center Desktop
Athlon 64 3800+ 2.9GHZ 939 socket
3G PC3200ram
ATI Raedon Xpress 200
Asus A8AE-LE motherboard
AC97 sound
Realtek 810L 10/100

First, I downloaded 8.04 386i with torrent and burned a iso CD and checked it twice. Then I got a new hard drive and swapped it into port 1 with my windowsXP drive. SATA makes that part so easy.
Then I snuck the CD into the player and rebooted, followed the directions to use my new hd and everything went surprisingly well until...

Problem 1.
Until I tried to log in. The log in screen kept returning and then telling me I used the wrong password blah blah and then restarting.
Fix.
I got in with Gnome safe mode and checked the logs and found that security/lib/pam_smbpass.so was missing, weird. So I got it with Synapytic and could then log in OK.

Problem 2
Then, I could not get into gnome or fglrx so,
Fix.
 back to gnome safe mode and system/administration/hardware drivers to get the ATI restricted driver and enable it. OK, fglrx and Gnome now working.

So then I fired up Synaptic and got Ubuntu Studio which is just great. Had to change the Jack input and output devices from default to ALSA AC97 to get stuff like Hydrogen to work and then have to turn Jack off to use rhythmbox but that is to be expected since they are both using the same hardware.

Then I got the Compiz Fusion Icon and fooled around with the settings for Compiz. Seems like everything works except the cube. wtf?
I thought it may be the ATI driver so I ran EnvyNG and that changed nothing.

I still get some problems in the syslog:

gdmgreeter[5692]: GdkPixbuf-CRITICAL: gdk_pixbuf_new_from_file_at_scale: assertion `width > 0 || width == -1' failed 

 gdmgreeter[5692]: GLib-GObject-WARNING: invalid (NULL) pointer instance

  gdmgreeter[5692]: GLib-GObject-CRITICAL: g_signal_connect_data: assertion `G_TYPE_CHECK_INSTANCE (instance)' failed 

 gdm[5388]: WARNING: Didn't understand `' (expected true or false) 

 dhcdbd: message_handler: message handler not found under /com/redhat/dhcp/eth0 for sub-path eth0.dbus.get.reason

 dhcdbd: message_handler: message handler not found under /com/redhat/dhcp/eth0 for sub-path eth0.dbus.get.host_name
 dhcdbd: message_handler: message handler not found under /com/redhat/dhcp/eth0 for sub-path eth0.dbus.get.host_name
 dhcdbd: message_handler: message handler not found under /com/redhat/dhcp/eth0 for sub-path eth0.dbus.get.domain_name
dhcdbd: message_handler: message handler not found under /com/redhat/dhcp/eth0 for sub-path eth0.dbus.get.domain_name
dhcdbd: message_handler: message handler not found under /com/redhat/dhcp/eth0 for sub-path eth0.dbus.get.nis_servers

kernel: [   23.378156] Failure registering capabilities with primary security module.

 audio[5342]: audio.conf: Key file does not have key 'Enable'
 audio[5335]: audio.conf: Key file does not have key 'Disable'
 audio[5335]: audio.conf: Key file does not have group 'A2DP'

Everything seems to be working except the cube in Compiz and I am on the hunt for the solution to that and finding the solutions to the rest of these pesky messages in the syslog.

Pretty fine product here, especially compared to "the good old days" when your monitor would catch fire if you made a mistake foooling around with X. 

I will be kicking XP to the curb any day now.

### Post by wmf on 2008-05-03
I've had two experiences with Hardy 8.04. First was an Update Manager upgrade. The PC was a Win98 vintage machine, 500mHz chip with 256M of memory. Opps! That is apparently not enough horsepower to run the distro reasonably. All kinds of silent fails and some general flakiness. Disappointing, but not overly surpised. The 7.10 version was pretty solid on this machine, but slow.

The next was a clean install on a PC with a bit more (2.6GHz, 1.5G RAM) already running a fully patched version of XP Pro. Grabbed the .iso, burned the disk and decided to make this a dual boot machine. Pretty straightfoward.. booted up just fine.

The real gotcha to me was the fact that Samba through Nautilus is pretty broken when it comes to Windows AD authenticated shares. If the share has its perms set to Everyone / Full Access, no problem. If there is an authentication required, no joy. This is a widely reported problem. See this bug report [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/nautilus/+bug/207072]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/nautilus/+bug/207072").

This was a real showstopper for me.

---

### Post by NullHead on 2008-05-03
I simply had all the same problems as before. They were, Broadcom drivers with ndiswrapper, Nvidia, X-fi. Those were it and easily fixed. ```
sudo apt-get install ndiswrapper
sudo rmmod b43
sudo rmmod ssb
sudo ndiswarpper -i driver
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
echo 'blacklist b43' | sudo tee -a /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
echo 'blacklist ssb' | sudo tee -a /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
```
for Nvidia: ```
chmod 766 NVIDIA*
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop
sudo ./NVIDIA*
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start
```
for x-fi: 
```
sudo dpkg -i oss-linux*
sudo apt-get install esoun oss-compat
```

all simple things, but they keep my experience from being totally plug and play.

### Post by ks1731-26 on 2008-05-03
I installed Ubuntu 8.04 on a newly-built machine:
  ASUS M2N-SLI motherboard
  AMS 64 X2 5000+ 2.6GHz processor
  2x2GB PC6400 RAM
  Samsung SH-S203N DVD burner on SATA 1
  500 GB Western Digital hard drive on SATA 2
  Linksys PCI wireless adapter

At first, the installer wanted nothing to do with my computer. The CD would 
not boot itself, so I needed to use a little applet on the CD to install a boot-option
window that would let me select "Ubuntu". 

It then would boot from the CD. However, the install would hang at a screen with 
the Ubuntu logo, and a progress bar of sorts. I didn't even get the "select a language
screen" (so don't tell me to hit F4! it wasn't an option!) So, before that came up, I hit Esc 
to get more detailed boot options. I eventually tried verbose mode.

With no extra boot options, the process halted at the message 
"JFS: ntxBlock=8192, nTxLock=65536"
and the whole machine would reboot before I could protest.

Looking on the forums, I found a slew of boot options to try, by using "e" to edit
the boot parameters for verbose mode. On the second line, after the --, I added:
acpi=off nolapic noapic nodma all_generic_ide irqpoll pnpbios=off pci=nomsi floppy=off nosplash
I believe I tried all 3,628,800 combinations of the above parameters, and the best I got
was to the "busybox" prompt.

At this point, I was near to giving up. I tried 7.10, and it did the same thing. 

Then I tried the one last option: go into the BIOS (del key right after the computer starts),
and turn on the RAID system. Lo and behold, with no boot options or any mucking around,
Ubuntu installed, right quick.

Of course, when I rebooted with the CD out of the drive, I got a "no system disk" error. So,
I got into the BIOS, turned off the RAID system, and held my breath. . . Both Vista and 
Ubuntu now worked with no problem.

Go figure. I've used Linux on and off since 1993 (a Slackware CD I got through the mail), and
I always seem to have problems like this. I'm back to Linux at the moment, though, because
I've had 6 Macs at work over the last few years (research in astronomy), and of those, there 
was 1 dead laptop, one dead iMac (ye gods, why can't I at least use it as a display!!), and a 
G5 desktop that shuts itself down at radom, inopportune times. By comparison, trouble 
installing Linux is much better than $6000 of junk computer equipment that is not cost
effective to fix (are you reading this Steve Jobs??).

So, anyway, to the Linux developers out there --- thanks for giving me another option.

I would suggest putting a "troubleshooting" link prominently on the downloads page, though.

Best,
Mike

### Post by sluthy on 2008-05-03
I notice that the number of people having unfixable problems is higher than the number of people having no faults, in both circumstances. My hat is off to all the developers and contributors to the project, but I can't help but feel that this is a little disappointing for us given Ubuntu is commonly seen as the forerunner for making ingrounds to MS's home and corporate stake, and more people are experiencing failure than success - particularly given Hardy is the safe, secure, LTS release.

Although I suppose, this poll would have a biased result - people posting on the Installation forums on here I imagine would typically be ones looking for help, and would naturally be the ones having problems and saying so - not many people would come through this particular thread and/or forum just to say "all is well, cheers", would they? :)

My particular Hardy install problem has started a new thread below. Whether it's a solvable issue or not remains to be seen.

### Post by kwebster on 2008-05-03
Upgraded an old Dell Laptop and somewhat newer desktop.  Both went smooth and was up and running in about an hour. Didn't loose any of my previous confiqurations including dual boot with XP on the desktop so had very little to do after the upgrade.  

Laptop had only one minor issue.  Slow wireless due to RT2500 native driver limited to 1MBS.  Found the fix in the forum.  Installed NDISWRAPPER and latest RALINK driver and now have 54MBS.  Love this forum.........Can always find the answer if you look hard enough.

### Post by ikester8 on 2008-05-03
It's been two days now that I've tried to install Hardy. I tried using the alternate CD, which at least loaded everything, but the sound did not work. Apparently there are some serious ALSA configuration issues in Hardy, and I've tried everything in the guides. So I tried installing from the LiveCD, which hosed my MBR at the very end. Sure, it kept warning me that Grub couldn't handle my setup, but where in the graphical install did it tell me where to change it to LILO? Well, I tried installing from an earlier version and reformatted everything, and now LILO is hosed, with the classic "Loading LinuxEDBA is big" error.

This is ridiculous!

Edit: Got it fixed, but it cost me a lot of data. Warning: Do not attempt to put the GRUB bootloader onto an XFS partition! I created a small /boot partition with ext2, GRUB loaded there just fine.

### Post by SonicJMC on 2008-05-04
Hardy has been a NIGHTMARE!
I upgraded via software update manager, or whatever it's called.
Performance was TERRIBLE, and eventually, it just fell apart, forcing a format and reinstall.
I've GOT to have this system up by Monday morning for my day job. Also, weekends were all I had to catch up on 3 months of overdue work for the radio station I manage. This is a DISASTER!

Although, I must say, when Ubuntu breaks, it at least leaves you the ability to fix it. Unable to boot the hard drive, I booted to Live CD, enabled root, and recovered my critical data. That is something I could've never done in ANY version of Windows (well, maybe 3.1 would've let me via a dos boot floppy).
This is why I'm re-installing Ubuntu and not reverting to Windows.
Ubuntu 8 might be heavily flawed, but...

I've run 6.something, 7.04, 7.10, and now 8.04
In all that, this is the only update or upgrade that really broke anything. Counting only OS f-ups (excluding user error and hardware failure)... this is the only OS clean re-install for Ubuntu on my system caused by the OS just crapping out. 

In Windows...
I had to fix something an update broke at LEAST once a month. Back in Me, it was once a week.

On a dual boot...
Windows falling apart kills access to Ubuntu.
Ubuntu completely failing, requiring a full re-install... Didn't even TOUCH my ability to get back to Windows. All my stuff is in Linux, but I got Windows allowing me to at least run FireFox for web mail and repair info without suffering through a Live CD (CD Roms are slow as crap).

Like I said.
Ubuntu - F you at the moment, but hey, I can't stay mad. Overall, you're good to me.

### Post by ZootNerper on 2008-05-04
Hi Folks,

I have so far installed 8.10 on two of 3 computers. I couldn't upgrade on one as the root partition was too small. The other after uninstalling some games etc would let me upgrade. However, the resulting upgrade was very buggy.

Hence, I eventually installed 8.10 on both. One I could reformat the disk so that the boot partition is bigger and hopefully I can just upgrade next time, but on the second I could not do this easily so I did not reformat and just installed in the old root partition.

The install has mostly worked, except networking. I have a wired network and the network was set to roaming (by default? - I did nothing here). This would let me access the Internet initially, but at some point it would stop. Installing additional software would work all the way through (sometimes hours away), but using Firefox would fail within minutes. I have since set the network manager to use DCHP on both machines, rebooted both and the router and all is well (so far!!!).

That's all. Thanks to those you did all the work.

-- Zoot

### Post by simon_ives on 2008-05-04
Installed Hardy x86_64 in a partition alongside Gutsy x86_64 over the weekend and everything went great.  I'm on a Compaq Presario v3000 (further details in my sig below).

Graphics - Resolution correctly detected (1280x800), nVidia driver downloaded when I enabled compiz, which works great.

VGA Out - Works fine.

Audio - Worked out-of-the-box.  External speakers switch off when headphones are inserted.

Wireless - No idea.  Broadcom's are crap and mine died ages ago.

Ethernet - was connected to the net from the word go.

USB -All fine.

Firewire - Good too.

Multi media card reader - Works great.

The only issue's that I've had have been with some updated versions of software.  Firefox 3, for example, as is well documented, isn't compatable with some older extensions.  I also had to re-adjust my fstab to auto-mount some drives on my wife's Windows box.  Nothing major and nothing really specific to Hardy itself.

### Post by badactress on 2008-05-04
1st attempt on release day was ok, but had to install Nvidia drivers manually. Sound would stop working after a random period & getting it to recognise that a USB device had been plugged in was hit & miss so, I quit & went back to using Debian Lenny which has always worked perfectly. Until now! I had problems there with java & flash sound which I've always been able to fix 2 mins after install, but not this time!

So, a week later, wondering if Hardy might have had some updates released to sort my problems, I re-installed it. Only 2 updates to download, neither seemingly having any relevance to my previous problems, but never-the-less everything is now working perfectly. Even the nvidia driver was installed & activated by ticking the box in the Hardware-Drivers app, which didn't work before!

The main reason I wanted Hardy to work for me was because I'm tired of re-installing my current-distro every 6 months, or downloading 100Mb of updates daily (Debian Lenny). I want something modern & up-to-date but stable that I can stick with for at least 18 months. I do get itchy to try the latest versions of software, which is why I can't resist clicking the Updates Available button in Lenny! but I'm hoping the Hardy Backports repo will satisfy that urge and keep me using the 'buntu for the foreseeable future. :o)

Congratulations to everybody involved in putting 8.04 together. IMO it is head-and-shoulders the best desktop o/s available. :KS

### Post by hookzilla on 2008-05-04
I'm a relative noobie, so I've been following this thread and just begun to tryout 8.04.  I installed 8.04 on an old machine - P2-450, 512mb ram, 3dFX VooDoo Banshee, Linksys WUSB54G V4.  From the live CD, wired, everything seemed to work fine.  I could get to the internet and my home lan files.    Wireless also seemed to work, networks were scanned and recognized.  However, no internet, and no home lan connection. So, I tried install.  I was surprised that it seemed to go well and was quick, about 30 min.  (7.10 would only install via upgrade and took a couple of hours.)  Again, all worked fine wired, but not wireless - no scan or anything.  I have not tried to install additional software or anything else on the PC.  I expect I'll need to manually install the wireless adapter, or use WiCD, or RUtilt, as I had to do in 7.10.  One curious problem I had was that on the initial reboot after installation, when I ran Firefox, the top menu bar jumped from top to side repeatedly.  When I closed Firefox, the menu bar was locked to the side.  When I ran Firefox again, the menu bar was still locked to the side.

I have run a quick test of the live CD on a newer machine - AMD64, 2GB ram, WUSB54GSv1, no wired network.  No wireless, so no internet.

### Post by Dakiraun on 2008-05-04
So far, upgraded/installed two systems:

Dell Precision M65 Laptop, stock - upgrade from 7.10 Gusty:

[LIST]
[*]Processor: Intel Core2 Duo (1.66GHz/2MB L2 Cache)
[*]Hard Drive: 80 GB SATA @ 7200RPM
[*]Screen: 15.4" Widescreen (1280x800)
[*]Graphics: NVIDIA Quadro FX 350M 256 MB TurboCache
[*]RAM: 2 GB DDR2 SDRAM @667 MHz (2 x 1GB)
[*]Optical Drive: 8x CD/DVD burner (DVD+/-RW)
[*]Wireless: Intel PRO/Wireless 3945 802.11 b/g Mini Card
[/LIST]

The upgrade was pretty painlessly.  The only issue I had on restarting was related to xorg.conf in that the video was a bit messed up.  I created a new xorg.conf and copied over some settings from the original (which I had backed up first) for some customized touchpad settings.

I also had some issues with Java Runtime which were resolved by completely uninstalling anything Sun-Java and reinstalling them.

All other systems work great - Suspend/Hibernate was a problem that was resolved in a prior Ubuntu version and the resolution carried over just fine.  Power saving systems seem to all be in place, though I find it does run a little hotter in Ubuntu than in XP (it has dual OS's with XP as a secondary).  Performance is excellent.


Second system, a Compaq Presario R3000, custom/upgraded - fresh install:

[LIST]
[*]Processor: AMD Athlon64 3200+ (2.2GHz)
[*]Hard Drive: 100 GB PATA @ 5400RPM
[*]Screen: 15.4" Widescreen (1280x800)
[*]Graphics: NVIDIA 440 GO 64 MB
[*]RAM: 1.25 GB DDR SDRAM @333 MHz (1 x 1GB, 1x256M - max'd out)
[*]Optical Drive: 8x CD/DVD burner (DVD+RW)
[*]Wireless: Broadcom BCM4306 802.11b/g mini-card
[*]Card Reader: Texas Instruments 5 in 1 media card reader
[/LIST]

Here I had a number of problems.  The suspend/hibernate didn't remotely work from the default install.  Now... this I'm not surprised at given that it seems to never work off any default install until you do some tweaking in the /etc/default/acpi-support, which I did for other systems included the Dell above.  For the R3000, however, no amount of troubleshooting has so far gotten Suspend and Hibernate to function, which is a major problem given that this laptop uses those functions multiple times a day.  As such, it may warrant the laptop going back to XP because using it without time-saving suspend/hibernate functionality would be mega frustrating.

The TI card reader also does not function.  I know that earlier Ubuntu versions did support it, but it looks like something got broken along the way.  There are several bug reports submitted to the development team on the issue, but currently no fixes.

Everything else worked out well.  The nVidia driver went in without any problems, and the wireless drivers work as well.  Power saving features (other than Suspend and Hibernate) all seem to be working, and sound works fine too.  Performance is very snappy.

I have not had time to upgrade the home PC yet, but will eventually and post the results.

---

### Post by rvandegrift on 2008-05-04
Hi everyone,

I upgraded my Dell Latitude D820 to hardy (from gutsy) last weekend.  The upgrade process went smoothly, as it always has.  However, upon the completion of the upgrade process, I have run into a number of notable regressions:

1) LED support in the new kernels needs to be enabled for wireless LEDs on some Intel cards to work.  This appears to be broken, and I have since lost my wireless LED notifications.

2) Resume from S3 with the nv driver is broken.  This worked perfectly well with gutsy, but Xorg never restores the display in hardy.

3) To fix #2, I tried using the restricted driver manager to switch to the nvidia driver.  The restricted driver manager broke my Xorg - there appears to be a problem getting the module installed correct.  Despite this, xorg.conf as updated, which prevented gdm from starting.

#3 seems rooted in the fact that activating the Nvidia binary drivers resulted in an nvidia_old.ko and nvidia_new.ko, which prevents automatic loading of the module.  If I manually modprobe nvidia_new, X will start with the nvidia driver.

This fixes resume from S3, but not gracefully - the display takes longer to come back from S3, and goes through a few seconds of display corruption first.

These may have been related to the fact that the upgrade failed to install the new version of linux-restricted-modules to go with 2.6.24-16-generic.  I just manually installed that and will test the restricted driver manager with this package installed.  Would be better ot have nv working though.

All in all, this is the first Ubuntu upgrade to have problems.  All the others have been 100% seamless.  These aren't difficult problems for me to fix, though I am very conversant in GNU/Linux.  These could be major problems for someone with less expertise.

---

### Post by fixitdude on 2008-05-04
On upgrading from Gutsy to Hardy I could get to the log in GUI screen, mouse working and all that but then when I logged in it would show a black screen and then after a little wait return to the log in screen again. (black screen is where X is restarting under the new user, X was obviously working at that point because I had a GUI)

After hours of work on this, here's the solution.

I moved all the ".font" files (from my users home dir) into a new directory (effectively removing them).

(Press ctrl-alt-F3 to get to console, log in as user)
mkdir oldfonts
mv .font* oldfonts/

How I found out is I created a new user and when I logged into that a new KDE started, asking me all the default questions etc. After that I had to figure out what was different from a new user and my old user. A new account didn't have the ".font" files. But of course I figured that out last.

Point is, IF YOU ARE HAVING PROBLEMS AFTER LOGIN, try creating a new user to see if it works then work in "reverse" to figure what the differences are.

(from root, if you wanted to try this new user thing)
adduser newusername

The logs showed things like "ksplash" and a couple others were segfaulting. Really stupid, they should just say bad format in ".font" file or something. Talk about throwing you off the trail.

tail -500 /var/log/messages | less

But I tried a lot of other stuff people suggested here first, so possibly something else helped me get to this point, so I will post some of the other stuff that probably won't hurt to do.

Don't forget to make sure you have room left on your HD.
df

Make sure you are upgraded completely on kde
apt-get install kde

If you are using kubuntu, this may help
sudo aptitude install kubuntu-desktop

I didn't use this one but it may help you get X running.
sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

By the way, this command "sudo update-manager --dist-upgrade" doesn't work if you can't get X to work.

Another error in the log was for "run_parts", so I edited /etc/X11/xorg.conf

Replace line:
RESOURCEFILES=$(run_parts $SYSRESOURCES)

with
RESOURCEFILES=$(run-parts $SYSRESOURCES)

Notice the underscore is changed to "-", this is a known debian bug.

And a side note, for those who tried to switch to "xdm" you can run this
sudo apt-get install --reinstall gdm
---- or ----
sudo apt-get install --reinstall kdm

To get it to ask you which one you want to start with (kdm for the default ubuntu style login screen).

Hope this saves you some time. Cost me a whole day. (I may post this in more than one thread, sorry in advance)

---

### Post by mfm3 on 2008-05-04
Well, well....
My 24 hour stint with installing Hardy (Ubuntu Studio, hey why not eh?) on my Toshiba Satellite A75-S231 is coming to a close. It's been fun as usual, I personally get some satisfaction from troubleshooting most little issues, as long as it doesn't take DAYS.

I think I discovered a couple of things that might be useful to someone else out there:
**Graphics**
Read up on your particular ATI card if you have one. There's a big difference between the way Hardy drives newer ones and older ones. My card is a Mobility Radeon 9100 IGP, one of the older models, and therefore not able to run under the **fglrx** driver. I should have known something was up when the card did not show up in Hardware Manager at all, but I took that as a problem when it really wasn't. It just means that Hardy wants to use the open source ati driver for your card, not a restricted driver. This is actually a good thing in some ways. These links should be useful if you find yourself reading this for help:

[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/RadeonDriver](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/RadeonDriver) (worked for me)
[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/BinaryDriverHowto/ATI](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/BinaryDriverHowto/ATI)
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=550082&page=9](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=550082&page=9)

Also key to get nice desktop effects was: (from [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=4303968#post4303968](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=4303968#post4303968))
> I have changed /usr/bin/compiz to include these two lines at the very top:
Code:

LIBGL_ALWAYS_INDIRECT="true"
SKIP_CHECKS="yes"

SO, graphics acceleration is working decently, 3D desktop effects are a bit too much for my card to handle, but the wobbly windows and things like that are working. I'm still going to play with the settings a bit to see if I can get the cube working without bringing everything to a snail's pace...

A lot of that is probably not news to some out there, but if I had read it yesterday it would have saved me hours of trying to force the fglrx driver to work.

**Bootup**
I did discover that the splash screen while Ubuntu Studio 8.04 is booting freezes my system. For the past day I have been having to boot into recovery mode, then resume normal boot to get X/gdm to start. I thought it was because something was going wrong with the graphics card/driver/configuration, another reason I was beating my head against the ATI wall. ](*,)
So I removed the "quiet splash" option from the grub menu to see what exactly the error was and voila! No problem booting right up. I don't know if that's something with my system or with the splash screen itself, or a combination of the two, but without it there is no more freezing.

This laptop would never play nice with anything over Dapper Drake, which it ran like a champ since right when that release came out. I always reverted after upgrading to Edgy, Feisty, (didn't even try Gusty). But the Dapper install wasn't getting used. This is really my XP machine, dual-booted because the hardware is so XP-specific (fancy touchpad features, etc. that need special drivers) and so I can use windows programs for work. And Ubuntu Studio seemed so cool, I couldn't resist replacing Dapper. Now this laptop has a nice, fast XP install, if there is such a thing, with my CAD software and such, AND a sweet Ubuntu Studio install for creating/managing graphics and media and everything else one might need a laptop to do. Thanks linux and Ubuntu for allowing me to keep this 3-year-old laptop feeling like it can still hang.

Now to decide if it's time to start upgrading my desktop from Feisty...Maybe I'll wait a week or two. It's got nvidia so I'm not as scared of graphics, but vmware player has been a nightmare to maintain through upgrades, not to mention a royal pain to get installed in the first place, so I'm hesitant. Maybe I'll see how it installs on this laptop right now. Anybody got a convincing reason that Hardy is way better than Feisty for home/desktop use? Experience with Hardy and VMware?

---

### Post by erginemr on 2008-05-04
> **XemeX said:**
> Hi again :)

This time I'm after clean installation of Kubuntu Hardy with KDE4.
The whole process was quite successful this time - after installation I was able to enable the restricted drivers for my NV card.

However, all in all there are still many bugs remaining. Not to mention KDE4 with the KNetworkManager not being able to connect, menu having problems with refreshing, problems with saving changes in configuration due to conflicts with KDE3...

The last thing that made me quite annoyed is that in Polish version they have changed the name of the folder Desktop into *Pulpit* (which is the Polish equivlent). Apparently, many applications still look for Deskotop folder. Eventually I created a symlink to the proper directory.

To revert the Desktop folder back to its default English name, you can edit the file:
```
gedit ~/.config/user-dirs.dirs
```
in your home folder in such a way that its contents should be somewhat like:
```
# This file is written by xdg-user-dirs-update
# If you want to change or add directories, just edit the line you're
# interested in. All local changes will be retained on the next run
# Format is XDG_xxx_DIR="$HOME/yyy", where yyy is a shell-escaped
# homedir-relative path, or XDG_xxx_DIR="/yyy", where /yyy is an
# absolute path. No other format is supported.
# 
XDG_DESKTOP_DIR="$HOME/Desktop"
XDG_DOWNLOAD_DIR="$HOME/Desktop"
XDG_TEMPLATES_DIR="$HOME/Templates"
XDG_PUBLICSHARE_DIR="$HOME/Public"
XDG_DOCUMENTS_DIR="$HOME/Documents"
XDG_MUSIC_DIR="$HOME/Music"
XDG_PICTURES_DIR="$HOME/Pictures"
XDG_VIDEOS_DIR="$HOME/Videos"
```

Besides, there is a service running in the background, called "Users folders/dirs update" or similar, which is responsible of updating those folder names whenever you change the language of the Gnome desktop. You may consider disabling this service too, via:
```
Main Menu -> Preferences -> Sessions
```

### Post by mfm3 on 2008-05-04
> **Trance56k said:**
> flawless :)

Why thanks. 

You know, I'm feeling pretty good about Hardy Studio. It's pretty fun. 

I feel like there's some tension in some of the threads and posts about Hardy; being a "beta" and what-not, being "the worst release yet," but I have to disagree. While those who are having problems with it have some valid points, it's a skewed point-of-view to call Hardy the worst release of Ubuntu because of certain individual issues with this and that. Every release gives some people trouble, and that's true for OSes in general. Anyone noticed how bad the Vista release went? That should put Hardy's initial release nuances into perspective. It is what it is, and what it is is pretty sweet compared to the alternatives. I don't have to make that case. If people don't like it as much as a previous release, they should stick with the previous release. It's that simple. Upgrade later, when you want to. That's a great benefit of free operating systems.

### Post by igoddard on 2008-05-05
Ugraded Advent (Connexant) laptop from 6.06.  Upgrade itself went OK.  Also updated Virtualbox to 1.6.

Network has to be restarted manually with Wireless Assistant.

Share to NAS device no longer works.

The one app (Enterprise Architect) I need under Wine doesn't work with anything more recent that 0.9.59.  There is no deb of this version for 8.04.  The old deb wouldn't install - needs libldap2 which is no longer available.  Installed libldap2 from Debian and installed the .59 deb for Gutsy, the last version for which it was available.  It broke.  I could download source, fix the bug introduced in in .60 and recompile; however when I tried that some time ago I then had problems with fonts - Dapper has too old a version of fontforge and later version wouldn't compile because of libc6 version - ant so it goes.  We don't have DLL hell in Linux-land; we have shared object swamp which is just as problematic.  I could, however, run Enterprise Architect under Windoze in Virtualbox except:

VB 1.6 has various problems including performance.  I'd need to revert back to 1.5 but I'm not convinced that this would fully sort the performance issue.  There seems to be a lot of swapping going on.  Looking at top, even without VB loaded there seems to be a lot of memory in use.  Unless I find the memory use is down to some unwanted service I can kill I'll restore Dapper if only to find out if it really did use that much memory.

Ian

### Post by frodon on 2008-05-05
In case you don't know, when using nvidia drivers outside the ubuntu repositories (which is the case with ENVY) you have to reinstall drivers again and again after each kernel update, so expect this on next kernel update too.
It is just how this works, if you don't want to bother installing drivers again after each kernel update use the drivers from the ubuntu repositories.

### Post by flyingtiger on 2008-05-05
I noticed the Upgrade to 8.04 Hardy in the update window the past couple of updates and ignored it. I should have at least looked at the Ubuntu web site to see if there were any known problems and/or decided whether to download an ISO for burning or upgrade from the Internet. I blindly choose to upgrade via Internet without backing up. I am lucky that I didn't lose any data and I did have a problem after restarting post download and install.

A program called BusyBox came up in CLI (initramfs). I wasn't able to see anything other than / directory. I went to the Ubuntu forum after panicking for several minutes to determine if my upgrade was isolated or others had similar problems. I discovered others had had problems. I read through the forum looking for a solution. I didn't find a clear solution but I did follow suggestions of a couple Ubuntu forum members:

On start up of computer, press Esc key after GRUB has loaded for options.
Tried to restore earliest kernel.
When that didn't work, I was presented with CLI. Entered "cat /proc/cmdline" to see the value, then
"ls /dev/disk/by-uuid" to see what possible values were present. I saw what I presumed to be the old uuid. Performed ls and saw initrd.img and initrd.img.old, and vmlinuz and vmlinuz.old. Copied initrd.img.old to initrd.img and vmlinuz.old to vmlinuz, then restarted PC. Booted to gui login and noticed all my files on the 8.04 default background. Relieved. Checked all files and found they were, luckily, intact. I will read the Ubuntu site regarding upgrades next time, 3 yrs, before performing an upgrade as well as performing a backup. I am feeling very lazy for not backing up prior to the upgrade.

---

### Post by cmnorton on 2008-05-05
Thinkpad T61p  -- Upgrade
----------------------------------------

This went poorly. I have never had an Ubuntu update not complete. I had installed the KDE desktop while running 7.10, and started using KDE. Most of the installation problems were gnome-related, so I am assuming the installation problems were due to having two desktops installed and/or switching desktop managers from Gnome to KDE.

Thinkpad T61p  -- Installation (after failed upgrade)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

Based on my knowledge of the last installation, I installed with the Thinkpad not hooked up to an external monitor. I reconfigured X; chose the vesa driver; and a monitor size compatible with my Acer AL1914 and the T61p's screen. The installation was then successful.

The only embarrasing problem is the network will not come up automatically, but sudo dhclient takes care of this. I added my name to this bug [https://bugs.launchpad.net/bugs/193964](https://bugs.launchpad.net/bugs/193964) in Launchpad.


Acer Travelmate 630 -- Update (1GB RAM with decent PIV non HT CPU)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Also failed KUbuntu update for the same reasons as the Thinkpad T61p (listed above)

Acer Travelmate 630 -- Full Install (1GB RAM with decent PIV non HT CPU)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

I tried an XUbuntu install, and that failed. (The is a slow laptop; I had heard good things in these forums about using XUbuntu in low speed/low memory conditions; and decided to give XUbuntu a try.)

I went back to try a KUbuntu full install, and that failed. I kept getting disk hanging/DMA errors. 

I am now burning an XUbuntu alternate DVD and will try installing from that.

Knoppix boots this laptop just fine.

### Post by mwasoft on 2008-05-05
My previous Ubuntu upgrade experience from 7.04 to 7.10 was very good. In comparison, the upgrade to 8.04 has been very disappointing. There is still one application that is completely broken (see Bug #210741) - and I hope someone is looking at this bug because it seems to be giving a lot of grief.

Worse still was the upgrade of our server. This left it in an unusable state with the RAID disks failing to start up (see Bug #224171), and a broken sendmail (see Bug #226948). The only good news was that the upgrade manager apologised for leaving the system in an unusable state before exiting:(

I am now starting to see problems with the X Server ignoring Shift, Control and Alt keys, which is rather worrying. This may be an interaction problem with vmware player.

All in all, I would recommend caution with the Hardy upgrade.

### Post by petermck on 2008-05-05
I did this upgrade on two different machines (a vanilla PC and a Toshiba Satellite M50 Laptop)and I lost my ssh network shares on both.
Apart from that the upgrade worked flawlessly on the PC, but I had a few problems on a Laptop.
The major problem on the Laptop was that the Update Manager GUI failed. It would just hang and I had to do the upgrade using apt-get dist-upgrade as root.
There was one point where this text based upgrade hanged as well but I was able to ctrl-c out and the upgrade continued.
Now everything seems to work OK except I kept getting a 'Crash report detected' warning in the panel regarding mono-apache-server and gcc-3.3-doc not upgrading properly. Initially the crash report would not open and I could not find out what was causing it, but I was recently able to open the report and identify the problems.
I don't need these packages so I've removed them and hopefully this fixes the problem.

### Post by stevethefiddle on 2008-05-05
Upgraded Xubuntu 7.10 to Xubuntu 8.04 (Hardy Heron) - dual boot with Win XP.

Mostly the upgrade went smoothly, although it took a long time (about 6 hours including download and installation time).

_Good points:_

I had a problem with the screen resolution getting messed up after using XP (dual boot). This is now fixed.

On the whole, Hardy seems to be performing well and is stable. I have been able to enable rt-access (using rt-kernel) which did not work with 7.10


_Problems I am left with:_

Audacity 1.3.4 is far less stable than before the upgrade - seems to be mostly related to the attempt to include JACK support. Audacity _will_ work with JACK provided no other audio programs are in use (kind of defeats the point of using JACK). Audacity will crash badly if Jack Control is opened. I guess I will have to wait for an update for this one.

I had a problem with booting the previous OS (using 'save default' in boot/grub/menu.lst ). I'm not sure how I fixed it, but this is now working.
[B]
Desktop icons and Places icons for Windows (ntfs) partition and "Data" (fat32) partition no longer appear. I have re-installed "ntfs-3g" and "NTFS Configuration Tool" but still have the problem, The drives DO automount and are accessible for Read / Write, but the are no Desktop icons and they do not appear in "Places".[/B]

Any help with this last point would be much appreciated.

### Post by ilmw on 2008-05-05
Unfortunately, I encountered the following problem when upgrading from Dapper LTS to Hardy LTS:

E: /var/cache/apt/archives/xkb-data_1.1~cvs.20080104.1-1ubuntu6_all.deb: trying to overwrite `/etc/X11/xkb/types.dir', which is also in package xkeyboard-config

I found the solution from [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/xkeyboard-config/+bug/213566/comments/5:](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/xkeyboard-config/+bug/213566/comments/5:)

This bug was fixed in the package xkeyboard-config - 1.1~cvs.20080104.1-1ubuntu5

---------------
xkeyboard-config (1.1~cvs.20080104.1-1ubuntu5) hardy; urgency=low

  * preinst:
    - when upgrading from hardy, check the conffiles against
      xkeyboard-config not xkb-data (was not available back then)
  * debian/control:
    - add conflicts against old xkeyboard-config to ensure the
      upgrade happens in the right order (LP: #213566)

Can someone helps me to solve this? I understand the above statement but don't know how to to it.

THANKS!

### Post by duddy on 2008-05-05
I did a fresh install of Hardy Heron on a separate partition next to my Vista installation. The install went perfectly!

I wasn't able to import my settings/Firefox bookmarks though. But not a big deal.

Here is my setup:

Intel Q6600 2.4Ghz Quad-Core
500GB SATA HDD
4GB RAM
ASUS P5N-E SLI Motherboard
EVGA GeForce 8800GT 512mb
Netgear WG311v3 PCIe WiFi card
HP Photosmart D7460 WiFi Printer
Samsung SyncMaster 206BW LCD


Here are the things that didn't work post-install and how I got them working:

8800GT: To get it working, I installed EnvyNG using Synaptic Package Manager and did the Automatic NVIDIA Install. Work's perfect now!

Netgear WG311v3: [http://www.jimbo7.com/wiki/index.php?title=WG311v3_LINUX_WIKI]("http://www.jimbo7.com/wiki/index.php?title=WG311v3_LINUX_WIKI")

HP Photosmart D7460: [http://hplip.sourceforge.net/install/index.html]("http://hplip.sourceforge.net/install/index.html")

I'm now living, for the 1st time in my life, in Ubuntu Perfection!

Thanks Everyone!!!

### Post by HelmutG on 2008-05-06
I have to say that I was pretty disappointed how hard it was to upgrade from 6.06 to 8.04. I did not expect to face so many new and fundamental bugs in basic system applications which worked before. The most annoying problems in particular were:
[LIST]
[*]Fritz Card PCI capi driver fcpci did not work (had to install other source tarball)
[*]isdn_net device ippp1 did not start up (had to modify source of kernel module !)
[*]system sounds not working (no solution yet)
[*] Module 8139cp and 8139too wer both loaded (had to blacklist 8139cp) eth0 sometimes still does not come up after reboot
[*]no eth0 after suspend2ram (Had to add sleep 2 after ifdown in etc/init.d/networking)
[/LIST]

### Post by lngndvs on 2008-05-06
I worked my way up through the alphas and beta, did an "update-manager -d" on three machines.  Two of them cannot connect to the network at work.  At home, I have been able to connect to a wireless router by dhcp without any problem at all.  

This has been a remarkable upgrade.  For some weeks, massive upgrades were the rule, large downloads.  From the release date on, there have been few at all.  For some days, there were NO upgrades.  

Networking on the two machines at work bothers me.  I have been able to log on with a wireless laptop (Intel wireless) using Ubuntu for quite some time, but for the first time, I am stumped.  I have edited and tried over and over.  The router is seen by the system, but I am not asked for a WPA key, as I have been frequently asked before.  

The other machine at work is hooked up by wire, sortof to the same network, but I've been able to connect either with static IP or dhcp for years.  Suddenly I cannot connect.  Perhaps this machine is a problem with the network.  

The wireless is acting differently though, and also some Mac notebooks are able to get aboard the wireless net the same as before.  At least sometimes. 

Has something changed?

Everything else is working really, really, amazingly well.  Fantastic distro.  I was running Gentoo for two years, because debian based distros had too many nits I couldn't solve.  Ubuntu is working very, very well.  I can live with most of the issues, and eventually I'll post bug reports or at least report issues to devs.

Alan

### Post by Fraoch on 2008-05-06
By far the best upgrade yet.  I really have no issues.

This was on an older 32-bit testing machine that I upgraded from 6.06 to 6.10 with many issues, 6.10 to 7.04 requiring a complete rebuild, and 7.04 to 7.10 with many more issues.

This upgrade want the best of all.  My desktop and settings have been preserved so precisely that at first I wasn't sure if the upgrade was successful, my desktop is identical and all the apps are still there.

I just have a minor issue getting [Rubyripper]("http://code.google.com/p/rubyripper/") to work, and installing [Squeezecenter]("http://www.slimdevices.com/su_downloads.html") required some AppArmor reconfiguration work.  samba needs some readjustment as well, but I believe it's a minor issue.

All in all, the best upgrade yet.  Next comes the upgrade of my production machine - it's 64-bit so it could be more complicated.

### Post by ph1 on 2008-05-06
Well, at first things were awful--I had forgotten to uninstall Envy, and my update manager was broken.  So I gave up, moved /home to a separate partition, and performed a fresh install.  However, I've found Hardy to be VERY unstable when it comes to using suspend, and it has issues mounting my ntfs partition automatically, as well as with i8k.  This would have all been fine, if I hadn't've had to do hard shutdowns (or reboots using alt+sysrq+b) several times now when it hangs during suspend.  I'm quite disappointed, actually.  I haven't noticed many upgrades or advantages at all, even from a fresh install.  Mainly I'm just ticked that I had to set everything back up again and that I'm suffering from system instability.  Perhaps this will improve as fixes appear and updates get downloaded, but right now I would actually discourage with stable Gutsy systems from upgrading to Hardy, as I feel that it's a risk to stability.

### Post by Samhain13 on 2008-05-06
> **frodon said:**
> 
Did it work flawlessly ? 
Did you get problems ? 
Did you manage to solve them ? 
if yes how ?

The installation worked flawlessly.

Problems + Solutions:

1) Our local mirror seems to be having a problem so it took me a long time to finally set-up my box for my needs. Not really that big a deal as I'm on an extended break from work so I took my time.

2.a) When I enabled desktop effects and therefore nVidia drivers, my screen resolution was limited to 1280x1024 when it should have a ceiling of 1600x1200. I used Applications > Other > Screens & Graphics to get new settings.

2.b) Login screen was huge! (It didn't fit, showing only part of the login screen.) So I edited xorg.conf and replaced the values in the "virtual" parameter in the SubSection in Display.

My box is working fine now. I still can't comment on performance though as I'm only on my second day in Hardy.

I have one question though: who do I stop it from automatically opening Movie Player when DVD/VCDs are inserted? I can't seem to find the thing where you set it.
