### Post by zaipai on 2008-05-06
Ok so what I have so far,

[SIZE="5"]_What works:_[/SIZE]
**Video** <- tho some times flaky when using Compiz advanced features.
**Internal Network card** <- Works great no tweaks needed.
**Internal Wireless** <- Works great no tweaks needed.
**Track pad mouse** <- Works great no tweaks needed.
**Media Keys** <- Works great no tweaks needed.
**Card reader** <- Not tested but shows up and seems like it would work
**Blue Tooth** <- Not tested but seems to be working
**USB Ports** <- Works great no tweaks needed.
**Dual Monitor support** <- Works best if one monitor is off and the other is on. It seems to recall the correct settings for my 22" flat panel. However dual support only seems to work if the display is cloned, exended is still not working but I feel with some tweaks it should work fine.
**Modem** <- Not tested but shows up and seems it should work.
**DVD/CD Rom burner** <- Works great no tweaks needed.
**Power Saving Mode** <- Works but see Suspend and Hibernation info below.
**Internal/extenral Mic** <- Had to install Envy24 program so I could tik the capture box.

One note even if you don't want to use the mic, you will still want the Envy24 program to get Ubuntu to sense when headphones are plugged in.

Also you will want to add the following line to /etc/modprobe.d/options file. This should make your headphones work correctly.

Add this line below to any spot in the options file using sudo.
    snd-hda-intel model=vaio


[SIZE="5"]_What does not work:_[/SIZE]
**Suspend.** Looking into issue
**FN Keys.** Looking into issue. I found a fix for another version of this laptop but it did not work and actually caused me to have to reinstall the OS as I could not boot into Ubuntu it just hung at boot up.
**Hibernation** (seems flaky).
**Internal Camera** <- Note, odd thing is I have Vista installed still because I have to dual boot for work. If I go into Vista and use the Webcam program under Vista, then reboot Ubuntu sees and can use the camera (I use Cheese, cool program its in the repo) however if I just boot directly into Ubuntu it can not see or use the camera. Still looking into what I have to do to get it working.

Hope that helps.

### Post by MarcelloSav on 2008-05-07
I had many problems in upgrading on my laptop and on my desktop too. I tried to upgrade from update manager but:

### Post by none-letmebe on 2008-05-07
Hardware: Sony Vaio SZ90
Media:    Alternative CD

**UPGRADE from 7.02**
CD upgrade.
Installed and and d/loaded from Internet.
Rebooted and system booted off old Gutsy kernel and not the new one. Suspect that it did not update grub.
Partition was encrypted with AES
Result: wiped partition & started again.

**FRESH INSTALL:**
Install worked fine.
Absolutly no sound even though the sound device is recognised and claimed.
Thunderbird and Firefox profiles were not initally recognised by the new versions on the install CD.

*Firefox*: Edited the profiles.ini to point to previous profile and all is well.

*Thunderbird*: Moved profiles from ~.thunderbird to the new dir for version 12.n.n.n which is ~.mozilla-thunderbird and edited the profile.ini.  Thunderbird won't read the profile and states that Thunderbird is still running. In fact, this usually has something to do with a .lock file present in the profile directory, but there are none there (find . -name .lock (or lock)) yields nothing.  Profiles work fine on another computer so these are not corrupt.

Gutsy and Fiesty were better with regard to sound and Thunderbird

---

### Post by TFrog on 2008-05-07
I installed Hardy Heron in a dual boot on a 160 gig HD on my Compaq laptop.  Prior to the install I downloaded the firmware for my broadcom 4318 wireless card from within Windows so I had it.  I'm only able to connect to the Internet wirelessly so this was an absolute must prior to installation.  My install went flawlessly and after setting up my KDE 3.5.9 desktop to my liking, I issued one command in the CLI after copying the broadcom firmware to the partition and rebooted.  Upon reboot I had wireless without issues.  I was then able to upgrade and download any other software I intended to install.  One thing I'll state here cause I've seen reports of sound issues.  Sound worked flawlessly out of the box both on my laptop's internal speakers as well as the Klipsch Promedia 2.1 external speakers I have.  I opted for the AMD/ATI 8.4 drivers instead of the repository drivers as they were a month or two old.  I followed the wiki at:

 [http://wiki.cchtml.com/index.php/Ubuntu_Hardy_Installation_Guide](http://wiki.cchtml.com/index.php/Ubuntu_Hardy_Installation_Guide)

then went to the troubleshooting section at:

http://wiki.cchtml.com/index.php/Troubleshooting

issued two necessary commands in CLI in order for the system to shutdown/logout properly.  Now I'm enjoying glxgears speeds of 1750fps without compiz though I opted to use the desktop effects custom setting.  Still unable to set up the cube in compiz but the effects are stunning to say the least.  With compiz running I'm still getting glxgears at 1250fps and can run games like Scorched3D without issue at default settings.  Lost my octet-stream setting in konqueror after a couple of days but was able to fix it.  May have been my fault when trying to set up the bit torrent association within konqueror (accidental deletion).  Otherwise everything seems to just work, including my Logitech Pro 9000 webcam.  Haven't tried the KDE 4 desktop version yet.  Have an extra drive for this computer will try that later and report back on it.  All and all, the best install/upgrade experience ever and I've been running Kubuntu since Dapper Drake 6.06.  Prior to that I ran Fedora Core which I now don't care for because of the menu structure.

---

### Post by wjwh on 2008-05-07
I first tried Ubuntu (or any Linux) with Dapper Drake.  I was very impressed and have upgraded now through Edgy Eft, Feisty Fawn and Gutsy Gibbon with no problems whatsoever!  However, my attempted upgrade – through Upgrade Manager – to 8.04 has been an unmitigated disaster!  Certainly, the system works in so far as I can do word processing and I can access the internet and send and receive emails – however, many things that happened flawlessly in previous versions simply do not work at all!  For example, I have a notification saying that I now have 45 updates waiting to be installed – but they won’t install!  Almost every administrative task which I used to do with ease cannot now be performed.  The problem can hardly be a password matter since the system does not even get as far as asking me for a password.  It either churns over for a short time and then does nothing or else it keeps on churning over without achieving anything.  In this latter case I can’t even switch the particular tool off!  I have to restart the computer in order to get rid of it!  If I try to execute any command under sudo, I am simply told that it is “unable to resolve host”.  Here is what happens when I attempt to edit the GRUB menu:

wjwh@linux-desktop:~$ sudo gedit /boot/grub/menu-lst
sudo: unable to resolve host linux-desktop
wjwh@linux-desktop:~$ 

Can anyone please help me to sort this out?  I am not very computer-literate so the answer may need to be kept simple.  I have already reinstalled 8.04 three times and it makes no difference.  If I can’t get it working soon, I feel that the only thing to do will be to un-install it completely and to reinstall 7.10 – which worked perfectly.

### Post by sgroarke on 2008-05-07
The upgrade experience this time around was a complete nightmare.

I love Ubuntu and really appreciate the work that goes in to it. But that does not avoid the fact that, at least for me, the upgrade was awful. I upgraded 3 systems from 7.10:

1) A server upgrade
After upgrade samba sharing was non-existent. Upgrade had ripped out the (deprecated, yes) smbfs and replaced it with cifs. Yet cifs is NOT a guaranteed drop-in replacement for smbfs. Required an intensive hour of messing with mount options to work. Yet the release notes had **NO** mention that this change was being made.

2) A laptop upgrade
The least bad of the lot, but certain parts of kde (it was kubuntu upgrade) e.g. systemsetting crash and are unusable.

3) A workstation upgrade
Completely unbootable. Some weird ide/sata issue which make key disks not seen. Not solved. System still useless.

I still like Ubuntu, but my confidence it the upgrade process is nil. There are some very severe issues here.

---

### Post by jhunter on 2008-05-07
Upgraded, but I'm not quite sure it's better.

1) Screen blanks for no reason, just like Gutsy and Feisty before.  There is a fix and I'm looking it up again.
*Edit: Looks like adding the following to xconf and restarting helped:*
> Section "ServerFlags"
        Option "DPMS"   "0"
        Option "BlankTime" "0"
        Option "StandbyTime" "0"
        Option "SuspendTime" "0"
        Option "OffTime" "0"
EndSection

2) Getting ATI cards to work is still absolutely horrible.  Seeing as a LOT of people have ATI, it would have been nice if they improved things a bit.

3) My video settings were greatly diminished on upgrade.  One would think that if custom tinkering with xconf was done to make the monitor work before, then don't replace xconf with a basic file on upgrade unless the settings don't work post-upgrade.

4) I can't change screen resolutions through the menu anymore.  This is basic functionality that is broken.
*Edit: see this post for fix: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=768357&p=4812717](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=768357&p=4812717)*

5) Full screen video is choppy.  Normal sized screen is fine.  I'm not sure what is different between Gutsy and Hardy that did that, but Medibuntu isn't helping.

---

### Post by donfletcher on 2008-05-07
I have run the upgrade from 7.10 and while it is slower, it appears to work ok with the exception that the update manager does not work.

What happened in 7.10 is that I would be asked for my password, then the download and install would proceed. Sometimes the updates would not be effective until a reboot.
But with 8.04 it does not ask for my password.
It just sits there with the spinning wheel spinning, and never accomplishes anything.

So I am up to 46 unapplied upgrades.

I can not imagine how I will get an upgraded upgrade manager with the upgrade manager not working. 

New situation:
I went to a little used administrator account and from there upgrade manager worked as normal in 7.10.
The original account is and always has been an administrator account.
So, as of now I have no problem left beyond slightly slow performance.

### Post by fudoki on 2008-05-07
Installing Hardy on an AMD 3800+ box failed to properly configure the Xserver. This same machine had 6.06 and 7.10 install perfectly, detecting the Asus M2V-MX features and the Asus Nvidia 7650 graphics card and multi-sync monitor perfectly. This was a real disappointment.

The Nvidia drivers did not automatically install.  When the computer restarted I got a mouse cursor and then a lock-up.  Went through a "rescue" re-boot, selected "re-configure Xserver' and got a 1024x768 framebuffer display instead of the 1280x1024 Nvidia display, and an icon [essentially] blaming me, claiming that "Restricted" drivers needed to be installed.  Gutsy installed these automatically and configured them correctly, first time through.  I manually installed the Nvidia driver.  The computer came up with a black screen....  I configured by hand...

Every attempt to reconfigure results in the generic FB driver (nv) being loaded.  The xorg.conf file is incomplete, with no details of ANY hardware.  Just says "Configured Monitor", "Configured Video", etc.; which would be fine IF ANYTHING WAS CONFIGURED, WHICH IT IS NOT.

I'm glad I did not recommend this to any of my clients!

An Upgrade on my AMD 5600+ box went fine, as did the upgrade on this old Pentium box, but a fresh install on a naked hard disk (removed the dual-boot and re-formatted) is very disappointing, and the "corrective actions" don't work.

I have considered Ubuntu the "Windows killer" for about 5 years, but must say that Hardy is a step backwards on a clean box, at least on this clean box.

If the display don't work right, the product is automatically considered a failure by "regular users".

In a production setting, having to configure manually would be a real deal killer.

Now I am having to dig for answers and do everything by hand, just like 10 years ago....

Yet Gutsy installed and auto-selected 1280x1024 using the Nvidia drivers from the get go when this box was new.  No changes whatsoever in the computer, and 4 versions later the install is back to 1998 in terms of installation.

You guys may want to have a look at the scripts; they are not configuring ANY of the hardware correctly on this install; and you had it working so reliably and smooth....

fudoki

---

### Post by Asteroza on 2008-05-07
Hi folks. Did an Alternate upgrade on a Dell 9200C from 7.10 on an LVM filesystem. Failed do to inability to read /var/ cached files, and hosed the system. Did a clean install without LVM, that finished correctly, but on reboot, got a mess on the screen when it was about to load the greeter. Digging through xorg.conf, it apparently didn't detect my Nvidia GeForce 7300 at all and so had no display configured.

A little fiddling by manually starting the failsafeXServer got me started, then had to twiddle around getting nvidia-glx-new installed. Once that was up things were alright but I was let with two problems.

I can't shutdown or restart, and have to do it the hard way. It hangs at the end. I hadn't seen this since edgy.

I at first thought it was the Network Manager, because it was throwing the Dbus errors, so I yanked that out since I didn't need wireless. That got rid of the error but it still hangs. Watching some console screens seems to indicate ACPI issues, and reboot being disabled. I've dug around the forums, and it seems to indicate that I might have luck messing with kernel parameters, but it's a mixed bag between turning ACPI off, forcing it, or some other power shenanigans. I wish this sort of stuff was a little more clear cut, since I don't feel up to messing with kernel settings.

### Post by brink on 2008-05-08
Worst installation experience I've ever had.
Fresh Install:

* Graphical installer repeatedly froze at partitioning step. Had to use alternate install disc and text mode

* Nvidia drivers do not install properly. One must use envy to get them to actually work.

* Segfault with tab completion in Vim/gvim (come on, how old is vim at this point) [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/vim/+bug/219546](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/vim/+bug/219546)

* High cpu usage due to new scheduler (cannot find ticket#)

* Periodic gvfsd segfaults which cause mounts to be inaccessible
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/gvfs/+bug/212789](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/gvfs/+bug/212789)

* GVFS screws up file permissions
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+bug/227808](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+bug/227808)

* Most fixes for the above suggest using hardy-proposed, which broke my system even more because 1) nvidia still doesn't install properly and 2) envyng in hardy-proposed does not work at all, it breaks at python import.


I also attempted an upgrade to Hardy on a different box. That left me with a system that would only boot to console, with errors, because lots and lots of packages failed to download, then something threw an error, but I don't know what because ERROR REPORTING threw an error.



I still don't trust this box, even though it appears to be working at the moment. Sucks for a LTS release that it's such a hunk of crap.

### Post by RgnadKzin on 2008-05-08
The main problem is that the new kernel does not recognize /dev/hda as the primary drive.  When I fdisk /dev/hda, it says there is not such drive.  Funny, because it mounts /dev/hda3 as root.  None of the other partitions are mounted and it cannot fsck them, either automatically, during bootup, or manually, stating that these partitions have a bad blocksize.

The secondary problem was with the xserver.  I use a KVM and the system could not determine the various settings thru the KVM.  I plugged the monitor directly into the video port, rebooted with the (older kernel) recovery mode, did the xfix, and now its good.

However, I would like to be able to boot using the new kernel.  
Any suggestions?

AMD Sempron(tm) Processor 2800+
nVidia nForce3 integrated motherboard

### Post by erginemr on 2008-05-08
> **RgnadKzin said:**
> The main problem is that the new kernel does not recognize /dev/hda as the primary drive.  When I fdisk /dev/hda, it says there is not such drive.  Funny, because it mounts /dev/hda3 as root.  None of the other partitions are mounted and it cannot fsck them, either automatically, during bootup, or manually, stating that these partitions have a bad blocksize.

The secondary problem was with the xserver.  I use a KVM and the system could not determine the various settings thru the KVM.  I plugged the monitor directly into the video port, rebooted with the (older kernel) recovery mode, did the xfix, and now its good.

However, I would like to be able to boot using the new kernel.  
Any suggestions?

AMD Sempron(tm) Processor 2800+
nVidia nForce3 integrated motherboard

AFAIK, all hdx references have become sdx (sda, sdb, etc.) with the latest versions of Ubuntu. It can also be a /dev/sdx vs. wrong UUID problem. Did you check the outcome of the commands "sudo fdisk -l" and "sudo blkid"?

### Post by Martje_001 on 2008-05-09
Install worked good, but shutdown crashed when I had NFS share mounted. So I wrote this script:
```
#!/bin/bash
function stop
{
count=0
lines="`cat /etc/fstab | grep 'musicserver:' | wc -l`"
while [ "$count" -le "$lines" ]; do
   fstab="`cat /etc/fstab | grep 'musicserver:'`"
   share=`echo "$fstab" | head -n $count | tail -n 1 | sed 's/ /\n/g' | head -n 2 | tail -n 1`
   count=`echo $count+1 | bc`
   umount "$share"
done
}

case "$1" in
  start)
   # No-op
   ;;
  restart|reload|force-reload)
   echo "Error: argument '$1' not supported" >&2
   exit 3
   ;;
  stop)
   stop
   ;;
  *)
   echo "Usage: $0 start|stop" >&2
   exit 3
   ;;
esac
```
And I did put it in /etc/init.d:
```
sudo cp umount-nfs /etc/init.d
sudo chmod +x /etc/init.d/umount-nfs
sudo update-rc.d -f umount-nfs defaults
```
That was all (took a month for me to find out that is was NFS that was causing the problems). :)

### Post by HyRax on 2008-05-09
All up, my 64-bit Hardy install (not upgrade) on my new machine worked like a charm - Q9450 CPU, GA-EX38-DQ6 motherboard, 4GB DDR2 PNY RAM, 750GB Samsung SATA HDD, generic USB bluetooth adapter, 8800GT gfx card and a Compaq 1710 monitor via DVI. Full Compiz eye-candy running. No freezes or crashes of any sort.

All my hardware works. My boot time is extremely (!) fast and it handled my dual-boot install of 32-bit WinXP (for games) perfectly.

The only issue that has come up has been a weird networking one. My PC is connected to a Netgear DG834Gv2 ADSL modem-router and my machine is also overclocked from the stock 2.66GHz to 3.2GHz. At long-ish random times (sometimes an hour, sometimes a day), my PC will just suddenly lose connectivity with the network. I'm not talking "cable disconnected", I'm talking, just straight out "Host unreachable" - I cannot hit my router at all. This is definitely not the router at fault because other Ubuntu PC's on my network still work perfectly at the time of failure. Re-running DHClient does NOT work. Disconnecting the network cable for a few seconds and reconnecting it does NOT work (the system tends not to acknowledge the disconnection at this point). Rebooting SOMETIMES works, but the only guaranteed way to fix it right away is to power-cycle the router (while PC is running or not)!

Knowing that the overclocking may have something to do with it, I reverted the machine back to its native 2.66GHz a week ago and ever since then I have had NO network issues.

So, does Hardy have issues with overclocked machines? There are absolutely no other anomalies occurring - only networking.

I have not yet tried overclocking at a lower level yet, eg: 2.8GHz or something, to see if the problem resurfaces.

### Post by Malta paul on 2008-05-09
Three times I have used a clean installation, but This time I tried an upgrade, after first backing up my system!!
The upgrade when very well, now after a few tweaks its running  great.
The items I resolve were:
a) The graphic definition - Fix was install Envy.
b) Grub still used the original Kernel - Fix edit Grub menu to  latest  Kernel.
c) XMMS player did not work. - Fix used VLC instead.
d) Canon printer didn't work with original driver. - Fix used latest driver.
e) HP Laserjet printer didn't work. - Fix reinstall firmware and driver.

### Post by stderr on 2008-05-09
I had a host of problems during the upgrade, but I think they were largely a result of a couple of minor problems I was having in gutsy (which I probably should resolved first in retrospect).

 - When the X-server was installed, and at another point, it really struggled to find a free display to put it on as it was started (during the upgrade), and I was almost certain the upgrade would end there, but after a lot of probing of the same few displays it eventually came up...  I wasn't sure if that was right. Should the new X server start during upgrade? I was pretty confused, as I imagined it would be started on a reboot. However, if it should have started during upgrade, should it really have been attempting to replace the current X server, on which the upgrade process was running?

 -  Various configuration windows appearing through the installation process, such as Samba's (that one I recall), appeared on the screen blank, and would only have their content displayed by minimising and restoring them. 

 - In gutsy, I had stunnel4 installed, which attempted to start on each boot and locked the boot process - instead of solving this, I would just login at Ctrl+Alt+Fx (after all, the need to reboot is typically rare in Linux!) It is also listed as having an install problem, probably for the same reason. However, this also occured twice during the upgrade  - the first time I Ctrl+C'd the stunnel4 proc within the upgrade terminal, and the upgrade continued. The second time, *just* prior to beginning the cleanup stage, I Ctrl+C'd it, and the whole upgrade process was killed! (Maybe I accidentally hit c twice...?)

Whilst Hardy seemed to work largely OK after that (after a few reboots, which progressively solved rather major problems) there are still issues, such as a split-second audio/video interruption every, say, 10-20mins. However, given my upgrade issues, and the fact that it died (or I killed it) before it could even clean up, there's no telling if they're  genuine issues with Hardy, or merely a result of the upgrade.

Barring the various confusions I faced during the upgrade, Hardy does seem very nice. I'll hopefully be able to figure out most issues and get a nice, stable system.

### Post by paulvandenberg on 2008-05-09
> **paulvandenberg said:**
> I installed Hardy and for the most part everything went well. However, for the first time, Ubuntu is giving me the VESA driver by default. Previous versions had always configured the 'nv' driver for my NVidia graphics chip. Being greeted with a 800x600 screen is not very newbie friendly. Fortunately, I am not a newbie and went to install the 'nvidia' driver through the Hardware Drivers option. However, this indicated to me that the restricted driver was already installed, which is was not. Again, fortunately, since I am not a newbie, I went to Synaptic to install the nvidia-glx-new package and rebooted. Still VESA! Again, fortunately I am not a newbie and found the Screens and Graphics tool to configure things myself. It took me a bit to find it, since it is hidden in Hardy and was easily spotted in System | Administration in Gutsy.

Don't get me wrong, I love Linux, but these kind of things make me reluctant to recommend it to anyone who is not technically inclined. Since this all worked perfectly in Gutsy, it seems to be a serious regression. If the autoconfiguration is not going to work all the time, the configuration tool, Screens and Graphics, should be not be hidden. (It is under Applications | Other, but is not visible by default. You need to edit the menu to enable it.)

Other that, I am pleased with Hardy. The 64 bit version runs quite fast and I've had no poblems with multimedia after configuring the Medibuntu repository.

Cheers....Paul

OK....I retract this! I did a reinstall for another reason. Apparently, I wasn't patient enough last time. This time after a bit of a delay, the prompt to install the Nvidia drivers came up. My apologies, it all seems work A-OK!

Paul

### Post by Canobuntu on 2008-05-09
I upgraded Kubuntu and then Ubuntu. The Ubuntu upgrade died before completion - screen locked, nothing responding. I rebooted the laptop and still no-joy. 

So I gave up and did a clean install to a new partition, leaving the old partition as a Data Partition. New install was reasonably ok except:-
a) Video Display came up as 800*600 when it should be 1024*768. After a number of abortive attempts to clear the problem, I copied the xorg.conf file from a Kubuntu partition to the Ubuntu partition and it worked. (Ubuntu 8.04 doesn't seem to detect Trident drivers, whereas Kubuntu 8.04 does!)
b) Wireless failed to work. I have tried lots of stuff, but cannot get it working. It worked under Ububtu 7.10, and I think I have done the same things, but no joy. Unfoirtunately I cann copy files fron Kubuntu as I have never managed to get Kubuntu to work with wireless (7.10 or 8.04) but I have spent quite a lot of time trying.

So, at the moment I have a Kubuntu 8.04 partition which upgraded easily and works well, apart from no wireless. An Ubuntu 7.10 partition which failed the upgrade and is now in an unknown, but broken state, and an Ubuntu 8.04 partition which is working, apart from the wireless, but took a bit of mucking about to get the video working properly.

Is it ready for the masses - not on an old Toshiba Satellite Pro. Am I having fun playing - yes, but if I needed wireless for real, it would rapidly become a complete pain.

### Post by Zelandeth on 2008-05-09
Upgrade...worked.  Sort of.

Upgrade was initiated by clicking on the button in the upgrade manager.

First two attempts led to me losing patience with it when the download speeds dropped to 1-2K/sec and stayed there.  Third time however I left it running while I went and did something else, and it seemed to sort itself out.

Was going fine until it actually started installing - then something distinctly odd happened, and the system appeared to lose access to a whole bunch of resoruces on the harddrive (all icons disappeared for one thing, the network managed claimed it couldn't access a critical resource and would terminate etc) after fighting with dialog boxes which lead me around in circles for about five mintues, I gave in and rebooted.

Ubuntu seemed to pick up fine from this, upgrade manager claiming that a partial upgrade was necessary as some updates could not be installed - I played along and followed its recommendations.  This thing things proceeded in a crash-free manner, right up until the point where it told me that I needed to reboot to complete the install.  I found that I had in the corner of the screen a notification telling me that the upgrade was finished and to click here to finish - and dialog box appeared in the middle of the screen as I clicked this telling me that the upgrade couldn't be completed, and that a recovery would now take place.  The system got as far as completing the progress bar for the shutdown routine - then hung.

Upon completion of the reboot, I found that for some reason I have to log in twice now, first attempt invariably boots me back to the login screen after two seconds or so.  There's a pink halo around my menus (I've seen this documented as a compiz issue elsewhere though, so can probably sort it), and for some reason my harddrive has been churning away non-stop ever since.  I don't know if this is maybe due to an improper shutdown requiring the harddrive to be "verified" or something - I've never had Ubuntu crash before, and as a result will sort itself out after a while, or if it's an indication of further problems ahead.

AMD Athlon 64 X2 5000+
Nvidia GeForce 8500GT 512Mb PCI-e
Gigabyte GA-M52S-S3P motherboard with onboard sound.
2GB of memory.
750Gb SATA-2 Hitachi Deskstar HD
80Gb USB2 external harddrive (Hitachi Deskstar ATA133) - which has Ubuntu on.  It's a rather crude dual-boot arrangement, but works!

If things don't settle down, I'll probably burn a LiveCD and start over.

### Post by shdriesner on 2008-05-09
My clean install experience has been both good and bad.  I do not yet have a stably working system due to some unexpected issues related to video resolution.  Actually, video resolution has been the only really frustrating aspect of the entire experience.

First of all, my motivation for installing Ubuntu is to have a more newbie friendly distro for use by my kids (4 & 6 years old).
I have two computers, both of which have been running Gentoo for over 4 years.  The computer I am installing Ubuntu to is a P4 1.7Ghz with 768MB of Ram and a 64MB Geforce3 video card which has an old install of Windows XP on a primary partition that we keep around in case we ever need to run Windows (very rare, but it still happens).

I'm going to relate the good, the bad, and the ugly about my install experience thus far:

_The Good:_
1. After booting from the install CD, my wife asked me to print off directions to someone's house, and even while the install was being performed I was able to use FF3 to get directions from mapquest.com and print them using our autodetected HP PSC1210 printer.  Very slick.

2. The installer preserved the Windows partition and I was able to dual-boot to either Ubuntu or Windows with no issues.  Well done.

_The Bad:_
1. The default screen resolution after install using the default 'nv' driver was 1024x768, but the monitor I have is capable of 1280x1024, and the 'Screen Resolution' applet did not list the latter as an option to adjust this, no were there any explicit screen resolutions listed in /etc/X11/xorg.conf.  Now, I can edit xorg.conf if need be as an experienced Linux user, but I would hardly expect a newbie to have to do this.

2. I wanted to use the restricted 'nvidia' driver to enable compiz, so I used the 'Video Hardware' applet to enable the restricted driver.  Unfortunately, after reboot, the new **maximum** allowed resolution was **750x350**!!  Thankfully I was able to access the forums to find out how to install and run the 'nvidia-settings' package, but at that resolution, the entire nvidia-settings GUI doesn't fit in the available screen, and the only way to even access the higher resolutions is to click on the 'Full Screen' button at the top of the GUI and then click down below on the GUI to access the lower buttons, click on the button, and then click again in the exact same place (even though the GUI scrolls back up at this point and it does not appear you are actually clicking the intended button).  This was frustrating at best, but I was eventually able to fulfill the clicking gymnastics required to attain a 1280x1024 resolution.  *Notable at this juncture was that higher resolutions were actually available at this point* (more on this below).

_The Ugly:_
1. I then wanted to install the kubuntu-desktop and xubuntu-desktop packages to have the option of either using KDE or XFCE4 (I prefer KDE, but I like choice - my other Gentoo box has KDE, GNOME, XFCE4, blackbox, and enlightenment installed).  I am used to using kdm instead of gdm, so during the install I chose kdm as my login manager.  Installation happened with no other issues until after reboot.  After reboot, I logged into KDE, and my screen resolution had returned to 750x350, and when I ran nvidia-settings, the KDE window manager wouldn't let me even click on the lower buttons to change the resolution, so I was completely stuck at that tiny resolution.  I thought that maybe I could then log into GNOME and fix the resolution, since I at least could click the buttons in the nvidia-settings GUI in GNOME, **but when I did this, 750x350 was the highest available resolution!!!**

My only option at this point is to attempt a re-install and try installing kubuntu-desktop and xubuntu-desktop  without switching to kdm from gdm and or changing to the nvidia restricted driver until afterwards.  If anyone has any other advice, I'd appreciate it.

The most frustrating aspect of this was how incredibly conservative Ubuntu seems to be about screen resolutions, and the fact that higher resolutions are not even made available in the 'Screen Resolution' applet.  This causes needless frustration, and for a newbie would likely be a showstopper.  I know Gentoo Linux, but I am not as familiar with the Ubuntu/Debian/apt-get way of doing things, so the solution to this (other than a full re-install) is not obvious to me.  Part of the reason I have thus far resisted installing a more newbie friendly distro like Ubuntu/SUSE/Mandrive is that you have less control over config files (these distros tend to overwrite your changes), so I am unwilling to go in and hand edit config files to get what I need unless there is no other way to fix the issue.
I also shouldn't expect to only be able to run at 750x350 resolution using a *64MB* graphics card - this is just ridiculous.

At the very least, I am willing to try installing one more time to see if I can get a working system with Ubuntu/Kubuntu/Xubuntu installed using the nvidia restricted drivers.  If I can't I'm going to re-install Gentoo and stick with what I know.

My intention through this post is not to bad-mouth Ubuntu (which in all other respects is a high quality distro for less computer savvy users) but to highlight a potentially huge roadblock to Ubuntu's adoption related to how screen resolutions are handled.

---

### Post by sewmyheadon on 2008-05-09

Viva la Linux!

I find that most administrative tools do not ask for the password, either the whell spins for a few minutes and the tool does not appear or else it appears and the wheel continues to spin indefinitely. When this happens only a reboot will get rid of it!

Trying to make changes as "sudo" doesn't work either!

I've had all 3 of these! I fixed the sudo thing first (there appears to be a number of ways of doing this). Have a look at this link:
  [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=770883](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=770883)

For the Update Manager "hang with no password prompt" issue, all I did to fix it was to right-click on the "Updates are available" icon in the tray and select "install updates" from the little drop-down that appeared. It immediately asked for the password & then did its stuff. Since I did that (on Wednesday am) both Update Manager & Synaptic work as expected.

### Post by lunus on 2008-05-10
I used to have problems with Gutsy. Gnome settings daemon would not start up. This happened almost every time. Other people have had the same problem:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=260689]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=260689")
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/gnome-settings-daemon/+bug/199960]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/gnome-settings-daemon/+bug/199960")

[https://launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/control-center/+bug/60477]("https://launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/control-center/+bug/60477")

### Post by kagashe on 2008-05-10
I use my Laptop as a Desktop (through external VGA monitor) since its LCD screen is damaged. Moreover, the CDRom drive does not work, therefore, I install through ISO.

I use [this page]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Installation/FromLinux") on Ubuntu Documentation to install from ISO. For Hardy Heron, I used the alternate CD ISO.

I could install successfully but the external VGA monitor did not work. It use to work till Ubuntu Gutsy Gibbon.

Through some patches on LCD screen of the Laptop I could see the GDM login screen. I entered my userid and password blindly and was pleased to hear the welcome music of Ubuntu desktop and see that it was loaded.

I pressed Ctrl Alt F1 logged into console and used xrandr -d :0 -q
and found the output identical to Gutsy. The VGA-0 was shown connected but it was not working.

I tried to configure through dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg but it did not alter the generic file. I understand that with xrandr and its GUI displayconfig-gtk dpkg-reconfigure does not work anymore.

I tried to configure through xrandr but could not.

I went to bugs.freedesktop.org and found a bug reported for my video card ATI Radeon IGP 340M. I also reported a bug.

I studied xorg.conf manual and made xorg.conf file and tried it with ati driver. It did not work.

I changed the driver to vesa and it worked.

Now I am using Hardy Heron and posting in this thread.

Earlier output of xrandr -q was showing information VGA-0, LVDS, S-Video similar to Gutsy but this configuration shows the following:
~$ xrandr -q
Screen 0: minimum 640 x 480, current 1024 x 768, maximum 1024 x 768
default connected 1024x768+0+0 0mm x 0mm
   1024x768       61.0* 
   800x600        73.0  
   640x480        86.0  

I am attaching the xorg.conf file.

kagashe

The updated ati driver is available in Debin Sid. I have downloaded the required drivers from Debian Sid repositories and they are working on Hardy:
xserver-xorg-video-ati_6.8.192-1_i386.deb
xserver-xorg-video-mach64_6.8.0-1_i386.deb
xserver-xorg-video-r128_6.8.0-1_i386
xserver-xorg-video-radeon_6.8.192-1_i386

I have deleted xorg.conf file, xrandrr -q is showing VGA-0 and LVDS outputs and I could turn off LVDS.

### Post by RDV on 2008-05-10
This is the third machine that I installed Hardy Heron on. My desktop PC, a relative's laptop and now my eeePC. 

Using a USB drive with the Ubuntu Hardy Live ISO, I have just completed a fresh install of Hardy 8.04, replacing eeeXubuntu r3. I following various wiki and forum posts to get all the eeePC hardware working. Through that information I avoided several of the common problems such as:
> lack of sound volume control
> slow boot times
> recovering the wired Ethernet connection using the "pull the eeePC battery" tip

There are great scripts on the Web to automate the various eeePC specific tweaks. The most complete one at [http://eee.ricey.co.uk/](http://eee.ricey.co.uk/) is called "RiceeeyTweak.sh". I had to edit the section called "Installing ACPI modules" as that section is kernel (2.6.24-16) specific.

Once I install Hardy and did an immediate update the kernel changed to "2.6.24-17". I found a more generic script at [http://www.x2on.de/tutorials.html#eee-ubuntu804](http://www.x2on.de/tutorials.html#eee-ubuntu804) and it's APCI section is not kernel specific. Just cut/pasted the ACPI section into the RiceeeyTweak.sh script. The Ricey script adds more tweaks such as CPU overclocking control.

In total with the wiki, scripts and forum suggestions, I gained:
> CPU scaling (when you are overclocked idle activity will scale the CPU down and you will get heat reduction)
> Hot selectable Virtual screen size of 1024x768 / normal screen size of 800x480
> Full function key and OSD functionality (I just could not get this to work in eeeXubuntu 7.10)
> Up/Down CPU clock control (I had previously been overclocked via changing the BIOS and was stuck at 900Mhz)

An improvement with Hardy (which the tweaks may have helped) is that I no longer get mount errors on a reboot even though my /home and /usr are installed on a SDHC card. Under eeeXubuntu I always had a "improper unmount" error and a fsck performed on my /usr SDHC partition.

I would advise that you keep at least the Madwifi driver source around as just this morning (May 9th) the Ubuntu updates broke my Wifi after a reboot. A quick make, make install and reboot returned my Wifi connection. I keep both the Madwifi and the overclocking source on my eeePC just in case updates break their functionality.

The microphone works out-of-the-box but I had to fool around with "alsamixer" to unmute the microphone through "alsamixer" then turn up the volume on the digital capture with "alsamixer -V Capture" then the "Sound Recorder" application worked with the source as "Digital".

I am also using AWN which I prefer over the gnome panel. Compiz-Fusion and AWN work fast enough for me even when I am streaming video from my Jinzora2 media server or from a Web source.

Of course full Ubuntu out-of-the-box support of the eeePC would be nice but I am very pleased so far with Hardy on my eeePC. Overall I gained a few features that I could not get working under eeeXubuntu r3 and now have the same windows manager (Gnome) as my desktop/media server. As yet I have not seen the down side.

Here is a picture of my customized desktop.
[[IMG]http://img398.imageshack.us/img398/5532/hardy804uq3.th.jpg[/IMG]](http://img398.imageshack.us/my.php?image=hardy804uq3.jpg)

### Post by irv on 2008-05-10
I wish I could vote twice because One upgrade went flawless and the other was a nightmare. I updated my server from 7.10 to 8.04 without a hitch. But my laptop which is a Dell Inspiron 1521 rewrote the grub so it would not boot in to Vista. I added the entry I found in another post but all my Dell would do is go to a Dell test screen. The entry I added to the grub.lst was done with the command
> gksudo gedit /boot/grub/menu.lst 

and the entry was:
> title Windows
rootnoverify (hd0,0)
chainloader +1

How I fixed the problem was to delete the Linux partition and load 8.04 fresh from a CD.
The problems I still have are: it does not see my network and  other issues are no built in cam, and it won't come up on my external monitor. There might be more I need to look at yet, but I will find out as time goes on.

### Post by tstaddon on 2008-05-11
I've got a few annoyances to share with you after a fresh deployment of 8.04:

1. Even when the keyboard settings in System/Preferences/Keyboard are set to something other than US (in my case UK), location is somewhere outside the US, and at no point in history have I ever specified or wanted any US locale settings, it appears that 

sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

sets the keyboard to US format in xorg.conf every time. I call this the "Sod the user's regional preferences, we'll pick the US locale" mentality. We'll have less of that, if you don't mind.

2/ The entire way the display drivers are configured, doesn't appear to have one iota of common sense attached to it; no wonder newcomers to Linux are baffled. It's a mess - imagine what a noob would make of it: 

"Something other than me keeps rewriting my customised display settings in xorg.conf after I've configured it"

"Screen and Graphics Preferences reports every test I perform, as "Configuration test failed" even if VESA is selected and is already working."

"EnvyNG installs the Radeon driver but I'm still left with VESA driver selected."

I'm technical enough to beat it into submission, but even so it took me the most verbose amount of fiddling to get a clean install going with a Radeon X1050 card.

I'm still stuck with VESA because the one time I did manage to force it to use the ATI driver, my mouse refused to click on anything on the desktop, and doubleclicked everywhere else. I had to switch back.

### Post by hellion0 on 2008-05-11
Well, I upgraded to Xubuntu 8.04 on my Thinkpad 600. The upgrade itself went smoothly. The aftermath, however, was not so smooth.

* It took five minutes between the login and a functional desktop.
* XMMS was lost in transition.
* Rhythmbox didn't want to load mp3s, even with the proper gstreamer plugin.
* Everything runs extremely slow. Even typing into a terminal is slow going.

There are a few good things as well as the bad, it's just going to take a ton of tweaking. At the least, my wireless, sound and resolution stayed intact throughout. Those were the hardest things to get working during the initial install under Feisty.

Once all the bugs get ironed out and I can fix everything else that's broken, I'll be satisfied with it. However, at this time, I won't upgrade my other machines.

### Post by beebelo on 2008-05-11
This was my first Ubuntu upgrade that worked!  I've been a user since 5.04, and I've always ended up reinstalling.  

I'm running Ubuntu Studio now, so I was especially wary.  People in the forum said Ardour had problems with upgrading, so I removed it.  Then I decided what the heck, I'll just remove most all the apps.  I removed all the Studio apps, plus a bunch of other extra stuff I'd installed.  I did keep Open Office.  I also kept my non-free codecs from medibuntu and other sources--only because I didn't remember everything about how I installed them, and I hoped they'd slide through un-noticed.  

I used the Ubuntu Studio DVD ("alternate install disk"), and ran the upgrade from the prompt upon mounting the disk.  I selected 'use internet connection to get the latest updates'.  

As it turned out, there were only two issues:  
- The first was the Ubuntu Studio Theme didn't install properly.  Fixing it was a simple matter of clicking it on.  
- The second was a problem I didn't solve but worked around instead.  After the upgrade finished, Ubuntu didn't recognize my Ubuntu Studio install DVD.  It kept saying insert the disk, but ignored my insertion as if it was the wrong disk.  Fixed the problem by unclicking it in the repository.

After that, no problems that I am aware of.

Thanks, Ubuntu team!

PS.  I use the Nvidia restricted driver.  It was installed and working after the upgrade!  I do not use Compiz or any of that stuff.  I do not use wifi.  My system is Asus motherboard (I forgot the model #), with Sempron 2800 processor.

### Post by perchance on 2008-05-11
In short, it's been a disaster. 

I purchased a Gutsy Dell a couple months ago and took the leap upgrading to Heron tonight by answering the auto-updaters call to action. I wish I had done a little more homework and known that I could expect problems, but that comes with the territory and I guess I should hope to find a few, as the educators say, "teachable moments" in here.

I'm really hoping somebody can help me through the problems, so here goes.

The upgrading process seemed to go fine, but then after I rebooted at its conclusion I found that my box hangs on startup and just grinds through a series of cryptic error messages I've never seen before that I suspect having something to do with my harddrive. They read like the following, with ever increasing numbers in the left hand bracket. (Please forgive any typos as I am reproducing this by hand on my laptop)

[  427.106648] ata2.00: exception Emask 0x0 SAct 0x0 Serr 0x0 action 0x2 frozen
[  427.106648] ata2.00: cmd a0/00:00:00:24:0/00:00:00:00:00:00:00/a0 tag 0 pio 36 in
[  427.106648] ata2.00: status { DRDY }
...
[  583.564700] ata2.00: exception Emask 0x0 SAct 0x0 Serr 0x0 action 0x2 frozen
[  583.564700] ata2.00: cmd a0/00:00:00:24:0/00:00:00:00:00:00:00/a0 tag 0 pio 36 in
[  583.564700] ata2.00: status { DRDY }

Anybody know what's up with that? I worry that it means something is wrong with my hardware, but I never had any problems until this upgrade...and the box is only a month or two old.

If, on startup, I hit ESC for the boot menu and select "Ubuntu 8.04, kernel 2.6.22-14-generic" instead of the default "Ubuntu, 8.04, kernel 2.6.22-16-generic," the operating system with successfully launch, but my monitor config is (the default Dell NVIDIA that I had tweaked in Gutsy to run across two 20" monitors) totally shot and running only in 800x600, plus the boot up routine is weirded out and asks me to login from a command-line like environment in addition to the standard Ubuntu login prompt. 

Anybody have any idea what's up with this stuff and what I can do? Any Dell Ubuntu users experience similar problems?

Really the only thing on the box I'd hate to lose is my collection of CDs that I've ripped to MP3. I don't currently have them backed up anywhere and it would be a huge pain to rerip -- plus I've given many of them away to friends now that I have them on digital. =(

### Post by shakma on 2008-05-12
Here to add to the fray:

I _upgraded_ from Gutsy and it works flawlessly for me.  Smooth as silk.  Have not encountered any app errors in 2 weeks.

### Post by Complexed and Many Ways on 2008-05-12
Only real issue I m having with Ubuntu is trying to figure out how to use my second hard drive as data open data storage bank. But since Im on that step now mines as well give my 2 cents. wont hurt any lol. Anyways from what I know (or thought I did) Oems are my least favorite platforms. Like M$ they make everything about themselves. You should (not saying it would work) be able to do 3 things I can think of now. 

1. Try to uninstall 8.04 using your previous version without partitioning. (old files)
2. Reinstall your previous version without partitioning. (old files)
3. Attempt a safe mode to back up your data, then reinstall whatevr you like.

Worst come to worst install a smaller hdd to access both disk drives (the step Im on) and worst than that (no offence) attempt a factory default and attempt a rescue in the process. Im not sure if it is legal to post this but you can use a P2P network to get all your mp3s back at the cost of searching and remembering your music. I used my SATA from my main pc with an usb enclosure box to copy 600+ songs at least 5-6 times on computers. takes about a half hour but you could rescue all your info with this type of hardware. GL

With these figures it's clear that I'll never be able to use VirtualBox without a memory upgrade.  For now I'll revert to 6.06 and investigate the possibility of swapping to another distro unless I hear of substantial fixes to reduce memory consumption.

### Post by Cragrat on 2008-05-12
Gutsy -> Hardy
I had a problem during the upgrade which others have come across.
Blank screen and unable to wake up the computer (even though) I have no Power Management etc configured). After powering off the PC and starting it up again, I had to get into Safemode and go to the Command Line. Anyway, after doing some research at work I did the following:
mkdir/usr/bin/dbus (actually I didn't 'cos it was already there)
dpkg --configure -a
sudo apt-get autoremove
sudo apt-get autoclean
sudo apt-get clean

Not sure if I needed to do all this but anyway, I'm on Hardy Heron now and it seems very stable and better (bug wise & functionality) than Gutsy Gibbon. 
Well done all - keep up the good work and many thanks.

### Post by Dean Powell on 2008-05-12
Hi:

A follow-up to my previous posting on this thread.  I'm running an Acer Aspire with an nVidia GeForce 6100 video card.  I was running Feisty with no video problems at all.

I upgraded to Hardy, but left my /home partition alone, so I could keep my old personal files.  After upgrading to Hardy, I couldn't get better than 800x600 screen resolution.  I tried all the tips and tricks given in the forums regarding the video driver updates, but none of them worked.  I ended up downgrading back to Feisty.

After re-backing up my old personal files, I tried the full installation of Hardy again, this second time re-formatting my /home partition.  Upon reboot, I still defaulted to 800x600, but now, when I enabled the restricted drivers, the system automatically downloaded and installed the new driver (this did not happen on my first attempt).  After reboot, I was automatically set to 1280x1024 resolution, working perfectly.

Obviously, there was some Gnome Display setting on my $HOME that was preventing the newest driver from being downloaded and used.  If you are experiencing video driver and resolution problems, and you preserved your /home directory from your previous install, you may want to think about backing up $HOME, then reformatting it along with the rest of your system.

Video is now working better than it was with Feisty (which never offered me 1280x1024), and I've experienced no problems with sound or anything else.  My one minor remaining complaint is that the "Electric Sheep" screen saver, which I installed from the repositories, gives me only a blank screen.  I have downloaded a "kickstart" package, and they display fine, so I have no idea what the problem is at this point.

Dean Powell
Edmonton

---

### Post by wjwh on 2008-05-12
> **Alpha1Dash1 said:**
> I've had all 3 of these! I fixed the sudo thing first (there appears to be a number of ways of doing this). Have a look at this link:
  [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=770883](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=770883)

For the Update Manager "hang with no password prompt" issue, all I did to fix it was to right-click on the "Updates are available" icon in the tray and select "install updates" from the little drop-down that appeared. It immediately asked for the password & then did its stuff. Since I did that (on Wednesday am) both Update Manager & Synaptic work as expected.

Many thanks for this.  I repaired the sudo problem as in the link that you gave and then found that this had solved most of the other problems as well. Update Manager and most other administrative functions are now working.  The biggest problem remaining is that I downloaded and installed Samba, but it crashes every time I try to use it - so my home network does not work.  Thanks again!

### Post by cpudney on 2008-05-13
Dapper -> Hardy
Dell Inspiron 9400 w/ Nvidia GeForce 7800

[The upgrade went well but I had a few problems]("http://ubuntu-linux-dell-inspiron-9400.blogspot.com/2008/05/dapper-drake-to-hardy-heron-upgrade.html").  Foremost amongst these was getting the Nvidia video driver working.  In the end the solution was straightforward - installing the correct set of packages, namely,

nvidia-glx-new
nvidia-kernel-common
nvidia-settings
linux-restricted-modules (-common, -generic)

However, the upgrade had installed a completely different, mismatched set.  EnvyNG got me some of the way but not completely.

Other minor tweaks, easily fixed:

No NFS client support
No sound from Firefox Flash plugin when other audio applications were running
Thunderbird Lightning extension not working

Overall, worth the effort.

YMMV,
Chris.

### Post by tlg on 2008-05-13
Persistent Network Shares in 8.04
I have network storage set up using a Linksys NSLU2 running Unslung firmware. It has two discs attached which are shared on the network using samba. The unit also runs ssh.

I want the discs to be available on Ubuntu PCs under the Places menu and on the File/Save menu in applications.

To mount the shares in Ubuntu 7.04 I go to Places/Network and browse the Windows network to find the NSLU2 device and open to see the shares.
Then right click on the required share and select Mount Volume from the menu.

I then get a dialog box which allows me to rename the share if required.
After this the share is added to the Places list and an icon appears on the Desktop.

Alternatively I can select Places/Connect to Server and select Windows Share or SSH and specify the IP address of the NSLU2 and create a share in a similar way.

So far so good.

However, having upgraded to 8.04 I have noticed a couple of differences.

First there is no dialog to allow renaming the share.

Second, and most critical, the share is not persistent ie it disappears after a restart or logout, both from the Desktop and the Places list.

I understand that significant changes have been made to the way this stuff is implemented in 8.04, but are these changes in the UI made for some good reason, or are they just a inadvertent side effect?

Am I missing something basic here or is there some way to set this up in the same way as works in 7.04?

### Post by GrandpaLeaman on 2008-05-13
Wow, I'm flabbergasted. When I decided to upgrade two days ago I feared the worst and backed up everything in anticipation of disaster.  My experience could not be farther from the truth.

The upgrade only took 2.5 hours, with the download taking only 1.5 hours (I think I even smelled smoke coming from the router, ha, ha). 

After the restart I didn't have wireless, which was to be expected since I have a Broadcom wireless card. But I was prepared. I had copied to file the process to get my wireless working from here: [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx/Feisty_No-Fluff](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx/Feisty_No-Fluff)  
It took me a whole 15 minuted to get my wireless to work again. I had to make sure I had everything ready before hand (such as having the driver file on hand prior to the upgrade) since I would not have wired access to the internet.

Next I restarted Compiz, which restarted AWN (I shut off Compiz prior to the upgrade). Everything was working great.

Later, I noticed the folders in Nautilus were the default folder icons even though I had Gartoons set up for my theme. So I Googled the forum to see what I could find and sure enough, I found this thread: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=768847&page=2](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=768847&page=2) . This fix worked, so another problem solved.

As I was testing all my favorite programs, I found that Streamtuner didn't work. It couldn't seem to find XMMS. That's funny, I know I have XMMS installed. WRONG! Seems this was one of the packages removed by Hardy. Back to Google. I was lucky to find a thread that linked me to here: [https://launchpad.net/ubuntu/hardy/i386/xmms/1:1.2.10+20070601-1build2](https://launchpad.net/ubuntu/hardy/i386/xmms/1:1.2.10+20070601-1build2) . I downloaded the most recent stable .deb file, installed it, and have my uncensored comedy back!

I know that I will run into a few minor glitches in the next few weeks (which I will resolve through the forums) but this has to be the smoothest upgrade I have ever been through, and I have been upgrading OS's since DOS (oops, showing my age now). A smile, a wave, and a tip of the hat to all you fine developers, as well as everyone who contributes to these forums with a sense of ubuntu. I am proud and honored to be a member of this excellent community. Keep up the good work!!!

---

### Post by bousozoku on 2008-05-14
I had re-partioned my drive because I had originally had a problem with the documentation on the partioning during 7.04.  (Someone should really explain whether 74 % is 74 % of the free space or 74 % of the total drive space.  The fancy "Official" book recites the same text without an explanation.)

After I eliminated all of the Linux partitions, I re-installed 7.10 from a recent DVD and did the release upgrade to 8.04.

The odd thing was that the Broadcom wireless (restricted) driver was loaded but the nVidia (restricted) driver was not.

I read various threads, but found no solution that worked until I did more checking the system itself.  In the Software Sources, Proprietary drivers for devices (restricted) was not checked/ticked.

One this was ticked, the kernel bits were downloaded and I was able to go to Hardware Drivers and tick the nVidia selection.  After the restart, everything is back to the way it was prior to the earlier upgrade.

Thanks to the developers for an interesting and continuing, (mostly) positive experience with Linux.

### Post by lavinog on 2008-05-14
Upgraded from Gutsy:
After reading some of the posts on the forum I decided to make a copy of my root partition so I can revert back. I had to resize twice (The first attempt the updater told me that I needed 1600mb free on root...after resizing the partition an extra 2g it said I still needed another 600mb) completed the upgrade and went to sleep. The next day I went to try it out and it booted to the desktop...no wireless...no ethernet...no touchpad, oh no...well I looked around and found that I was still booting to gutsy but with the 2.6.24 kernel. Long story short it was booting to the gutsy backup partition because both partitions had the same UUID. After deleting the backup partition everything worked except sudo...which was fixed by fixing my host entry (what changed with sudo that required this?)
I just have to tweak the wireless and ati driver and everything is cool now.
I like the bookmark feature on the remote desktop connections.

SUMMARY:
Don't backup your partition by copying it to another partition.
If sudo complains or gksu acts weird (why doesn't this give feedback when there is an error anyway?) check that your computer name is listed as an alias to 127.0.0.1 (this was changed by my school's network at some point)

Edit: Forgot to mention, that for some reason with the mesa drivers I get 400 fps on glxgears...I used to only get under 10 fps with the mesa/fglrx-xorg driver...I'm a little confused on how that happened, but not complaining.
Overall the upgrade worked great...the problems I had were my doing.

### Post by omnibot on 2008-05-14
well, i'd tried the demo over vista on my compaq c700 laptop.

### Post by AcidHawk on 2008-05-15
After being quite happy with 7.04 I made the mistake of upgrading to 7.10  Problem was, that that was when Ubuntu introduced the build in compiz stuff so all my customizations broke.  (was also probably due to the fact that I didn't(don't) really know what I was doing)

I went back to windows as I just couldn't get 7.10 doing what I needed it to do and under pressure from the office took the easy(noob) way out and re-installed windows.  I did vow however that I would come back a look at the 8.04 LTS release.

I have been very happy with Hardy (Fresh Install completely removing Windows).  I do get concerned that so many people are having problems with 8.04 (esp since 8.04 is supposed to be long term support) but none of them really effect me.  

I am very satisfied with my developement environment and the virtualbox implementation for other os testing.  In fact this is the most comfortable I have felt about running linux ever.  I am at a point now where the only thing I need to do in win is test my Windows service installer and if the darn service thing runs.  Other than that I do EVERYTHING in Ubuntu.

### Post by irv on 2008-05-15
I have installed Hardy three times now, and this last one was the best. The first was just an upgrade on my server which went very well. The Second was on my New laptop which was a disaster. Like I said in another post it was a Dell Inspiron  1521. Hardy didn't do a very good job finding hardware. I finial wiped it out and gave the whole HD back to Vista.
Now comes the good news. I went back to my old laptop which is a no name. 1.8 Pentium M and I thought I had a 60 Gig HD but the Disk Usage Analyzer tells me I have a 106.7 GB HD using 16.5 GB an have 90.0 GB free. (Does Hardy do some doubling?) I did a complete fresh install this time, and it found everything and everything works; Network, Video, (even my 22” LCD), Sound, etc. Now I have two laptops one with Vista and the other with Hardy. Guess what? Hardy boots and runs much faster on my old laptop then Vista running on my new laptop. There is no doubt in my mind that Hardy running on the right Hardware will out perform Vista. 
I was a faithful Linux user until my old laptop quite working (memory problem) so I bought a new laptop which came with Vista. (Vista sucks). It keep doing thing and I just have to wait until it is done. I was right in a middle of a Power Point, when it decided to do something so I couldn't even change to the next slide. I hope this happen to Bill G when he is doing a show. I have never had this problem with Linux. After putting new memory in the old laptop and loading Hardy I now have the best of both worlds.
I plan on using my old laptop for everyday use and my new laptop for running the software that is not available for Linux yet.
In closing, let me say it feels good to get back to my Ubuntu Linux and now with Hardy.

### Post by Mander on 2008-05-15
So far, so good.

I'm using Hardy in a Wubi installation, and it seems to be fine.  Using Envy for the Nvidia drivers it now has the correct resolution on my wide-screen laptop.  The sound works which I couldn't figure out in 7.04. Even with all of the whizzy effects enabled it is so much faster than Vista on this same machine that it seems like a totally different computer.  

Even though it sometimes won't start Synaptic, it takes less time to just restart the whole thing than it would to open "add/remove programs" on Vista. I haven't tackled the wireless card yet but since I am about 2ft away from the router I am just using a cable.  This laptop is so huge that it's really just a compact desktop anyway, so it's not really an issue for me.

### Post by bill516 on 2008-05-15
My machine is an ASUS laptop.  Profile as below:

Model:  Asus Z96JS, v. 1.0.  Assembled by J&N Computing (jncs.com), 2007.

BIOS:  American Megatrends 080012 (07/14/2006)

CPU:  Intel Core2 T7200 @ 2.00 GHz, socket 478M, 64 bit width.

L2 CPU Cache: 4 MB

System Memory:  2 GB in 2 1 GB DIMMs

Host & PCI Bridges: Intel Mobile 945GM

Display:  Hardwired video "card", ATI Radeon Mobility X1600, 32 bit width  (LCD size is 15 inches)

Audio:  Intel 82801G (ICH7 family)

Network Wireless:  Intel Pro Wireless 3945 ABG; driver, ipw3945

Network Wired (Ethernet):  Realtek RTL-8169 Gigabit Ethernet

IDE Interface:  Intel 82801 GBM/GHM

NOTE:  HD is 7200 rpm nominal 100GB esata, reported as a SCSI Disk, something apparently common with esata drives.  This has caused no operational problems.

SCSI Disk : ATA 93 GB formatted, nominal 100 GB @ 7200 RPM
Vol. 0:  NTFS 60 GB
Vol. 1:  Linux File System 31 GB
Vol. 2 extended: 1435 MB
Logical Volume: Linux swap, 1435 MB (Swap size since increased to equal RAM)

CD/DVD +/- RW, vendor not specified

Vol. 0: holds WindowsXP, Vol. 1: holds Linux, Originally Ubuntu 7.10.

Note:  Hibernation/Suspend works under WinXP.  It did not work under 7.10.

Battery life with this machine is meager, just barely one hour fully charged.

The Upgrade to 8.04

I first download an iso file and created a LiveCD to investigate Hardy.  This suggested that suspend might work, which was very good news.  It also suggested that my HP 4070 scanner would be operational, something else I could not do in 7.10. I decided to do the upgrade.

First I checked for and installed one last round of upgrades for 7.10 so that it would be as current as possible before the upgrade.

Next I used Heliode's first-rate HowTo on how to back up a Linux system using TAR.  I also tested my ability to restore from the TAR.  This works extremely well.  If you do not know about it, do check it out.

Next I went to Synaptic and did the plain vanilla upgrade to Hardy by simply selecting that option and following along.  Put some good music on, this takes a while.

On reboot the proprietary ATI driver seemed to be installed but I also showed the resolution problem seen by others.  I believe I had 600x320 and no way to change it!

Removing the proprietary ATI driver did not help at all.  This was unexpected because I had not seen this problem when using the LiveCD

I next snooped the forum for help and I got it.  Thank you one and all!

1.  Following suggestions there I opened a terminal and I first did

"sudo apt-get remove xserver-xgl --purge"

This apparently has worked for some people.  it did not work for me.

2.  I next did

"sudo su"

followed by

"displayconfig -gtk"

Note that attempting to use displayconfig without first establishing root privileges did not work.

Once in displayconfig I selected generic LCD Panel and I set resolution to  1080X1024.

I closed dsplayconfig and rebooted.

On reboot Administration\Preferences\Screen Resolution showed a range of choices maxing out at 1080X1024.  I selected 1024X768 as the best compromise for Old Guy Eyeballs.

Closed out, rebooted and came up with the appropriate resolution.

Problem solved.

One last note:  As you upgrade you will have the choice of retaining your present CUPS settings.  f you miss that (as I did) you will have to reinstall your shared or wireless printer.  I had no problem doing so.

My thanks to the many people who contributed to this latest release and also to the several people who made really helpful suggestions on the forum for repairing the resolution problem.

I'll hope to return the favor at some point.

### Post by huck416 on 2008-05-15
My upgrade from 7.10 to 8.04 was not without problems although mostly minor.  The upgrade itself from an alternate install cd went smooth with no errors and my system booted up as normal. 

First thing I noticed on the boot was there was already two kernels to choose from: 2.6.22.14 and 2.6.24.16 with the latter being the default. Naturally I selected it. Below is a summary of experiences so far since I have tried both kernels several times and I have tried several tips on the forum to resolve some issues.

System: Dell Inspiron 1521 (AMD), ATI graphics, BCM4401-B0 100Base-TX (rev 02) ethernet, BCM94311MCG wlan mini-PCI wireless card using ndiswrapper.

2.6.24.16

Normal boot
- Hibernation workes with no further tweaking.
- Knetworkmanager worked although would not find my wireless unless I selected broadcast at the router. 
 -Wired networking no problem.
- Fn+UP / Down now controls my brightness however in adjustments of 3 (25-30% each time). This did not work at all in Gutsy
- System will not shut down properly at all. Hangs on a blank screen. Eventually can get to a terminal and see messages regarding network manager errors and CIFS VFS unable to connect. The latter is resolved by unmounting my shared network drives before shutdown - never had to do that before. But even then the system will still hang at the network errors.

[COLOR="Red"]EDIT: System will now shutdown with 2.6.24.16 kernel using the following:

 Originally Posted by lesergi  View Post
The solution is the next:

You must edit the file /etc/ati/authatieventsd.sh.

The suggestion is to update the change by changing

XDM_AUTH_MASK=/var/lib/xdm/authdir/authfiles/A$1*

to:

XDM_AUTH_MASK=/var/run/xauth/A$1*

Bye!

Still have the networking errors and CIFS VFS errors which slow the shutdown process somewhat but it's progress.
[/COLOR]
2.6.22.14

- Normal boot
- Hibernation does NOT work - system hangs and must hold power button to shut it down.
- Networking same as above
- Fn+UP / Down contorl brightness but in 10% increments - much better. Why the difference between the two kernels? Things that make you say hhmmmm.
- System will shutdown but is slow - same network and CFIS errors as above but it seems to work through them by itself and eventually shutdown. It is quicker if I unmount those same network drives first.

The rest is pretty much the same between the two kernels; my built-in webcam works, sound is good (not sure about the mic), video is good with the supplied ATI restricted drivers. Bluetooth works great although once in awhile I have to reconnect to my mouse. Usually it connects on its own and very quickly - as soon as I have a cursor and before any desktop actually appears.

The wireless is getting speeds up to 54mb/s although is usually settles out around 24-36; at time of this writing - 48mb/s. Router is setup with WEP. Haven't had a chance to try WPA yet.

The big questions are:

Why the shutdown errors / problems? What is the system really looking for and can't find that causes the delay or even a hangup? I've tried many ideas found here and none have worked on my system. This is actually an excerpt from another post but my messages are basically the same:

NetworkManager: <WARN> nm_signal_handler(): Caught signal 15, shutting down normally.
NetworkManager: <INFO> Caught termination signal
NetworkManager: <DEBUG> [1209404787.824467] nm_print_open_socks(): Open Sockets List:
NetworkManager: <DEBUG> [1209404787.824467] nm_print_open_socks(): Open Sockets List Done.
NetworkManager: <WARN> nm_hal_deinit(): libhal shutdown failed - Connection is closed
NetworkManager: <WARN> nm_dbus_init(): nm_dbus_init() could not get the system bus. Make sure the message bus daemon is running!

This is an extract from dmesg

[   30.964000]  CIFS VFS: Error connecting to IPv4 socket. Aborting operation
[   30.964000]  CIFS VFS: cifs_mount failed w/return code = -101
[   30.984000]  CIFS VFS: Error connecting to IPv4 socket. Aborting operation
[   30.984000]  CIFS VFS: cifs_mount failed w/return code = -101

Similar errors occur during shutdown and hold the process up.

At least it's working for the most part although I kind of wish I'd stayed with Gutsy. The only things that didn't work in Gutsy was hibernate but the boot times were so fast I didn't care; and my Fn keys for brightness control - had to go through the power manager in the task tray.

Anybody with any other ideas why it would behave this way, I'm ready to try them.

---

### Post by Cato2 on 2008-05-16
Hardy is quite disappointing - I can't get the default Firefox 3 beta 5 to work at all on an installation upgraded from an Ubuntu Hardy beta, as follows:

- the Back button is greyed out on every tab even when there is history
- the Address Bar doesn't work at all - I can type in a URL, but hitting Enter does nothing - instead I have to use bookmarks or the Google search bar

I did manage to install Firefox 2 from the repositories (aptitude install firefox-2) but that doesn't work properly either: extensions are loaded but mostly disabled - I can't set Preferences for extensions, nor can I Enable them, though I can Disable or Uninstall them

I also tried upgrading a Kubuntu Feisty installation to Gutsy (and then to Hardy) - this mostly worked after the Gutsy upgrade, but Opera and Firefox 2 would crash as soon as soon as they started due to content causing a crash in the tabs that they were re-loading on startup (they were OK on a new session without any saved tabs).

This system was running Feisty quite reliably - same installation I've been using for 6 months on an older PC.

A live CD did have a working Firefox 3, so perhaps the problem right now is that the upgrade from Hardy beta to final has messed things up.  The problem is not Firefox extensions, as I only have those loaded in Firefox 2.  

Overall I'm very unimpressed - without a working web browser it's hard to even debug things...

I'm now trying to work out whether it's worth trying to debug Hardy or just revert to Feisty until Hardy stabilises.  When I do upgrade, it will need to be a clean install, which is time-consuming due to various config changes I've made in /etc so the system works as a backup server, TWiki server, etc.
Has anyone seen the Firefox extensions issue?

UPDATED: Actually I've almost solved the Firefox 3 beta issue - it appears to be a problem with my old Firefox 2 profile (I'm using the same /home partition as on Feisty).  I was using both FF2 and FF3 beta 3 on Feisty, switching between them without profile problems - however, creating a new profile has got rid of the problem.  See [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=767944](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=767944) for some more details on how to do this.

### Post by imtrekd on 2008-05-17
I was disappointed when I installed Hardy due to the fact I can't watch streaming videos.  Youtube worked, but others like Metacafe and the like didn't.  After reading about the fixes and the elaborate subroutines and editing, I found a fast trick to make flash and streaming video work on any site (at least for me)  Here it is: Use synaptic package to completely uninstall Gnash, yes, all of it.  Use the add/remove application and install all the gstreamer plugins and any other gstreamer you might need.  Worked like a charm.  Note: Check synaptic to see if gstreamer is applied.  

Now if I can get gnome splash manager to work, it would be great.

### Post by sparky64 on 2008-05-17
Have to agree with most of the comments here.
I am "in the words of gary oldman" Very disappointed with this release.
My main production desktops which i use for work are usually 1 month behind release date.
  Having finally got heron to run on my laptop "not bug free-still have problems with sound/updates"
  I had problems getting libdvdcss to work as i am in the habit of manually installing-Never know if the riaa mafia will succeed in taking down servers hosting it,which caused kaffeine to say "codecs installed" but would still not run.It seems making it easy for Complete newbies is making it harder for me.(I still class myself as a newbie as i rely on this and other forums for problem solving.)
 Getting the correct res to work "1025x800" was hard work.
Auto updates is still hit and miss, Just had to use apt-get update to update my system, which included an 100meg update with updates to xorg.
  This caused no end of problems as my screen res refused to be recognized  
and reset to 600x400.
I had to completely uninstall the nvidia drivers and manually reinstall and still have to set the monitor res to the setting higher on the list to get it to the right res "bug report filed"
  Why Oh why get rid of reconfigure xorg as it was so usefull.
 I have still not been able to get my sound system volume control to work correctly, without going back to alsa, not newbie friendly.
 I have also have had trouble trying to get it to recognize my network for file sharing and printing, I've solved it but again if i was new to linux i would have had no chance at all.
  It seems that all the distros are in a race to utilize the latest and greatest forgoing stability.( When i tried suse 9.4 pro /edgy/fedora7 etc they had greater stability). I had driver problems but not with stability, than the latest releases. (Excluding the xorg problems which are also problem on the other new releases.) 

Sorry if post seems slightly disjointed but my new Romanian "sparkys skivvy"  Has introduced me to his local beer:biggrin: and it's D****D good. Am almost tempted to give him a pay rise.
