### Post by arkhitekton on 2008-05-17
I've successfully installed Hardy Heron/Ubuntu 8.04 on Dell Inspiron 9100, Dell Latitude D510 and HP Compaq 6510b.  My major issue was with the distribution of Firefox-3.0 Beta.  I replaced it with Firefox-2.0 so I could install the extensions that I used previously - Foxmarks, McAfee SiteAdvisor, RealPlayer, Adobe Flash.  My thanks to those that have posted the instructions for sorting out Firefox - links are below.

The wireless worked "out-of-the-box" on the Latitude D510.  But I did not install the restricted wireless drivers on the Inspiron 9100.

In case it is of use to others my installation procedure is listed below:

I installed Ubuntu 8.04 LTS on Dell Inspiron 9100, Dell Latitude D510 and HP Compaq 6510b..  I installed directly off the Alternative CD, in Text Mode.

eth0, 10/100T, used as primary network i/f at time of install.

[Inspiron 9100] eth1, Broadcom a/b/g detected.  Did not install Restricted Drivers.

[Latitude D510] eth1, Intel wifi detected and automatically installed.  WPA worked when key supplied.

[Inspiron 9100]  Did NOT install Restricted Drivers for ATI graphics card.  Screen resolution & layout better without ATI drivers.

Soundcard working correctly.

[Inspiron 9100]  The Audigy 2ZS Notebook pcmcia soundcard was inserted and seems to have been detected automatically.

Removed Firefox-3.0 using instructions at:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4745743](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4745743)
"Firefox 3 removal from Ubuntu 8.04"
Installed Firefox-2.0
Update Preferred Applications, Internet tab, Web Browser with the Custom command "firefox-2 %s".

Added Foxmark Bookmark Sync addition to Mozilla Firefox by downloading from Firefox web site.  [Foxmarks not available for Firefox-3.0]
[http://www.foxmarks.com/](http://www.foxmarks.com/)

Added Macafee SiteAdvisor to Firefox.
[http://www.siteadvisor.com/](http://www.siteadvisor.com/)

Downloaded and install RealPlayer11GOLD for Linux.  Remember to run ./realplay as sudo to set up and configure player.
[http://www.real.com/dmm/superpass?pcode=cj&ocode=cj&cpath=aff&rsrc=1175270_10301041_14dLP](http://www.real.com/dmm/superpass?pcode=cj&ocode=cj&cpath=aff&rsrc=1175270_10301041_14dLP)

Installed Adobe Flash Player for Linux as sudo.
[http://www.adobe.com/shockwave/download/download.cgi?P1_Prod_Version=ShockwaveFlash&P2_Platform=Linux](http://www.adobe.com/shockwave/download/download.cgi?P1_Prod_Version=ShockwaveFlash&P2_Platform=Linux)

Firefox configuration at about:config
[B] browser.backspace_action 0
network.dns.disableIPv6 true[/B]

Installed w32codecs and libdvdcss2 for Medibuntu repositories according to instructions:
[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Medibuntu#head-b7483038fe4d8989bff6ac4ce3bf34060659b4ac](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Medibuntu#head-b7483038fe4d8989bff6ac4ce3bf34060659b4ac)

Installed mplayer media player:
** sudo apt-get install mplayer**

Installed Thunderbird mail client:
** sudo apt-get install thunderbird**
along with suggested items.

Installed partition editor:
** sudo apt-get install gparted**
along with suggested items.

Configured printing for Photosmart C3100 series on osx-imac20 using CUPS.

Installed networked HP 2500 Laserjet via System -> Administration -> Printing, Add Printer.

[9100]  Installed Canon BJC-8200.  Printer automatically detected. 

[9100]  Installed Epson 1200 U scanner.  Xsane automatically detected it and used it.

[Inspiron 9100]  Prolonging  battery life.  After installing the cpu frequecy scaling monitor  applet, it complained that the hardware did not support the cpu frequency function.  This was resolved by:
[B] sudo chmod +s /usr/bin/cpufreq-selector
sudo modprobe p4_clockmod
sudo apt-get install cpufrequtils
[/B] Consequently:
[B] cpufreq-info
[/B] reports information on processor(s).
Also inserted the line "p4_clockmod" at the end of /etc/modules.
Selected "ondemand" from list of governors.  Rebooted for new governor to take effect.

[Inspiron 9100] Installed Firestarter firewall GUI:
[B] sudo apt-get install firestarter
[/B]

### Post by Laser Dude on 2008-05-17
Ugh, this was a tough one.  Unfortunately, when I first installed Ubuntu, I only set aside 10GB.  So, I'm really running out of hard disk space.  When I tried to upgrade, I, of course, received an error that there wasn't enough hard-disk space.  So, after cleaning up apt, cleaning the trash, deleting .cache, removing files and programs I didn't need, I finally managed to scrape together enough disk space to upgrade.  Besides the previous, the upgrade process itself went rather smooth, although it did stop the installation a few times to tell me some things (like that it's removed my custom repos) that it shouldn't have.  These things usually slowed it down a lot, because I left the upgrade running overnight.

So, I get my new Hardy, and there's problems galore.  My screen resolution was changed from what it was previously, so I had to change it manually, and the login screen was stuck at a lower res.  My previously installed Emerald themes disappeared and reappeared randomly, the custom option in visual effects disappeared, so I couldn't turn my effects off temporarily for games (compiz has a known bug).  To top it all off, Audacity wouldn't work, VLC's sounds is incredibly choppy, and my Ubuntu partition was STILL super low on space.

At the point where I was having extreme difficulty doing things because I couldn't remove any more, I decided to redo things.  Windows Vista was refusing to give up any space with its shrink-drive (not-)feature.  As such, I did a full format, and started from scratch.  Luckily this was a perfect opportunity to install 64-bit, and hopefully have some of the bugs fixed.  I partitioned (easy), installed Vista again (easy), and installed Ubuntu again (easy).  Unfortunately, it wouldn't detect my screen resolution correctly, although installing Drivers fixed that.  Unfortunately, I still can't seem to find a way to add my removable hard-drive with my backup-data on it, nor the slew of i386 applications I was looking to install.  I was actually in the process of searching for the latter when I found this thread.  (:confused:)

### Post by Charlie Echo on 2008-05-18
I upgraded from 7.10 to Hardy (8.04), and went into slight problems.
(for information, I'm running the default AMD64 version, but I presume it has no impact on my problem).

I had installed Motion software (a software that enables to detect motion from webcams), and this software was by no way running when I was upgrading.

During the upgrade, Motion packages were probably upgraded, and when installing them, the software launched the motion detector (with details in the "installation window" : upgrading this and that packages, running motion, recording images...).
So it began to record moving images (me in front of my webcam :) ). After 30 seconds, as it was not stopping automatically, I had to use Ctrl-C to stop it.
Next packages' installations were fine, until some point later (in the configuration, I presume), where the upgrade software raised an error about Motion, and then closed itself with the message that I may be unable to reboot properly.

Luckily enough, the Motion upgrade was apparently one of the last ones in the process (or at least very late in the process), so I don't know if everything was well installed, but I could boot without any problem. And the packages database seems to have been updated, so it shows I'm running Hardy.

I couldn't have the "cleaning" step running (the one before "reboot").

Questions / suggestions:
- how can I be sure that all packages were installed? Is the database updated with every single package upgrade?
- does the "cleaning" phase only do a "apt-get clean", or does it remove other files?
- I suspect some other software may induce the same issue: automated launch is not the best solution when upgrading the system.
- moreover, I wonder what would have happened if the Motion package had been upgraded / configured in the middle of the pack, and not at the end... This means that relaunching all servers / deamons should be done at the last time, when sure that it won't break the system.

Thanks anyway for all, I really appreciate Ubuntu, the work done for this distribution, and the great quality of the result.

---

### Post by signseeker on 2008-05-18
I've been dabbling with gentoo for several years now with varying degrees of success/happiness. 

My system - Q6600 on an Asus Striker Extreme. Asus 8800GT 512MB, 2GB RAM, 250GB x 2 HDD, an old Soundblaster live 5.1. 

I recently added a spare Asus 8600GT (and swapped out a Realtek Ethernet card) for a second display. I then enabled one of the 2 onboard network ports (nVidia MCP55 I think)

My (then) current gentoo installation did not like it one bit, and I could not get the network card to work. X refused to start which really annoyed me. 

I tried installing debian using a netinst CD with no success (problem with nVidia ethernet) - and so I booted into windows (yeuck) and upgraded my bios. 

I then tried Xubuntu and everything just worked. (of course the BIOS update helped)

Am currently very impressed with Xubuntu.

### Post by Flashfox on 2008-05-18
** UPDATE **

My dual-boot XP SP3 / Ubuntu 8.04 is now up and running. 

What did I do? 

Nothing... except that yesterday I was "rebooting" my system between tries. Last night, I got fed up and shutdown the target computer. This morning when I powered-it up, Ubuntu completed the installation and it is now functional. I have access the Internet via the WiFi card and everything seems to be running (did not need new drivers).

I assume that performing a full shutdown (i.e. power off) did the trick as this is different than simply rebooting. 

FWIW, I did not need to edit the BOOT line. 

My "second system" --> Pentium 4, 2 GHz, 2 GB RAM, nVidia 6200SE video, Logitech EX-110 kbd & mouse, dual booting with Windows XP Home SP3.

### Post by meganox on 2008-05-19
> **igoddard said:**
> In order to run KDE, one terminal session and top 8.04 needs nearly 900M of a 1G laptop.  To do the same 6.06 needs less than 400M.  With these figures it's clear that I'll never be able to use VirtualBox without a memory upgrade.  For now I'll revert to 6.06 and investigate the possibility of swapping to another distro unless I hear of substantial fixes to reduce memory consumption.

top is showing you cached memory.  This memory is returned when an application asks for it.  Hardy is NOT eating all your RAM.

### Post by messelink on 2008-05-19
The upgrade itself went relatively smooth, however there were some problems:

SOLVED:
- ppscsi-beta2 module did not compile under Hardy (package itself has been broken since at least Gutsy) due to changes in the way teh kernel handles scatterlists. SOLUTION: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=782242](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=782242) .

UNSOLVED:
- .config of my old custom kernel together with a make oldconfig with all the default settings for the new options did not compile to a usable kernel for me. SOLUTION: Review all options in more detail.
- The TorrentFlux package (a WebGUI to BitTornado) crashes the box. Installing the latest Debian TorrentFlux and BitTornado packages does not solve the problem. The Transmission bittorrent client works flawlessly.  SOLUTION: unknown.

### Post by EoRaptor013 on 2008-05-19
A tale of two computers. 

Desktop, a brand-new custom built desktop. ASUS P5K-E Wifi mobo with onboard J-Micron raid controller, Intel q6600 quad, nvidia 8600GTS, two WD Raptor 250Gb drives, and Samsung DVD+-RW. 

Since this machine is for my mother (79 years old!) to host a community-service organization web site, I went with 32 bit Hardy with WinXP in KVM. The RAID was a royal pain! When I set up a RAID 0 with the J-Micron controller, Linux still saw them as two separate drives, went through the whole install with no apparent problem, then bombed on the reboot -- I guess because it didn't know where it was. When I tried to use the HW RAID, but also set up sofware RAID in Linux, install bombs. Only recourse was to break the HW RAID and go strictly with software RAID -- which simply isn't as fast as the HW raid. (Don't get on my case about the RAID 0, we got backups covered and were looking for good capacity with blazing I/O.) 

With the 32 bit config, and SW raid, installation went well. With the usual wireless configuring, everything worked fine. And then I tried to install the restricted nVidia drivers. I guess my brain wasn't working, so it didn't occur to me until MUCH later that I needed to use the Source Manager to include the Hardy restricted repository. This is one place where a single quick reminder -- or enabling the restricted repository by default -- would have saved hours. Indeed, why isn't the restricted repository enabled by default? In the alternative, the restricted drivers manager could use some improvement in error messages. With that simple checkmark in Source Manager, nvidia drivers installed quickly and easily and all is well. 

Second Computer.
Dell E1705 Laptop. Dual core T2500 processor, nVidia 7600 graphics, _Intel 3945 wireless_, 2gigs RAM, 250 gig HD. 
WHAT a pain! First, I tried to install the 64bit Hardy. Researched here and determined that I needed the alternate CD. OK, got that, and tried 64bit again. Click enter at the language prompt, click on install, and immediately get a message that it needs a 64bit processor. Well, I gots a frakking 64bit processor and the bios claims the 64bit instruction set is enabled. Hardy doesn't care. So, back to 32bit. Installation completed without problems.

Having learned from the Desktop, nVidia driver installation also went without a hitch. I noticed, however, that Hardy doesn't include a restricted driver for the Intel 3945 wireless like Gutsy did, and wireless wasn't working. I read through the release notes but didn't notice anything about the Intel wireless so I did some research here and Googling. Found a nice handy-dandy howto, for a different Dell model, using ndiswrapper; so decided to try that, with appropriate changes to reflect different wireless. Seemed to work fine, brought up wireless, and connected to Internet with unplugged network cable. And then I rebooted... Kernel panic! Rebooted with CD, using fix option, and removed the reference to ndiswrapper from /etc/modules. Machine came back up but still no wireless. After a lot more looking around here, I finally see that the 3945 is supposed to be supported natively. But, I still haven't gotten it working. (BTW, any help with this, and the 64bit install, would be appreciated.)

Other than that(!), all is right with the world. And, I'm still committed, albeit also committable. 

EoRaptor

Upgraded 7.10 to 8.04 on IBM ThinkPad T42 with SXGA+, Radeon 9600, Fingerprint, etc.

No problems during the update. Everything works except visual effects. I get white screen when I try to enable them. 

[COLOR="Silver"]I know it's Mesa messing around, but I can't make any other video driver work, I tried everything, every guide Google can supply. All the steps complete without errors but the result is still the same - 3D rendering performed by Mesa.

Thinking to install fresh 8.04.[/COLOR]

Problem SOLVED by remarking "blacklist fglrx" line in /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist-local. Seems like I have previously overlooked this step.

---

### Post by linux4me on 2008-05-21
Overall, I am VERY pleased and more impressed than ever with Ubuntu's developers.

The upgrade went smoothly for me. I only had a few things to fix after the installtion:
[LIST=1]
[*]Had to reinstall the driver for my Intel 536 modem. Just followed the instructions here on the forums for that got i working in no time. I've had to do that with every distro upgrade, so it's not specific to Hardy.
[*]I have an Intellimouse Explorer (PS2) that worked fine in Fiesty and Gutsy with some modifications in /etc/X11/xorg.conf. The thumb buttons didn't work after upgrading to Hardy, but here on the forums I was told to comment out the changes in xorg.conf that were necessary for previous versions and to install imwheel and that got it all working.
[*]I had to download and re-install (over the previous version) the Innotek version of Virtual Box. Now it works, too.
[/LIST]

Hardy starts faster, seems to open programs faster, and just LOOKS better. The rendering of text seems to be sharper. I haven't had any trouble with Firefox 3 beta; it seems to run much faster than v. 2, and it has some really nice features. 

The only downside I have found doesn't relate to Ubuntu at all: there's no Firebug for version 3.0 of Firefox yet, and I am pining for it.

### Post by stephenb on 2008-05-21
This is my result after an upgrade path of Feisty > Gutsy > Hardy

I've listed these problems over at the 64 bit forum, but I'm listing them here again for people that wouldn't look there and because all this is just as relevant in this forum.[U]

SEGFAULT ERRORS DURING BOOT UP
[/U][INDENT]     nautilus[26679]: segfault at 43000034 rip 3987c23f0f rsp 7fffffbbdf90 error 4

    gnome-keyring-d[5943]: segfault at 60 rip 60 rsp 7fff9eba8908 error 14

    firegl1.isse.a0[5409]: segfault at c rip 4a6dcf39 rsp ff98aeb0 error 4

    gdm[5531]: segfault at 392e520 rip 397e22b55a rsp 7fffc30db6e0 error 4
[/INDENT]I'm not sure what these mean and they don't seem critical because most things seem to be working. Launchpad had something about the keyring, so that could be fixed soon. 

_WINE IS COMPLETELY BROKEN_

I get this with the last two versions as well as the earlier 0.9.54[INDENT]wine[20049]: segfault at 4a6c3f68 rip 4a6c3f68 rsp ffecaeac error 15
[/INDENT]_SKYPE (the non-static variant) IS BROKEN_

I didn't hold out much hope anyway when I'd heard there was a 64 bit version.[INDENT]     skype[24687]: segfault at 0 rip 0 rsp ffdcceb0 error 14
[/INDENT][U]F-SPOT WOULDN'T RUN

[/U]This has recently been fixed in a proposed update.[U]

STELLARIUM RUNS LIKE MOLASSES

[/U]The only way I could solve this was to uninstall xgl-server[U]

NETWORKING IS UNSTABLE [/U]

Often when I select Places > Network nothing comes up. Then after a while I'll get a popup with this message:[INDENT]Couldn't display "network:///".

Error: DBus error org.freedesktop.DBus.Error.NoReply: Did not receive a reply. Possible causes include: the remote application did not send a reply, the message bus security policy blocked the reply, the reply timeout expired, or the network connection was broken. Please select another viewer and try again.
[/INDENT]Sometimes selecting Places > Network works. It shows my workgroup, however, it cannot display anything within the workgroup, as it used to. Sometimes it just processes, finding nothing, until I hit the reload button. Then I can see my machine but nothing else. 

_BACKGROUNDS AND EMBLEMS HAS A BUG_

Unless I use the "View as list" option I get crazy background patterns that make folder names illegible. I have to add another background to read them or view everything as a list in every window.

[U]NSPLUGINWRAPPER NO LONGER WORKS

[/U]nspluginwrapper: no appropriate viewer found for /usr/lib/mozilla/plugins/libflashplayer.so

So, I can't install flash for Firefox.

_NFS WON'T MOUNT_

Internal error when I attempt to mount with NFS. At the command line I get this:[INDENT]mount.nfs: internal error
[/INDENT]If attempting to mount via fstab, I get this:[INDENT]rpcbind: server inspiron not responding, timed out
[/INDENT]_HAL_

Occasionally, I get this, something I don't think I've seen since 2006:[INDENT]HAL failed to initialize.

[/INDENT]NOTE: some of these have been reported as bugs and fixes may be on the way.

### Post by briggella on 2008-05-22
Installed Hardy on 2 machines. I started with a beta on a dual core athlon 4200 with nvidia 6100 graphics card. Steadily ran updates to current day. Nice and speedy but no access to virtual consoles. This is not the corrupted video problem, this is when I hit Ctrl-Fn and it does absolutely nothing. If I exit the X server with Ctrl-Alt-backspace, I can access them if I am quick. Is this an issue with the keyboard layouts. I have played with it quite a bit with no success.

I have also installed production version onto an IBM thinkpad A31p. Virtual consoles worked in this initially but now after updating it, they have developed the same problem as the other machine. This is enough to make me stop upgrading my other machines as to date there is no solution on google or ubuntuforums, yet there are many people with the problem.


Display problems. With the above specs on both machines, I was only able to get 800x600 as my best resolution on install. I had to open a console, and enter sudo displayconfig-gtk to get a configuration utility that I knew about but wasn't on any menu. This enabled me to add the LCD monitor and up the resolution to 1024 x 768. Took me about a couple of minutes as I knew what I was doing. A new user will just get the XP disks out and reinstall. This used to work well. Why is it now broken.

I have had problems with network printing. I have enabled Listening to port 631 and seen the network printers, but all print options are greyed out in Gnome-cups-manager and the more recent print system. Eventually I worked out that you have to have IP of the host server in /etc/hosts. Not very intuitive  as all you had was a series of greyed out boxes and a cryptic error message in /var/log/cups/error_log. An on screen error or prompt to add an entry to hosts would have been helpful. This has caught me out before and I have spent many hours trying to deal with it. A new user wouldn't have a chance. Why don't we fix it. All it needs is a dialogue box to add the host server IP into.

Overall a nice distro with a lot more tools but still some glaring issues to trap new users. The Virtual consoles is a deal breaker.

---

### Post by Robsteranium on 2008-05-22
> **ThomasWM said:**
> Upgrade worked well.
Only problem I've found is that my bank's website refuses FF3 and I had to load FF2.


I can't be bothered to read all 103 pages of the upgrade thread to find out if ThomasWM got this workaround so sorry for any repetition:
Get the User-Agent Switcher add-on for FF and pretend that you're coming from IE Microsoft Vista.  If your bank is the same as mine, which I'm guessing it is, then it's only a user-agent check and not a real incompatibility issue.  You'll be able to log-in no problems.

The update [lost my FF3 icon on the xfce panel]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/firefox-3.0/+bug/232506") and I'm having to rebuild all the "open with" options for some reason!

I'm also having spontaneous shutdowns when I leave the computer running - perhaps this has something to do with power management/ screensaver.

### Post by luke_lukem on 2008-05-22
Hi sveinung,
The most likely bug when activating the driver is that it tries to use the maximum resolution possible, and you can solve it just like mcgrvyb did, if you can't switch to a usabe resolution with a few Ctrl+Alt+NumPlus combination hits
---------------------------------------
The login window problem was fixed by editing /etc/X11/xorg.conf. I changed the virtual setting from 1920 1240 to 1440 900. Everything works now.
Thanks to whoever posted that suggestion.
-----------------------------------------

### Post by grikdog on 2008-05-22
I have a Dell Inspiron 1525 and two other family members who prefer Windows Vista SP 1.  I wanted Ubuntu, especially since Hardy Heron had Wubi.

The (non-)partition and install went pretty much as advertised.  I was pleased.  I haven't actually seen Linux since my last Redhat install (and a hoopy YDL thingie on one of my Macintoshes that I scrubbed off fast), so it was kind of cool to see Ubuntu recognize my hardware without asking me to doing anything with drivers.  (I haven't tested wireless, yet.  Ethernet was up and running as soon as I plugged it in.)  ESPECIALLY, my trackpad works, something I was not expecting, and my USB wireless mouse from Microsoft works.

The problems came later.  I decided Wubi was a wimpout, and what I really wanted to do was partition the hd and install the real thing live.  All this, again, went well -- except that now my users are confronted with GRUB, and they want Windows Vista to boot without asking ANYBODY's permission!  Emphasis: !

It took me awhile to find qgrubeditor on the Web.  When I got this dangerous bad boy running, I found myself staring at a way to edit my one and only Master Boot Record without any handholding dialogs whatsoever.  Fun.  I felt like swallowing a few razor blades, just to get myself in the right Zen-like mindset.  One very technically easy command to select Windows and slide it to the top of the list, then OUT.  QUIT.  DONE.  I forget how I did it.  Your mileage may vary, and probably will, gods of Linux help you.

There's almost certainly a better way to do this.  I wonder what it is.  I wonder what I coulda/shoulda done in the partitioning step to avoid the terror...:lolflag:

Aside from that one LITTLE thing, no hay de que.

---

### Post by DarKnyht on 2008-05-22
The upgrade when fairly smoothly, but then it was more or less a fresh 7.10 install moved to 8.04 on a new laptop. I found myself a little disappointed in my first LTS experience (started with 6.10).

My problems began once I started customizing the desktop experience.  I quickly found that PulseAudio was apparently rushed into use with a show stopper conflict, that Firefox 3 Beta 5 is just as unstable on Linux as windows (google crashes the browser regularly), and Avant Window Manager was released in a condition less than stable.

Fortunately, I was able to locate other sites and places even here that walked me through enabling more newer versions of the above (except Firefox, which I rolled backwards).  The negative is that I find myself forced to run a lot of beta software.

I think next time, it would be well worth the Ubuntu team's time to delay a release than to release with so many show stopping bugs.  I also believe that perhaps it would be wiser next time to release a LTS with fully tested and proven packages than to try for a flash that just makes the distro look bad.  Save that for the other releases where stability is not the selling point.

### Post by kc8hr on 2008-05-23
> **kc8hr said:**
> I had a perfectly good, working Gutsy install. Then I tried an upgrade. It failed. So I decided to re-install with Hardy.

Now I cannot even delete my own file:

rm: cannot remove `/home/tim/.fluxbox/.gkrellm2/themes/3051/meter_labels.png': Permission denied

What the hell?


Tim

Well, I managed to get everything fixed.

Video: I have an antique TNT2 card that can use the legacy driver. I tried installing it from the repositories, but no go--messy screen. Found the EnvyNG script from here: [http://albertomilone.com/nvidia_scripts1.html]("http://albertomilone.com/nvidia_scripts1.html"). Ran it three times before I got a usable screen, but found that it was a virtual screen--1600x1200. Edited the /etc/X11/xorg.conf file, found a virtual screen entry and changed it to 1024x768, and now things are back to normal.

Permissions: Switched into Gnome--I prefer Fluxbox--to run a tool that controls permissions. The tool does not appear in the Fluxbox menu. Finally got control of the permissions in my own /home/tim/. . .

It was a struggle, but I finally have my system back to working as well as it did before I decided to upgrade.

Except for Firefox 3, of course!:lolflag:

### Post by khanoftartary on 2008-05-24
To be honest, I wasn't entirely satisfied with the upgrade. Most everything went smoothly, but I have one of the (infamous) broadcom chipset network cards, which have always been painful in Ubuntu. The upgrade supposedly had new drivers to use the card, but they failed horribly, and I was forced to write a new init script that disabled the built in Hardy drivers and started up NDISwrapper (the program I had used in Gutsy, which, by the way, I've always loved and has worked flawlessly). 

The other thing is that I'm now forced to use Azareus(sp?) on torrent downloads. The torrent downloader that came with the upgrade, ironically enough, does not work, and it disabled my favorite program: bittornado. Azareus is the only one I've found that works with Hardy, and it's not that it's dysfunctional, but it uses up far more memory than tornado did. On a scale of 10, I give the new system a 9, but the upgrade process only gets a 6.

---

### Post by kwilliam on 2008-05-24
Near flawless upgrade Kubuntu Gutsy -> Kubuntu Hardy. The only problems were caused by timidity, which borked due to the new ALSA not liking some bluetooth devices I'd manually added to my old config file (I understand there's new better ways to configure bluetooth audio now) and virtualbox-ose. The Adept Upgrader froze on me at the very end, but running ```
$ sudo apt-get remove timidity virtualbox-ose
$ sudo apt-get install -f
``` took care of that, and upon reboot...

Edit: **(Almost) EVERY THING WORKS! Sound, Bluetooth, X, Flash, USB**... but Suspend to RAM is now broken, which sucks not having suspend on a laptop. Aw well, suspend was fairly flakey before. I must figure out how to make suspend work on my machine.

Aside from that, an absolutely lovely upgrade. Just those two package conflicts, and nothing broken except suspend, which is traditionally hit or miss anyway on Linux. Congrats to the Ubuntu Team.

### Post by lsutiger on 2008-05-24
I had a cloned hard disk to test this out, thankfully.
Loading screen is askew ( the ubuntu logo is large and to the right of the screen )
My sound broke - fixed that with a tut on linuxforums.org
Bottom panel is just a white strip until I change a property
Lost all of my plugins for Firefox when I downgraded to 2.0.0.14(FF3 is way too processor intensive, buggy+beta, and does not support my favorite plugins). I can see them in the add-ons, but I can not enable
Surprisingly, the broadcomm wireless driver worked :)
Virtualbox is broke. The error log states that I need to recompile the linux kernel!?!???? (If I uninstall and reinstall, will it recognize my vm's I have installed???)
Lost the back and forward buttons in firefox - easy fix under view-->toolbars-->customize

All in all I haven't seen enough improvements in order to upgrade. I would give it a  6 out of 10, though I have/will not be playing with it much.

### Post by phillywize on 2008-05-25
I upgraded (over the update manager...what is it, "dist-upgrade?").

Pretty much everything was fine...nothing major...EXCEPT (and this is a show stopper) -- nvidia proprietary support was completely broken.  I was thrown back on the gnu drivers, which, while dependable, don't fully exploit the gpu's capabilities.

Turns out, this is a pretty common problem.

[SIZE="6"][COLOR="Red"]So just what the hell were they thinking releasing an LTS with a serious nvidia shortcoming?[/COLOR][/SIZE]

It's not as if nvidia drivers are uncommon.  This is stupidity for ubuntu/canonical from two standpoints:  to the extent it's supposed to be an accessible distro for the common person, it's a setback since normal people won't be mucking around in their xorg.conf's, with a printout from the bugzilla clenched in one hand, and furiously using the other to navigate to the download link for the beta (yes, BETA!) nvidia driver that works...ON THE NVIDIA WEBSITE!  Yup. Have to go completely outside the safe world of apt for that one.

And then, to the extent Ubuntu is supposed to be something that can be used as an OS for large numbers of machines in an institutional setting...welp, the IT people aren't going to want to hack new drivers onto every machine.

What a drag.  I fixed it, but that's just me.  I'm obsessive and tracked down the answer.

But I think this is the most disappointing experience I've had with Ubuntu.  After I switched over from Fedora, I was amazed by how well Ubuntu worked, and what promise it had for greater linux penetration.  But too many gaffs like this, and forget about it.

[SIZE="7"][COLOR="DarkOrchid"]AAAAAAAAAAGHGHGH![/COLOR][/SIZE]
](*,)

Can you tell?  I seriously want to take the name of Ubuntu CE in vain.

---

### Post by poonla on 2008-05-25
I just installed 8.04 yesterday on an old Dell Latitude D505 with 256MB of RAM, a 1.5Ghz Celeron M processor, and a 30GB hard drive. It was my first time to install Ubuntu, and I ran into a few problems.

My first problem was that the graphical installer CD did not work.  I left it running for several hours, and it just sat there with that orange screen and the chupacabra-like monster while the CD whirred and whined.  I verified the CD before the install and it passed.  I tried the graphical installer twice, same results both times.  

I downloaded the text-based installer CD, and that one worked much better.  That is, it finished and didn't hang.  The first time I ran it I selected the first disk formatting option, I forget what it said but it was something about a SCSI partition and automatic partitioning.  I got all the way through installation, but couldn't logon until I switched to a plain x terminal login.  Then I discovered accidentally by issuing the DF commmand that my drive had been partitioned such that it had 0% free space.  

So I started over with the text-based install, and this time I selected the 2nd disk partitioning option, which was one of the guided options.  I made it through that, and all was fine.  After installation I was able to logon.  

I used a wired internet connection for the install.  But I had an Intel Pro 2915abg wireless card that I'd recently purchased for this laptop, so I installed it and tried to get wireless to work.  That was difficult.  The Network configurator tool in the System Administration menu wouldn't work.  It kept losing my pre-shared key.  Finally, with the help of a little tutorial somewhere on the web (it was specifically for Ubuntu), I manually edited /etc/network/interfaces and built the /etc/wpa_supplicant.conf file, and then I was able to get wireless networking to start up.

My system seemed very slow, much slower than the old Windows XP system that used to be on this laptop, and that seemed odd to me.  In the process of looking for any hints to improve performance, I came across the posts in the hardware forum about the HDD head-parking problem.  I tested and found that my hard drive head parks itself frequently, so I installed the 99-hdd-ugly-fix.sh script and that made a noticeable improvement in system responsiveness.  But it is still too slow.  I am considering upgrading the memory to 1GB.  

Another problem I currently have is that Firefox browser hangs when I tried to install the Adobe Flash plugin from the plugin installer that pops up when I go to a site that has Flash.  I tried it several times.  

Also, I can't start the Synaptic Package Manager or the Software Sources applications from the System Administration window.  They try to start, and there is a tab at the bottom of the screen for a bit, then they just disappear.  

But overall, I think Ubuntu is pretty slick.  I just have to continue working on these remaining problems.

---

### Post by tibellus on 2008-05-25
Hello. This is the second time I upgrade from 7.10 to 8.04. First time, I had a completely functional system, with lots and lots of documents, music, apps and even some small software I created. I had an itch, and I had to scratch it: I upgraded to 8.04. Big disappointment! First time, it brought my system into an utterly unfunctional state, by borking everything that stood in Hardy Heron's way. So, after the upgrade, I had the same software sources (those for Gutsy), a different wallpaper and no NVidia drivers. The nastiest thing was the fact that before I got into the graphical mode, I only had text mode, as Ubuntu didn't know what drivers to use.

As always, when problems tend to chain up, my DVD-writer started to act weird (I could not write anything at all) and I had no Ubuntu 8.04 ISOs or CDs at my disposal. Sooooo I installed Ubuntu 7.10, completely formatting my partitions. After this, I thought that an update from a clean, basic distro would work fine. I waited for it to update, and at a moment, I got some errors in the terminal, referring to blackjack. Here's what I got:

```
///usr/share/gnome/help/blackjack/blackjack.xml:402:parser error Entity '&#914;&#959;&#942;&#952;&#949;&#943;&#945;' not defined
      <para><guimenuitem>&#928;&#961;&#959;&#964;&#953;&#956;&#942;&#963;&#949;&#943;&#949;&&#914;&#959;&#942;&#952;&#949;&#943;&#945;; </gui
```

Did someone else encounter this error? I believe it is something related to a help file, and I'm curious if this could create some problems in the future. (Sorry if I misspelled some of the Greek characters, I wrote the message from a note I took when the system was updating).

### Post by djrakun on 2008-05-25
Upgrading my PCs from Gutsy to Hardy has been unpredictable and troublesome.  I am making progress on my work pc, which is a Dell Latitude but I can't forget the model number ( about three years old and has a pentium 4 proc)  with the help of many forums describing the same issue that I've had.  Hardy apparently changed the permissions of the home drives of my users, which results in errors on startup about the $HOME/.dmrc file being locked.  A chmod to 644 lifted the error message on startup but some applications that I have installed previously ( particularly wine) fail when I run them from the terminal reporting that the file is not owned by me, even with sudo.  Anyway, making progress with wine specifically.  I have another bug that appeared in hardy where wine is unable to find a starting point for dosmem after upgrading to Hardy. Upgraded to wine 1.0 RC1 and that fixed that bug.

My home pc is a fujitsu lifebook t4010.  The stylus doesn't work after the upgrade.  It looks like this has already been reported on different models of Fujitsu-Siemens laptops so hopefully this will be resolved in haste, otherwise I might revert this pc.

The only fresh install I did was on another Dell Latitude at work, which is a D600 and has a core2 duo cpu.  I have this dual booting Vista and Hardy via Grub.  Vista works well on it ( except that it is a mem hog and a disk space killer and well, if you're on this forum you know the rest).  The Hardy OS however shuts down the pc randomly throughout the day.  I started it up and just after startup, with no apps running but a terminal the cpu temperature was 52 degrees C, which seems pretty hot for idle, IMO.  I'm searching around for like-problems with core2duos and ubuntu and saw one post describing how to kick your fans into high gear constantly, which honestly is over my head at the mo, but I'm learning quickly.

I started Ubuntu at the near end of Feisty, and on day 1 of the version release I upgraded to Gutsy.  Everything I did on Feisty remained intact and Gutsy just improved my performance dramatically.  I was so impressed with the upgrade process in Ubuntu I didn't think much would go wrong when upgrading to Hardy.  I know better now.  My next machine will have a partition for the OS only, so I can blow it away when these pesky quirks arise.

If you're on Gutsy still, I would suggest reading the forums carefully and look for your model.  Hardy just isn't ready yet.

### Post by Hopworks on 2008-05-25
Reinstall from Feisty install (my first Ubuntu)

I really was unable to play with Hardy very much as I can't seem to get past my nVidia driver issues. I've bounced around a few different flavors, finally agreeing on Ubuntu 8.04 Server (LAMP, SAMBA) then installing the gnome desktop (apt-get install ubuntu-desktop).

But I couldn't detect my monitor (Dell 2208WFP) and had a mess of a time getting my resolution to 1680x1050. After all that, I magically got a dialog box telling me about the nvidia restricted driver. I enabled it, let it do its thing, then it said a reboot was necessary (in the column showing the driver with the checkbox). After a reboot however, everything went downhill again.

I've worked fixes all day, the last one was here
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/xorg/+bug/173418]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/xorg/+bug/173418")
unfortunately, after I was finally successful shutting down the xserver (it would just hang after a few lines of text), I ran the recommended driver file I got from nvidia, and it refused to install, telling me I'm building for a XEN kernel. ???

So with my google search results exhausted, I guess I'm going to step back a bit to gutsy and see what happens, at least until I can find a solution that works, that will give me 3D acceleration at 1680x1050 again.

EDIT: Did a full fresh install of Gutsy Server 7.10, and after very little effort my video card is performing well, and accelerated at 1680x1050. The Gnome GUI seemed to load faster even before I updated the drivers from 2D performance. So it must my hardware falling into a small percentage of 'Hardy' gutsy people that have nvidia driver issues, but I'm sure after a little time the issues will be fixed. I'll try again in a few months.

### Post by lone13angel on 2008-05-26
Well, I'll admit I've been a windows xp fan for years.
however, recently, their customer/tech support has sucked, and I had decided to try a linux based system.
I ran across ubuntu. 
So, obviously, I am completely new to this. 
This is my experience.

my current system:
pentium 4 1.6 ghz
640 mb of ram.

1. I read thruogh the site, Downloaded the ISO, and burned it to a cd.
2. On a fresh/clean hard drive, I booted from the CD for installation.

here are my problems.
1 - it took me nearly 20 minutes to figure out how to do anything considering my ps2 mouse wasn't recognized at all, and there were no explanations for new users for strictly using keyboard commands.
2 - after fighting with that (btw...I've yet to solve this problem with my mouse. Its a standard 2 button with a wheel ps2 mouse manufactured by compaq. easily recognized by windows xp... but not ubunto....) I attempted to install.

NOTE: if you press control + alt + delete, the pop up box will appear for logging off, shutting down, etc... simply press "cancel" and this will allow you to access the two options on the live cd desktop "examples" and "install"....
unfortunately, that's all you will be able to access considering the MOUSE does NOT work.

I entered the installation screen.
language, region, keyboard (woah. still nothing about mouse...okay....irritating...) and then finally the partioning section.

...at that point, I decided to terminate my first attempt. put my hard-drive that still had winxp installed on it, and went back to the website to read some more.

tried it again.
i/o error with the cd drive. $#%@$$#5

tried it again.
success. finished. reboot. no operating system detected. $#@%#$
swapped hd's again, ran windows xp (ironically) to find out wtf was going on with this unbuntu thing.
read through it. okay. 
just to be sure... downloaded it AGAIN, hardy heron here we go, burnt it AGAIN (waste another cd...) and tried it again.

still, mouse problems, and the installation crashes half way through.

I'm about to attempt my FIFTH installation... however, I think I'm almost completely sick of wasting my time. 
still, extremely irritated that the installation has no mouse interface.
I've read the forums... never dealing with linux before, I can't find anything that actually constitutes as a simple "answer"... a lot of theories, excuses, and redundant replies, however no answers to this problem.

and there are a LOT of posts about problems with ps2 mice.
a lot of USB solutions, however, nothing that explains why the simple to use installation cd continually fails to do what its supposed to...
and... well, maybey I'm extremely misled here, but I thought that the installation was supposed to be user friendly, and very stable.

unfortunately, this has absolutely NOT been the case.
and, i still don't see why I've got to use windows xp to be able to solve problems to an operating system that originally I wanted to use to REPLACE windows xp with permanantly.

and, considering that's what I had to run to be able to get to the forum to be able to get an answer...
why am I wasting my time trying to fix an o/s that won't install or work when I've got one that, despite my irritation with it...
ACTUALLY WORKS.

????

I'm sure I am not the only one who's encountered these problems, unless the forum moderators filter posts to be able to create a positive bias for this unbunto whatever... 
I'm very sorry that this installation process didn't work.
I really had my hopes up with all the hype.
only to find out... it...doesn't...work...at all.
??????

now, I'm going to attempt to install "inside of windows"...
(which I think is retarded...I should only need ONE operating system....) to see if it even works or not.

I'm not going to get my hopes up at this point.

### Post by jpkotta on 2008-05-26
I did a clean install on one machine and an upgrade on another.  A third will get upgraded eventually.

I'm kind of disappointed with how long this problem was known, how easy and unobtrusive the fix is, and how it still not a regular update.  Sure, it's going to get pushed soon, but it's causing problems all over the place.  Those problems I heard about made me wait this long to get Hardy in the first place; looks like I should have waited longer.

No other major regressions.  No trouble with installations/upgrades.

### Post by mooz123 on 2008-05-26
updating was very easy, I could continue using my computer without much trouble.

I had an irritating issue where my desktop background went black and dead, it did not respond on commands. I discovered that logging out and in solved it. Amazingly, the problem slowly disappeared: my desktop came up immediately more and more often, and now the problem seems gone.
It surprises me always when computers display seemingly random behaviour, ones and zeroes do not allow for it. However, the level of complexity we have reached can give erratic behaviour, because not one startup is now similar to another one. Still, it amazes me how the problem gradually disappeared, as if there was a learning ability involved.

I also have problems getting hibernation to work properly, but I think I inherited that from the last version.

:KS Otherwise I feel like a fish in the water with Ubuntu, I am grateful to the whole community of people that has shared knowledge, time and skills in order to deliver this incredible set of software free of charge! I can only hope to find a way to contribute as much as I benefit.

---

### Post by robeec on 2008-05-26
i think i just fixed the prob: i quit the updater installer, ran synaptic, under 'edit' dragged down to 'add cd-rom', inserted cd. error messages went away.

i suppose i could have had also just unchecked 'cd-rom hardy heron' in the 'repositories' under 'settings' in synaptic, right? 

thanks, good ppl!
robby

### Post by starcannon on 2008-05-26
I keep /home on its own separate partition, this makes install upgrading painless.

### Post by drewan on 2008-05-26
New install on dual core athlon machine, networked to a Linux & Solaris environment. And a simple install on home PC.

1.) autofs5 failed to mount exports because it does not understand nobrowse option. Workaround is to create custom auto.master

2.) NIS failed to bind because HAL service was not started yet. Moved hal symlink before nis symlink as fix.

3.) fast user switcher applet hangs the login session when NIS is enabled. Had to kill/remove the process from the user's desktop.

Oh, the molecule screensaver on my PIII at home hangs the systems. No ctl-alt-Fn console, desktop non-responsive. Was able to reboot via ssh login.

Annoying inconsistencies between the two installs. One example; Applications menu has Add/Remove on one machine only.

This is sad - spent several days debugging all of this :(

---

### Post by Crank-N-Jam on 2008-05-27
Two upgrades:

First was my Dell Inspiron 2200 laptop.  Upgrade from Gutsy to Hardy.  Worked flawlessly.  I've always had to fight to get the darn broadcom wireless working in it (Edgy (never worked) to Feisty to Gutsy).  The upgrade didn't break it.  It loaded the proper video drivers as well.  Smooth sailing.

So I wait a month and update my main home PC.  The update itself went well.  Problem was it borked my NVIDIA drivers and my audio drivers.  System came up 800x600 and I tried everything to get them going.  No dice.  Ended up doing a fresh install from CD, however, and it worked right off the bat.  My audio now works in Ardour, but I get no playback from anything else (I'm using a M-Audio Delta 1010LT).  Still playing around with that.  I'm assuming the video (and possibly audio) issue was kernel related.

So all-in-all, not a horrible experience, but I am getting tired of backing up my files (180Gg worth) just so I can do a fresh install.  Course that also calls for reinstalling all of my favorite software as well.  I really should have created a separate partition this time for my home directory, come to think of it.  Oh well, next upgrade.  :)

I do appreciate all of the hard work the Ubuntu team is doing though!!!  Keep it up!  You've gotten this computer geek off of Windows for good!

Jason

### Post by alguin2 on 2008-05-27
I bought an HP a6400f (came with 3GbRAM, Intel E2200 Dual-Core 2.2GHz processor, and 500Gb disk -- a bargain @ $560 from WalMart), with the intention of installing Linux.

I created and tried the latest Live CDs of the following distros:
- Debian
- Mandriva
- Gentoo
- OpenSuse
- Fedora
- Knoppix
- Ubuntu  32 & 64 bit (the Intel E2200 is a 64-bit CPU). 

The good:
- The 32-bit Intel Live CD for all distros that I tried booted successfully. 

The bad:
- The 64 bit Ubuntu Hardy Heron 8.04 Live CD sometimes caused my machine to hang with a dark screen.  I suspect some problem with support for the on-board graphics, because booting in safe graphics mode was successful.  So was a **"powerdown/unplug/wait 20 secs"** reboot cycle. 

- IN ALL CASES, there was no internet access. From the Ubuntu forums, it was apparent that the network hardware (Realtek RTL8168C/RTL8111C) had an incorrect driver.  I was able to get the 32bit Ubuntu Live CD to bring up the network after a **"powerdown/unplug/wait 20 secs"** reboot cycle.  But this was not a good solution. 

After mulling it through, and after doing real installs of Ubuntu, Debian, and Knoppix, I re-formatted/re-installed one final time and settled back to 32 bit Ubuntu 8.04. (I cut the 500 Gig disk in half, creating a dual boot disk. 1/2 to Vista, and 1/2 to Ubuntu).

Now to get the network working.  I downloaded the latest driver from [http://www.realtek.com.tw]("http://www.realtek.com.tw/downloads/downloadsView.aspx?Langid=1&PNid=13&PFid=5&Level=5&Conn=4&DownTypeID=3&GetDown=false#2") and tried to re-compile and install the new module.  I found myself in a Chicken/Egg problem: compiling/installing the new driver required a download of the kernel build package, which required the network to be up, but I need to compile/install the new driver in order to do that... etc.

Finally, I found two postings that gave the solution:
  [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=5017262](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=5017262) and
  [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=5002506](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=5002506)

Ubuntu 8.04 (kernel 2.6.24-16-generic) re-booted and was able to bring up the network (ie. after minor adjustment to enable DHCP).

 [B][U][SIZE="5"]
But wait!  That was not the end of it![/SIZE][/U][/B]

So,,,, Being happy with my network-enabed Ubuntu Hardy Heron 8.04 Linux box, I ran synaptic package manager, and upgraded to the latest and greatest of everything.  

This was a bit of a mistake, because the latest kernel for 8.04 (2.6.24-17-generic) was using the old, incorrect NIC driver.  I lost the network once more.  Luckily, the driver build/install works for both 2.6.24-16 and 2.6.24-17.  

After one final build and install of the RealTek driver, my system is now happily humming with the latest distro.   I'll hold off on any more kernel updates until I know that support for the RealTek RTL8168C/RTL8111C is inherently part of the update.

In the end, I'm actually glad to have gone this route, because I now have experience re-compiling/installing a new driver, and experience on how to install packages even with a non-working network.


Cheers. :)

---

### Post by variona on 2008-05-28

### Post by ScottyP on 2008-05-29
First problems I've had with Ubuntu came with Hardy.

Downside - Synaptic wouldn't start and Update Manager would freeze. It reminded me of Windblows and I almost cried.

Upside - Problems made me use these forums to find help and I found it fairly quick.

Easy fix to scary looking problem.
> run Terminal type in command: sudo gedit /etc/hosts
the output of the Terminal should look like this:
127.0.0.1 localhost
**127.0.1.1 xirowei.WORKGROUP**

# The following lines are desirable for IPv6 capable hosts
::1 ip6-localhost ip6-loopback
fe00::0 ip6-localnet
ff00::0 ip6-mcastprefix
ff02::1 ip6-allnodes
ff02::2 ip6-allrouters
ff02::3 ip6-allhosts

xirowei = username
WORRKGROUP = domain name

Change to below output by delete away ".WORKGROUP":
127.0.0.1 localhost
**127.0.1.1 xirowei**

Save the file. Now try run any administrative applications, the window that prompt you to enter password will appear now.

### Post by PQB on 2008-05-31
My upgrade had problems relating to the network side of things. I am running Hardy on a laptop with a Belkin PCMCIA card that uses the ralink driver.

The ralink driver that came with Hardy has issues and so I had to blacklist it (rt73usb) and get the latest down via SerialMonkey. I also had to redo /etc/network/interfaces to reconfigure it for my ralink PCMCIA card.

I also had to tidy up some network files such as nsswitch.conf. However, they were problems of my own doing as up until recently I had a server running SUSE Linux Server and had configured nsswitch to use DNS as the first search order. That was OK when the Linux Server was the DNS server however with it down and switching back to my ISP DNS Servers Gnome had the "localhost" problem which was resolving to my Internet domain resolution and slowing things down quite a lot.

Interestingly enough last night I let the system update with the latest Linux core stuff and once again this loaded the bad driver so I had to reload the working one from SerialMonkey. The problem I saw was again with Gnome with similar 'localhost' issues but that was it, it was a general network driver problem.

I always expected issues as I use wireless around the House and whenever I upgrade or try another flavour of Linux I have to battle with it to get it working in my network. I have two versions of Ubuntu: Hardy on the laptop, v6 LTS on an IBM Desktop. I also have SUSE on a HP Desktop, and various flavours on a Dell laptop (I switch around on that to try different distros). All give me endless fun getting the wireless network working. I am not grumbling as I enjoy the challenge!

Regards,
Peter

### Post by Gnubu on 2008-05-31
Great to see linux finally work more or less.

Tried about 30 CDs over what must be almost 10 years and this one is the first to more or less work out of the box so to speak.

The resulting install is a working computer that has major issues about sharing on networks but few others that have come to light so far. That's all related to the mess that is samba share in this version apparently. So the setup that came out the other end of the install process is mostly working and fine as a solitary computer with web access, but useless for any kind of work duty on my lan.

Given that outcome, I have ordered some hardware of it's own for it to play with, for further testing, research and investigation.

Having Googled and then read through the valiant efforts of another user, who clearly knows their stuff, try to report this bug at the root of the problem, it's clear that the next release later this year is highly likely to still contain this bug as the person handling the report filing closed the bug when it had not been solved, this because they did not bother to read and adequately comprehend what the user was reporting.

So, history made (11/10 to Ubuntu for being the first linux to show true potential without any kind of fudging), and an uncertain future (1/10 for the bug filing process clerk) as far as usefulness of the resulting machine is concerned.

Dedicated hardware will now be used for testing future releases and evaluating them for viability in daily use for doing actual work with. Interesting times ahead then. <g>

### Post by viulian on 2008-06-01
The problem I've encountered installing Ubuntu 8.04 was that it gave me 'grub-install (hd0) failed - this is a fatal error' and upon rebooting the system said -> "Could not find operating system'.

I hate this kind of errors!

Turned out to be an issue with grub-install script which assumes that using 'df' is perfect when detecting on what partition a file/directory resides. Relying on df for that is not very smart.

Because on my live pen drive:
```

root@ubuntu:/mnt# df /boot/grub/
Filesystem           1K-blocks      Used Available Use% Mounted on
-                      1027488    837448    190040  82% /
```

Fix was to edit the /usb/sbin/grub-install script and hardcoding the the partition value.

```
find_device () {
    # For now, this uses the program `df' to get the device name, but is
    # this really portable?
    tmp_fname=`df $1/ | sed -n 's%.*\(/dev/[^   ]*\).*%\1%p'`
    **tmp_fname=/dev/sda3**

    if test -z "$tmp_fname"; then
        echo "Could not find device for $1" 2>&1
        exit 1
    fi

        tmp_fname=`resolve_symlink $tmp_fname`

    echo "$tmp_fname"
}
```

After which I got:

```
root@ubuntu:/tmp# grub-install /dev/sda3
Searching for GRUB installation directory ... found: /boot/grub
Due to a bug in xfs_freeze, the following command might produce a segmentation
fault when /boot/grub is not in an XFS filesystem. This error is harmless and
can be ignored.
xfs_freeze: specified file ["/boot/grub"] is not on an XFS filesystem
**Installation finished. No error reported.**
This is the contents of the device map /boot/grub/device.map.
Check if this is correct or not. If any of the lines is incorrect,
fix it and re-run the script `grub-install'.

(hd0)	/dev/sda
(hd1)	/dev/sdb
```

I'm now going to reboot and see if everything is OK.

### Post by xavierorr on 2008-06-01
I am a computer technician and I service about 40 machines running ubuntu that i have installed for home users who are generally sick of viruses and windows problems and want to try something different. most of them are families. apart from a few minor issues most of them have been quite happy with ubuntu 7.10

recently I have started upgrading them from 7.10 to 8.04 and while there are some considerable advances in the new ubuntu hardy that the users have liked, there are a two recurring complaints

1. what happened to the camera photo importer ? i can't type in a name for the film and it puts them all in the same folder. i have to go through and sort them manually. 

[COLOR="Red"]many users really don't like f-spot, they prefer to put their photos in folders to keep them organized and to be able to copy a particular folder of photos to a usb key etc so they can get them printed/share them with friends etc. out of all the people i have upgraded only one is happily using f-spot, the rest who use digital cameras i have had to change back to gthumb. this has happened so often that i am now manually changing it to gthumb when i install ubuntu for anyone.[/COLOR]

2. where is the shared folders tool, when i right click a folder and choose share it asks me to get my administrator to put me in userspace or something.

[COLOR="Red"]in my opinion this is only a minor inconvenience, but one which means that a standard user has to call the technician. i have had to go through and edit the samba config file and add the userspace option to all the users who are sharing files[/COLOR]

one other major issue with the new ubuntu is the inclusion of openjdk. on all machines i install ubuntu i add the ubuntu-restricted-extras package. unfortunately instead of the sun jre it installs the openjre which doesn't work with 90% of java apps. while i am all for the idea of the openjre/openjdk i think it definately isn't ready for deployment in ubuntu yet. on every install i have to manually remove it through synaptic and then install the sun version.

apart from that it has been pretty smooth sailing for the new ubuntu and has been very well received by my users with the most popular new feature being the instant search icon in the tray.

---

### Post by viulian on 2008-06-01
> **xavierorr said:**
> 
one other major issue with the new ubuntu is the inclusion of openjdk.

I 100% agree!! I mostly use standalone Ubuntu installs to verify my open source Java application. And nowadays I have to put READMEs to notify users that their Ubuntu operating system plays tricks on them and even if they install Sun's JDK, the default JVM will remain openjvm.

Also, about my boot issue:

I was able to get a boot menu after rebooting, however I still needed to manually boot the system, as menu.lst was bad.

1) press c in grub menu (after pressing ESC to get it)
2) root (hd0,2) [Instead of (hd0,0) which is the default]
3) kernel /boot/vmlinu[TAB here]
4) initrd /boot/initrd[TAB here] <- this was missing from the kernel line in generated menu.lst hence root=/dev/sda3 did not work.
5) press b to boot.

Why do I feel this distro is less well verified than the previous ones ?

### Post by newmo on 2008-06-01
Did an install from CD

A few unexpected moments during install - such as allowing me to have a triple boot XP and Gutsy and Heron set-up which I opted for.  (I had spent the best part of a week backing -up in my haphazard way expecting Gutsy to be complettely written over).

Display issues were an irritation - it all looked too big as well as off centre.  I was convinced I never had noticeable borders before but this was a flash memory. I was expecting things to be messed up on the graphics side.

It was otherwise running really smoothly and I was enjoying FF3 a lot.  I came across a tutorial on the find command and accidentally moved a load of my files from their normal place in the Home directory.  When I logged back in it was all magically fixed and looking great just like it had been in Gutsy - I am now cautiously moving some of those files back (the /.mozilla and /.metacity to name two).

I have had a few FF crashes and I lost my broadband connection but that could have been the network.

Its early days but its looking really promising - only problem has been the computer-widowing of my partner over most of this week. (As I was typing this she had a little nag -perfect timing).

### Post by Stefanie on 2008-06-02
I upgraded yesterday. There were a few problems. While upgrading i had this scrollkeeper parser error, which was apparently related to greek language support (the greek words for "help" and "preferences" ware part of the error message). I don't know what this error is about so I haven't fixed this yet. The upgrade went on after a few minutes so I stopped worrying about it.

A more irritating problem was that "sudo" didn't work anymore when i first booted into hardy, i got this error message "unable to resolve host". Synaptic wouldn't launch either. I fixed this by editing /etc/hosts : 
```
gksudo gedit /etc/hosts
``` 
and then I had to change
```
127.0.0.1 <username>.<network group name>
```
to
```
127.0.1.1 <username>
```

A third problem was synce. i had it working on gutsy, but the upgrade broke it apparently. when trying to install the needed packages i got tons of error messages. there was a problem with libgtk and also with python, i think. Eventually i sorted this out, but I don't know how exactly i did it. 

so this was my experience. apart from the hassle with the libgtk package I think it was quite a smooth upgrade.

### Post by nbiz on 2008-06-04
As an Ubuntu fan I finally removed my last windows PC recently and am now 100% linux, but was saddened at yet another Ubuntu installer nightmare (I'd had one about a year ago, on a new Gutsy x86/64 installation). I'd thought that with Hardy and on plain [AMD] x86 it would be a lot easier..

Whilst mostly fine, somewhere between the depths of SATA / udev, mdadm, LVM and the grub installer, I went round in circles for hours (nearer days actually) being given strange, strange errors at various interdependent stages (usually at install grub to MBR stage). 

I wanted to install separate boot partitions, two RAID-1 devices and LVM on top, keeping my existing NTFS partitions. The installer itself crashed several times (though this could be creaking hardware), the raid setup screen failed to realise you had to STOP a raid device before you could delete it (luckily switching to a VT and issuing commands helped thanks to a post here), and would forget that raid existed until you tried to either set something else up, or go and (re)configure raid devices (at which point, presumably it queried mdadm). I ended up going through the install process literally dozens of times. Only when I gave up completely and asked for Lilo one time (not the first though) did I get a bootable linux. I'm still not 100% sure why, I think it's grub not playing ball with udev ids and md devices, but I just don't care any more. 

From then on, though, plenty of Ubuntu goodness (except some missing sound kernel modules) and the *desktop* experience was what I've come to expect: polished and well thought out. 

So close, but left me with a sour taste, especially having done a very similar Fedora 8 install at work, which went without a single hitch, taking around an hour. Yes mine was not a completely vanilla install, but it's hardly odd either. The rescue modes mostly got me nowhere (let alone some horrid bterm / framebuffer issues with using most CLI tools) until some blind luck kicked in. Either way, the problems faced (and misleading error messages) were way outside the realms of even most technical users. IMHO, the low level tools (Grub / parted / mdadm / lvm stuff) MUST be more resilient before Hardy can be truly 'competitive'.

---

### Post by Vince-0 on 2008-06-04
I've installed Hardy Server alternative at work with the objective of setting up a workable terminal server for thin clients in a call center environment.
I HAD to have the LTSP (i386) server separate from the DHCP (amd64) server (point the dhcp with " next-server=192.168.0.25 ") Once this was in place it was all GOOD ! 

I also use Hardy desktop on my Acer 5720G laptop - what a pleasure! Sound / Grafx / Wireless works great. Wireless needs some tinkering with the gui manager though.

I'm also using Hardy on some desktops at work with ndiswrapper for wireless usb adapters - its all good. Boss is happy because of lowered costs and users are happy with nice PCs!

WHOOT for Ubuntu!

### Post by Dizzle7677 on 2008-06-04
[SIZE="1"]My 4 cents worth.

The Science: Installed from a USB.x64 version.
- Format with XFS filesystem not Ext3.Add 'noatime' for partition in /etc/fstab.
- Disable unnecessary  services & startups in Services and Sessions
--bluetooth,computer logger,multicast dns,task schedueler,hotkeys,printer service,evolution alarm,trackers,vis asst. Turn off indexing and tracking.
--remove avahi-IPD pkg [causes wierdness with wlan0]
-Remove restricted hardware modules [nv,ati,atheros,etc]
-Remove Network Manager.
-Install NDIS libs/gtk previously downloaded to USB. upgrade to Ndiswrapper1.53.
--http://ndiswrapper.sourceforge.net/joomla/index.php?/content/view/23/2/
-Install Wicd Network Manager
--http://wicd.sourceforge.net/download.php
-Install NDIS Windows driver for Atheros 5006EG.x64 version.
--http://blakecmartin.googlepages.com/ar5007eg-64-0.2.tar.gz  
-Blacklist Ath_pci and IPv6[/etc/modprobe.d/blacklist]
-Edit Menus to include all unchecked. Root terminal,Screens and Graphics [?],etc. 
*Reboot
-Add 'pre-up /sbin/ifconfig wlan0 hw ether {the MAC address}' to /etc/network/interfaces. Put above the wireless-essid.
--Settings don't stick if I don't do this w/ NDIS.
-Adjust Network settings 
--add AP from dropdown in Network.
--leave autoconnect off in Wicd for AP.
-UPDATES! UPDATES! UPDATES! [if all goes well with the network]
-add 'noresume' on the kernel line to menu.lst in /boot. Delete old kernels in /boot.
*Reboot
-Libc Headers!
-Install XFSprogs from SGI site. 
--ftp://oss.sgi.com/projects/xfs/cmd_tars/
-XFS_FSR drive.
-Install alsaconf and associated libs to get the Realtek HDA driver to Install.
-Install Realtek Driver HDA.
--http://www.realtek.com.tw/downloads/downloadsCheck.aspx?Langid=1&PNid=14&PFid=24&Level=4&Conn=3&DownTypeID=3&GetDown=false
[You know it works when you get the volume controls in the terminal.:)]
-Install the newest NVIDIA driver and xconfig.
--http://www.nvidia.com/object/linux_display_amd64_173.14.05.html
-Install flashplugin-nonfree plugin and restricted pkgs...ia32,etc.
*A shiny new Ubuntu has been born*

suggestions for things to have installed as base:
-Device Manager,Sysinfo,Hdparm as service?[it did in Gutsy],Should leave partition editor in,Home User Backup.

p.s. Urban Terror is tight. D/L it.[/SIZE]
AMD64,1GigRam,nForce4,Atheros 5006EG,NVIDIA IGP.

### Post by soleille on 2008-06-04
None of the downloaded ISOs would even start the install, so now that my "real" CD has arrived, I tried again...the CD worked LOL

I had wanted to do an upgrade from Gutsy- and the first 3 times or so it gave me the option. However, the installations all froze at some point (mostly during the partitioning sequence or the actual installation)

I noticed it gave me fewer and fewer options with every time I retried (thought it was just eliminating the things that had not worked at first!) - then realized it had been destroying partitions like crazy- Gutsy went, as did my data partition (bye, bye, precious 12 GB of data - thankfully the really important stuff is saved elsewhere just in case, but I was still mightily annoyed), as did this weird Acronis data recovery thingy with its own boot manager that someone had installed on my puter (not so sad, I couldn't find a way to nuke that and am glad it's gone, for some reason since this was added my Gutsy had stopped working except in slow motion) 
- the last few tries, I told it to use the guided - continious free space (something) option, and now both Hardy and my previous Win2000 partition are fine.

It took me about 6 hours and about a dozen tries until I finally got there though - but then I have a very old computer (IBM T20, don't know exact specs, but definitely less than 256 RAM), it defnitely couldn't cope with running the live CD or with installing Ubuntu though Windows.

I'm still a bit miffed about the lost data partition (and now only have 2 partitions, is there a way to resize these and add a third so that Win and Ubuntu can share data? from inside an OS and GUI if that is possible?)
-but overall I'm happy, it works fine and I've noticed some (actually rather minor, but which make life much easier for me) improvements over Gutsy.
The two things that thrill me the most are that without doing anything special, two things that were issues in Gutsy are one now: Although my 1999 BIOS still fails cutoff age, this doesn't result in not booting anymore, and the power manager finally detects whether I'm running on battery or have it hooked up.

### Post by washeddang on 2008-06-07
First post! :)

On my R61, everything major worked automatically after a fresh install:
[LIST]
[*]Proper resolution detected (1280x800)
[*]Compiz (once the nVidia restricted driver was enabled)
[*]Wireless (once the atheros restricted driver was enabled)
[*]Sound: playback and recording
[*]Most special keys: volume (up, down, mute), brightness, suspend, lock screen, battery status, hibernate and thinklight
[*]Touchpad
[/LIST]

I had to fix a few minor things:
[LIST]
[*]When waking from suspend, the screen would turn solid white. Fixed by installing the latest nVidia driver with Envy.
[*]The Forward, Back and media control (play, next, etc) keys were not mapped. I mapped them in xmodmap.
[*]ThinkVantage and Zoom special keys were not working and could not be mapped. Fixed by adding
setkeycodes e017 148 (for the ThinkVantage key)
setkeycodes 0082 192 (for the Zoom key)
to /usr/share/hotkey-setup/ibm.hk
[*]The usplash image on boot was stretched horizontally. Fixed by adding vga=865 to my boot options and setting /etc/usplash.conf to 1280x969 instead of 1280x800.
[/LIST]

There are a couple of minor things I can't get working:
[LIST]
[*]The wireless LED does not light up of show activity consistently. At first, it didn't light up at all. However, adding sysctl -w dev.wifi0.ledpin=1 to /etc/sysctl.conf makes the LED turn on, but it doesn't stay on or show network activity (it just blinks).
[*]The hardware switch to turn the wireless on/off doesn't do anything.
[/LIST]

Overall, it was a very smooth install! I'm quite happy!

### Post by saransk on 2008-06-07
I'm relatively new to Ubuntu but I work with systems for a living.  I am a firm supporter of simple hardware - if you push the boundaries with hardware, the system will be unstable.
Basic setup 
Tyan Tiger i750 with 2 Xeon Processors (4 cores)
ATI Radon 128MB PCI
Adaptec 29160 SCSI card - Seagate Barracuda 181GB drive
USB PCI card
DVD Writer, DVD-CDRW drives
1 GB of memory
Network printers - Epson Scanner

As you can see it isn't cutting edge but for the photo and video work I use it for it is great - and fast.  (Once you have more than 1 processor you'll never go back)

Decided to do a clean install of Hardy.  Went great - no problems, The video, sound, and SCSI card all had native drivers (I don't even have the ATI proprietary drivers installed)
The HP AIO installed easier than it di on our XP system, the Samsung 2510 had native drivers in the distro, as did my Epson photo printer.
Sane found my 1640 Scanner and it works great right into GIMP.
My Canon DSLR connects via UFRAW without a problem.

From start to finish the initial install took about an hour.  Another 30 minutes to update email, favorites, passwords, etc.

While I don't play games, or use really high end hardware - I do use it hard, the RAW files from the camera are very large and I create DVD backups of all the images.  I also make DVD's from all of my movies (and the ones the kids make) as well as converting some 1500 LP's to digital files.

I have always tried to stay within the boundaries of the the Hardware Compatibility List and avoid those products with ???.  Yet Hardy really has matched most of the basic hardware out there.  At work I've gotten it to run really nicely on a DELL Optiplex 745 - right out of the box without any upgrades.  Even VISTA won't do that.

Once again my decision to leave both Windows and Mac OSX and concentrate on a solid Linux system has paid off

Saransk
20 plus years of PC's (Still have my original Mac Plus)

### Post by saransk on 2008-06-07
Just an observation as a systems guy
I noticed that many of the users with problems are using laptops, and most trying to dual boot.
Any IT guy will tell you that laptops are the problem child of systems.  We have the latest Dells - 12 bought in one batch and every one had something different that was flaky - and these were XP systems.
Also, dual booting has it's own set of problems.  We have stopped using it an now rely on either a boot disk (like a live distro) or a virtual machine.
We can store data in separate partitions, but we don't try to have "real-time" dual boots.  I have found that for most applications, WINE works fine in Linux.

Just my 2 cents worth

### Post by tonye on 2008-06-08
Being new to all of this, some of you might go (well duh), but here was the biggest issue I had with the 8.04 install:

If anyone is using an ASUS M2N-MX SE Plus motherboard (AMD 64 bit), unless you go into the BIOS under the Power Menu and set "ACPI APIC Support" to disabled, you will have an error with Ubuntu.

Between attempting different install techniques, as well as following some of the directions from different Ubuntu forum posts (I don't remember everyone that had mentioned this issue.)  I am very appreciative of all the help and am now up and running.

Thats my two cents.  Hope this helps others!
