### Post by PgTips on 2008-06-08
I think I must be a bit unusual. I am a relative newbie with linux although I do go back to the early days of Byte Magazine and homebrewing 4-bit micros to tx my amateur radio callsign in morse. I came to ubuntu about 6 months ago after procrastinating for about 1 year and loaded 7 from a CD in a UK Magazine.Now I am not a control line freak! If I had my way, ALL computers would work right out of the box and be voice or thought controlled! Let's be realistic here. Computers are just a means to an end and should require less effort to drive than a car! And i say that having both in business and as a hobby been just as much a geek as anybody! I have a number of computers the most lowly of which is a Samsung GT7000 laptop with 500mhz cpu,, 256Mhz (it wont upgrade to more), 80Ghz Hd (upgraded from 4Ghz) CD Rom (read only) Ethernet on a PCMCIA card etc etc. !
UBUNTU HARDY Heron WORKS OUT OF THE BOX!
Why? I haven't a clue. My original install of 7.04 WAS troublesome - mainly caused by my ignorance (note order of priorities) and video driver problems.
Previously, my old laptop ran Fenestras 98SE without any problems and the only reason I decided to change was that,like many others, I believe open spaces should not need windows and gates. Also running a number of machines for both hobby use and business gets prohibitive in cost terms. On other machines i have XP, XP Pro, XP Media Centre - but only because they came pre-installed!

My living room machine (750Mhz, 850Mhz Ram, Nvidea (cheapo, crappo), 120GhzHD, Display=Evesham HDTV 40") is also nearly OK after upgrade. Only problem I cannot now change wallpaper - but what the hell! I might just have to buy a decent speed machine and build a media centre.

Getting back to the old laptop. For me it works fine. I do a bit of surfing, email, word processing and similar. Some number-crunching and play a few CD's. Games? I play a bit of chess, mahjong? Some electronic design and CAD gets done also. I did that on 98SE too! But..............this has just cost me a little bit of time!

So, what's the secret? I don't know the answer but I suspect that many of the problems arise from a combination of expectations that are too high, Ubuntu releasing stuff a little "raw" and folks trying to be too clever!

So it didn't work out of the box? Remember that Fenestration didn't either!
Before doing the upgrade, go on the forums and note comments from others. There are folks out there who know a hell of a lot more than we do and are worth taking note of!
[U]Back up your data before doing anything so drastic as an upgrade
      [/U]
While it is a sensible thing anyway, it gives you the confidence to "play" to get your new "upgrade/install" going when you know your data is safe.
Most of all be patient! D'you really need to get it all working 100% ten minutes after burning the iso?

Life's too short!!!

### Post by Christopher_Bailey on 2008-06-08
Well I attempted to upgrade to hardy three days ago. At the time I was running a fresh install of gutsy due to a recent HD crash. About halfway through the update installation process my system locked up and I was unable to get out of it without manually restarting. After rebooting the system would crash right after grub started up without giving me enough to see what the errors were. After about 4 hours of trying to figure it out I zapped the drive and tossed in a freshly burnt Xubuntu (Hardy). Installation went quickly and smoothly without so much as a hiccup. I booted it up and was greeted with 87 critical updates. After downloading and installing all of the updates I grabbed samba and fusesmb. I rebooted after installing fusesmb and the system would hang at the Splash screen while loading the desktop manager. Icons/systray and everything would load up just fine but the splash screen would not disappear from the screen and then loads of the "wired network connection" icons would appear from the system tray all the way across the screen. I tried several times to get around it but had the same problem after every reboot. Booted into failsafe xterm and apt-get remove'd samba. Rebooted and have the same error. Came back and apt-get remove'd fusesmb. Still had the same error. Ended up freshly installing Xubuntu again and now I have just finished installing all of the updates. I'm going to reboot with just a clean updated install and hopefully it will come back working this time. :)

### Post by bearslumber on 2008-06-09
Hi.

I am one of the culprits who voted that I had a few hiccups in a fresh install of Ubuntu.

I did resolve each issue one by one and I am now writing this reply straight from my fresh install.

I have to say I am very happy despite the issues. :)

I shall explain the problems I had.

My aim was to install ubuntu on a 8gb usb stick, so that I can use my work laptop persistently without any effect on my work, which is on a tightly controlled Windows XP laptop.

I installed from the live CD.

My laptop is a Toshiba Satellite Pro A120 with 2gb RAM and a 60mb HD, wireless but sadly no bluetooth.

[B][U]Problem 1: 
[/U][/B]
The system did not boot at all. Got the grub menu, but kept getting error 15. unknown filesystem/missing file (if I remember correctly).

This was due to the root entry (hdn,n) in the grub menu list. When installing from the CD Ubuntu rightfully set the HD to hd0,0 and the usb stick to hd0,1, because this was the sequence of appearance when running from a CD. However the laptop boot changes this root preference to HD (hd1,0) and USB (hd0,0) when it detects that there is no CD so the drives had sneakily been swapped without grubs knowledge. Swapping them back did the trick, and it proceeded to boot.[B][U]

Problem 2:[/U][/B]
I got the log on screen but after that got a blank screen. I then found out about Ctrl, Alt, F1, deleted the X lock as suggested by an error message when I first issued the command startx, and then reissued startx and viola.

I was immediately prompted that many packages were out of date which I accepted and downloaded and rebooted and all has been very good ever since.

My only remaining gripe is that I can't seem to install themes in firefox 2 on the fly. I guess this has something to do with the "sudo" prefix. Any help on this will be greatly appreciated but I shall post the request elsewhere.

I am still very happy despite the last glitch.

Regards

Bearslumber.

### Post by Dale61 on 2008-06-10
> **jean01 said:**
> 
-i can't use my keyboard correctly especially with the number keys and the dot.

I did this to get my number pad to work properly.

```
 (In a terminal)
sudo apt-get install numlockx
sudo cp /etc/X11/gdm/Init/Default /etc/X11/gdm/Init/Default_backup
gksudo gedit /etc/X11/gdm/Init/Default

Find this line 
...
exit 0
Add the following lines above it 
if [ -x /usr/bin/numlockx ]; then
 /usr/bin/numlockx on
fi
Save the edited file 
```

This is a 6.06 command, but it seems to have worked for me in 8.04.

### Post by sundman on 2008-06-10
This was a great update with many fine enhancements.

I had to fix one minor thing - OpenOffice started to complain about  missing WebWizard modules. The solution found in [the OpenOffice community forum]("http://user.services.openoffice.org/en/forum/viewtopic.php?f=15&t=135") was to get fresh versions of the to files to the user directory
```
cd ~
cp /usr/lib/openoffice/presets/basic/*.xlc .openoffice.org2/user/basic/
```

### Post by Audiosears on 2008-06-11
For us, the update to Hardy went more or less without a hitch, though there were some installer errors due to a bad package of bacula that did not install correctly and could not then be uninstalled either - the overall upgrade worked but even in Hardy errors are still thrown by apt upon installation or update of any other packages.

At the end of the upgrade process an error was thrown that stated that the upgrade FAILED and had to initiate the recovery process.  I almost soiled myself.  But upon restarting, HArdy seemed to boot up and operate anyway :)

My one complaint so far was that there seemed to be no indication that the new samba package (3.0.28a) would include a security update against legacy lanman hashes of Samba passwords.  Basically (this was something I learned after a few hours of troubleshooting and forum trolling), this version of samba forward, you have to add 'lanman auth = yes' to your [global] parameters in order for legacy machines like Win 98 and NT (pre-SP4) to be able to authenticate to Samba shares using encrypted passwords.  We were doing this all along, but when they suddenly couldn't authenticate, it was an interesting journey to disciver the cure.

Normally, you have alphanumeric hashes for both the lanman and NT sides of each username's password string in /etc/samba/smbpasswd, but without the 'lanman auth = yes' option on, the lanman hash resets to 'XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX' upon the client's first attempt to connect!

If anyone out there was affected by this too, once you set the option in your /etc/samba/smb.conf file, you then have to reset samba passwords for any affected usernames (i.e. smbpasswd <username>) to reset the lanman side of the hash again.  Then after that you will be good to go.

So far everything else seems OK, but time will tell.

### Post by muncrief on 2008-06-17
After five attempts trying to install Hardy x86_64 since its release I finally gave up and went back to Gutsy x86_64.

The problems I encountered were typical. Non-functioning or non-existent browser plugins, a lack of 32 bit libraries so things like RealPlayer 11 can't be installed, no Compiz-Fusion integration so setting up independent dual screens mandates that you be left with a zombied Compiz, the inability to use the last fully functional Nvidia driver (100.14.19), etc.

It's a good thing it's an LTS, because it looks like it's going to take a long time to fix.

I've been using Ubuntu for years now, and this is the first release that is still non-functional over a month after its release.

I think it's time for Ubuntu to reevaluate its "we'll call it done even if it isn't" release policy. Hardy has caused some serious damage to Ubuntu's reputation.

### Post by HyRax on 2008-06-18
> **bbarcelo said:**
> I just installed Hardy Heron and have encountered a problem that was not present in any earlier version. After setting Ubuntu to auto-login as a selected user and enabling VNC, I can't simply boot up the computer and get to VNC. This is caused by some problem with X11 recognizing my monitor, of which I have none, and stops ubuntu-desktop from loading as a result of some prompt that notifies me of Ubuntu's failure to find a monitor present.

Has your video card being detected properly? What is it? Ubuntu really shouldn't care what video card you have, let alone monitor. At the very least it should boot up to standard VESA resolutions.
> **bbarcelo said:**
> 
My question is this: How can I set up my Ubuntu 8.04 installation to be plugged in anywhere with a network connection and a power source, have it boot up and be accessible without a monitor via VNC.

Set BIOS to have power set to "Always On" so when you plug in power, it turns on. Also set it to ignore "no keyboard present".

Have Ubuntu login automatically as you've already done and have Remote Desktop configured to accept any connection without a password.
> **bbarcelo said:**
> 
Doing this exact thing in Ubuntu 7.10 took me about four minutes and I've Googled all I can to find out any way to do this in 8.04. I hope this is some easy fix. :confused:

That's essentially it!
> **bbarcelo said:**
> 
Other than this (though this is very important to me), 8.04 installed fine.
Sounds to me that once you have the monitor issue sorted, you'll be fine.

---

The purpose of this thread is to share your experience installing/upgrading to Ubuntu Hardy Heron.

Did it work flawlessly ?

[COLOR="Navy"]It did not work flawlessly either installing nor upgrading to new versions[/COLOR]

Did you get problems ?

[COLOR="Navy"]Yes, I had problems![/COLOR]

Did you manage to solve them ?

[COLOR="Navy"]Fortunately, the support for ubuntu is phenomenal, and I have been able to fix all of the problems I have encountered by searching the forums and documentation.[/COLOR]

if yes how ?

[COLOR="Navy"]I own a Compaq Presario V6000, AMD X2 TL-52, 2GB, 120 GB, Dual boot with XP PRO. My laptop uses Nvidia for both the video and sound, while using the infamous Broadcom wireless card. Three strikes should call me out, but I am tenacious.

Hardy installed initially with version 14 fairly easily. I had to tinker with my wireless until I understood how to make it function. After that, Network manager took over and has been good to me since. My first upgrade caused screen resolution problems, sound problems, and no wireless. After many hours of following every post I could find, I tried changing from the "generic" module to the "openvz" module. BAM!!! Everything came back like it was before the upgrade! It has worked twice now. Both 18 and 19 caused huge problems that were fixed by switching. 

I love my ubuntu and hardly ever use XP anymore. I can see a future without Microsoft as I learn the applications native to Linux. I also love Wine, as it is easing the transition.[/COLOR]

The Heron rules!

---

### Post by Caliban51 on 2008-06-20
I'm an absolute beginner with Linux.
Saw the blurb on how this edition would work "Straight from the Box"
But not taking any chances, so installed to spare PC with clean HD.

Install Log:

Had problems initially as I could not get past some options such as Language, Time Zone & Country.
Twigged on that the monitor resolution had been configured to the minimum & the enter boxes were hidden way below the bottom of the screen (& no scroll bars were available). I got around this by changing the default fonts from 10 to 8. Managed to get it to install eventually, but on reboot the monitor reports "Cannot display this resolution"
Played about with the monitor buttons & found that if I put it into test mode & came out again I could get the GUI, but at the minimum screen res of 720x400. Looking at the available resolutions but this is the only one there is.

Found the nvidia driver in the restricted drivers list so installed it. On reboot now have Black/Blank screen, monitor light is green, can't even get it to go into test mode.

Searched Google & found how to get to the command prompt & the command:
"sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg"
This got me back into the GUI

In another search on Google the was a suggestion to enter my monitor Horz & Vert rates into the Xorg.conf file. (Didn't think I'd ever have to got back to command prompt stuff again, just as well I was brought up with DOS!)

Well this seems to have fixed the monitor "Cannot display this resolution" problem & the resolution has improved to 800x600.

Another search on Google & found many people had similar experiences with Nvidia drivers & screen resolution problems. Some advised downloading & installing "EnvyNG".

Spent some time thrashing about finding the download, but got there in the end.

PC spends about twenty mins churning about & install completed. 
Re-booted to another Black Screen! [http://ubuntuforums.org/images/smilies/icon_sad.gif](http://ubuntuforums.org/images/smilies/icon_sad.gif)
:(

Another search on Google, turns out Linux doesn't seem to like Nvidia or ATI drivers (strange as they are the two major video card manufacturers)

I guess the next step is to uninstall the Envy package & possibly use the 
"sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg" command again, but I'm starting to lose interest. I'm beginning to appreciate Bill Gate's efforts more (Well apart from VISTA!)[http://ubuntuforums.org/images/smilies/icon_mad.gif](http://ubuntuforums.org/images/smilies/icon_mad.gif)
:mad:

### Post by shakyone on 2008-06-20
installed on a dell d620

upgrade went okay.  

issues - 

1.  There still seem to be some issues with the graphics, occasionally gnome will not boot up with the title bars, and I have to reboot X.

2.  Something does not like it when I open more than two windows of Firefox.  When a link forces open a third Firefox window I get a Firefox crash.  This does not occur when I run Firefox in Windows XP.  More of an annoyance.

3.  ¨Removable drives and media¨ window havs changed, and now I cannot manage what happens when I plug in a USB drive, or a cf card in my cf-pc card reader.  That functionality in the window was removed since 7.10 for some reason.

Positives:  

Glad to see some other improvements.  

Prior to my system update to the most recent kernal, my dual core processor only used one processor at a time, according to the system monitor.  It would be at 1.5% on one processor and 22% on the other at idle.  Then it would reverse the loading, about every 15 seconds.  As of last weeks kernel upgrade, it appears that is fixed!  THANK YOU LINUS if you made that happen.  

The alternate installer is gouge.  It is much easier to get the wide screen setup properly on the Dell Laptop.  Wine is improved too.  Runs portableapps.com applications very well.  

Video playback is no longer cross-hatched.  Previously I had usable but irritating video issues playing any .wmv or .mpeg files that created subtle but visible cross-hatching on the video window.

Do not get me wrong.  I really like the freedom of Ubuntu.  I have been a dedicated user since 6.06.  I have opted it over MAC, and I do not see myself going back.  I like the ability to use the windows Apps I need, the freedom to build the computer I want (contrary to MAC ideologies), and reliability of Ubuntu/Linux.  I also have happily gone to FOSS and am slowly converting the family.

### Post by JamesLavin on 2008-06-20
After reading several reports that Hardy installs well on Dell 1420s, I bought one at Costco and installed with just one glitch.

I configured a pre-loaded Ubuntu laptop at Dell.com but didn't want to wait a week or two to receive mine, so I bought the Windo$e version selling at Costco. The Costco laptop also came with a 250GB hard drive and 3GB RAM, whereas Dell.com offered a maximum 130GB (?) and 2GB RAM (?) on its website. (Why doesn't Dell offer larger drives on its pre-loaded Ubuntu 1420s???)

I didn't use Dell's special Ubuntu ISO, instead going with the standard Hardy ISO. I chose the default option of re-partitioning the drive to give the pre-installed Windo$e about 8% of the drive and give the rest to Ubuntu. This way, I won't void my warranty.

Most everything worked out of the box. But WiFi didn't. My USB WiFi (ZyXEL G-220 v2) worked automatically, but the built-in Dell WiFi required NDISwrapper. Eventually got that working.

I haven't tried the built-in video and some of the fancier features (8-in-1 card reader, etc.). But most everything I've used so far seems to work fine.

--James

### Post by Tommy on 2008-06-22
I installed Hardy 8.04 on an older machine (A HP Pavilion 8754c -- 800Mhz Pentium III). First of all I realized the box didn't have enough RAM so I found a SIMM sitting around and upgraded it from 127mb to 256mb. With that much RAM the machine actually FINISHED booting the live CD (previously it never quite made it to the desktop). :)

Then I started the install, and it went OK. It seemed to take longer than some previous installs, HOWEVER, the installer only asked me ONE question which was good. This is the first time I've ever installed a "dual boot" with that "other" OS ... but this is a "borrowed" machine and I wanted to leave the original data alone. It has a 30-gig drive so splitting the sizes down the middle worked great.

After it booted, The first thing I had to do was figure out how to turn off the desktop effects because this box just can't handle them. (Artifacts on the screen just from moving and clicking the pointer, etc.)

After that, it's mostly OK, though the system monitor reports it's using more CPU just sitting idle than the 400 Mhz machine next to it running Gutsy 7.10. I haven't used it actively yet so I may uncover other quirks. Overall, however, it seems a solid installation.

### Post by Anlace on 2008-06-23
My Kubuntu 8.04 installation went well except for video.  I experienced this exact same problem in 7.10 and was hoping that the issue was resolved in 8.04.  Interestingly enough this all worked beautifully in 6.06.

I have either extremely low resolution (640x480) or my monitor registers "Out of Range" and I have no video at all when I try to use any of the Nvidia drivers.

Here is my set up:
Nvidia 6800XT Video card
ViewSonic VX2025wm monitor

I get the exact same results when I install using the built-in proprietary drivers helper program, when I installed nvidia_glx_new, when I installed the Nvidia driver from Nvidia's web site, and when I used Envy.

Here is what I have done to try and fix the problem:

Changed the usplash configuration to use native 1680x1050 resolution

Commented out all of the earlier kernel versions in menu.lst so that only the most current boots.

Hand edited xorg.conf to include ViewSonic's horizontal and vertical refresh rates specific to my monitor.  I also either added or made sure that 1680x1050@60 was included.

The only way that I can have 1680x1050 resolution is to use the Xorg nv driver at the refresh rates determined by that driver.  Using the nv driver means that I am not able to view any of the nice visuals that compiz provides but at least I have a decent resolution for my setup although with this driver I experience tearing and playing video is not so nice.

So, in my experience and with my set up it is impossible to get native resolution on my monitor using either the Ubuntu provided Nvidia driver (nvidia_glx_new) or Nvidia's driver either manually installed or using Envy.  I would love to have the accelerated graphics that my card is capable of but with this set up it isn't happening.

### Post by Craig73 on 2008-06-23
Had to dig around for a utility to defrag Windows so that I could repartition.  I find it amusing that the various instructions I found on the web suggested partition magic - commercial software to prepare for open source?  I did find a couple GPLed defrag utils that work well.

I'm disappointed that the install wizard didn't take the GPLed defrag and make use of it, or that the wizard install didn't handle the repartioning (I had to do most of these steps by hand).  I guess the install over VISTA will be smoother (using windows APIs to repartition) but that doesn't do much for those avoiding VISTA.  [I realize there is WUBI, but I wanted Linux running on the metal...]

Other than that things work well - Using a Inspiron 700m and it picked the correct resolution, software runs great, can burn CDs, audio, WIFI, Ethernet all working.  Even hibernate and suspend work (although sometimes it claims it didn't even when it appeared to).

What doesn't work - build in Ti SD card reader worked the first time and now hangs the system every time.  Not sure why SVideo doesn't work.  And it hangs occasionally on videos from the web (either they work flawlessly or lock the system with 'black screen of death' ;-)  ).

I'm happy to have returned to Linux.  (and happy that WINE works well too as part of the transition)

### Post by newbie618 on 2008-06-23
New install to HP Notebook dv9628nr

Need to install restricted drive for Ndvia
Problem with wireless.  Many sites show the wrong method.

Ndvia
Install restricted drive from Synaptic Manager
and activate restricted driver

Wireless
use ndiswrapper to install window driver SP36684A

Found this bug problem.  Used it and then I can installed the ndiswrapper

If you type in lshw -C network you might "module=ssb" instead of "module=ndiswrapper" This is because Hardy has a [WWW] module loading bug that takes control away from ndiswrapper.

To re-load the modules in the proper order, issue the following set of commands. Ignore potential errors from the first three commands, but let me know about any errors from sudo rmmod ssb down.

Note: The commands below are sort of a "shotgun" approach. It's not really "lean" and customized for your machine as it can be. For instance, among the rmmod ("remove module") commands, you can see b43, b43legacy, and b44 modules being unloaded. However, your particular machine will only have one or two of these "b4*" modules loaded in the first place, and removing the remaining "b4*" modules from your machine, while harmless, is completely unnecessary. Similarly, the last command run sudo modprobe b44 is only really required if your machine has a (Broadcom) wired ethernet card that uses the b44 module, so adding it back to systems that don't need it is also harmless, but unnecessary. Eventually, I'll make some sort of script that has the intelligence built in; however, in the meantime, if you would like a lean sequence that is customized for your machine, post the output of lsmod to [WWW] the accompanying forum thread.

Hope this will help others.

---

### Post by normanecker on 2008-06-24
I recently installed Ubuntu 8.04 on my Toshiba P100 laptop. Setting up dual boot with XP Pro was no problem, as I have edited partition tables before. After install, I had issues with both sound and overheating, but both have been solved. Upgrading BIOS to 4.3 fixed the sound, and modifying the DSDT fixed the ACPI fan control, however I do not recommend a novice trying to install Ubuntu on this model. btw: I blame Toshiba, not Ubuntu for all the hassling around trying to stop my computer catching fire. There was a recall involving early P100's and overheating under XP.

### Post by skb on 2008-06-25
I first tried Ubuntu on my laptop about 2 years ago, and eventually gave up because of wireless issues with NDISwrapper. Wireless not working the way I wanted it to was a complete deal breaker for me. I went back to my previous dual boot FreeBSD/WinXP configuration and was happy for a while.

My laptop died due to mobo failure, and rather than pay more than the machine was worth on a new mobo, I bought a new laptop. Given all the love for Hardy at Slashdot and other hallowed halls of geekdom I gave it a shot. The install was effortless. Total kudos to the team on the installer. It ranks among my simplest SOFTWARE installs of all time, much less OS installs!

My new lappy (AMD Turion 64 X2) includes an Atheros 5007 wifi chipset, which Hardy incorrectly detected, and I had to dig for a few hours to find a solution. The thought of playing around with ndiswrapper again was none too appealing, but I finally found a solution using madwifi that made things work perfectly. I'm finally getting on to the stuff I -want- to do with the new install as opposed to the things I -had- to do just to make it work, and it's looking great so far.

I chose the "worked, but had a few things to solve" option.

PS: How cool is it that I hadn't posted here in 2 years, and yet my account was still here waiting for me? It's as if the devs knew I'd eventually come back to the fold... ;)

### Post by STPitcher on 2008-06-25

I'm installing on a <2 year old HP laptop.  2 gig.  100GB harddrive

1) I couldn't get it to preserve my system's recovery partition.  Would have been nice to save that... but this way, I get to use all 100GB for Linux.

4) I tried setting up my wireless... hasn't worked yet... That's why I'm here in the fora.  Likewise, I need to update my video driver.

### Post by kessaris on 2008-06-26
Second time installing a Ubuntu release.

The first time it was an earlier version, it seemed a bit light so I opted for SUSE.  Glad I came back around.

This time, I went with SUSE on a relatively new Japanese notebook.  Under windows the thing would boot and then freeze.  Under SUSE it seemed to work but no touchpad support.  Went mad.  Tried UBUNTU.

Still no touchpad support for some reason, but what a great operating system!  It took like three tries with the install CD to get things right. At first it wouldn't boot at all, and the partition manager gave no end of problems.  I'm not sure what a guided installation is, but I had to manually specify the partition setup in the end.

Up and running, I compiled a custom kernel with the hopes of reviving the touchpad and in the process made a lean mean operating system for my laptop.  [Finally found out that the touchpad was based on an i8042 chipset and needed a couple of kernel boot parameters to straighten it out.]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=5264968#post5264968")

At the time of this writing I could not possibly be any happier.  (Well.. if my SD and memory stick card slots would work.. and the TV tuner could catch any channels.. umm.. but CD burning works like a charm and the volume buttons on the laptop even activate a little volume level thingy on the screen.

### Post by bcrowell on 2008-06-26
I ran into this bug: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/wvdial/+bug/50041](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/wvdial/+bug/50041) It's frustrating that something like this still hasn't been fixed, two years after it was first reported.

Workaround: Hit alt-f2 to get a busybox console on virtual screen #2. Do a ps x and find the process ID of wvdialconf. Do a "kill nnnnn," where nnnnn is the process ID. Alt-f1 to get back to virtual screen #1, where the install has gotten going again.

### Post by Midwest-Linux on 2008-06-26
I installed Ubuntu 8.04 Hardy on the Compaq 762NR Vista laptop.

The live CD worked good. So I decided to try a test install first with WUBI. While Wubi installed good and the Ubuntu Hardy installed I ended up with a bunch of errors and stuck at busy box. I deleted Wubi and the Ubuntu 8.04.

I read some Windows forums and discovered that Vista has a built in shrink partitioner. I followed the instructions on the site and shrank the Vista partition by 12 GB. This was now free space. I used the alternate text installer CD version for Ubuntu Hardy 8.04. I got to the partition section. I selected to install hardy on the now freed space. Everything went well and Hardy installed fine. I later found out that the wireless uses the Atheros 5007 and used the common script to install it. My only regret is not giving Hardy more space. I am intending of wiping Vista off this computer anyway and doing a complete re-install.

///////////////////////Wireless script for Atheros 5007////////////////////////////////

sudo apt-get install build-essential

wget [http://snapshots.madwifi.org/special/madwifi-ng-r2756+ar5007.tar.gz](http://snapshots.madwifi.org/special/madwifi-ng-r2756+ar5007.tar.gz)

tar xfz madwifi-ng-r2756+ar5007.tar.gz

cd madwifi-ng-r2756+ar5007

sudo make 

sudo make install

sudo modprobe ath_pci

sudo reboot

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////

Note! Be aware that the mad wifi site goes down sometimes. If you have the Atheros 5007 and plan wireless. Maybe you might want to download it to your desktop if you plan to install it later on. As "later on" could be when the mad wifi site might be down.

One important note to all. A recent update killed my wireless and the Add/Remove program. I re did the wireless script from this point. Note you should not have to go back to mad wifi to re-install that way as the original tar xfz madwifi-ng-r2756+ar5007.tar.gz should still be in the files. So this modified script should work.
//////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
cd madwifi-ng-r2756+ar5007

sudo make 

sudo make install

sudo modprobe ath_pci

sudo reboot

/////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////

Like I mentioned the kernal upgrade also killed the add/remove. Here is the script to get it back working normally.
/////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////

sudo apt-get update

//////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////

Forum staff and Moderators......

If kernel updates are killing wireless, add/remove and other problems. Maybe these issues should be should be made into a sticky in the "installation/upgrades" and "Wireless/networking" forums.

Thanks

### Post by deNoobius on 2008-06-28
Well, I'm a lot happier with HH now that I've fixed my install (I was complaining further up in this thread).  It's a fast, clean OS--far and away a better user experience than Windows.  

Still, as with all Linux distros (I've tried a few), the commercial OSs have a better installation experience. Ubuntu has one of the easiest, comparatively.  Now that I have my computer up and running again, I feel that it was worth the extra time and hassle.  I'm impressed by the visual package manager--the last time I used Linux, you pretty much had to compile and install from the command line for a lot of things.  Firefox is fast on this machine--far faster than on Windows Vista, and right up there with FF 3 on OSX.  Maybe even a bit faster. Open Office, for what I need (word processing mostly) is great, and makes me wonder why I paid for Microsoft Office.  

In my case, for some reason my first installation didn't get my wireless card to work, and there was a driver helper software called b43-fwcutter that did not properly install, and then I had cascading problems resulting from trying to fix that.  I eventually got it sorted out with a fresh install and manual installation of the aforementioned software, but maybe this is something the developers could look at.

So bottom line--first install attempt was a lot of frustration, second one was pretty smooth.

### Post by tomWright on 2008-06-30
UBUNTU is broken.

Dapper to Hardy upgrade, dual boot HP ZD8000.

Dapper worked ok for almost 2 years. Wireless was fine, using a d-link card with an atheros chip. 5 button mouse worked fine. remote admin of a winXp box worked fine. I rearely booted into WinXp, and then just to use software available only on windows.


But man! They really screwed the pooch on this one.


While the upgrade worked well enough that it booted first time, some things are broken. Important things.

1) Wireless has stopped working for no apparent reason. It worked at first, after the upgrade, but just stopped. probably due to one of the many upgrades released by Canonical since then. All I can get now, is that the interface ATH0 does not exist. 
I have to use a 50 foot LAN cable stretched across my house from where my router is to where my laptop is. Way to go back to the 1990's there Canonical.

2) My 5 button Logitech mouse will not configure as anything other than 3 button. Again, worked fine under Dapper. The few things I have found here and tried have not worked, the others are too complicated to do. It is ridiculous that they actually made mouse compatibility WORSE in what was supposed to be an upgrade.

3) RDP and VNC both have ceased to work, I can not get them to work again. I can see, read and edit the shared files on the winXp box, but can not log into it to administer anything.

I have not even TRIED printing. I am too disappointed at this "failure to launch" to attempt it, since the printer is attached to the winXp box I can no longer log into.

I spend 9 hours a day, plus, working in IT on large databases. I do not need a second job administering my home PC's. They just need to work. While I have no problem doing basic config things, the amount of work  needed to correct these very basic failures is absurd.

Canonical must be nostalgic for the Reagan era of U.S. politics. All the software seem to have regressed that far back.

I just ordered a Lenovo laptop with SuSe pre-installed. I hope it does better. At this point though, I may just order one of the few remaining copies of winXp still in retailer inventories. Heck, even Vista doesn't have these problems.

Linux will never compete with Windows while it fails basic things like wireless and mouse configuration. While RDP/VNC is something the average user will not need, to screw up wireless and mouse is like screwing up automatic transmissions and heaters when building an automobile.

---

### Post by cjv8888 on 2008-07-01
I have installed Hardy in 3 computers so far, 2 in a dual boot with XP and 1 in a triple boot with XP and XP Pro all with a separate HDD.  All installed without problems and all devices were generally recognised and worked 'out of the box'
Booting with grub presented a different problem in every computer I found.
In 1 computer, that was solved with help from the forum here. In another, a simple change in the booting order in the BIOS sufficed. In the triple boot computer, it resulted in a BSOD with the XP pro and while I was able to rescue it with restore MBR command I got scared and removed the HD containing the Ubuntu.
I may still go back one day and retry the install again when I plug up the courage.:)

### Post by ozfalcon on 2008-07-01
Previously a long time Redhat/Fedora user.
Started out running (The now ancient) RH5.2 as servers.
Have been using Fedora as desktop since 2005.
Just changed to Ubuntu desktop on the LTS release 8.04.

Machine Spec:
   AMD Athlon 2400 (2ghz)
   nVidia 5200 
   1gb ram, 120gb HD
   Mobo: Gigabyte 7VT600P-RZ
    Onboard Audio, Lan, USB.

Install was good with no problems.
Waiting on 8.04.1 release to install on an identical machine.

Setup had a handfull of hickups - Some still not resolved.

* Passwordless login was a pain. Had to manually edit config files.
   A simple option in the user edit section should be made.

* Google earth didn't play well with Compiz. I Disabled Compiz.
   Note --> [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=809695](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=809695)
   
* No preferences available to enable desktop icons ("computer" & "home").
   Had to run gconf-editor --> apps/nautilus/desktop

* Trying to get my Bluetooth headset working resulted in hard lockup.
   This problem is currently unresolved - I'm not game to try often. :confused:

* gnome-nettool doesn't think my eth0 exists when I try to configure it.
   This problem is currently unresolved - However "Network Administration
   Tool" allows the configuration needed. 

* I would dearly love to MOVE "Places" from the menu bar and either put it on the desktop or in a submenu under System.
   This problem is currently unresolved. :confused:

* I wish to make my default media player xine or mplayer for both mouse clicked media and cd/dvd insertion.
   This problem is currently unresolved. :confused:

* Codec and Video driver install was fairly painless. :popcorn:

* Setup of my networked HP2500/HP620n colour laserjet was easy. :D 

* My (Old) Agfa snapscan e50 scanner just worked with no config or manual firmware installation. :D

* My (Brand new) external Firewire HD and associated Belkin Firewire card worked with no issues. :D

### Post by gjk on 2008-07-01
The installation worked quite nicely. The battery monitor no longer works, which worked under 7.10. Although I lost the battery monitor; suspend now works. 

I am editing my original post since it really deviated from the purpose of this forum which isn't really the place to bring up issues on particular applications. In my original post I complained about FF3 continuously crashing. I decided to re-install Hardy from scratch to see where things went wrong. In the end my FF3 issues were attributed to the Google Toolbar.

The installation Hardy Heron from CD worked flawlessly on my Fujitsu N3410 Lifebook. (2G Ram, 1.7G Pentium M)
Initially the sound didn't work. This was easily corrected by opening the volume control and turning on the internal speaker. Even under 7.10 the internal speaker isn't recognized.

Since my wireless router has the SSID broadcast feature disabled and WPA2 security, it was a simply matter of adding the appropriate configuration parameters and the wireless was immediately recognized. Since I was installing from the original 8.04 CD release immediately following the installation I was prompted with 216 updates. I applied all the updates without any errors.

I ran a few checks and all looked to be working first rate. The next step is the tailoring of my development environment with some eye candy (compiz) settings I find just to neat to ignore.

The entire processes of installing, checking and reloading my docs & bookmarks from a USB stick was somewhere in the order of 2 hours. That is a really rough estimate and as I said I did a fair bit of testing along the way.


Gary

### Post by Tanker Bob on 2008-07-04
I just upgraded to 8.04.1 from 7.10 over the web. My home-built computer specs are in the sig below. My box is wired to my router. The upgrade process itself went very smoothly and took about 30 mins or so over broadband (didn't time it precisely). I rebooted at the end as requested.

The computer booted right up. It went into a maintenance shell because it decided that one of my ide disks was a sata drive. After I exited that shell, Kubuntu went right to the login and then to my desktop. It updated the drivers for my NVidia 8800GTS card fine and the display was perfect.

I had three (sorta four) problems of which I'm aware at the moment:

1) Hardy decided that one of my IDE drives was SATA, so used the wrong designation in fstab. It took me a while to sort out because I have 4 drives in that box, but I finally got it to mount OK. That took quite a while.

2) No sound initially. Best I can tell, it didn't even install pulse audio. I tried to install all the pulse audio components, but it never worked. I reverted to alsa, and after manually unmuting all the appropriate items it worked fine. Not sure why core sound components muted on upgrade.

3) Compiz-fusion isn't working correctly yet. It loads and can be configured, but the cube and most effects don't work right or at all. That's not a major thing for me as it can be sorted out over time.

4) Not Hardy-specific, but Firefox 3 isn't working entirely correctly. It doesn't remember settings between sessions for some reason. However, streaming video and java work fine.

I'm very pleased that my RAID 1 setup survived the upgrade, as did the bulk of my software settings. The overall upgrade to 8.04.1 was pretty painless. I avoided the earlier 8.04 version because of all the silly problems that it seemed to cause. I was hoping that the installation problems of that version would be solved in 8.04.1, which appears to mostly be the case.

### Post by misterbk on 2008-07-05
I posted above that in GRUB I was getting Error 15: File not found.

This was resolved by unplugging my second hard drive. [edit - unplugging and then going through the install again.]  I did nothing differently [during the new install] and Kubuntu now boots.

NOTE:  I had no mount points defined on the second hard drive.  The installer was not instructed to touch it.

I would guess that the Ubuntu installer is not able to correctly write GRUB configs for some setups that have multiple hard drives.  I say this because in some brief searching I came across a few other people reporting the same problem who had multiple hard drives like I do.  Maybe it has something to do with my setup having a PCI add-on controller for SATA for the secondary drive, and not built-in motherboard and BIOS support.  (Not sure what to think because even built-in SATA controllers are still PCI devices, just hard wired not slotted.)

I'm not sure what the procedure is for reporting that, and this post will probably be buried within days, but there you go...


Also, reconnecting the second hard drive after the installation worked.  The system now boots fine.

After this successful boot I notice the following:

In the installer with both drives connected, the "storage" drive on the SATA add-on card was seen as 'sda'.  The "Linux" drive connected by PATA was seen as 'sdb'.  

Now that the system is booting with both drives, running the installer with only the PATA drive connected, the "Linux" drive has 'sda' and the "Storage" drive has 'sdb'.  I did not have to change any configs to get the system booting again after connecting the second drive.  (I remember having to change configs at some point in Debian when I added a drive; Debian wanted the boot drive to be a different letter all of a sudden.  Memory vague though.)


Now that's over with, the system seems to have sound and graphics and network without any effort on my part, which is more than I can say for Windows!  I can already tell that KDE4 will be an absolute pleasure to use!

---

### Post by JohnLM_the_Ghost on 2008-07-06
> **misterbk said:**
> I would guess that the Ubuntu installer is not able to correctly write GRUB configs for some setups that have multiple hard drives.  I say this because in some brief searching I came across a few other people reporting the same problem who had multiple hard drives like I do.  Maybe it has something to do with my setup having a PCI add-on controller for SATA for the secondary drive, and not built-in motherboard and BIOS support.  (Not sure what to think because even built-in SATA controllers are still PCI devices, just hard wired not slotted.)

It must really be because of the PCI slot SATA controller. I have 1 PATA and 2 SATA drive. Ubuntu used them with ease.
Specifically GRUB might have problems with "sloted" controller because it probes BIOS for drives, and BIOS might not return the drives, because not-built in SATA.

Just my thought...

p.s. I you are not afraid of getting Hands dirty... you can edit **/etc/fstab** to make mount points for your drives. (you can make them read-only, if you are worried how drives would play with Linux)

### Post by DrewT on 2008-07-06
I just installed Hardy a couple of weeks ago. It has been was my first experience with Linux. I can't begin to remember all the problems I've tackled; nor would anyone want to read it. But here are some remaining issues:

Ubuntu does not see my wireless card. Apparently I would not be having this problem if I had installed the x86 instead of the AMD-64 version. I stopped working on this issue when I realized that I could get onto my Airport network by running an ethernet cable from my computer to my Airport Express, which is acting as a range extender. Only problem with that is if I forget to unplug the cable when I boot into Windows 2000, the latter can get confused.

I do not have a satisfactory method of working on my Microsoft Word documents in Ubuntu. Open Office reformats them (e.g., footnotes), which is unacceptable in my work environment. I got Office 2000 installed under Wine, but Word froze within 30 seconds of getting it installed (I think I invoked the Help command via menus just for yuks), and I decided that just wasn't a stable enough environment for mission-critical work.

I seem to have my non-Synaptics touchpad working more or less, though for all my efforts I now wonder if it was working all along and I just didn't know how to use it. (I was mainly interested in the scroll feature.)

I do not have a satisfactory suite of software for working with Usenet downloads. I have found nothing that un-rars as well as MacPar, and MacPar isn't as good as several Windows programs. Nor have I found a robust mpeg/avi editor, or a reliable file splitter/joiner. This is not a criticism of all the heroic efforts that go into the existing apps, but I find myself spending a lot more time trying to get things working than I do actually working with them.

Still, I'm having a blast. I haven't used a command line for anything serious in 10 years!

### Post by Renovatio on 2008-08-03
After installing Ubunto 8.04 the first time on a Compaq Presario 2200 with a Broadcom BCM4306 chip, the computer would not work, although at some point the blue light that showed that the wireless card works was on. After trying ndiswrapper (according to several how to's) with no success, I tried other Linux distros - again to no avail. I then switched back to Hardy Heron, connected to a wired network and downloaded and installed all available updates. Only after that I installed the wireless card driver (the computer prompted me to install it as soon as I started it). I left the wireless mode on "roaming" and before I knew it, the network manager showed me all available wireless networks, including my own.
So, to "summarize" all actions that got the connection up and running:
1. perform a fresh install
2. ignore the prompt for installing the bcm43xx driver
3. download and install all software upgrades available (via wired connection)
4. reboot
5. install the wireless card driver
6. reboot
7. right-click on the network manager (two black screens on the top right of the screen) and highlight the wireless network you want to connect to.

I must add that at this point I have not tried to connect to a secured network yet.

---

### Post by Renovatio on 2008-08-03

### Post by coffeeaddict22 on 2008-08-07
Running Hardy on a clean install on 2 machines.  A Dell Inspiron 530, 2.4 GHz Core 2 quad, with an Nvidia 8600GT video card; pretty much the whole thing worked off the setup disc (Hardy 8.04).  Both of them manage a dual boot OK (the desktop a triple boot for all those XP games); I wasn't game to dump Win completely as I need to sync my PDA and I'm still chugging through the various solutions for Linux (no joy so far).

Second one is a Dell Studio 17 laptop with ATI Radeon HD 3650 video card, a Broadcom 4322 wireless card, and Fingerprint reader based on the Authentec 2810 chip.  The wireless card took a bit of work (I've posted elsewhere on the solution, which continues to work).  The CD controls all seem to work out of the box, touchpad is fine.  No idea if the webcam works and I'm too ugly to want to find out.  Fingerprint reader doesn't work, googling it the chip is very new and I'd guess it'll be a while before the gurus come up with a driver.  SD card reader is fine, haven't tried firewire or HDMI.

Silliest problem: fiddling around with the USB drivers I managed to lose the keyboard and mouse on the desktop.  Wasn't a biggie from a solution point of view fortunately, but there weren't too many buttons left to push...

Biggest problem: The gparted doesn't always play properly.  On the desktop I've wiped the HDD twice trying to get the triple boot install working; part of that was probably that XP wasn't on the first partition.  Wiping the HDD the second time in the Vista disk manager only partially wiped it in gparted.  On the initial screen, where the options are a manual or two guided installs, it wouldn't see the free space I'd created in Vista.  Moving to the manual partitioner, though, they were seen in gparted.  When I manually deleted partitions in gparted the install program still saw them when I moved back a page to a guided install.  I ended up having to do it the hard way and manually size the partitions; while I don't mind too much it made the whole thing a lot more of a guess.  I hadn't installed Linux since I had a play with Red Hat 3; there isn't a lot of documentation on how/ where to put all the partitions required; particularly, I couldn't find clear information easily on what manual partitions I should make.  Could have been the search engine having an off day, I guess.

options bttv tuner=38 to /etc/modprobe.d/options and rebooting fixed that problem.

### Post by ERIC H on 2008-08-15
This is the 3rd or fourth time I've run linux in the last 5 or six years, dual-booting still of course. None of the other installations ever lasted very long though as I either got frustrated or totally screwed something up. I was able to get a laptop for free which I think is the biggest reason why I'm enjoying it so much this time. I can leave my main computer alone, which I haven't touched in the last 3 or 4 days since I installed xubuntu, or chill downstairs and still be social and not locked away in my office sweating away, especially in the summer.

Only problem I had was getting my Netgear WG511T to work on startup. The drivers were recognized just not in use. I finally got all that sorted today, pretty much myself with searches and using the command line. I wasn't done there though as I had to configure /etc/network/interfaces w/wpaassistant to get WPA2 to stick on startup. Very satisfying.

It was a rather long and somewhat complicated process for someone at my knowledge level but it was all worth it. I explained to my wife that what I had spent the last 3 hours doing could have been done by clicking on an executable or just plugging in a device in XP. She asked why people are excited about linux. "Sense of accomplishment, baby", I said. Sense of accomplishment...

### Post by HousieMousie2 on 2008-08-18
The upgrade from Gutsy to Hardy was a total NO GO... I ended up having to do a clean install from Live CD.  Hardy still has issues, mostly just annoying.

The major problem with the upgrade came at the point of:
'Generating locates...'
'en_AU.UTF-8...'


The Live CD install went fine.

I think maybe there are some "improvements" to Hardy that to me are highly inconvenient.  It would be nice if the options for these were obvious, but I am sure I will get them sorted out.

I am using KDE (Kubuntu) and decided to give KDE4 a try... but I didn't like it and it didn't seem to work right... so it is a thing of the past and KDE 3.5.9 is now ruler of the roost again.

But I am happy that I now have and LTS version installed.

Cheers and best of luck to those who choose to upgrade instead of clean install via disk!  Back up your data. lol

EDITED:
Just thought I would add some info here, in case someone reads this thread looking for a way to solve the same problem I ran into... since I could not leave well enough alone... reinstalled Gutsy, then upgraded to Hardy... I just had to know if the suggested solutions really worked.

Have a terminal open when you start the upgrade, when it gets stuck, run "top" and see what is hogging your CPU, "q" back to your prompt, "ps -fe | grep locale" (it was locale for me, the en_AU.UTF-8 locates,)  Note the second set of numbers listed in the result after 'root,' use that number with "sudo kill -9 #####" (replacing ##### with the number... for me it was the local-gen set, not the localdef set.)  It then went on to complete the installation, but not before giving me 7 different warnings/errors, starting with:
'Subprocess post-installation script returned error exit status 137'
Then several 'Could not install's':
'language-pack-en-base'
'language-pack-kde-en-base'
'sun-java6-jre'
'sun-java6-bin'
'sun-java6-plugin'
'ubuntu-minimal'

It then finished the installation and I rebooted, into recovery mode for the new kernel, and dropped into root from the menu.
Typed into terminal:
"localedef --no-archive -i -en_Au -c -f UTF-8 en_AU.UTF-8"
I think it asked me a few obvious questions along the way... don't recall specifically, but yes was the right answer each time.
And it finished the installation, I booted into normal mode, then checked for updates with Adept, got the drivers needed for my machine and so on.  Presto, working Hardy.

I do not recall now where/who provided this valuable info (if you know, please feel free to give the proper thanks and acknowledgment) but this certainly did not come out of my VERY limited working knowledge of Linux and the Shell!

Cheers!

### Post by jelofson on 2008-08-19
Wiped my Dell Inspiron 9200 laptop clean yesterday (was a Hardy/XP dual boot). Installed from the liveCD and everything went flawless from an install perspective. I was easily able to connect to my wireless with one click (then enter the key). Then I enabled the restricted ATI driver (more on that below). The package manager gave a 404 when trying to install the driver so I changed the repository location to the main server and it worked fine. By default, server for Canada was selected since that is where I live. Not sure why I got a 404. 

I installed flashplugin non-free. No probs
Installed libdvdread3 but not libdvdcss2 yet. 
Installed nfs-common and portmap
I like how totem searches and installed the correct codecs for me. Worked fine and was able to view .mov files and apple trailers.

ATI fglrx driver. In general, it works fine and allows compiz. Unfortunately, I cannot logout to switch users. They system completely freezes/crashes with no choice but to power off. I have read some articles about this but nothing works yet. So, I had to disable the proprietary driver. Now logging off works fine, but no 3d. I really HATE that ATI driver. It should work better than that! I tried disabling compiz but that has no impact. Its a mobility radeon 9600/9700.

I have some mount commands in my /etc/rc.local. I have it set this way in order to mount to my mythbox after the wireless connects. It worked fine before the wipe, but I am not sure if it is working properly now. I only require this because I don't give my wife sudo permission. 

Evolution runs fine, but I still have the unnamed imap folder. That bug is documented. 

Overall, it's great. I just get frustrated with the ATI driver. I have a crappy integrated card in my desktop and it works just fine. Compiz and logouts all work as expected. 

I give it a 9/10. I would like to see some sort of readme on the desktop for new users who don't know about getting DVDs to work and how to properly install flashplugin, Java, etc. Just those little things that would frustrate a novice/new user. I was very impressed how easy it went. I have installed on several machines. Always goes well. No sign of windows in my house anymore :)  

Have I mentioned how I hate that ATI driver?

---

### Post by KuBala on 2008-08-19
Hi,
I will also share my experiences with Ubuntu 8.04.

I saw Linux for the first time in late July. First l was trying the live CD, the l made a fresh install on this laptop:
ECS G713 mobo
sis 746fx NB
mobile Athlon XP 1800MHz
512Mbyte ddr-sd.
mobility Radeon 9000
Samsung 160 GByte HM160HC (pata)

I created a 7 GB partition for the system, 1GB for swap. (I left the rest ntfs for xp) The installation was going easily, not too complicated questions. The the freshly installed system could boot.

Wlan (surecom ep9001g), tuchpad, audio, lan, usb, logitech webcam were recognized without issue at first boot. At xp you need to find and install drivers for all of these. Flash drives and a camera are recognized withouth issue.
The Totem finds and installs the necessary codecs for mp3, divx, mpeg. Better than xp.

*The first challenge was the Compiz. I installed it, but it was using only software rendering, no GPU acceleration and slow really. I could not install the new ati proprietary drivers, because they do not support my model any more. I found an old driver package allegedly supporting the mobility 9000, but it was impossible to install it to Ubuntu 8.04, because it's kernel is not supported. Anyway somehow I could make the open source driver work: now the *compiz* is perfect, the effects are very nice, even the cube works. It looks fantastic, much better than XP!
but there is a set-back: In firefox the *flashes* are *not hardware accelerated*, only software. They are mostly commercials, but unfortunately the youtube flashes are slow too.


*The laptop has only one hdd, and the system is on it. The *operation system* is **always* accessing* the *hdd*. The ubuntu 8.04 uses the default power saving settings of the hdd, and in my case they were quite strict. The heads were set to parking positions after few seconds of inactivity, and then the linux accessed the drive (without obvious reason), back to parking positions etc. The number of power cycles in smart has grown significantly.
I could overcome on this problem thanks to this forum, l had to use the hdparm -B 254 command. This turns off the power-saving mechanism of the drive.
(It would have been more elegant to restrict the op system from accessing the drive unnecessarily, but l could not do that. Is it possible, could anyone make it? Because of these continuous accesses the spin down can be forgotten. Even if I do nothing but leave the computer on for the night the *drive* will *not spin down*.)

**Ubuntu* 8.04 does not allow to use my drive at udma100, but *udma33* due 40 wired cable. This is not true, *Windows xp* uses *udma100*, reading speed with hdtach is from 60Mb/sec to 35. This issue significantly *slows down* the system. Hdparm -tT readtest reports 22MB/s reading speed.
(a kernel modification is mentioned somewhere in the forum for a similar problem with compact flash disks. hmmm, they were a bit complicated :S)

*at win xp using the program central brain identifier l could reduce the cpu clock and voltage to 667MHz@0.925V. I use the default power management system now at 8.04, and the minimum is 800 MHz. I have no information about the voltage, l estimate this around 1.1-1.2V.
(The windows also has automatic frequency scaling. the minimal clock is 800MHz there too, and the voltage is 1.125. I think Linux uses the same values. This must be some default from the bios or somewhere, but the central brain identifier overrides it somehow.) 667 MHz would be enough for downloading and mp3. And the system would be cooler/more silent because of lower fan rpm.

**suspend* and *hibernation* malfunction. I can apply them, but the system will *never wake up again* (it wakes with strange, colorful stripes and patterns on the screen. The system freezes.)

*skype works, but sometimes it quits randomly. I can not full screen the received webcam stream.

*linux dc++ is unstable. sometimes it turns gray. No multiple threads (like in strong dc). There is problem with the letter with accent or umlaut.

*Transmission torrent hashes the files every time I start it. Sometimes it finds error in them and download pieces (if l want to seed). Deluge is somewhat better, it has the same problems only when l forget to mount the ntfs partition (for xp, but the downloads are on that partition).
(there must be some way to automuont the ntfs partition too, but l have not found it so far.)

*The installation using the menu is really easy. Installation of deb files is also good, easier then installing a program on xp.
But I wanted to upgrade Linux dc++ from 1.0.1 to 1.0.2. The older is in the package, but the authors say they repaired some bugs and the new release is more stable. I downloaded it. Extracted and tried to *install*, but it was so *complicated*. It wanted at least four packages, like c++, g++, libs, etc. I was trying to find these in synaptics, but it was not obvious what to choose. I gave a try but I had to *give up*. I may need some more experiences though.


I summary.
These difficulties are overwhelming for a Linux newbie like me. I will try to solve them, so if you have hints please note or pm.

Even if I have to roll back to XP finally , I will come back and try a new 8.xx, or 9.xx release and hope they are better. I like Linux, looks good, no MS. I have no antivirus program for two weeks now and l have no infections. Compiz cube rocks. But there are too much problems.

whaa, that was long, l hope someone will read it :D

Hints are welcome.

Bye now

---

### Post by yabbadabbadont on 2008-08-19
Because the install CD used the generic ata driver for my IDE drive, the installer got the device order completely backwards when configuring grub.  In my system, IDE is the boot drive and SATA is not.  I knew it would screw it up when I saw the device order while manually partitioning.  For a new user, being presented with a grub error upon rebooting would be a show stopper.  I just booted a live CD, chrooted in and fixed it.  In the next version, the installer should ask the user which is the boot drive before installing grub.  At least if the install is done in expert mode it should.

Once I corrected the grub issue, everything has worked perfectly.

### Post by InspiredIndividual on 2008-08-19
I'll add my two cents. I was switching from Windows XP without notable computer skills, can't even network two computers, have never managed to do that. The install went very smoothly. Wireless mouse & keyboard worked out of the box. Installation with Synaptic is very easy, easier than Windows XP.

My printer worked after I manually chose to use an older driver. This was a hint from the Linux Foundation Open Printing Database. I had trouble with multimedia in Firefox, but I managed to solve the problems mostly being helped by reassuringlyoffensive's great multimedia guide at these forums.

> **KuBala said:**
> 
(...) there is a set-back: In firefox the *flashes* are *not hardware accelerated*, only software. They are mostly commercials, but 
not found it so far.)


I've got the same issue.

> **KuBala said:**
> 
But I wanted to upgrade (...) The older is in the package, but the authors say they repaired some bugs and the new release is more stable. I downloaded it. Extracted and tried to *install*, but it was so *complicated*.(...)


Again, this is difficult. I haven't managed to do something like this yet.

Weighing up I can safely say my choice to switch to Ubuntu from Windows XP was a success. Full control, no virus scan services you're 'not allowed to stop' or similar crap. Unfortunately, my employer uses a synchable Sharepoint calendar I really need to download, so I still have to use Windows for that. Obviously, this is not an Ubuntu problem. The only seemingly conscious thing I really like much better about Windows is the way keyboard input is being handled. I really fail to get why linux seems to prefer to display nothing when pressing a dead key followed by a non-assigned key. Logically, it should just be clever and behave like the key isn't dead in such cases... (for example: ' + g outputs nothing instead of 'g in linux.).

### Post by jr4902 on 2008-08-21
I posted this as a new thread yesterday under "Installation and Updates" and will repeat it here:

I have been trying to install Xubuntu 8 since early May but so far have not succeeded. However I have been able to install Xubuntu 6 LTS. The live CD works flawlessly for Xubuntu 6.06, 7.1 and 8.04, at least until I try to do an actual installation.

I have checked the CD integrity and ISO chksum. I have tried the Live and Alt CDs, burning them at 4x speed. After reading other "install freeze" posts I have tried the options suggested when hitting F6 at the install screen. I have also tried Wubi.

The Live CD instal freezes at 81%, "configuring target system". I have tried this at least 6 times, leaving the computer overnight, just to make sure it is frozen, not just taking time to install something. It freezes at the same freeze point for the Live Cd and Wubi.

The Alt Cd freezes at 68%, "selecting and installing Software - configuring gnome-keyring". I have tried this at least 4 times, leaving it overnight after the first freeze.

My latest attempt is to use the Alt Cd and at the install screen, hit F4 and select "install a command line system". (I also tried the "normal", OEM and LTPS Server modes; all froze at configuring the gnome keyring). The command line system installation worked. I then typed the following lines which I found on another thread: sudo nano /etc/apt/sources.list (uncommented internet addresses); sudo aptitude update; sudo aptitude install xubuntu-desktop.

The computer froze during the installation of the Xubuntu desktop at "setting up capplets-data (1:2.22.1-Oubuntu4.1)".

I am still new to Ubuntu, and appreciate any and all suggestions.

My system info: 5 year old computer, EliteGroup motherboard, AMD 3100+ CPU, 480 MB RAM, integrated video and audio, Xubuntu 6.06 installed on primary 40 Gb drive; had moved Windows XP to 10 GB slave drive, but am now attempting to install Xubuntu 8 onto slave drive.

On the whole, I'm quite satisfied with Ubuntu, I just wish it would also work on my desktop.

### Post by Sporty on 2008-08-24
I started last week to switch to UBUNTU 8.04.  I had a great deal of trouble getting my broadcom 4306 wireless to work.  I tried a lot of the suggestions found in this forum and others to no avail.  In frustration I did a fresh install on another computer (read newer laptop) and found a link provided during the install/update process to:  [www.linuxwireless.org/en/users/Drivers/b43#devicefirmware](www.linuxwireless.org/en/users/Drivers/b43#devicefirmware) this site proved to be my wireless salvation for that computer.  Then I did a fresh install on my Dell Latitude D500 and did all the updates via wired connection.  Then I went to the site referenced above, did the down load and in a total of less that two hours am up and running wireless.  I am really impressed with Ubuntu and the community so far and am hoping to learn alot and maybe eventually be able to contribute something useful.

### Post by moody_mark on 2008-09-10
For me: like most people I started out as a windows user. Ive had a "fling" with several distros of Linux over the years. Im not a linux expert, Ive just picked up bits and bobs on the way and my experience of Linux always been a bumpy ride, but ive never been one to complain. Linux is a different world to windows and always required a more in depth knowledge or your PC and hardware etc.

Anyway... I tried out Ubuntu just before Release 8.04 came out, onto an old Dell Optiplex PC. The install was easy and I even managed to get an old BT usb wireless dongle working eventually. When 8.04 came out I run the upgrade which was a breeze too. I got on so well with it I decided to install kubuntu on an old 15GB laptop drive in a USB caddy and booted my work laptop off that. I got to say I was supised how well it performed compared to the Windows XP Pro that was installed on the internal drive. So I decided to bite the bullet, get my work VPN working and try it out for work. It went so well I eventually swapped out the internal drive and just used Kubuntu for the next week or two. Then last week I installed ubuntu to try out the gnome desktop on another unwanted laptop drive I managed to find from a friend.

In the past few weeks Ive been using ubuntu every day for work and general stuff. The only problems Ive had is some of the internal sites and applications at work only run on a windows desktop.

So, well done all you ubuntu staff, your helping to bring the benefits of Linux to the masses and help the little people like me enjoy a bit more peace of mind knowing my OS is more secure and I dont always have to think "virus, virus, virus..."

### Post by VorDesigns on 2008-09-20
Hi,

Just got back into Linux post a lot of gifting and a bunch of hardware replacements; one being a new Dell Lattitud D830.

I'm planning on travelling out of the counry and with the new laws governing the TSA's authority to copy my hard drive before letting me leave, I figured a nice clean Linux installation was the trick for me.

I installed Hardy and after a little tweaking, the NVidia drive was loaded but, the Broadcom wireless wouldn't connecto to my WPA access point.

After dieseling around here, I stumbled over the Dell Laptop testing and then ended up at Dell's Linux forum.  I found that they have a dell iso for their systems so I downloaded and installed that; same problem only the NVidia worked out of the box.

I applied the Broadcom fix I found on the forums which instructed me to install ndiswrapper and an older driver.  A manual uninstall of the lw driver and a reboot, I'm untethered and after I finish this post, I'll be respooling my 100' shielded CAT5 cable.

My only complaint so far is that the sound volume at max is like listening to clock radio in the other room.

### Post by Landie_UK on 2008-09-30
Upgraded from Gutsy to Hardy a month or so ago. Had a few problems such as Update Manager showed updates that couldn't be installed. My FF3 beta wouldn't work with my bank and I couldn't upgrade to FF3 alpha when it came out, and a few other small problems. 

I wiped my hard drive and did a clean Install everything worked fine except I had problems setting up Samba. Wanted to share my 2nd hard drive with Wifeys XP couldn't get it to work, but I did manage to get a new shared folder in my HOME directory to work so I transfered the files to there.

All in all I am very happy with Hardy, had a few freezes nowhwere as near as many as I had with windoze. I will keep using Hardy for quite a while as it's a LTS version.

### Post by Kuroyume on 2008-10-01
Works flawlessly on my Sony Vaio VGN-C240FE after bringing it up to date. The original Hardy kernel required booting without "quiet" enabled due to some acpi issue, but it's been fixed.

### Post by Tanker Bob on 2008-10-01
> **voodootech said:**
> I cant get my built in SD card reader to work , I am currently running Hardy Xubuntu on a 3 year old MSI laptop . can someone help ? ps ditching XP is awesome :)BTW
I'm not sure what you've tried, but Hardy like Gutsy before it has trouble automounting some devices. Try putting a card into the reader, then opening up Dolphin, click on Storage Devices on the left bar. If the SD card appears in the list, either open the card or select it and click on "Mount" on the right bar. That works with my USB card reader.

### Post by iansane on 2008-10-22
The install was easy and flawless. It must be since I add third party apps and unsupported apps till I kill it and have to reinstall every few weeks.

Works great as long as I don't install or mess with things I don't know about and that's a lot.

Installed on Gateway with AMD64 X2 4200 and all onboard Nvidia network and video. Also using the dreaded netgear WG111 wireless USB Dongle with ndis and it works great.

unfortunately it doesn't work great with 64 bit Hardy. Most other stuff did but I had to go back to 32bit to use the wireless since they don't make a 64bit driver at netgear so that wasn't Hardy's fault.

Have the usual problems like not playing certain videos until I figure out the player and codecs but once again, not Hardy. Had that prob with all distros till I figured it out.

My rating is that it's the best. Squashes XP/Vista even though the install is more dummy proofed for MS OS's

### Post by Katerine on 2008-11-04
I'm a beginner. Installed Ubuntu because I wanted to learn Linux better, and because I didn't particularly like the Xandros that my Asus EEE PC came with. In particular, I didn't like that it is *so* customized that you have to hold your breath every time you want to install something that isn't in one of Asus's repositories. I wanted to get something with more diverse options.

So I installed Ubuntu EEE (the Ubuntu kernel that's specifically designed for EEEPC's).

Didn't much like the idea of limiting my disk space, especially since I had no intention of ever using Xandros again, so I just repartitioned the entire drive. The installation itself, as I recall, went by fairly smoothly (although I could be misremembering. It was only a week ago, but this was also about the same time I discovered a small dark spot on my bedroom wall and wound up stuck in my living room for several days while mildew removal people ripped up my bedroom and my bathroom, so I was a bit distracted. :lol: ).

I do remember that I had some difficulty getting a working ISO file on my flash drive, and that I wound up having to do it through Windows. I also remember that I had to reinstall a couple of times, but I don't remember why. Probably something to do with the wireless not working.

The wireless was a big problem for a long time. I did everything people suggested, updated the kernel, etc., and nothing worked. It would find my network, but it wouldn't connect to it. I finally found a post recommending that Asus people just ditch the gnome wireless program and install Wicd instead. Once I did that (after stealing the ethernet connection from my desktop computer in order to download the package in the first place), the internet worked like a charm.

I think there were a few other, more minor issues after that, and a major headache getting GSynaptics to work (finally did after not only editing etc/X11/xorg.conf, but also editing /etc/modprobe.d/options per [this thread]("http://ubuntuforums.org/archive/index.php/t-942400.html"). That was yesterday. Today I've been wrestling with Gimp (when I haven't been being sick), and fixing the hard drive bug which I just saw during my many Google explorations. Otherwise, everything is now working great. :)

---

### Post by Tamlynmac on 2008-11-05
Started to install 8.1 and was notified (pop up screen) that my NVIDIA card was not recognized and no driver existed, except 800x600. I canceled the upgrade and contacted NVIDIA. Was told the driver was available only in beta and they provided me with the install procedure. I opted to wait until my driver was available in an update (not beta). Historically, have had to many problems with beta video drivers.

Still using Hardy on all 5 systems and it functions great. No reason to risk video issues. I only endeavored to install 8.1 on one system (all our systems are basically the same) to track it's performance and identify when to actually upgrade all systems. Obviously, not in a hurry.

One real nice benefit was the fact that Ubuntu 8.1 notified prior to installation that my driver wasn't supported. Don't recall Windows ever notifying me of that until well into the install. Thanks Ubuntu - I actually said that when it happened.

---

### Post by ancientrustic on 2008-11-12
I did a clean install on my desktop with absolutely no problems. However laps tops presented more of a problem. on a one year old Toshiba Satellite an upgrade proved a total disaster. The first reboot took about fifteen minutes and everything ran very slowly. After several reboots, in recovery mode, most of the files in my Home Directory disappeared. I then tried a clean install. The live cd stalled at "Checking battery state". An install with the alternate cd worked but with the same problems as the upgrade. I am now back with Hardy. On an old Packard Bell an upgrade and a fresh install both worked but I couldn't get screen resolution higher than 800x600. While these experiences occur Linux will never replace Windows,

---

### Post by ikkepjo on 2008-11-19
> **ikkepjo said:**
> Just upgraded to 8.04 Xubuntu , I wanted LTS ... upgrade went smooth, but bluetooth mouse and keyb only work after un- and replugging the usb dongle ....

very annoying, and apparently very widespread, issue, with no solution for hardy ... maybe for ibex ... how does one deal with that if LTS is needed?

Peter
First it got worse, the keyboard blocked out completely. Friendly support line at Dell provided the right pointers.
SOLVED. In synaptic search for all things bluetooth and completely remove them. Find an XP or VISTA pc and install the latest keyboard repair tool from Dell/Logitech. Repair keyboard (the default installed bluetooth crap messed up the passkey setting).
Replug, restart and hopla, it works.

Frankly, not impressed by this regression ... not to mention that it does not solve anything for Bluetooth devices other than keyboard/mouse

Peter

### Post by anoxan on 2008-11-25
well, it runs. I got a thread to express my feelings for 8.04/8.10 over [COLOR="Red"][here](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=956183)[/COLOR]

I've heard a few excuses as to why my board magically has no support after 7.10, but they're just that. excuses. lol, one of the excuses was that asus made my board for an overseas oem and have no documentation on the board, nor any support. I don't speak chinese, so I haven't confirmed the tech support allegation, but then I find that excuse to be unbelievable, at best.

in any case, this experience, along with my last motherboard and gfx card, has taught me to never *ever* buy anything made by asus again. those commies can blow me.

and the hardware compatibility list REALLY needs updating. it's 2008/9, not 2006. how am I gonna choose a compatible, current board with 2 year old data? I'm not.

### Post by LuckyLeprechaun on 2008-12-14
Had problem with hanging at configuring network interfaces on startup.  Fixed by editing /etc/network/interfaces and commenting out everything but loopback.
